```
remove.this.scubajohn@claranet.fr wrote:
> 
> On Sat, 07 Jul 2001 21:47:23 -0600, Frank Swarbrick
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> John


In the particular case I was working with, the validity of field 2 is
possibly dependant on the value of field 1.  In other words, if field 1
is one thing that field 2 has certain valid values, but if field 1 is
something else then field 2 has different valid values (than the first
case).  Therefore if field 1 is not at all valid there's no way to
determine the validity of field 2.

Not something that happens often, I'll grant you, but it was true in
this particular case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T01:06:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lys27.2321$HV1.282909@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B4578C2.97E83781@Azonic.co.nz> <3B457CF9.98E86EFD@Azonic.co.nz> <9nmcktkdsf0rlmnqm1m178m75tlstto9ub@4ax.com> <N8w17.7021$G_1.698881@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <1blfkts8mkb6m9ojpedvv1h1h1n1bq9888@4ax.com>`

```
Re: validating multiple fields

Lots of good discussion here. Setting a flag is still much better than
using exit paragraph or goto. If fields are dependent it makes sense to
nest the if's so that e.g. field 2 isn't tested if its validity is
irrelevant or meaningless when field 1 is invalid. However, if they are
independent, it is indeed desireable to avoid giving users the same
frustration we faced with the old compilers that would only detect one bug
each time.

I can see where a flag and keeping if's at the same level can avoid lots
of nesting, but this is even better when it's desireable to detect
multiple errors anyway. My preferred approach is more like this:

#errors = 0

if field-1-bad
  #errors = #errors + 1
  highlight bad field 1
  move bad-field-1-err-msg to err-msg-line
endif

if field-2-bad
  #errors = #errors + 1
  highlight bad field 2
  move bad-field-2-err-msg to err-msg-line
endif

You can even have multiple error message lines at the bottom and subscript
them, showing the first n error messages, making sure n doesn't exceed the
number of error message lines. You can also include a line that says e.g.
"#errors errors detected" so that you get some use out of the #errors
count aside from it also being useful as a return code e.g. if it's >0 at
the end then you know the screen's bad.

If things are organized well with tables of messages you can perform
internal subroutines from each validation portion above to handle the
error messages and error counting easily, then you only need
field-specific code in each validation portion.

After all the single-field tests finally pass, you can test relationships
between them etc. using a similar approach.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-08T08:06:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B480679.62C0DC78@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B4578C2.97E83781@Azonic.co.nz> <oimcktgk8ibnsmj6ba31u411ks615goto2@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> I only use IBM mainframe, and I know you can pass data on the PARM= card, which
> is then accessed through the linkage section.  Is this not the same when using
> the PC command line.  If I were to exit program CBLPGM by typing "CBLPGM PARM1
                                     ^^^^ execute ??
> PARM2" would I not have to use the linkage section to access the command line
> parameters?

No.  LINKAGE SECTION cannot be used in a main program executed from the
command line (in all Cobols that I have used and as specified by the '85
standard). I get the command line parameters using:

          ACCEPT Run-Parameters FROM COMMAND-LINE

or similar.
```

```
On Fri, 06 Jul 2001 17:19:58 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>> One thing you'll have to be able to do--establish a callback.
>> Can your COBOL's dialect do this?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>gets into OO.
=========
Jimmy,

You don't need OO to do callbacks with NetExpress, we did them years
ago when we first got into Windows API stuff - not with NetExpress,
but with an earlier 32-bit compiler from Micro Focus.  

I'm sure the technique is the same today as it was in the early
versions.  I discarded the Micro Focus code long ago  - I checked -
otherwise, I'd have posted the basic stuff.  We ended up implementing
all our Windows API calls with CA-Realia primarily because of problems
with the (very) early Micro Focus 32-bit compiler.  Those problems
don't exist with the current version of NetExpress...

There is, alas, some finagling involved and as I have observed before,
API level stuff is not for the timid.

Kevin

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
--------------------------------------------------

registration fee for COBOLWorld 2001.
```

```
Kevin,

You may be right, but currently the reference to callbacks points to OO
Classes :-

"You need to understand Callbacks in order to use the Collection classes,
the GUI classes and the ExceptionManager. An instance of the Callback
class contains an object and the name of a method. When you send the
message "invoke" to the Callback, it sends a message to the object it
contains, invoking the named method. ".

<G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: System tray

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T16:46:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b46170b.0@newsman.viper.net>`
- **References:** `<3b408e08.5764045@news.eunet.be> <mRa17.4450$i8.524653@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B45F324.CF3590A1@home.com> <3b45f94b.611166821@news> <3B460440.37840969@home.com>`

```
> "Kevin J. Hansen" wrote:
> > There is, alas, some finagling involved and as I have observed before,
> > API level stuff is not for the timid.




* To do a windows callback with NetExpress, simply set a procedure pointer
to the address of the routine windows is to call...In this case ePointer is
set to the entry point "CF001030AS"...

       01  ePointer                    procedure-pointer.

           Set ePointer to entry 'CF001030AS'
           Call WinAPI 'EnumChildWindows'
             using by value AutoScalehDialog
                   by value ePointer
                   by value asPointer
             returning WinBoolResponse.

          .....do whatever is next.....


* Code the entry point that windows will call using the parameters windows
will pass to the procedure...

          Entry 'CF001030AS' WinAPI using by value lshControl
                                                   lsPointer.
          .....do whatever.....

          Exit program returning +1.

There is NO finagling involved...Pretty simple once you do one...Certainly
can be done by even the timid...

kenmullins
atlanta, ga
```

```
Jimmy,

Nothing wrong with being timid about some things.  

I must have missed something here, but in the context of Windows
stuff, a "callback" is a subroutine you provide that is called by
Windows to handle certain events, messages, whatever.  You're often
required to write callback procedures in order to take advantage of
Windows facilities.

I'm not sure how that relates to OO Classes, per se.

or, knowing I'm somewhat of a Luddite with respect to OO (the light
dawns) are you simply pulling my leg?  ;-)

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
```

<a id="t-45df7f5213"></a>
## Thread: Keeping a COBOL process alive — 16 msgs

### Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T10:29:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Aqg17.24$Kk5.7121@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com>`

```
If you launch the second program as a monitored process (using either
CreateProcess of ShellExecuteEx), the main program can monitor the
WM_QUERYENDSESSION and WM_CLOSE messages; when recevived, you can either
disallow closing the first program or can do some kind of "orderly"
temination of that launched process.

I do not know if "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" provides an appropriate process handle
for this, but if you can use the "raw" Windows API.....
.
```

```
> 

This is just what I was looking for.
But for the record, regarding the other posts:
1) I cannot make the process a service, that is not an option.
2) I cannot start the process from autoexec.bat.
3) The process is started from a Commend Prompt session and must stay
alive after the user had logged off.  There is no alternative to this.
Now, to continue on with this answer, I need some more elaboration, if
you please.
Indeed, CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT does return the process ID of the new
process.  What I need to do in that process is call a Windows API that
eats, sinks, ignores (whatever you want to call it) all Windows
messages to close, shutdown, die (whatever you want to call it) - the
exception being when the entire system is going down, then it is okay
to die.
How can I do this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-09T18:34:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <Aqg17.24$Kk5.7121@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107090752.3af73527@posting.google.com>`

```
What you need to do is install a hook process. A hook process can intercept
all messages on the system or for a thread, and prevent WM_CLOSE from
reaching your launched process. See the WinApi doc for the
SetWindowsHook[Ex] function for details.

If the launch you make in your "first" program is what triggers the start of
the "monitor this application and don't let it close until Windows
shutdown", then that's actually not too hard to write. It's easier if done
at this point because you have a process handle and thread id (or maybe a
thread handle, but that's good enough). Otherwise you have to hunt thru all
the open windows on the system, or hunt through the task list and get the
EXE names, in both cases hoping you can match a text string ( and hope no
one renamed it, etc). (Plus, task list enumeration specifics vary across
Win9x, NT & 2K).

But, if the second (launched) process is one you wrote, there are a lot
easier ways (and I can think of two off the top of my head) to do this
within that application. (And for the proper consideration, I'm sure there
are people available to show you how). (Hint, hint).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T19:20:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vAI27.2045$Nt5.131866@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <Aqg17.24$Kk5.7121@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107090752.3af73527@posting.google.com> <MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com>`

```
Scott Davis <scott.davis@principia.edu> wrote in message
news:43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:<MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>...
> >
> > But, if the second (launched) process is one you wrote, there are a lot
> > easier ways (and I can think of two off the top of my head) to do this
> > within that application. (And for the proper consideration, I'm sure
there
> > are people available to show you how). (Hint, hint).
> >
>
> I wrote and am in control of the second (launched) process.
> Please don't make me beg for the answer...

Don't beg. You have the easiest possible situation: In the second
("launched") program, simply intercept the Windows message WM_CLOSE and eat
it; i.e, don't let your program call DestroyWindow or PostQuitMessage until
you've received WM_ENDSESSION with a "true" flag, which means "Windows is
going to end now, because all programs responded "OK by Me" to
WM_QUERYENDSESSION."

But.. now that you bring this up, if your launched program creates no
Windows, well, <expletive>, if it had no Windows, you couldn't kill it
except from Task manager or if some other input is accepted to "end."

Hmmm... I wonder how your "RUN_EXEC_CBL" or whatever is starting this
process.... If it's a console or windowless program then perahps your
"RUN_EXEC" is creating this program as some kind of child process, which is
AUTOMATICALLY terminated when the first program ends.

In this case, I hope you are also in control of the first program, because
you're going to need to change how you start that second program.

Since I am, deep down inside, a hell of a guy (also single, straight,
gainfully employed and good-looking, for any of the ladies who happen to be
lurking!), if your second program is not anything "object oriented" you can
send me the source code in COBOL, BASIC or C and I'll annotate it and send
it back to you. (COBOL and BASIC I can code, C I'll just mark up for you).
(Use email address below, and please ZIP it up. I do not need any copylibs
for files/ general purpose working storage, but if you have procedure
division copylibs at all related to message processing, please do include
those).

But... Fujitsu, MF Net Express and probably all the popular Windows COBOL
compilers all use "object oriented" syntax (although Real Men write their
own message loops and call the API themselves) and I can't do dick with
those. But I'll bet you can talk someone who DOES know the OO-stuff into
marking up your source code to show you how to eat the WM_CLOSE  as above.

----
Michael C. Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scott.davis@principia.edu (Scott Davis)
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T16:48:43-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<43b3cfd7.0107111548.711d3b8d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <Aqg17.24$Kk5.7121@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107090752.3af73527@posting.google.com> <MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com> <vAI27.2045$Nt5.131866@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
> Don't beg. You have the easiest possible situation: In the second
> ("launched") program, simply intercept the Windows message WM_CLOSE and eat
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> WM_QUERYENDSESSION."

Okay, tell me how to "eat" a WM_CLOSE message using the WIN32API.  And
how can I tell that I have received a WM_ENDSESSION message, again,
using the WIN32API.
 

> Hmmm... I wonder how your "RUN_EXEC_CBL" or whatever is starting this
> process.... If it's a console or windowless program then perahps your
> "RUN_EXEC" is creating this program as some kind of child process, which is
> AUTOMATICALLY terminated when the first program ends.

The process spawning the second process is a COBOL console app that
makes a "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" call, which in MF COBOL parlance means to
create another process from the current one.  The parent process
however, can die, and the spawned child process lives on - only to be
killed when the user logs off (bad) or when the system shuts down
(fine).

 
> In this case, I hope you are also in control of the first program, because
> you're going to need to change how you start that second program.

It's not the case, so no prob there.
 

> But... Fujitsu, MF Net Express and probably all the popular Windows COBOL
> compilers all use "object oriented" syntax (although Real Men write their
> own message loops and call the API themselves) and I can't do dick with
> those. But I'll bet you can talk someone who DOES know the OO-stuff into
> marking up your source code to show you how to eat the WM_CLOSE  as above.

There is no object oriented crap in our COBOL code.  It's straight up.
I have the process ID of the spawned child process.  Using that, I
should be able to enumerate all other information I need to react to
the messages you previously described.

For all I care the child program could be (and is) as simple as:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

    PERFORM UNTIL EXIT

        *> CHECK SOME FILE FOR NEW RECORDS
        *> PROCESS THEM
        *> SLEEP A BIT

    END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T00:40:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m637.1415$FS1.422328@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <Aqg17.24$Kk5.7121@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107090752.3af73527@posting.google.com> <MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com> <vAI27.2045$Nt5.131866@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107111548.711d3b8d@posting.google.com>`

```
(quotes kept for benefit of lurkers, most recent response at bottom)


Scott Davis <scott.davis@principia.edu> wrote in message
news:43b3cfd7.0107111548.711d3b8d@posting.google.com...
> > Don't beg. You have the easiest possible situation: In the second
> > ("launched") program, simply intercept the Windows message WM_CLOSE and
eat
> > it; i.e, don't let your program call DestroyWindow or PostQuitMessage
until
> > you've received WM_ENDSESSION with a "true" flag, which means "Windows
is
> > going to end now, because all programs responded "OK by Me" to
> > WM_QUERYENDSESSION."
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > "RUN_EXEC" is creating this program as some kind of child process, which
is
> > AUTOMATICALLY terminated when the first program ends.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > In this case, I hope you are also in control of the first program,
because
> > you're going to need to change how you start that second program.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > But... Fujitsu, MF Net Express and probably all the popular Windows
COBOL
> > compilers all use "object oriented" syntax (although Real Men write
their
> > own message loops and call the API themselves) and I can't do dick with
> > those. But I'll bet you can talk someone who DOES know the OO-stuff into
> > marking up your source code to show you how to eat the WM_CLOSE  as
above.
>
> There is no object oriented crap in our COBOL code.  It's straight up.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-PERFORM.

Well, no, it can't be quite that simple to use the message technique. But..
the logoff situation (a new situation not previously described) is covered
by one of the parameters of the WM_ENDSESSION message. The lparam tells you
the difference if Windows is ending or the user is logging off. (One source
I have from 1996 says this only works under Win 9x (not NT);  but the other
source (newer) says it works on all Win/32 systems.)

But if your spawned program does not have a Window procedure (e.g., your
sample
program), this method cannot work. So, it is not a matter of "any" child
program; rather, it is a child program with a window procedure.

As far as "eating" messages, you need to add a window procedure to your
program. That, or launch a program which has as its sole function creating a
window, and IT launches the "real" server and watches for important
messages.

But now you are getting into an area beyond my experience: trying to do
something different between "shutdown" and "logoff", and perhaps involving a
console program.

If your child program is that straightforward, you might try another
approach. To check for new records, you can use WaitForSingleObject and
Windows will tell you if the file has changed. Or, you can use an event an
pulse it when the first program needs the services of the second.

Given your launched program is only checking for new records in a file, I
don't understand why the program has to keep running; is what's  required
just that it be available when the first program runs?  In this case, have
 the first program create a mutex when it starts. If the create succeeds,
the second program is running and does not need to be launched, because...
you have the second program create the same named mutex object whenever it
starts. If the create there fails, you don't care!

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Keeping a COBOL process alive

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scott.davis@principia.edu (Scott Davis)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T10:33:35-07:00

```
> I cannot try another approach.  I need my spawned console app to

Sorry, but you can't do that, because if it is a "pure"  console program, it
does not handle ANY messages at all.

You MUST try another approach, or change your console application to be not
a "pure" console application.

MCM
```

- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <43b3cfd7.0107090752.3af73527@posting.google.com> <MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com> <vAI27.2045$Nt5.131866@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107111548.711d3b8d@posting.google.com> <9m637.1415$FS1.422328@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107120933.316fc882@posting.google.com> <HaA37.221$Lk1.10195@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107130817.4feac016@posting.google.com>`

```
Scott,

Maybe you need an entirely different approach -- maybe you should make
this process a service that is not launched -- but rather is ACTIVATED
by your COBOL program.  Then have the startup Automatic - but have it
listen (maybe with an IP socket) for activation messages and then do
it's thing.  I think you might be able to manage it's activation
programatically if you make it startup "Manual" then have the program
start it.

This the method that Windows uses for "running" processes outside of
particular logins (although the services do run with a particular
login's privlages).


scott.davis@principia.edu (Scott Davis) wrote in message news:<43b3cfd7.0107130817.4feac016@posting.google.com>...
> Oh well, I'm screwed.
```

- **From:** scott.davis@principia.edu (Scott Davis)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<43b3cfd7.0107160935.f7c4c0f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<43b3cfd7.0107021426.5fd6742e@posting.google.com> <MOm27.1419$8T1.133880@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107100901.684af549@posting.google.com> <vAI27.2045$Nt5.131866@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107111548.711d3b8d@posting.google.com> <9m637.1415$FS1.422328@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107120933.316fc882@posting.google.com> <HaA37.221$Lk1.10195@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <43b3cfd7.0107130817.4feac016@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0107131407.291a1d1c@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote in message news:<bfdfc3e8.0107131407.291a1d1c@posting.google.com>...
> Scott,
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> login's privlages).

This isn't a bad idea at all, but let me elaborate (since this thread
has gone way beyond my size intentions anyway) why this won't and
can't work for me.

My company's software package, a warehouse management system, runs on
COBOL because it must run on both Unix and Windows NT.  On Windows NT
it runs as a console app because that is the way character based apps
work on NT.  The codebase, except for some unusual places is the
essentially same, and the look and feel on both platforms is EXACTLY
the same.

Now, on Unix, there is no standard nice way of starting, stopping, and
monitoring servers.  So, what did we do?  We build our own Server
Monitor program into our package.  It starts, stops, and monitors all
servers that pertain to our package.  Our documentation outlines how
to use this Server Monitor.  Now, before this current release, the
Server Monitor was not supported on thr NT version of our package
because of the extreme differences between Unix and NT with regard to
this functionality.  However, it was required to become part of the
current release with the goal that it must work exactly the same as on
Unix as far as the way the user sees it is concerned.

Making the process different for NT, such as requiring additional
setup in NT's server control panel is not an option for me because of
the aforementioned requirements.

I appreciate the alternative suggestions, but they are not options. 
As far as I'm concerned, I'm almost there.  The server monitor works
to the point that servers start, stop, and run properly.  They just
die when the user logs out.  That was ONE SMALL hurdle I though I
could overcome, rather than re-engineering the way things work.

What we've decided to do in the interim is the following.

1. Require that the customer run Windows 2000 with Terminal Services.
2. Log onto the console.
3. Start a terminal services client session to itself under a unique
login name reserved for the person running Server Monitor.
4. Run Server Monitor in that session and then disconnect (but not log
off) that terminal client session until needed again.
5. Activate that session when returning to Server Monitor.

This is a kludge, but it works!

Thanks alot guys.
```

```
Thanks for the great explanation.

How about using Task Scheduler to start your processes under the Admin account
upon server startup?  Task Scheduler can run these services under a different
account than the logged in account and thus a logout will not kill the
processes.
```

```
Now that is interesting.  In fact I was thinking just that a few days
ago.  I could use the AT command on BOTH Unix and Windows (2000) to
start my processes rather than calling CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT (which has
given us fits for other reasons too).  I just lost this idea in the
messy jumble that I call my head, and by the time I got back to work
it was gone, replaced by other issues to be resolved...

Thank you VERY much for reminding me.

Oh, golly, I just remembered a problem with this.  You see, I also
need to know the process id of the server, and it gets returned to me
via CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT, but AT does no such thing.  Well, maybe I could
include some patch code into all our servers that reports back the
process id to some known place where it can be picked up and read by
the Server Monitor.

I guess this can still work with that stipulation.

Not that anyone cares, but I'll let y'all know how it goes when I try
this.
```

```
> Thanks for the great explanation.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> processes.

BEGIN RANT

Hello!  Is anyone from Microsoft listening!?  You are complete morons!
 Using the AT command to schedule my job was an almost perfect answer
- almost, because Microsoft went and did something VERY VERY stupid
that prevents me from being able to implement this solution.  As of
W2K Microsoft "upgraded" (and I use the term loosely) the Scheduler. 
The old Scheduler had a 1 second resolution for starting up jobs, the
new improved Scheduler, hence renamed Task Scheduler, is limited to
starting tasks with a 1 MINUTE resolution.  The doofus who thought
this wasn't going to break A LOT OF PEOPLE's code aught to be shot
dead RIGHT NOW.  It didn't break my code - it prevented me from
writing code. At least I'm not in the lurch that I have found lots of
others are who have to explain why their code doesn't work on W2K
anymore, and there's possibly no workaround (given their unique
situation).  Our server monitor has 10 seconds to successfully spawn a
process or things get ugly.  If I use AT, I need to schedule a server
to start the NEXT minute.  If it is early in the current minute then
this just will not do.  Also--and here's another glorious MS
decision--if you schedule a job to run in the CURRENT minute, well,
GOOD LUCK, because it won't run till TOMORROW.  Idiots! Dunderheads!
Morons!  Will someone please explain how Microsoft can position W2K as
an "industrial strengh" OS when their built-in Scheduler is time
resolution crippled?  Anyone?  Aw gosh, maybe I should just run away
and become a lumberjack...

END RANT
```

<a id="t-17618083c7"></a>
## Thread: database connectivity — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### database connectivity

- **From:** saugatad@hotmail.com (saugata)
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T06:51:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ae33cd.0107060551.63e3ae59@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anybody give me any idea that how can I connect any rdbms with my
cobol program any manipulate/retrive data from the database
```

#### ↳ Re: database connectivity

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T17:12:08+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9i4grv$ccl$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<b9ae33cd.0107060551.63e3ae59@posting.google.com>`

```
The most used way to access a relational is through embedded SQL statements.
This is a technology used for a long time in Cobol and you will need a
precompiler in order to access the specific RDBMS you want to use.

If you are developing Windows applications, you might want to use ODBC, so
your application can access (almost) any relational with the same code. ODBC
statements are quite tedious to write and are documented (usually) in C/C++,
so you have to do the translation to Cobol data types.

If you are using Net Express from Micro Focus, you can still use embedded
SQL statements that are automatically translated (transparently) to ODBC
calls. Also, Net Expess allows you to use the latest Microsoft technology to
access data : ActiveX Data Objects (ADO), but this needs a fairly good
knowledge of Object Cobol.

Hope this helps.

Panos


"saugata" <saugatad@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:b9ae33cd.0107060551.63e3ae59@posting.google.com...
> Can anybody give me any idea that how can I connect any rdbms with my
> cobol program any manipulate/retrive data from the database
```

```
>cobol program any manipulate/retrive data from the database

Hi, you can check out a solution for my company, Acucorp, called 
"Acu4GL" which will allow you to access an RDBMS from your COBOL program 
without needing to embed SQL. More details can be found at 
www.acucorp.com.

Regards

Nigel
```

<a id="t-25566ac474"></a>
## Thread: Object Cobol — 4 msgs

### Object Cobol

- **From:** rangarajan_vr@infy.com (ranjay)
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T08:02:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b37f3ff.0107060702.561d7976@posting.google.com>`

```
What are advantages of migrating legacy code to object cobol?. Is this
a good solution?
```

#### ↳ Re: Object Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T17:12:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B45F149.2AEE9AE2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7b37f3ff.0107060702.561d7976@posting.google.com>`

```


ranjay wrote:

> What are advantages of migrating legacy code to object cobol?. Is this
> a good solution?

This has to be your decision based on 'knowledge'. Pete Dashwood has
recommended learning Java to complement COBOL. If Java gives you the right
feel for OO, then you can start translating your thoughts and 'visualize'
how it works in OO COBOL.

But before you actually make the move - look at two good introductory
books - I'm not going to repeat details here - but Will Price and Ed
Arranga of cobolreport.com. Check with them on availability of their
respective books.

Armed with the Java/OO COBOL background - now you can make a reasoned
decision. At this point in time OO COBOL compilers are - Fujitsu, IBM
Visual Age and Micro Focus - Net Express. There is also Hitachi, but it is
only marketed in Japan.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Object Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T15:02:21+12:00

```
 Can someone tell me why a batch COBOL program running within a DB2 region
under TSO should immediately abend with a S0C1? I'm guessing it's something
to do with the linkedit, but am getting nowhere.....

 Thanks
 Alex
```

```
> under TSO should immediately abend with a S0C1? I'm guessing it's
```

```
At some installations, at least it holds true in the one I'm working at, DB2
entry points vil be included thru the module DSNHLI which is normally used
to connect to DB2 via IMS. When creating TSO/DB2 modules you will have to
force TSO connection to DB2 with the module DSNELI by adding the control
card which Bill mentioned, to linkage editor input.

Kjeld P. Hansen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Batch COBOL Immediate S0C1

- **From:** engelhardt@dvoid.org (Heiner Engelhardt)
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T11:07:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<225570b2.0107131007.4183562d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9i56m1$eki$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <3B466083.C811FB98@optonline.net> <3b47a1d5$0$304$edfadb0f@dspool01.news.tele.dk>`

```
The abend S0C1 is comming if the system detects unexecutable
instructions (like exec cics in an batch environment). A DB2 Module
thet you whant to call in a CICS- and also in a BATCH-environment you
must have 2 load modules: for batch link the DB2 Modul including
DSNALI and for CICS link DB2 Module including DSNHLI and your
prpogramm will run. You have to be sure thet you dont use cics
instruction in your programm.

> entry points vil be included thru the module DSNHLI which is normally used
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-07-06T13:24:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0107061224.2bc071e7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
"Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC"  

wrote

> I apologize for not having the time to read this whole thread, and not
> having looked as this newsgroup lately, but to save time, let me just say
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> exit program).

I have the opposite preference, I use full stops to terminate all
statements that are not nested within other statements.  I do this as
a defensive measure to prevent the unintended overruns that may arise
from errors in maintenance activities, so that even if someone
(including myself) forgets to include a scope terminator the fault is
limited in its range, even if it wasn't detected during testing.  I
would prefer semicolons to full stops as sentence terminators because
they are less easily missed, but it is too late to do anything about
that now.  I do indent for levels of nesting and use scope
terminators, but the compiler takes no notice of indentation and
objects to superfluous full stops when followed by orphaned scope
terminators, whereas the opposite is not true.
```

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T06:40:09+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B462229.93F5C80E@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <fcd09c56.0107061224.2bc071e7@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert Jones wrote:
> 
> "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC"
> ... Never use goto, alter, perform thru, next sentence, or Exit

One thing to keep in mind is that not all code is hand coded.
GOTO may be perfectly OK, even necessary in generated code.  Have
a look at the code generated by bison the parser generator for
example. 

Anyway, languages have many uses.  People often extrapolate from
their own needs and practices and sometimes seems to feel the
need to inflict their preferences on others. (not suggesting this
is the case for either of the people listed above).

Sometimes a goto can save a lot of tedious IF statements, and you
end up with a simpler program overall.  The linux kernel contains
huge numbers of gotos, and a result it runs a lot faster than it
would otherwise.

In the COBOL compiler I put in a warning about goto but only at
the highest level ("FYI Go to considered harmful") and it is
mainly just there as a joke.  

In java they have a construct EXIT LABEL, which makes it viable
to not have goto in the language.  With this construct you can
exit several layers of 'perform' in one statement.  This is very
useful for error handling when you don't want the overheads of
exceptions (create object, later garbage collect it).  

Tim Josling
```

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **Date:** 2001-07-09T20:33:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4A06EC.2DC642D3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <ZtO07.30484$J91.1061302@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <kemcktsi7ovea0in13apv98ivrcu40mmun@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> Is that right?  I concidered your approach, and threw it out.  What if there are
> 30 fields to validate?  Can you honesty say you would continue your code as show
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> avoiding goto's, exit paragraph, etc.

Just perform each validation but from the last to the first:

     PERFORM Validate-field-30
     PERFORM Validate-field-29
     ....
     PERFORM Validate-field-1

The error message will be set for each invalid field but the result will
be that of the 'first' error message (ie the one with the lowest number
here).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>

```
> My version support ODBC ....and SQL but I don't know sql. I
use Access
> database in .mdb !

You can use the Access front-end to help with your SQL.  Form a
query using the Query-By-Example grid, then take a look at the
SQL representation of your query.  Copy and paste if you like,
but I think you'll find SQL has a shallow on-ramp.
```

```
One thing I like about C is you can do the following

    while (readfile(filename) ! = EOF) {
        processrec(record);
    }

in more of a cobol style you have to do something like

    result = readfile(filename);
    while (result != EOF) {
        processrec(record);
        result = readfile(filename);
    }

In both cases readfile(filename) is performed one more time than
processrec(record), with the final call of readfile() returning an EOF
condition.  Does the new COBOL standard allow for anything like this?  I guess
it probably does for user-defined functions, but not for performs.  Alas.  I
like COBOL, but sometimes it's frustrating.
    
Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

#### ↳ Re: need help for ODBC

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T14:09:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B470A15.6EB5BD8B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3b3f501d$0$20375$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-9m9Ik5nbcdwZ@thishost> <3B41F0C5.47F7C524@home.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-E5d22sRHKevi@thishost> <3B4364E0.77B5C549@home.com> <23nckt0t8t0iu6sl8jjo40eoq0jdhjg12e@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> One thing I like about C is you can do the following
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     }

This is allowed in C because an expression can appear anywhere.  However
it can lead to many problems for the unweary.  For example in short
circuited comparisons a function call may be skipped if the final result
can be determined without needing all comparisons to be made.

      if ( a() || b() )

will not execute b() if a() returns TRUE.  If b() produces a side effect
then this will not occur.
  
 
> in more of a cobol style you have to do something like
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> it probably does for user-defined functions, but not for performs.  Alas.  I

This is the safe way of ensuring that programmers write correctly
operating programs.

> like COBOL, but sometimes it's frustrating.

All languages are idiomatic to some degree.  C has different idioms
because it has different language features.  With the quote "Real
programmers can write FORTRAN programs in _any_ language_" this
indicates that FORTRAN idioms can be used in other languages.  C idioms,
however, cannot.
```

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 04 Jul 2001 18:48:36 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Wouldn't -

    perform until file-status not equal zeros
        read filename record
            at end
                continue     (or exit perform 8-)

            not at end
                perform processrec

        end-read
    end-perform.

be closer to the example C routine? The C code will loop forever if the
returned value is an error code. The cobol perform will trap non-
sucess file status values as well as successful completion.
```

<a id="t-40bca03abb"></a>
## Thread: New Wording for EXIT rules. — 1 msgs

### Re: New Wording for EXIT rules.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T11:30:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B46E4E2.6FFBB0B9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0106292212.366496bb@posting.google.com> <3B3DFB51.7679F400@iinet.net.au> <tjt407g9fv626@corp.supernews.com> <3b3f186d_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <9hncd1$g95$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> In my opinion, this is a "misunderstanding" of the implicit CONTINUE
> "approach"  (for NEXT SENTENCE, EXIT whatever, etc).  In my opinion, these
> "implicit CONTINUE" statements are virtual (as someone said in an earlier
> note).  

Are they virtually implicit or implicitly virtual ?    ;-)

Will you be changing the wording to reflect that they are virtual and/or
that this is a run-time not compile-time implication ?

> They *never* exist - all they are, are places to go "as if".  In
> other words, it is IMPOSSIBLE to need the CONTINUE associated with NEXT
> SENTENCE and the one for EXIT PROGRAM at the same time - as they exist ONLY
> at run-time and only one of those statements can possibly be "in effect" at
> any one time.

I am not sure how useful to a compiler writer this 'clarification' is. 
If the language was being interpreted at run-time then it may be said
that it is only necessary for one to be 'in effect'.  However the whole
point of compiling, even in languages such as Python, is that these
issues are _not_ left until run-time to be resolved.  The points to
which the GO TOs, NEXT SENTENCEs, EXIT PARAGRAPHs etc are to resume
execution are resolved at _compile_time_.  The whole concept of
'implicit CONTINUEs' is seriously flawed if each time a NEXT PARAGRAPH
is encounted the 'run-time' has to work out where this 'implicit
CONTINUE' should be created, even if it is 'virtual'.

It should be possible for the _compiler_ to generate the equivalent of a
JMP instruction whenever it meets an EXIT PARAGRAPH rather than having
the run-time re-enter a compiling phase to create a new CONTINUE to work
out where it is going.

Fortunately the compilers only have to generate code to simulate what is
written and can ignore the convoluted and pointless mechanism you have
invented (and now re-invented with virtual and run-time requirements).
```

---

<a id="t-e8f5802e6f"></a>
## Thread: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods) — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T11:53:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:

> Main programs that have PARM parameters defined DO have LINKAGE SECTIONs.

I assume that you are writing using an IBM mainfram compiler.

The '85 standard for this is:

"The LINKAGE SECTION in a program is meaningful if, and only if, the
object program is to function under the control of a CALL statement, and
the CALL statement in the calling program contains a USING phrase."

as I said in my message:
 
> > It seems to be another IBM-ism that main programs can have a LINKAGE and
> > thus may be executed as a main or be called.
 
It is possible that other systems have this as an extension, but I have
not come across any other (though there are many systems that I have not
come across at all).

Consequently the 'problem' with EXIT PROGRAM is restricted to IBM
Mainframes (AFAIK), which is why GOBACK is advocated by IBMers, and
others don't necessarily see the point.

As I said: _I_ know when to use STOP RUN or EXIT PROGRAM so it will not
'bite' me.

I have also mentioned in another thread that the 2002 Cobol standard
should be written so that it can (or even should) form the basis of the
implementors Language Reference manual with marked annotations as
necessary to explain departures from the standard and extensions (as is
done by MF).  In this way the Cobol programmers will be aware of what is
part of the Cobol standard (or of various standards) and what are
extensions that may be specific to just their particular compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-07T12:27:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9i7h81$6mt$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
A) both Micro Focus and CA-Realia (possibly others) allow for a LINKAGE
SECTION in a main program (as an extension) - these are used BOTH for "parm
passing" *and* SET ADDRESS OF syntax.

B) Just because an ANSI Conforming program does *not* have a LINKAGE
SECTION, does NOT mean that it is (or is intended to be) a MAIN program.
There are often times when a sub-program is coded with no linkage section.
For example if the main program uses EXTERNAL data instead of LINKAGE
SECTION for passing "information" - or when the subprogram doesn't need any
data from the main program (for example, it does a READ of a file to know
why it was called).

  ***

Bottom-Line:
  GoBack is a relatively COMMON extension today.
  It is included in the draft of the next Standard.
  I have yet to hear any "good reason" (subjective of course) for NOT using
it - where available.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> B) Just because an ANSI Conforming program does *not* have a LINKAGE
> SECTION, does NOT mean that it is (or is intended to be) a MAIN program.

I know whether _my_ programs are main or called, and thus whether to
code STOP RUN or EXIT PROGRAM, independently of whether they have
LINKAGE SECTION.

> There are often times when a sub-program is coded with no linkage section.

Often ?  How do you quantify often ?  How many have you coded like that
this year ?

> Bottom-Line:
>   GoBack is a relatively COMMON extension today.
>   It is included in the draft of the next Standard.
>   I have yet to hear any "good reason" (subjective of course) for NOT using
> it - where available.

Maybe, but having used the standard EXIT PROGRAM and STOP RUN for many
years with zero problems I can't think of any reason at all, good or
bad, for me to use GOBACK.
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>   I think you have used Micro Focus compilers.  Check out the documentation

Maybe later versions, NetExpress perhaps.

I can't think of any reason, good or bad, to use this except on IBM
mainframes where it may be the only way to pass parameters to the
program from JCL.  ACCEPT FROM COMMAND-LINE works just fine and is
standard.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-08T18:09:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4893E7.AFE1A09D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <9i7h81$6mt$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> Robert M. Pritchett wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> limited in its range, even if it wasn't detected during testing.

I find this a very interesting approach, but not a very useful one.  You
seem to be saying that if you make a mistake you want it to be as
invisible as possible, only allowing the smallest number of statements
to be executed incorrectly.

Personally if I forgot a scope terminator (which has not been unknown
for me to do) then I want to maximise the effect, preferably by having
the test-run wave banners at me.  Having it run, or miss out, the
maximum number of statements increases the chances of noticing there is
a problem, and of being able to find where this is.

I suspect that your approach is more likely to result in a missing scope
terminator (and thus just one line being executed at inappropriate
times) not being detected during testing.

I also really dislike the thought of spending so much time adding the
full stops or taking them off just because I change whether some code is
to be conditional or not.  For example if some code is in an IF and I
find another use for these lines I'll drop them out to a new paragraph
and perform from the original and the new places.  Would you really
bother to add full stops to every one of these lines ?  I can't any
point in doing so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>

```
You misread the sequence of replies, I agree with you completely, the
person I was replying to was the one that likes the periods, I don't. And
you stated my reasoning much better than I did. I want errors to be as
obvious as possible, not hide and propogate them.
```

#### ↳ Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T19:06:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In some cases it's actually not possible to know whether a cobol program
will be called as a subroutine or executed as a main program. This is
unusual but cannot be ruled out. That's why I got into the habit of always
using GoBack, even on non-IBM systems, as long as the system accepts it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T12:47:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9inj8c$l27$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC wrote in message
news:%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net...

> In some cases it's actually not possible to know whether a cobol program
> will be called as a subroutine or executed as a main program. This is
> unusual but cannot be ruled out. That's why I got into the habit of always
> using GoBack, even on non-IBM systems, as long as the system accepts it.

I'd go one step further and comment that I'm not sure it's that unusual.
Parameters are optional in both the CALL and the PROCEDURE DIVISION header.
I can certainly envision an application in which a program without
parameters could be used directly as a program at will and also used by the
application process as a called program under the IPC rules, and I don't
know of any standards-based prohibition of just such a convention (though
some implementations may enforce a distinction).

In "GOBACK-less" dialects of COBOL, it may well be that the only difference
between a standalone program and a called one is the presence of EXIT
PROGRAM (and the default behavior as specified in the '85 standard in its
absence) in the latter.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T22:09:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lfoukt45vuak4oo7chiilp6i0611393fj2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <9inj8c$l27$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
>In "GOBACK-less" dialects of COBOL, it may well be that the only difference
>between a standalone program and a called one is the presence of EXIT
>PROGRAM (and the default behavior as specified in the '85 standard in its
>absence) in the latter.

I wouldn't say that.

Most of my program written in cobol 74 had the following construct.
(and they DID have a "GO TO" but don't look at that again).

bbb.
   if x = "stop processing"
      go to end-program.
......

end-program
   exit program.
really-exit-program
   stop run.


By doing like this a program could be used both as a stand alone or as
a callable program.
And I have a lot like these, specially the generic maintenance ones.

So I would say that a stand alone program would normally be identified
by having a "stop run" as a last instruction of the main group of
perform, or by a "stop run" in a paragraph identified as a "exit
program" one (either by being performed or by means of a "goto").

FF
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T05:33:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
    I can see one reason why one might want to intentionally use EXIT
PROGRAM in a subprogram:
<stuff>
EXIT PROGRAM.
DISPLAY 'THIS PROGRAM MUST BE USED AS A SUBPROGRAM, NEVER A MAIN PROGRAM'.
GOBACK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T23:39:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ir6ei$kc884$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I have done the following in an edit program (it runs both as a stand-alone
as a called module).

EXIT PROGRAM.
PERFORM 7000-CREATE-STATS-REPORT THRU 7000-EXIT.
STOP RUN.

In this particular case, I only wanted the statistics if the program was
running as a stand alone...


Dave
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T03:01:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B525868.9C055DC2@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:

>     I can see one reason why one might want to intentionally use EXIT
> PROGRAM in a subprogram:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

<snip>
Do you use a mainframe system or computer system similar to IBM's MVS or OS/390?
I ask this because in this environment a main program and a subroutine are the
same! Now the COBOL run-time make be able to make a distinction, but, once you
get past the NIP (Nucleus Initialization Program), RIM (Resource Initialization
Module(s)), and the like, all application, and most system level programs are
treated as subroutines!

Specifically, General Register 15 contains your entry point address, Gen Reg 13
contains the save area pointer (save chain), Gen Reg 1 points to the passed
parameter list (yes that PARM= stuff is pointed to by this...), and Gen Reg 14
contains the next instruction address for the calling program (if you are the
first one called upon TCB - Task Control Block- build, then R14 points to SVC 3
-- EXIT).

So, in the OS/390 arena, main programs and subroutines can call each other!

Later,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T22:30:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9itn8a$7bd6$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B525868.9C055DC2@neo.rr.com>`

```
    I was contracted to a company that had an IBM, OS/390 at the time.
Among the changes we were making, was to replace STOP RUNs and GOBACKs by
EXIT PROGRAMs when modifying called objects.  When we started changing the
calling programs, we changed a GOBACK to an EXIT PROGRAM and discovered that
the EXIT PROGRAM functions as a no-op if not being called by another
program.  We changed it back to a GOBACK and all was well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T01:34:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107160034.4773e570@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B525868.9C055DC2@neo.rr.com>`

```
Apparently. the IBM mainframe compiler checks to find out if the
program was called by the operating system or an application program,
because EXIT PROGRAM will not work unless the program is under the
EXPLICIT control of a CALL Statement in another program.

From the VS COBOL II Application Language Reference:
"  If control reaches an EXIT PROGRAM statement, and no CALL statement
is
   active, control passes through the exit point to the next
executable
   statement. "




Stephen J Spiro


Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3B525868.9C055DC2@neo.rr.com>...
> Terry Heinze wrote:
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> 330/335-9907
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop Run vs. Exit Program etc.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T08:20:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iv0na$i1m$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B46EA1F.7E177392@Azonic.co.nz> <%EH37.958$JS2.102327@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8RQ37.57$JD4.7503088@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B525868.9C055DC2@neo.rr.com> <4145699b.0107160034.4773e570@posting.google.com>`

```
So the standards require; this is not exactly new.

ANSI X3.23-1974:
Page XII-8, EXIT PROGRAM statement, paragraph 3.4.4 GR1:  "An execution of
an EXIT PROGRAM statement in a called program causes control to be passed to
the calling program.  Execution of an EXIT PROGRAM statement in a program
which is not called behaves as if the statement were an EXIT statement. ..."

Page II-64, EXIT statement, paragraph 5.11.4 GR1:  "An EXIT statement serves
only to enable the user to assign a procedure-name to a given point in a
program.  Such an EXIT statement has no other effect on the compilation or
execution of the program."

ANSI X3.23-1985:
Page X-33, EXIT PROGRAM statement, paragraph 5.4.4 GR1:  "If the EXIT
PROGRAM statement is executed in a program which is not under the control of
a calling program, the EXIT PROGRAM statement causes execution of the
program to continue with the next executable statement. "

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
There might very well be - but you'll need the following Sections for
each and every API call:

How to call this from NetExpress
How to call this from Fujitsu COBOL
How to call this from RM COBOL
How to call this from AcuCOBOL
How to call this from ICOBOL
How to call this from IBM Visualage for COBOL
How to call this from TinyCOBOL (Once they get Win32 support)
How to call this from REALIA COBOL

I think you get the idea - each vendor has a different way to call the
APIs.

All that said - I would like to see such a book and I think it could
be of value.
```

<a id="t-07a25b26b5"></a>
## Thread: Full Screen I-O — 2 msgs

### Re: Full Screen I-O

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-07-08T14:25:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b486cea.48659078@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3fe0244.0107040933.1a3ccb1f@posting.google.com>`

```
The X-OPEN screen section standard doesn't allow the dynamic change of
color.  This particular standard is the one used in the Realia and
Fujitsu compilers.  However, Merant and most likely AcuCorp's
offerings DO allow this dynamic access.  Simply assign the color to a
variable and populate it accordingly.


On 4 Jul 2001 10:33:41 -0700, bkeith@alumni.utexas.net (Colonel Buck)
wrote:

>How do you dynamically modify individual field attributes (background
>color, foreground color, reverse video, blink, etc.) when using full
>screen io?
```

#### ↳ Re: Full Screen I-O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-08T14:24:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iacag$g4j$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3fe0244.0107040933.1a3ccb1f@posting.google.com> <3b486cea.48659078@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Also the draft of the next Standard allows for dynamic changing of *all*
screen attributes via a new format of the SET statement.  As far as I know,
*NO* vendor has yet implemented this format - but you might ask your "vendor
of choice" - if they have support for this now - or plan on implementing it
in the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL and Email

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-07-09T03:01:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-OW4wRYV6hHwz@thishost>`
- **References:** `<t0%17.94915$2W4.14574337@news02.optonline.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-fdKZ4FUTdGJ6@thishost> <3b49173e_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Calling a routine in a DLL that is not written in the language that is
calling the routine depends on the calling convention used when the 
routines in the DLL were created and the ability of the COBOL compiler
to allow the user to specify the appropriate calling convention.

For example (using MF COBOL) you usually declare a "procedure-pointer"
and use the SET verb to obtain initialize the "procedure-pointer" with
the init routine for the DLL Once the DLL is loaded for the program 
you can access any of the routines with a call statement. Some 
compilers will require that you include the "xxxxxx.lib" file in the 
LINK command when you build the application.

If you have a DLL that contains several routines that were written 
using the PASCAL calling convention (this is the normal one for OS/2 
and Windows API calls)

This example is from a program I wrote to provide access to the list 
of local and network printers available from a workstation running 
OS/2

environment division.
configuration section.
special-names.
  call-convention 3 is os2api

data division.
* this is the procedure pointer used for DLL initialization
01  this-dll  usage procedure pointer.

linkage section
procedure division

* initialize the spooler api calls DLL
set this-dll to entry "pmspl.dll"

call os2api "DosPrintDestEnum" using
                   by reference szServerName
                   by value sLevel
                   by reference prdinfo3-array
                   by value usbuflen
                   by reference pusEntReturned
                   by reference pusEntAvail
                   giving return-status

I left out most of the data definitions for brevity. The actual 
arguments passed to the API routine are, of course, dependant on the 
particular API call. You note that there is a mix of "by reference" 
(passing an pointer) and "by value" (passing a value directly)

Normally SENDMAIL is an EXE program that takes command line 
parameters. You can look up the  comand line syntax for your OS 
implementation. This is sometimes changed between implementations on 
different operating systems.

I would use 

SENDMAIL -a mail-file-name -f me@mydomain.com 
theotherguy@destdomain.com

to send a file named "mail-file-name" to theotherguy@destdomain.com 
showing it is from me@mydomain.com

SENDMAIL uses a configuration file SENDMAIL.CF that must be properly 
set up before SENDMAIL will work....

On my system the help file shows the following syntax for sendmail


 
 The sendmail command sends mail without using an electronic mail 
interface. 

 Sending Mail 

 ���sendmail������-a filename������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������
 
 ���������������������-f user@localhost -t���������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������
       ���                      ������ user@remotehost������  ���
       ������-f user@localhost user@remotehost�����������������������������
       ������-t��������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������
       ���    ������ user@remotehost������                    ���
       ������user@remotehost���������������������������������������������������������������������������������������

 ���sendmail������������������������������������������������������������������-a filename������������������������������������������������������������

       ������-t��������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������

              ������-bp������
 
 Changing the Values of Options

              ������-oxvalue������
```

```
Don't search in the CDs!
Fileshare NLM is sold as a separate product (the only Fileshare product that
is not included in a development environment or the application server).

You should talk to a Micro Focus representative so you can have access to
it.

Panos Zotos
```

<a id="t-09d4b772da"></a>
## Thread: COBO to Java translation — 1 msgs

### Re: COBO to Java translation

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-08T15:01:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B48D839.FB042777@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3B394ED8.EEBA7F03@ican.net> <3b3d17b7$1_1@feed1.realtime.net> <3B3E985C.E9E509A0@ican.net>`

```
Howard,
You might have been convinced by those responding to you that you shouldn't try
to move COBOL application to the Java environment. Merant has in the past
discussed wrappering COBOL logic to create Java Beans.  LegacyJ PERCobol will
compile your COBOL and create executables that will execute (without wrappering)
in the Java environment as applications, applets, servlets, EJBs and Java Beans
directly from COBOL. A PERCobol compile might be significantly easier and less
costly than rewriting an existing COBOL application in Java.

The program compiled with PERCobol can access new Java functions or CALL to
existing COBOL routines.

Regards,

Charles Townsend
LegacyJ
http://www.legacyj.com
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02

Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

#### ↳ Re: Where's the COBOL world headed?

- **From:** cesullivan@aol.comsf (Cathy Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T03:22:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010712232253.22720.00002741@ng-cv1.aol.com>`

```
David,

I've been a COBOL programmer for over 10 years.  I used to work for the State
of New York as a Unisys A-Series mainframe programmer.  After taking a 3 month
full time training course in Unix/C and C++, I can't get a job doing C++
because I don't have any "experience".   This seems to be a bad time to be
switching my focus.

All the COBOL jobs here in NYC seem to be for IBM COBOL II, DB2, CICS and JCL. 
And you need financial experience.

I could pick up the IBM stuff in a short time.  Or Microfocus stuff, or
whatever.

Good luck!




Cathy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where's the COBOL world headed?

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T09:32:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4EF859.FEFC0C69@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<3B3B5A09.DA7774E3@mescoma.com> <20010712232253.22720.00002741@ng-cv1.aol.com> <jAA37.392$Lk1.18480@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Sorry.............as a person who "marketed" myself in just that way (enhancing
my skill), it doesn't work.  Employers don't want enhanced skill......all they
want is "experience" in the narrow field they're looking for.

What amazes me is that an employer is willing to take a person with 1 year of
Siebel experience....who may or may not have any idea of good programming
practices, may or may not be logical, may or may not have any decent work ethic,
and they're willing to take a risk with them, instead of someone that has a
proven track record in programming, consulting, etc..etc..etc.

Corporate America is a Dilbert cartoon!!



Michael Mattias wrote:

> Cathy Sullivan <cesullivan@aol.comsf> wrote in message
> news:20010712232253.22720.00002741@ng-cv1.aol.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where's the COBOL world headed?

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T09:28:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4EF767.56BC468A@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<3B3B5A09.DA7774E3@mescoma.com> <20010712232253.22720.00002741@ng-cv1.aol.com>`

```
Cathy,

I know exactly what you mean.  Believe me.  In addition, I'm having a problem
getting a mainframe job here in the Baltimore area.  Seems I have too much
experience in that area, and employers are only willing to pay for someone with
2-5 years of experience.

As far as the IBM stuff......I'm sure you could pick it up in no time.  When I had
my own company and needed an employee, I hired a friend of mine that I knew was a
good COBOL programmer, but he had worked on Tandem computers for his entire
career.  He wanted a change, so I brought him on board, and within 2 months had
taught him all he needed to know to be productive on an IBM mainframe.  After 6
months or so, I sent him to Seattle to head up an IBM project for me.

It's like I've said in the past.....programming, like most other disciplines, is
learned step by step.  Each step building on the other.  So, to learn another
language or technique, is just another step.  But, employers and recruiters are so
narrow-minded they can't see that.  All they see is no experience in this one
particular thing!!    Talk about myopic.

Well, good luck!   I hope things work out for you.

Dave

Cathy Sullivan wrote:

> David,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cathy
```

<a id="t-2822a051e4"></a>
## Thread: OO COBOL Texts — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO COBOL Texts

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2001-07-09T15:08:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ichd6$b58$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
I will be using Fujitsu COBOL 6.0 in class next fall.  I have access to
Wil Price's text, but am looking for recommendations for other
materials.  Are there other recommendations from the group?  We will be
using Thane's book for a look at legacy systems - but I also want to
introduce them to the object features built into the newest COBOL.  

Thanks for any help you can give.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL Texts

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-09T18:02:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B49F1A1.294B9EDA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9ichd6$b58$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```


Floyd Johnson wrote:

> I will be using Fujitsu COBOL 6.0 in class next fall.  I have access to
> Wil Price's text, but am looking for recommendations for other
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for any help you can give.

Floyd,

You haven't indicated length of course, so I'm assuming you want to cover
COBOL basics and perhaps move on to OO (Will's book) - how long on OO ?

Non-OO - at this point in time I would go for Thane's book. I haven't noted
others that are current apart from TYCI21Days - don't care for it much.
When I say 'current'., I mean with 'extensions' that will be a part of
COBOL 2002. (Then of course there's a whole slough that we have lost
reference to. I wasn't aware Don Nelson had written on COBOL - and Will has
written some TWENTY books on COBOL that I wasn't aware of). One other
possible source, and I don't know whether he has written books - is Gary de
Ward Brown. Some useful articles by him in cobolreport.com.

Specifically OO - it's down to Will and Ed Arranga/Frank Coyle. The latter
book gives you a complete coded example of an application. Unfortunately
you would have to key in the source yourself - Ed has lost the damned
diskette containing his source code !

OO books NOT to bother with :-

Ned Chapin -"Standard OO COBOL" - What 'Standard' ? - it was written in '97
- Syntax too general and conveys more 'structured' that OO Classes.

Doke & Hardgrave "An Intro to O COBOL' - simplistic and a repetition of
Will's book, but without the guts.

Andrew Topper 'OO Development in COBOL". '95. - He just loves tables and
diagrams.

So back to teaching OO using Will's examples. If you get stuck I'm sure one
of the following F/J users will help - Don Tees, Pete or Thane. You should
be able to adapt his source from the diskette examples - your only major
stumbling block would be to put in the Repository syntax to suit the F/J
compiler; plus where Net Express uses Object-Storage Section you are
talking 'Working-Storage Section' in F/J. (I can't recall without
referencing, but Will probably has Working-Storage in some methods - this
is a No-No in the new Standard - you can only have Local-Storage).

New Standard - watch out for file definitions and Declaratives which are
part of Global - can't be in methods.

If you want to get more specific - just sing out.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL Texts

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T13:59:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
