- **References:** `<9ichd6$b58$1@ins22.netins.net> <3B49F1A1.294B9EDA@home.com>`

```
I am still in the process of deciding how to put the course together.  For
the students, this is their first course in COBOL - but their third
programming course, including a very good background in OO Programming
using Java.  So - I will not need to spend a great deal of time on
"programming" or OO concepts.  Review, yes, but not new instruction.

My focus will be on COBOL and OO COBOL Syntax (1/2 of course) and file
structures (1/2 of course).  I want (I'm a optimist) to let Thane do most
of the COBOL instruction and I will spend time doing the OO COBOL
stuff.  Of course I will be learning as I go - lucky me [:)].

Thanks for the comments.

Floyd Johnson

Yes, businesses all do the same thing. Some even the same way. Yet, the
rules of book keeping/ accounting do not lend themselves to one approach
because of practices, laws, products, etc. Getting something from your
ancestors is not a very repeatable thing except at the lowest level IN MY
view.

Warren

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T05:25:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


"warren.simmons" wrote:

> Yes, businesses all do the same thing. Some even the same way. Yet, the
> rules of book keeping/ accounting do not lend themselves to one approach
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I *know* you didn't intend to Warren, but with your comments above, you have
made a sort of case for Grinder's desire for multiple inheritance. He could well
argue that your 'because of practices, laws, products, etc......' creates the
circumstances for an object to follow certain 'super rules', i.e. have multiple
inheritance from Class A for USA, Class B for Canada, Class C for UK etc...

Now of course, it's reasonably clear to understand 'traditional inheritance',
i.e. going up levels of classes through the Christmas tree until you hit the
pointy-bit with the fairy on top. (The ultimate super we call 'FJBase' in
Fujitsu and 'Base' in Net Express). This does work extremely well.

The difficulty is fathoming  Multiple Inheritance, which is, if you like,
turning the Christmas tree on its head with the pointy-bit at ground level. At
the moment I'm a doubting Thomas - show me practical code that works and I'll go
for it.

Just to absolutely confuse you <G> - a small group of us discussed 'Invoking Up
or Down'. We had a real ding-dong sorting this one out - and proved it to our
satisfaction by coding examples ! Surprisingly you can do both. (a) Up - invoke
super 'do-this'. (b) Down - PROVIDING you have established an handle (object
reference) to the sub-class, from your top super (MyClassA), you can - invoke
MySubClassG 'do-this". Again like Multiple Inheritance, Invoking 'Down' is a
little bit iffy - though I suspect you can make a stronger case for Invoking
Down than you can for Multiple Inheritance.

Jimmy

> Warren
> Grinder <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote in message
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T20:32:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ADJ27.38629$J91.1743251@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com>`

```
Well, down trees and up trees have buged me a long time.  I ran into them in
simulation classes. Again in compiler design discussions. Later, when I
began to work on my family tree, I discovered a practical use for them.
Practical because I understood the application. There is a standard record
format for this kind of application called GED, and files that follow this
standard, have many Genealogy Applications using them. One of latest I tried
maintains the file in the GED format, while those from Salt Lake City (Meca
of genealogy) first had files for people, places, marriages, notes, etc.
From their efforts, the GED format was created. Even later enhancements to
this record format to include recognition of realationships formerly ignored
or dismissed. Their latest system uses a single file for building, and
maintaining the data base, but is supported by another application designed
to display charts "sideways" on the screen, and to create a variety of
reports.

What the hell does this have to do with the discussion?  I don't know, but
it's how I process your discussion. The use of  the terms used by OO gurus
will never be understood unless some kind of usefull application can be used
to demonstrate it. The airoplanes thing made me think of bill of materials
which I consider a network organization that can handle any kind of tree.

But my tubes are rusty, and my brain is no longer able to remember what or
why I want to say things. I just want to know in layman terms, what is
multiple inheritence, etc. in the real world.  Give me something to hang
onto.

Warren
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T20:45:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9igb5c$tvl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com> <ADJ27.38629$J91.1743251@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I won't swear to it, but I think that the "classic" example of multiple
inheritance that is often used is a bank "environment".

Let's say that there is a type of object that is a CD (certificate of
Deposit) account - now this "inherits" (gets) some of its attributes from a
"normal" savings account - and it gets other attributes from "treasury
bills".

Now treasury bills are DEFINITELY not a type of savings account
 Neither are savings accounts a type of treasury bill.

Therefore, "how" do you define your CD account so that it can gets "some
attributes" (and/or properties) from savings accounts and others from
treasury bills?

Answer:
  Multiple inheritance !!!
   A CD account gets some of its "methods" from savings accounts and others
from treasury bills.

  ***

Note well:
  This may well NOT be how you would actually create a "full function
real-world" banking application, but I think that is show the "thinking
behind" multiple inheritance.

Note2:
  If you language does *not* support multiple inheritance, then you could
create a "dummy" (interface?) object for *all* types of accounts.  This
would have all the methods *names* for savings accounts, treasury bills, and
CD accounts.  However, in the actual savings account object definition, you
would override (and dummy out) the inherited methods that do NOT apply to
this type of account - and similarly you would dummy out the methods in the
treasury bill account object definition that ONLY apply to savings accounts.
While your CD account would have all the methods that it needs - without
multiple inheritance.

  ***

Warren - is this clear enough?

OO people - have I mis-stated this "simplistic" example?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T05:43:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4BE76C.5478F25F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com> <ADJ27.38629$J91.1743251@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9igb5c$tvl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I won't swear to it, but I think that the "classic" example of multiple
> inheritance that is often used is a bank "environment".
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that's pretty good for somebody who confesses to not coding anymore <G>.
Actually Warren, Bill has come up with a tangible example - with a caveat. You
guys who are into banking, you don't need to be savvy on OO but can you make
points illustrating the 'similarities' between types of accounts Bill is
illustrating.  Taking the following tree, what methods (PERFORM PARAGRAPHS if
you like), would be applicable to the following hierarchical tree .

Warren it's difficult to layout with Netscape but hope this helps. Note it also
finishes up with a 'Diamond shape' Inheritance, (Chequing, Savings and TBills)
as subclasses of Class Account. Now I don't *know* if it would be applicable to
ALL transactions, but let's throw in ATMs which theoretically could
multi-inherit from the rest :-

                                            Class Account
                                            Inherits from Base
                                            Methods 'common' to all/some
sub-classes :-

("get-AccountHolderName", "get-SpecificAcBalance, "get-AllAcBalances",
"make-deposit", "make-withdrawal", "calc-interest", "printATMspecificBalance",
"printATMAllBalances", "printDepositBalance", "printWithdrawalBalance" etc....)

Class Chequing A/c        Class Savings A/c             Class Treasury Bills
Inherits from A/c            Inherits from A/c                Inherits from A/c

                                       Class Certificate of Deposit
                                       Multi - inherits from
                                       Savings and Treasury Bills


                                        Class ATMs
                                       Multi - inherits from
                                       Chequing, Savings, Treasury Bills and CDs

Now without knowing banking the trick is, "Which methods are common to various
classes" - let's hope the bankers can tell us. (I've *guessed" and put under the
super Class Account, those methods which I think *might* be applicable to ALL
accounts. Having established what is common, then we could fill in the detail
for methods applicable to each level of class.

Without going into the nitty-gritty, from the above flowchart it is apparent
that Certificates of Deposit must have handles(object references)  or 'triggers'
to point as appropriate to Savings A/c or Treasury Bills. Note CDs could take
either route (Savings or TBills) to invoke methods contained in super Class
Account. Same logic applies if you say ATM has multiple inheritance.

Bill referred to "dummying-out" methods that you don't need. That's one
possibility, and obviously there are others given a tangible model to work from.

At the present time it is a split camp in the OO languages as to those that do
and those that  don't support Multiple Inheritance. For those that don't I'm
guessing it is related to Encapsulation (yeah that $10 word again). You aren't
supposed to know what a method contains, just use it, knowing the
sending/returning parameters. As I said before, this works well in a single
'upwards heirarchy', but isn't as clear with multiple inheritance.

Check out the FCD - Concepts - Appendix C, para C 17.4.1. onwards. The next bit
is at the theoretical stage - the new Standard illustrates and contains caveats
about using Multiple Inheritance. I *believe* (if my memory isn't failing me as
well Warren) that Thane said Fujitsu has Multiple Inheritance. Net Express
doesn't.

Given that Multiple Inheritance DID NOT exist in OO COBOL, then I would probably
make the following sort of approach to the ATM class, which tends to negate use
of the 'immediately above' super - but it would be a way of doing it.

Class ATM - invoke ChequingAc "new" returning os-ChequingAc
                    invoke SavingsAc "new" returning os-SavingsAc
                    invoke TBills "new" returning os-TBills
                    invoke CDs "new" returning os-CDs

From the ATM the user keys in which A/c type and transaction type he/she wants
and you access the appropriate object above.

Recall me mentioning invoking DOWN from super. Given that you put the ATM at the
top of the 'Christmas tree', plus to above list you add the invoke "new" giving
you os-Account, you could then invoke os-Account "do-this-method" (some of those
'common' methods I've already listed), or if it was particular to TBills, invoke
os-TBills "do-this-TBillTransaction".

Jimmy
```

Well, business application lingo helps to clear up things a bit. I'll have
to go to first grade to follow all of this with my mind reminding me of a
data stucture called a network.  I don't know if there is a word for that
idea as a method of coding. If it were intergrated data store, I'd be on a
long way to getting on board.  I keep thinking of this idea in terms of
pointers to a Master, to the next, and to the previous with one super chief,
and possible inter related pointers.  If that can be used to minimize the
"storage' on media, perhaps it could be used as a way to break down program
code to minimize it's use. If so, (and I'm damned if I know), then what we
are talking is a different set of terms for the same concept. How screwed up
am I.

Warren

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T05:09:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4D30E6.ADC1F1C7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com> <ADJ27.38629$J91.1743251@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9igb5c$tvl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3B4BE76C.5478F25F@home.com> <Wj837.1506$gj1.52722@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


"warren.simmons" wrote:

> Well, business application lingo helps to clear up things a bit. I'll have
> to go to first grade to follow all of this with my mind reminding me of a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> am I.

Well, I'm glad it helped a bit. And we are using a different set of terms,
basically for the same thing. The thing to remember is that OO terminology was
set in the late sixties by Simula, followed by Xerox PARC giving us Smalltalk.
Dreaming up new languages they had to have ways of describing concepts,
sometimes using ugly words like Encapsulation, Polymorphism. The equivalent of
what we call a Program and Paragraphs they called Classes and Methods. Your
'pointers' that you referred to are still a part of C - interestingly I have an
OOP book that devotes 53 pages to C pitfalls, largely attributable to using
pointers and because things are referred to as objects (object references). Our
good friend Ken Foskey (now working in C) once said "...if you do this you can
get the wrong object...." That left me befuddled - if I invoke os-CustomerFile
"get-data", how the hell does it return an Employee Payslip !!!!! But because
those other languages are object-centric, it does make it "easier" to return
that Payslip <G>

Within Net Express the Animator gives me your 'pointers' or object references
and I see them displayed with an Hex reference plus lots of other info. These
references are stored by the runtime as tables of references - one concern being
to destroy them when no longer required, to avoid memory leaks. (Hence current
J4 discussions on 'Finalizer').

Note while the other OO languages are object-centric, we truly do have the best
of both worlds, we can treat data as numerics/text/strings (pic 9 or pic x) and
only turn it into objects for Window displaying or passing to other languages.
(Of course OO purists would disagree with this 'dual role' - "It's OOP, so it
MUST be objects". BS!).

Now I'm not going to guarantee that this is a fair comparison, but as one 'more
little link in your learning path....' <G> :-

Non-OO                                OO
---------                                ---------
Edit Customers                        Y invokes Z (Z has method "get-abc")
Calls Program A                        ................
Program A calls B                    ...............
Program B calls C                    ...................
^
.......
...................                        .
.......
.................                           .
.......                                        B invokes
C                        ^
......                                         A invokes
B                        .
Program Y calls Z                   Edit Customers Invokes A    .

Non-OO - Calling 'DOWN" and using an Entry Point you could get a paragraph in
Program Z. You certainly wouldn't wiggle your way through A to Z to get at that
paragraph but probably make a call direct to Program Z.

OO - *knowing* that the method is available, I can from Edit Customers write :-

        invoke ClassA "new" returning os-ClassA
        invoke os-ClassA "get-abc" using... returning....

Although I'm invoking against the Class of immediate interest (Class A), not
finding it in A or "supers" B through Y, it hopes to find it in Class Z - if not
error message "Edit Customers does not understand message - get-abc".

If one of those intermediary classes (B through Y) does something different
because of the particular data object we are referring to, then for example
ClassH could also have a method "get-abc" - this would trigger the response and
the invoke wouldn't bother to travel further up the chain. (Maybe that seems a
bit dumb, but you would have to look at a business model to see what Class H was
doing as opposed to Class Z).

The only point you made that I would change the wording for :-

"If that can be used to minimize the "storage' on media, perhaps it could be
used as a way to break down program code to minimize it's use"

Change that to read " ................it could be used as a way to break down
program code to MAXIMIZE  it's REUSE"

My dear old friend - I don't think you are screwed up one itsy-bitsy bit <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T20:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GRn37.41676$J91.2038291@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <1av27.30910$C81.2422624@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4A9166.3EBF64FC@home.com> <ADJ27.38629$J91.1743251@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9igb5c$tvl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3B4BE76C.5478F25F@home.com> <Wj837.1506$gj1.52722@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B4D30E6.ADC1F1C7@home.com>`

```
Wow, and I thought about using the word re-use.  Another big step forward.
The OOP created for generalization because the OOP people thought big <G>
leaves a lot of the population out of the concept. In my effort to learn
what all the hype was about, I was turned of by the psuedo algebraic
notation. It serves to flilter out the dumbies like me for awhile. My first
"break through" had to do with trying to understand why WINDOWS depended
upon so many DLL's, and why 'others" replaced them with their own version,
or when installing them did not put them in the system folder.
I could see why all that GUI stuff could profit by building a library.
Things like that began well before OOP. I know Grace would say so.

Then I wondered about the generalized com or printer support. Seemed like it
was a last resort because code designed for those devices were funtionally
better than the generalized support of WINdos.  Then I read where MS
acquires to use trimed down versions of funtions from another company just
to have something. On top of that when I spring for a first class system, I
can't refuse the MS software that came with it. The price includes it. So
even if I was very happy with WordPerfect for example, I found myself loaded
down with OFFICE 97. For my needs that's a lot of *****.  Having those
applications "use" other "portions" of the OFFICE stuff seemed like it was a
good idea except often what I wanted to do was blocked by the built in
reliance of one piece upon another.  I believe as with anything like a new
method, there is a blind application to be among the IN crowd.

Your assistance helps me see that someone involved knows that by not doing
everything OOP calls for or in limited ways the result will be more
generally used and prove useful. However, NEW words are needed. You used a
few of them as have others during this exchange.  If I were KING, I'd tell
the group to settle on words that are more meaningful, and insist upon using
them.

Poly what?  Morph this!

Thanks,

Warren

>
> Well, I'm glad it helped a bit. And we are using a different set of terms,
> basically for the same thing. The thing to remember is that OO terminology
was
> set in the late sixties by Simula, followed by Xerox PARC giving us
Smalltalk.
> Dreaming up new languages they had to have ways of describing concepts,
> sometimes using ugly words like Encapsulation, Polymorphism. The
equivalent of
> what we call a Program and Paragraphs they called Classes and Methods.
Your
> 'pointers' that you referred to are still a part of C - interestingly I
have an
> OOP book that devotes 53 pages to C pitfalls, largely attributable to
using
> pointers and because things are referred to as objects (object
references). Our
> good friend Ken Foskey (now working in C) once said "...if you do this you
can
> get the wrong object...." That left me befuddled - if I invoke
os-CustomerFile
> "get-data", how the hell does it return an Employee Payslip !!!!! But
because
> those other languages are object-centric, it does make it "easier" to
return
> that Payslip <G>
>
> Within Net Express the Animator gives me your 'pointers' or object
references
> and I see them displayed with an Hex reference plus lots of other info.
These
> references are stored by the runtime as tables of references - one concern
being
> to destroy them when no longer required, to avoid memory leaks. (Hence
current
> J4 discussions on 'Finalizer').
>
> Note while the other OO languages are object-centric, we truly do have the
best
> of both worlds, we can treat data as numerics/text/strings (pic 9 or pic
x) and
> only turn it into objects for Window displaying or passing to other
languages.
> (Of course OO purists would disagree with this 'dual role' - "It's OOP, so
it
> MUST be objects". BS!).
>
> Now I'm not going to guarantee that this is a fair comparison, but as one
'more
> little link in your learning path....' <G> :-
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Non-OO - Calling 'DOWN" and using an Entry Point you could get a paragraph
in
> Program Z. You certainly wouldn't wiggle your way through A to Z to get at
that
> paragraph but probably make a call direct to Program Z.
>
> OO - *knowing* that the method is available, I can from Edit Customers
write :-
>
>         invoke ClassA "new" returning os-ClassA
>         invoke os-ClassA "get-abc" using... returning....
>
> Although I'm invoking against the Class of immediate interest (Class A),
not
> finding it in A or "supers" B through Y, it hopes to find it in Class Z -
if not
> error message "Edit Customers does not understand message - get-abc".
>
> If one of those intermediary classes (B through Y) does something
different
> because of the particular data object we are referring to, then for
example
> ClassH could also have a method "get-abc" - this would trigger the
response and
> the invoke wouldn't bother to travel further up the chain. (Maybe that
seems a
> bit dumb, but you would have to look at a business model to see what Class
H was
> doing as opposed to Class Z).
>
> The only point you made that I would change the wording for :-
>
> "If that can be used to minimize the "storage' on media, perhaps it could
be
> used as a way to break down program code to minimize it's use"
>
> Change that to read " ................it could be used as a way to break
down
> program code to MAXIMIZE  it's REUSE"
>
> My dear old friend - I don't think you are screwed up one itsy-bitsy bit
<G>
>
> Jimmy
>
```

### Re: COBOL Compiler

- **From:** "Melissa" <cobol696@compuserve.de>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T14:19:50+02:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3b4c4437$0$411$7d5a4a58@news.compuserve.de>`
- **References:** `<9hqobu$qir$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi, there!!!

I've been using Micro Focus Cobol for DOS (called Personal Cobol 2.0)
supporting  ANSI 85 since 1990(yes, nineteen-ninety)

While i was "crawling" thru the "best-buy-table" in my local book store i
discovered a really good book for Windows 32-Bit (NT, 98, 98 SE, ME) thats
called Mastering COBOL from Carol Baroudi: It is published by SYBEX, it does
contain a CD with the Fujitsu COBOL Compiler Nr. 4 (four!) (Freeware) and it
costs only $49.99 US-Dollar. The ISBN: 0-7821-2321-X. No joking!!!

The Book comes along with the Y2K-Problem and many more examples, solutions
and hints.

But don't be surprised. The book appears to be the same as the language
COBOL itself: It's big and heavy and it seems to consist of 2 bricks.(So you
can beat your customer to death with it, if  she/ he is trying to tell you
that Microsofts Access is much better and her / his 6 year old nephew is
writing "more colourfull programms" than you...).

If you like to have the lean COBOL 2.0 DOS-Compiler (which is in fact a
student version and it does not produce any *.EXE code but intermediat,
generic code that can be executed in the same way) just tell me via e-mail
cobol696@compuserve.de .(Funny e-mail-address, isn' t it?!)

If you like to i'm going to pack the 2 x 3 1/2 inch disks into one ZIP-file
for you. Just edit with an hex-editor my name out of the starting screen,
would you?

...And: Be assured: I don't have ANY problems with viruses, trojans etc. pp.

## Thread: SP2 Coding Conventions — 8 msgs

### SP2 Coding Conventions

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-10T21:06:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9igco201a7@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
Does anyone have a tip to a published set of SP2 Coding
Conventions.  Namely, stuff like:

(1)  When focussing an input control, the insertion point should
move to the end of the text.

(2)  For record entry screens, there should be an OK and Cancel
button.

(3)  When closing a record w/ unsaved changes, the user should
be prompted with "Save changes?  <yes> <no> <cancel>"

These are all detail examples, but I'm looking for a larger view
of how an SP2 application should present itself.  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: SP2 Coding Conventions

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T02:58:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4c6eb2_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9igco201a7@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
I don't think it is down to SP2. I write a lot of Windows applications using
GUI screens. I TRY and follow the (suggested) standards for Windows controls
which are published by MS. Not because I'm such a big MS fan, but because
this is the general way that Windows Users EXPECT a Windows Application to
behave. The more CONSISTENT a GUI is, the better for ALL concerned...

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SP2 Coding Conventions

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T15:47:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4c7018.1034856735@news>`
- **References:** `<9igco201a7@enews2.newsguy.com> <3b4c6eb2_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Jul 2001 02:58:50 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I don't think it is down to SP2. I write a lot of Windows applications using
>GUI screens. I TRY and follow the (suggested) standards for Windows controls
>which are published by MS. Not because I'm such a big MS fan, but because
>this is the general way that Windows Users EXPECT a Windows Application to
>behave. The more CONSISTENT a GUI is, the better for ALL concerned...
==================
Absolutely on target.  Sure, 'all Windows applications look the same',
but that's the whole point.  One of the greatest contributions that
Windows has made to software has been the imposition of a consistent
user interface.  Unlike the Bad Old DOS days, at least you know how to
close a Windows application without digging through the manual!

The hard part is learning all of the Windows 'standard' conventions.
There are a ton of keyboard conventions (which virtually no one uses)
that you still need to take into account, because it's a certainty
that if you use, say, F10 to trigger some action, someone will come
along and wonder why it doesn't enable the menu k/b interface like
tapping the Alt key does.

Much of this stuff is on the Microsoft site.  The problem is, there
does not seem to be a central location for standard keyboard shortcuts
and other design considerations becaues much of this stuff depends
upon which control is active;   Still, an hour of digging should yield
some good information.  

It's especially troublesome when you're converting an old DOS
application to Windows because so many key combinations that were just
fine in a DOS application, have default, standard Windows actions
assigned to them.  Then, the quandary is, should you retain the old
DOS key combos and pre-empt the Windows standard, or bite the bullet
and make the converted application truly Windows?

Personally, I favor the latter.  It's really nice to know your stuff
behaves like a Windows application 'should'.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com

--- See us at COBOLWorld ---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SP2 Coding Conventions

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T22:07:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ijcha0cne@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9igco201a7@enews2.newsguy.com> <3b4c6eb2_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in
message news:3b4c6eb2_2@news2.newsgroups.com...
> I don't think it is down to SP2. I write a lot of Windows
applications using
> GUI screens. I TRY and follow the (suggested) standards for
Windows controls
> which are published by MS. Not because I'm such a big MS fan,
but because
> this is the general way that Windows Users EXPECT a Windows
Application to
> behave. The more CONSISTENT a GUI is, the better for ALL
concerned...

I totally agree--but I don't think it's realistic to set that as
a standard for an application that's been "maintenenced" from
DOS into windows.  I didn't let you in on this secret
beforehand--sorry about that.

The screen-to-screen mentality of the application would have to
be snapped before those standards could be meaningfully
attained.  Re-factoring the interface wouldn't be so bad--we'd
make up some time in getting to reduce nearly duplicate screens,
but concurrency management would more than absorb that.
```

#### ↳ Re: SP2 Coding Conventions

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T14:45:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4db4d0.1532018@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<9igco201a7@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:

>Does anyone have a tip to a published set of SP2 Coding
>Conventions.  Namely, stuff like:

Grinder:

Sorry for my slow response, but I was staffing our booth down at CA
World in Orlando and just returned to the officve this morning.

We don't really publish a set of sp2 coding conventions, because the
product is designed to allow you to create user interface screens in
any manner which suits your specific needs.

Although we strongly advocate creating Windows screens according to
the Microsoft design standards, there really isn't any forced
compliance in the product.

Our philosophy is that programmers must have a high degree of control
over the user interface design, because sometimes, the Microsoft
standards really don't fit the needs of a very specialized type of
application.

>(1)  When focussing an input control, the insertion point should
>move to the end of the text.

This is accomplished automatically through the use of right justified
custom fields.

>(2)  For record entry screens, there should be an OK and Cancel
>button.

COBOL sp2 allows you to create "template panels" which contain
standard screen layouts.  These "template panels" can be dragged into
the Format Window from the Treeview Control when creating a new panel.


If you create a standard template panel for record entry screens, you
would simply create the OK and Cancel buttons on the template panel.
When you want to create a new record entry panel, simply drag and drop
the template panel from the treeview control to the Format Window in
the Panel Editor.  Make any cuystom changes to the record entry screen
and then click on the File menu bar option and the Save As pulldown
menu bar option.  Give the panel a new name.  Your template panel
remains intact and you created a panel without having to create all of
the standard controls on the panel because they were already contained
in the template.

>(3)  When closing a record w/ unsaved changes, the user should
>be prompted with "Save changes?  <yes> <no> <cancel>"

We suggest creating a standard "Save changes?" message box routine in
your program and invoke it anytime the end user closes a record entry
panel.  That allows you to have a single routine which is used by all
record entry panels ion your application.

>These are all detail examples, but I'm looking for a larger view
>of how an SP2 application should present itself.  Thanks.

Again, there really isn't any specific manner in which an sp2
application should present itself.  We *strongly* recommend designing
your user interface screens in the "Microsoft design standards" but
certainly don't force you to design your screens in that manner.

I hope that this helps.  If you have any specific design issues, we
would be happy to assist you.  Please contact me at rtwolfe@flexus.com
if you would like to obtain more assistance.  I'll refer you to
someone who can help you with panel design or programming questions.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

October 2-4, 2001 - Anaheim, California

Thanks for the input Bob.  The specific concerns were really
more of an example of what I would find in the imagined
standard, than actual dilemmas, but I think your ideas are good.

Since our application came from a DOS application, it would take
a significant re-factoring to get it to behave like a "standard"
windows application (if such a thing exists.)  Short of that, I
was hoping someone had documented a good compromise.

Thanks again.

### Re: Alternative Standard

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107102026.4d949591@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete's post was distributed to the J4 mailing list, and produced 2
e-mail comments, which I have attached at the end.

Stephen J Spiro




"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com>...
> This Forum has recently examined how the building of a COBOL Standard could
> be improved by OPENing the process up so that EVERYONE (programmers,
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
*-------------------------------------------------

From Dennis Booth

Hello All,

Regarding the concept of opening up the COBOL Standard process
so that EVERYONE can become involved in the development of the
Language
and that this USER BASE be EMPOWERED, wasn't this concept tried by
the Java Folks and it just about killed the Java Standard process?

If I recall correctly, the Java committee (or whatever it is
officially
called)
opened up the Java Standard process to EVERYONE and they got something
like
1,500 to 2,500 companies / individuals signed up as voting type
members.

After a short amount of time, it was realized that the operation
management
principles held true that the more decision makers in a process, the
longer
it will take to make a decision (or never to be able to make a
decision).

As a result, the 1,500 to 2,500 folks were basically down graded to
suggestion
providers.

Does anyone else remember the above and if so, could you provide us/me
with
a web
site address detailing the above.  I tried to find the articles but
could
not
find them at this time, I will also try later.

Thanks,

Dennis Booth

*------------------------------------

From Walter Murray:

Dennis,

Here's what my Java guru has to say.  The JCP web site that he
mentions
describes the current process.  We don't know about any discussion of
past
problems.

Walter

-----Mike Yawn's message follows.

Walter,

I'm not an expert on how the Java Community Process (JCP) has evolved.
You can see how it's currently structured at http://java.sun.com/jcp.
Some of the criticisms about 'letting everyone have a vote' may have
been true in previous iterations; I'm just not sure.

Currently, for each specification that is launched, there is an
expert group formed.   I know HP has participated in some expert
groups, and I also know of other vendor employees who participate;
my impression is that if you care about something, it isn't that
difficult to become a member of the expert group.  The expert group
has the real power; there is a community review process, and the
opportunity for anyone to provide feedback, but decision-making on
what is or isn't part of the specification rests solely with the
expert group, as I understand it.  This probably keeps the timeline
manageable, but if you don't trust the expert group to fairly
consider all community feedback, then I'm not sure there's any
way this process would be considered satisfactory.

One thing that keeps the Java process manageable -- the specifications
are very narrow.  They don't just throw open "Java" to the world at
large to debate on new features.  Instead, someone proposes something
very specific -- such as Generics, an Assertion Facility, a Logging
Facility, an API for XML parsing (all of these are recent
specifications).
An expert group is then formed to put together the specification for
just that piece of functionality.  Sun is a member of every expert
group,
and I think their role (well, at least their publicly admitted role)
is
to make sure there is some coherence between groups, so that each
group
is aware of what others are doing to the extent they may overlap,
conflict,
or complement one another.

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: Alternative Standard

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T01:38:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4c5cd8_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

Stephen,

I didn't send it to J4/Alphabet soup and, quite frankly, I couldn't care
less what they think about it.

There is no reason why members of the above cannot contribute to an open
forum if they so desire, they are part of the COBOL User  Base and some of
them may have very valuable contributions to make... I doubt that many will
actually do so because the mind set required to be part of the current
Standards Authority is diametrically opposed to the mindset I am seeking to
establish...

Still, you never know, and it may well be that  some people are there
because they see no other option...

As you have decided to introduce their deliberations into this forum, I'll
respond... but don't expect it to become a habit...<G>

see below...

"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0107102026.4d949591@posting.google.com...
> Pete's post was distributed to the J4 mailing list, and produced 2
> e-mail comments, which I have attached at the end.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com>...
> > This Forum has recently examined how the building of a COBOL Standard
could
> > be improved by OPENing the process up so that EVERYONE (programmers,

interested
> > party) can become involved in the development of the Language.
> >
> > Some (very limited) trials have been conducted and have shown that this
> > process can stimulate interest in COBOL and revitalise the whole process
of
> > ongoing language development. Valuable results can be obtained in this
way.
> >
> > For this process to be effective, it is ESSENTIAL that the User Base be
> > EMPOWERED to take decisions regarding what will and won't be in the

could

the

approved

> > expended in discussing the issues, pointless.)
> >
> > At the moment, COBOL is alive because the Vendors have a close eye on
the

implementers
> > to produce this. However, this means different views of what is
necessary,
> > and a consequent failure of cross platform capability due to the lack of
a

> >
> > My personal belief is that, if the existing "Guardians of COBOL" are not
> > prepared to open up the process of establishing a Standard, they should
be
> > bypassed and the User Base should simply reclaim the Language (which was
> > always supposed to be Public Domain anyway...).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Nelson,
> > for example).
> >
> >       1.3  The promotion and dissemination of the Standard produced. (A
> > small charge could be levied on copies of it to defray some of this
cost.)

least
> > the above are honest costs.)

and to

Standard.

it

criticism,

if

> > written in simple English (that can be used as the basis for a vendor
User
> > Manual, as well as an implementor guide), within a given timeframe,
without
> > excuses, prevarication, or disavowal of responsibility.
> >
> > If you can help with any of the above, please mail me privately.
> >
> > I am allowing a 4 month time frame to decide whether the above
requirements
> > are achievable or whether we just have to accept that there will be some
> > degree of platform dependence, due to the non-availability of a credible
and
> > usable COBOL standard. (Note that I do not consider continuing with the
> > current arrangement to be an option.)
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So, on that basis we should support Dictatorships as they "get things done"
(Well, everyone knows that Mussolini got the trains running on time and
Hitler built superb roads to move his armies around...<G>) Unfortunately,
J4/Alphabet soup, despite maintaining strict control of power over the
Standard, have FAILED to build any roads and the trains are running later
than ever... And no, it is no good saying there is a train due to arrive any
minute; it was supposed to be here 18 months ago and some of us are sick of
stale sandwiches and station coffee...<G>

The principle referred to above is only true in Waterfall environments.

I am proposing a RAD type evironment  with an OPEN Forum contributed to by
an EMPOWERED User Base. The process will proceed by iterative interaction
from the User Base within a series of TIMEBOXes.

Unlike the Waterfall (where phases have to be extended if they are not
completed on schedule, and consequently, the whole process "slips" as soon
as the slack runs out...)TIMEBOXes are RIGID and CANNOT be extended. (That
is why the assignment of priorities is crucial. You work through the
priorities within the time allocated to the Timebox. It doesn't matter
whether there are 10 or 1000 people involved, when the time is up, that's
it. You go with what you have, until the next iteration of it is budgeted,
resourced, and started (could be a year, could be 6 weeks, depends on the
priority and how critical an issue it is)).

This means the TOP priority items are "trickled out" as the process
proceeds.

I realise this concept is foreign to most of the people in J4/Alphabet Soup
who have mainframe or large Corporate backgrounds (and so understand the
Waterfall), and may not necessarily have had extensive experience in Project
Management. (Many people on the Committee are Technical and have programming
backgrounds; nothing wrong with this... it is very desirable, but the
Management of their effort has failed dismally, and I believe it stems from
failure to "manage" the process of Standard production properly. <it's an
OPINION, I could be wrong <G>, but the fact is we are still waiting for a
Standard...>)

This is not the place, and I don't have the inclination, to write a
discourse on "Rapid Application Development (RAD) vs the Waterfall".
However, I will be presenting a paper on "Dynamic Management of COBOL
Projects" at CW2001 and it will cover many of these concepts and ideas. I
would urge people who seriously want to see an alternative way to get
results (not just in relation to the Standard production, but for IT
projects in general), to attend this seminar.
(in a REAL Dictatorship, ALL J4 members would be REQUIRED to
attend...."Dashwood or the Salt mines..?"   "Arrghhh! The salt mines!, the
salt mines!!!").

All I will say here is that there are modern Project Management Methods
which have been proven in the Market place to achieve results. They have
nothing to do with implementing the Waterfall approach.

They have a lot to do with ITERATION, INTERACTION with the User Base, and
empowerment of the team involved.


> As a result, the 1,500 to 2,500 folks were basically down graded to
> suggestion
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks,

Even if Dennis is correct and this did happen to Java (I honestly don't know
whether it did or not, but I am a registered Java Developer, receive the
updates and news, and have not seen anything about this), it is completely
irrelevant when it comes to COBOL.

Java was NEVER a Public Domain Language; COBOL is.

I don't know how the alleged process above was managed, but it doesn't look
like what we are contemplating with the alternative standard for COBOL.

Whatever happened with Java is irrelevant to what we are proposing, because
what we are proposing is a COMPLETELY different process with a
non-proprietary Language.
>
> *------------------------------------
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snipped discussion on Java Community Project>

I have received enough support for this project to make me continue looking
at it. Discouragement from vested interests, based on parallells with Java
or Algol or Jovial or any other programming language that ISN'T COBOL are
not going to stop it.

The only thing that would give me pause at the moment is if J4/Alphabet soup
were seen to open up their process and accept changes to their existing
Management procedures. Feedback from them to date suggests that Satan will
be wearing an anorak before this eventuates...

If we are unable to get the necessary support (moral support from vendors
and the User Base; financial support from Corporates (and the jury is still
out... they are in a 4 month timebox <G, but there are some promising
indications...)), then, obviously it will not be viable and I'll give it up.

At least I'll know I did my best... I  still won't accept the relevance of
the J4 Standard because I don't believe it IS relevant.
(How could I, when we have seen that it is NOT targeted at the User Base, is
NOT written in plain English, is controlled by an unelected "elite" (who may
be "all honourable men" in Shakespeare's eyes, but they are still an
unelected group maintaining control of something which does not now, and
never has, belonged to them ...)?).

Fortunately, Standard or no Standard, vendors will produce COBOL tools and
compilers as long as there is a market for them (How do I know this? They've
done so for the past 10 years!).

As long as the User Base creates a demand, and Vendors and Implementers see
profit in filling it, we will have COBOL. It may not be exactly the SAME
COBOL across all platforms and implementations, but it isn't NOW...! (even
with the 85 Standard and official amendments since...), so I believe COBOL
will survive without a Standard if it has to. It just makes everybody feel
better if there IS a Standard that is credible.

The one about to be produced (irrespective of its content) is not credible,
because it has not been produced by a democratic consensus (so there is
therefore no basis to believe it reflects the wishes of the User Base...);
I'd like to see one that is. If it can't be done (and I believe it CAN be,
given the things I outlined in my original post above), I'd rather see no
Standard, than continue the present fiasco.

And that, Stephen, is really all I have to say about it <G>...

Pete.

PS to the Rest of You:

Please mail me with your thoughts, positive or negative. I need to get a
broader feel from the User Base as to how YOU feel about the standards
issue.

Is it really only important to the people involved in producing it? Even if
you feel it is of no relevance to you personally, drop me a line and say
that.

If you felt you could make a difference, would you participate?

Could/Would you contribute time to help run the public Forum? (edit prose,
count votes, etc).

Are you happy with what is being produced?

Are you happy with how it is produced?

 I would really value ALL inputs, and will respond to as many as I can. A
considerable amount of time and effort  (not JUST mine <G>) is going into
the exploration of an alternative. It would be ironic if no one really gives
a damn...(and funny <G>)

TIA,

Pete.

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:<9ii7b7$nej$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3b4c5cd8_1@news2.newsgroups.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

Had to look this one up:

Godwin's Law
Godwin's Law     prov.     [Usenet] "As a Usenet discussion grows
longer, the probability of a comparison involving Nazis or Hitler
approaches one." There is a tradition in many groups that, once this
occurs, that thread is over, and whoever mentioned the Nazis has
automatically lost whatever argument was in progress. Godwin's Law
thus practically guarantees the existence of an upper bound on thread
length in those groups. However there is also a widely- recognized
codicil that any intentional triggering of Godwin's Law in order to
invoke its thread-ending effects will be unsuccessful.
```

Nope. It invokes Dashwood's Codicil which states: "Sometimes Godwin's Law
can be used to stimulate and provoke further discussion in a thread, rather
than close it down. The rules require that a POSITIVE comment on Hitler or
the Nazis should be made in order to do this."

<tee hee>

Pete.

> > If you can help with any of the above, please mail me privately.
> >
> > I am allowing a 4 month time frame to decide whether the above
requirements
> > are achievable or whether we just have to accept that there will be some
> > degree of platform dependence, due to the non-availability of a credible
and
> > usable COBOL standard. (Note that I do not consider continuing with the
> > current arrangement to be an option.)
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So, on that basis we should support Dictatorships as they "get things done"
(Well, everyone knows that Mussolini got the trains running on time and
Hitler built superb roads to move his armies around...<G>) Unfortunately,
J4/Alphabet soup, despite maintaining strict control of power over the
Standard, have FAILED to build any roads and the trains are running later
than ever... And no, it is no good saying there is a train due to arrive any
minute; it was supposed to be here 18 months ago and some of us are sick of
stale sandwiches and station coffee...<G>

The principle referred to above is only true in Waterfall environments.

I am proposing a RAD type environment  with an OPEN Forum contributed to by
an EMPOWERED User Base. The process will proceed by iterative interaction
from the User Base within a series of TIMEBOXes.

Unlike the Waterfall (where phases have to be extended if they are not
completed on schedule, and consequently, the whole process "slips" as soon
as the slack runs out...)TIMEBOXes are RIGID and CANNOT be extended. (That
is why the assignment of priorities is crucial. You work through the
priorities within the time allocated to the Timebox. It doesn't matter
whether there are 10 or 1000 people involved, when the time is up, that's
it. You go with what you have, until the next iteration of it is budgeted,
resourced, and started (could be a year, could be 6 weeks, depends on the
priority and how critical an issue it is)).

This means the TOP priority items are "trickled out" as the process
proceeds.

I realise this concept is foreign to most of the people in J4/Alphabet Soup
who have mainframe or large Corporate backgrounds (and so understand the
Waterfall), and may not necessarily have had extensive experience in Project
Management. (Many people on the Committee are Technical and have programming
backgrounds; nothing wrong with this... it is very desirable, but the
Management of their effort has failed dismally, and I believe it stems from
failure to "manage" the process of Standard production properly. <it's an
OPINION, I could be wrong <G>, but the fact is we are still waiting for a
Standard...>)

This is not the place, and I don't have the inclination, to write a
discourse on "Rapid Application Development (RAD) Vs the Waterfall".
However, I will be presenting a paper on "Dynamic Management of COBOL
Projects" at CW2001 and it will cover many of these concepts and ideas. I
would urge people who seriously want to see an alternative way to get
results (not just in relation to the Standard production, but for IT
projects in general), to attend this seminar.
(in a REAL Dictatorship, ALL J4 members would be REQUIRED to
attend...."Dashwood or the Salt mines..?"   "Arrghhh! The salt mines!, the
salt mines!!!").

All I will say here is that there are modern Project Management Methods
which have been proven in the Market place to achieve results. They have
nothing to do with implementing the Waterfall approach.

They have a lot to do with ITERATION, INTERACTION with the User Base, and
empowerment of the team involved.


> As a result, the 1,500 to 2,500 folks were basically down graded to
> suggestion
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks,

Even if Dennis is correct and this did happen to Java (I honestly don't know
whether it did or not, but I am a registered Java Developer, receive the
updates and news, and have not seen anything about this), it is completely
irrelevant when it comes to COBOL.

Java was NEVER a Public Domain Language; COBOL is.

I don't know how the alleged process above was managed, but it doesn't look
like what we are contemplating with the alternative standard for COBOL.

Whatever happened with Java is irrelevant to what we are proposing, because
what we are proposing is a COMPLETELY different process with a
non-proprietary Language.
>
> *------------------------------------
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snipped discussion on Java Community Project>

I have received enough support for this project to make me continue looking
at it. Discouragement from vested interests, based on parallels with Java
or ALGOL or Jovial or any other programming language that ISN'T COBOL are
not going to stop it.

The only thing that would give me pause at the moment is if J4/Alphabet soup
were seen to open up their process and accept changes to their existing
Management procedures. Feedback from them to date suggests that Satan will
be wearing an anorak before this eventuates...

If we are unable to get the necessary support (moral support from vendors
and the User Base; financial support from Corporates (and the jury is still
out... they are in a 4 month timebox <G, but there are some promising
indications...)), then, obviously it will not be viable and I'll give it up.

At least I'll know I did my best... I  still won't accept the relevance of
the J4 Standard because I don't believe it IS relevant.
(How could I, when we have seen that it is NOT targeted at the User Base, is
NOT written in plain English, is controlled by an unelected "elite" (who may
be "all honourable men" in Shakespeare's eyes, but they are still an
unelected group maintaining control of something which does not now, and
never has, belonged to them ...)?).

Fortunately, Standard or no Standard, vendors will produce COBOL tools and
compilers as long as there is a market for them (How do I know this? They've
done so for the past 10 years!).

As long as the User Base creates a demand, and Vendors and Implementers see
profit in filling it, we will have COBOL. It may not be exactly the SAME
COBOL across all platforms and implementations, but it isn't NOW...! (even
with the 85 Standard and official amendments since...), so I believe COBOL
will survive without a Standard if it has to. It just makes everybody feel
better if there IS a Standard that is credible.

The one about to be produced (irrespective of its content) is not credible,
because it has not been produced by a democratic consensus (so there is
therefore no basis to believe it reflects the wishes of the User Base...);
I'd like to see one that is. If it can't be done (and I believe it CAN be,
given the things I outlined in my original post above), I'd rather see no
Standard, than continue the present fiasco.

And that, Stephen, is really all I have to say about it <G>...

Pete.

PS to the Rest of You:

Please mail me with your thoughts, positive or negative. I need to get a
broader feel from the User Base as to how YOU feel about the standards
issue.

Is it really only important to the people involved in producing it? Even if
you feel it is of no relevance to you personally, drop me a line and say
that.

If you felt you could make a difference, would you participate?

Could/Would you contribute time to help run the public Forum? (edit prose,
count votes, etc).

Are you happy with what is being produced?

Are you happy with how it is produced?

Over the past years, many of you have received help privately (and publicly)
from me. (Some of you even said "Thanks Pete..."<G>).

I am now asking for something from you.

Not your agreement to what I am proposing if you honestly don't support it.
Just your mark on a piece of electronic paper. Just your input.

 I would really value ALL inputs, and will respond to as many as I can. A
considerable amount of time and effort  (not JUST mine <G>) is going into
the exploration of an alternative. It would be ironic if no one really gives
a damn...(and funny <G>)

TIA,

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Alternative Standard

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T17:29:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4C8CE1.47D9CB84@home.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Pete's post was distributed to the J4 mailing list, and produced 2
> e-mail comments, which I have attached at the end.
…[93 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike

Well firstly, Dennis hits a 'negative'. If my memory serves me well, he also hit a 'negative' to John
Piggott's suggestion of 'teleconferencing'. Why do some look for demerits and ignore possible merits ? Then
he makes the observation :-

"After a short amount of time, it was realized that the operation management principles held true that the
more decision makers in a process, the longer it will take to make a decision (or never to be able to make a
decision)"

To be fair to Dennis I think he was 'new to the game' as of the Newbury May/June 2000 meeting. However,
without trying it, could one suggest that the proposed process would be as long as the current process -
1985 - 2002 ?????.

Walter then quotes Mike on the current Java mechanism. The important thing here is  'expert group' and how
you define it. Agreed you do not want 1,500 - 2,000 people trundling all over the place, side-tracking and
discussing EVERY COBOL concept. Democracy is great - providing you don't go nuts. What's the latest count on
how many governments there have been in Italy since WW2 ?

You have to rationalize the whole process. There is nothing in what Pete suggests that indicates a free for
all. The 'expert groups' are a coming together of those who have an interest in a particular topic and it
has to be closely monitored to stay on course.

Some possible examples :-

(a) VALIDATE - rules are set and we aren't going to be able to use it until probably 2005. When used one or
two note it has some 'flaws'. A topic group 'VALIDATE' is started to submit their comments; perhaps 50 join
in on this particular discussion, eventually arriving at an acceptable solution - from 50 people not 13
(Oops! 12).

(b) EXCEPTION CONDITIONS - again carved in stone and not available until 2005. Currently an ALPHABETICAL
list of conditions. I could make a case that these should be re-arranged into logical groupings giving them
numeric values so that I can call a sub-program or invoke an Error Class to handle the Exception display
messages - parallel to File Status codes. At the current time the standard puts out a 'text message' only.

(c) COBOL files - Pete isn't interested, with a preference for DBs - BUT he could, if he so wished, make
relevant observations. Again for ISAMs I could make a case that it would be useful to have Reference
Modification to handle (1) PrimeKeys, (2) Alternate Keys or (3) Split Alternate Keys, thus allowing for
generic file handling.

Each of the above creates its own topic thread. It may result in changes as a result of input, or the
original suggestion may just be nullified.

Jimmy
```

This "Operation Management Principle" does NOT apply to the kind of project
being considered. On RAD projects, the more "decision makers" the better.
That's what empowerment is about. Besides, the activities are Timeboxed so
it cannot overrun. When "decision makers" cannot decide and the timebox is
up, it must be put to a binding vote. Whatever the decision, that's what you
go with for that iteration.




> To be fair to Dennis I think he was 'new to the game' as of the Newbury
May/June 2000 meeting. However,

long as the current process -

here is  'expert group' and how
> you define it. Agreed you do not want 1,500 - 2,000 people trundling all
over the place, side-tracking and
> discussing EVERY COBOL concept. Democracy is great - providing you don't
go nuts. What's the latest count on
> how many governments there have been in Italy since WW2 ?
>
That's why I am suggesting moderated threads  that remain strictly on topic.

> You have to rationalize the whole process. There is nothing in what Pete
suggests that indicates a free for
> all. The 'expert groups' are a coming together of those who have an
interest in a particular topic and it
> has to be closely monitored to stay on course.
Absolutely! Thank you for pointing this out, Jimmy. It is certainly not an
undisciplined "free-for-all", rather, it is a structured "free-for-all"
within limited constraints, for a finite and fixed period of time. During
this period, EVERYONE contributes, then the general consensus is established
and run with, until it is refined at the next iteration. In this way TOP
Priority functions are "trickled out" of the process without having to wait
until EVERYTHING is 100% before it can be published.

>
> Some possible examples :-
>
> (a) VALIDATE - rules are set and we aren't going to be able to use it
until probably 2005. When used one or
> two note it has some 'flaws'. A topic group 'VALIDATE' is started to
submit their comments; perhaps 50 join
> in on this particular discussion, eventually arriving at an acceptable
solution - from 50 people not 13
> (Oops! 12).
>

"'Tis a consummation devoutly to be wished..."!

Your description here is right on the button. It is what we are currently
looking at for the alternative standard.

> (b) EXCEPTION CONDITIONS - again carved in stone and not available until
2005. Currently an ALPHABETICAL
> list of conditions. I could make a case that these should be re-arranged
into logical groupings giving them
> numeric values so that I can call a sub-program or invoke an Error Class
to handle the Exception display
> messages - parallel to File Status codes. At the current time the standard
puts out a 'text message' only.
>
Could be discussed and decided in exactly the same way with a consensus of
interested parties.

> (c) COBOL files - Pete isn't interested, with a preference for DBs - BUT
he could, if he so wished, make
> relevant observations. Again for ISAMs I could make a case that it would
be useful to have Reference
> Modification to handle (1) PrimeKeys, (2) Alternate Keys or (3) Split
Alternate Keys, thus allowing for
> generic file handling.
>
Whether I am interested or not is irrelevant, and I certainly have enough
experience to be able to contribute in this area. The point is that
EVERYBODY, should be able to comment and contribute on WHATEVER their
personal interest is. There could be cases where someone's personal
experience could lead them to post on something, even though they are not
necessarily passionate about that particular feature. It is still valuable.
The people who are passionate about it, can benefit from a more moderate
viewpoint.


> Each of the above creates its own topic thread. It may result in changes
as a result of input, or the
> original suggestion may just be nullified.
>
Exactly! Why is it so difficult?<G>

Pete.

It was developed for a particular customer of ours, and the contract has
been completed. We are looking for other Cobol developers who either have an

there legacy code converted.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

- **From:** "Lloyd Clarke" <lloydc@ndms.org>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T12:02:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote:
> i think you'll find that the best tool out there is a human being.

Ned,

The point about using a computer for a straightforward conversion is that it
can do it quicker, cheaper & more reliably.

Human involvement should be saved for any creative enhancements at the end
of the process.

For small software houses or in-house departments, conversion offers help in
that awkward changeover phase when you are too busy maintaining and
enhancing the old version to move on.

In addition, the conversion from Cobol to VB allows a Cobol shop to continue
to develop in Cobol, but to supply a VB version with GUI enhancements & SQL
access to a potentially new market.

Alternatively, it allows a VB shop or a new VB programmer to add
developments in a language that they understand.

Best regards,

Lloyd

Further abuse to Lloyd@NDMS.Org

http://www.ndms.org/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T12:07:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y9V57.235$ZM4.34267@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com> <3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`

```
Lloyd Clarke <lloydc@ndms.org> wrote in message
news:3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com...
>
> G Moore wrote:
> > i think you'll find that the best tool out there [to port code from
COBOL to Visual BASIC] is a human being.
>
> Ned,
>
> The point about using a computer for a straightforward conversion is that
it
> can do it quicker, cheaper & more reliably.
>
> Human involvement should be saved for any creative enhancements at the end
> of the process.

I'm with Mr. Moore on this.

I am a longtime (and some might say, "outspoken") (I accept) critic of
'verb-for-verb' conversions. In the case cited - two common source
languages, lots of available talent - my main criticism of 'mindless'
conversions is especially appropos.

If the target language does not offer better ways to do things, then why
convert at all? And I can see no way in hell an automated process can
include the kind of logic which goes something like, " in COBOL, you use
global variables and fixed-size tables because that's all that's available.
VB permits locally scoped variables and dynamically sized tables, which are
(at least arguably) a marked advantage over COBOL. So we will convert the
globals and fixed-size tables to local data and dynamic tables."

There are dozens more examples of "better ways" to do certain things; and
here we are dealing with two very similar source languages. Well written
COBOL looks a lot like well-written BASIC. (Poorly-written COBOL looks like,
um, poorly-written COBOL).

Maybe there is a case for "move to VB for future maintenance needs," but it
sure seems to me that version 1.0 of anything - on which significant
maintenance is anticipated - should not be some contrived, bloated and
inefficient amalgam of some hard-wired verb-swapping.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Lloyd Clarke" <lloydc@ndms.org>
- **Date:** 2001-07-23T10:27:44+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b5bef97_3@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com> <3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <Y9V57.235$ZM4.34267@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Mick,

It would be silly of me to argue the relative merits of modern Cobol
toolsets & VB in a Cobol newsgroup. I suspect that they'll both be all right
when they're finished.

The reality for users of many Cobol systems is still character-based screens
and critical company information locked away in legacy data files. There are
other solutions, but I obviously feel that from the users' perspective the
converted product would offer significant improvements.

The resultant package would be version n + 1. A few, choice, inexpensive
components could then add the sort of modern functionality that users now
expect.

The opportunities for improving the structure of code whist converting are
similar to those available without conversion. A more powerful conversion
can be obtained by replacing standard code elements, copy files & library
routines as logical entities rather than at the individual verb level, but
this requires a little more 'mindless' effort.

Best regards,

Lloyd

Further abuse to Lloyd@NDMS.Org

http://www.ndms.org/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T11:50:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jrh9k$vqi$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com> <3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <Y9V57.235$ZM4.34267@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b5bef97_3@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`

```
Why why why try to convert the whole app? You don't need to.

With Micro Focus NetExpress (and probably also the competition) you can keep
your existing cobol code and call that from your user interface written in
VB. You can wrap the code as a COM object. Any code produced by conversion
utilities and other "sausage machines" is invariably awful and this utility,
I bet, is no exception.

NetExpress has the ability to write gui interfaces and also handles SQL. The
need for gui and sql does not mean a need to move away from cobol, by far
the best language for business rules. VB is good for user interfaces so use
the two together. White-papers on doing this can be found at
www.microfocus.com

news:3b5bef97_3@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com...

screens
> and critical company information locked away in legacy data files. There
are
> other solutions, but I obviously feel that from the users' perspective the

and
