<a id="t-0583e2cac2"></a>
## Thread: Style vs Substance — 67 msgs

### Style vs Substance

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T11:47:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Hi:

Recently, in another thread Mr. Dashwood accused me of calling 
him a 'liar' and of 'denigrating' his COBOL code.

To get the facts behind the 'liar' accusation, read the thread
'Dear Mr. Dashwood' posted some time ago.

Now for the next topic.

Mr. Dashwood accuses me of 'denigrating' his code. What I actually 
said was 'Mr. Dashwood's post gives us all an opportunity to compare
a structured program with one written in a sensible manner. . .'.
(see foodman123.com/post.htm)

As I said somewhere else, I considered this to be mildly sarcastic
rather than denigration. Mr. Dashwood also made the point that if
one was going to post code to the forum, one had better be prepared
for the consequences, or words to that effect. 

Anyway, since I am accused of denigration, I will go ahead and denigrate.

Mr. Dashwood described my code as an 'eyesore'.  

This suggests to me that Mr. Dashwood judges COBOL code primarily
by what it LOOKS LIKE rather than whether it is efficient, simple,
compact, sensible, easy-to-comprehend, and so on.

I think the reason Mr. Dashwood thinks my code is an 'eyesore' is
because it LOOKS SO LIKE OLD-FASHIONED COBOL, which is what it 
is, of course.

On the other hand, Mr. Dashwood's code is markedly different - upon
a casual glance it might even be C or Java or something with
its nested construction and mostly lower-case style and lots of lines
and wasted space.

I think that there is is a conscious effort on the part of the modern
COBOL programmer (as exemplified by Mr. Dashwood) to try to make 
their programs not LOOK like COBOL.

It seems that the trend is to try and make them look like programs
written in those other languages. To accomplish this goal, the code
is written in a nested style to take up as many lines as possible 
and to contain really useless stuff. 

A good example of cluttering programs up with useless stuff in order
to make them seem, perhaps, more stylish, is Mr. Dashwood's 
use of 'binary-fields', indexing, COMP-5 and SECTION.

It is categorically impossible to justify the use of these things
on TECHNICAL GROUNDS, i.e., that the compiled program will be
faster, smaller, better, to a measurable and worth-while extent. 
Maybe in the olden days, when indexing was introduced, there could 
have been some justification for some of these features, but 
today?  Why bother?

The only time the use of COMP-5 would be justified would be if 
you had to interface with something which required the parameters 
to be in that format. In the Dashwood case, this does not apply.

It follows that if it is categorically impossible to justify the use
of something on technical grounds, then its use must be in the
name of style, pretence, ostentation, or something else.

How would you justify the use of indexing or COMP-5 to a new student?
How do you explain to the student that using COMP-5 doesn't really do
anything of significance but use it anyway, because it looks good
and is part of the language?  If you use COMP-5, will your program 
be faster, smaller, more efficient, or what? What is the technical 
advantage? There is none - so why bother?

(I have no problem with the existence of things like COMP-5
when they have been included in the language for compatibility
with other stuff. Of course, why that other stuff required
the invention of COMP-5 is another question.) 

Why bother with indexing?  It doesn't provide any technical
advantage so using it can only be a matter of style. Why is 
'set x1 up by 1' better than 'ADD 1 TO X1'?  Would you encourage 
a student to use indexing, and, if so, on what grounds?  All
students are going to learn about tables and subscripting, why
bother teaching them indexing, because it is part of the language
invented in the olden days for some purpose or other? The 
attitude of use-it-because-it-exists is pointless if there is
no technical advantage. Why waste time typing it?

In his original post Mr. Dashwood pointed out that he had used
SECTION and did not wish to get into a big discussion over it.
This is good because it is impossible to justify the use of
SECTION on technical grounds today. It follows that use of
SECTION is stylistic.

I am probably the only person in the entire world who increments
level numbers by 1. Just about every COBOL program I have ever
seen incremented them by 5. Unusually, Mr. Dashwood increments
his level numbers by 12. 

The comparable code to move all the spaces to the end of the
string, as some wag said, requires 23 lines for Mr.Dashwood
and 9 lines for me. This is style over substance, if you ask
me but it sure makes his program look more C-like or something
and not like that horrible old-fashioned COBOL like I write.

On the other hand, my code is totally devoid of any unnecessary
frou-frou, is utterly simple, compact, easy-to-read, neat,
efficient, and, although one could change it, it would be hard
to make a significant improvement. My approach to programming
is to do what I need to do with the utmost economy both in terms
of the time it takes to write and the efficiency of the code.

Execution-time efficiency is totally irrelevant these days but
I personally enjoy reducing something to its essence rather than 
decorating it with useless stuff and trying to make it look
like something it isn't in the name of style.

I don't really care if people write inane programs full of
ostentatious stuff, but I do care when people criticize me for
not doing the same thing. 

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T14:30:41+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.ggbnf50.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com>`

```
On Wed, 11 Jul 2001 11:47:00 GMT, Foodman wrote:

>Hi:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
First of all, I don't want to denigrate anyone here, Mr. Dashwood and Mr.
Dilworth have a different way of coding and both ways are different from
mine.

>As I said somewhere else, I considered this to be mildly sarcastic
>rather than denigration. Mr. Dashwood also made the point that if
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>compact, sensible, easy-to-comprehend, and so on.

I have read both examples, and to me, Mr. Dashwoods code is easier to
comprehend than yours.
>
>I think the reason Mr. Dashwood thinks my code is an 'eyesore' is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and wasted space.

Wasted space? I don't think there is anything wasted and anyway, the way it
looks has nothing to do with the final compiler output.
The nested construction is easier to follow for someone who hasn't written
the program than GO TO style programs.
>
>I think that there is is a conscious effort on the part of the modern
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and to contain really useless stuff. 

Useless stuff? It's use for me is that it takes me less time to understand
the logic flow of the program.

>
>A good example of cluttering programs up with useless stuff in order
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>today?  Why bother?

For me, the best reason to use indexes is that an index belongs just to one
table, making it much easier to trace references to the table and how and
where it is modified.
Have you ever tried to find an error in a program that uses one index field
to access more than one table?
>
>The only time the use of COMP-5 would be justified would be if 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why? as long as you don't see a field, I don't think its internal format has
anything to do with style or pretence.
On IBM mainframes I used COMP-3 a lot, on other machines binary is faster
than DISPLAY.

>How would you justify the use of indexing or COMP-5 to a new student?
>How do you explain to the student that using COMP-5 doesn't really do
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>no technical advantage. Why waste time typing it?

Indexing CAN have advantages, and don't forget, to be able to use SEARCH or
SEARCH ALL on a table, you'll have to use indexes.

>
>In his original post Mr. Dashwood pointed out that he had used
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>SECTION is stylistic.

It is just as impossible (or possible) to justify the use of SECTION as it is
to justify the use of GO TO, PERFORM THROUGH etc.

>
>I am probably the only person in the entire world who increments
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And so what?  The only use of level numbers is to show the division of fields
in sub-fields. If I have to read a program, I don't care if the numbers are
1,2,3 or 1,5,10 or whatever. It is always clear enough.

>The comparable code to move all the spaces to the end of the
>string, as some wag said, requires 23 lines for Mr.Dashwood
>and 9 lines for me. This is style over substance, if you ask
>me but it sure makes his program look more C-like or something
>and not like that horrible old-fashioned COBOL like I write.

The number of source lines is not directly related with the size of the
resulting compiled program.

>
>On the other hand, my code is totally devoid of any unnecessary
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Execution-time efficiency is totally irrelevant these days but

???. EWver heard of 24 hour businesses?  A process in a bank that needs to be
executed once a day with the teller machines off-line is still time critical.

>I personally enjoy reducing something to its essence rather than 
>decorating it with useless stuff and trying to make it look
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Of course you are free to write the programs in the way you like, but if I
had to choose between modifying one of Mr. Dashwood's programs or one of
yours, my choice would be Mr. Dashwoods.

>Thanks
>
>Tony Dilworth
>


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com

Here's my response:

"Foodman" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message

> Hi:

Well, it MIGHT be C or Java (I use modular constructuction and lower case
there too) but it HAPPENS to be COBOL...

> I think that there is is a conscious effort on the part of the modern
> COBOL programmer (as exemplified by Mr. Dashwood) to try to make
> their programs not LOOK like COBOL.
>
Thank  you for calling this COBOL veteran (now in my 36th year of writing
it) an exemplary modern COBOL programmer. Don't have too many lucid moments;
I may have to take you seriously...<G>


> It seems that the trend is to try and make them look like programs
> written in those other languages. To accomplish this goal, the code
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Oh absolutely! Nothing quite so useless as the above, especially for
performing efficient arithmetic and getting at tables. See, I think tables
should always be referenced modified or subscripted and the ONLY time you
need an index is when you confuse the issue with that stupid SEARCH verb
which is totally unCOBOL...why would you use SEARCH when you can use PERFORM
...VARYING?

You're quite right. I do it to confuse the newcomers and to make my COBOL
look stylish. the fact that the programs work is purely coincidental.

> It is categorically impossible to justify the use of these things
> on TECHNICAL GROUNDS, i.e., that the compiled program will be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, I COULD justify all of them on those grounds (so it ISN'T
"categorically impossible") but I won't, because it really doesn't matter
and the finer points of the justification would be lost on you anyway.


> The only time the use of COMP-5 would be justified would be if
> you had to interface with something which required the parameters
> to be in that format. In the Dashwood case, this does not apply.
>
ROFL! I'm coding for a PC... It's native arithmetic is performed in this
mode.


> It follows that if it is categorically impossible to justify the use
> of something on technical grounds, then its use must be in the
> name of style, pretence, ostentation, or something else.
>
And it follows that if it is NOT "categorically impossible", your whole
argument
is demolished...<G>

> How would you justify the use of indexing or COMP-5 to a new student?
> How do you explain to the student that using COMP-5 doesn't really do
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I won't waste time typing a response here...


> In his original post Mr. Dashwood pointed out that he had used
> SECTION and did not wish to get into a big discussion over it.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> his level numbers by 12.

No I don't. I use the the column number of the item I am defining. This goes
back to the days of punch cards (see, I'm really a luddite like you
underneath my smooth modern style, Tony...<G>) when sometimes a source
program needed to be re-punched from a listing (the cards had been chewed in
the famous 2401 reader). It was a help to the punch girls (note deliberate
political incorrectness - I never met a guy who did this job, and I doubt
that any man could have exhibited the nimbleness and manual dexterity these
girls did...).

It was easier for the girls if the level number was in the column where it
was to be punched. So, apart from 01 levels which HAD to be in column 8, the
first subsequent level was in column 12. As I always indented my data
definitions 3 columns, the next level would be in 18, then 21, then 24, etc.

>
> The comparable code to move all the spaces to the end of the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What a shame it hasn't spilled over into your prose...<G>

> Execution-time efficiency is totally irrelevant these days but
> I personally enjoy reducing something to its essence rather than
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well stop caring about it. I have no further intention of criticising your
code. It stands on its own merit.

Pete.

```
Thank you, Paul.

It was really just the truth, and I didn't INVENT this technique, but I have
carried it through until this day. (I know it sounds corny, but part of the
reason I do it is out of respect for the people I worked with then, many of
whom are dead now...)

I guess the things we learn in our "formative years" tend to stay with us.

The important thing is to review them from time to time in the light of new
knowledge and make sure that what we "have always done" is still relevant.
Keep what's good, bin what has been superseded, or no longer works as well
as the new feature.

This is something which Foodman has a major problem with.

His stuff is like a Time Warp.

Actually I have no problem with what he does; I really don't care.

It wasn't I who started this thread...<G>

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** ringmeist@aol.com (Ringmeist)
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T08:33:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010714043328.02486.00003642@ng-mj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4c6b1d_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
I am sorry to be jumping in here without qouting anybody ; but , this thread
has gone on for a while.  Here I go showing my age.  But some of these points
depend on the systems , versions of COBOL , operating system , and the time
frame required.  I started programming banking systems on Burroughs medium
systems (MCP operating sytem and NDL for networking) using COBOL 64 (had to
keep enter symbolic capabilities to run reader sorters) and indexing boiled
down to an increment to an offset vs an add/multiply/move to an offset using
subscripts.  Indexing was much faster in that environment.  Also SECTIONS were
very helpful if used thoughtfully as those machines were multiprogramming using
head per track for  virtual memory.  Later I moved to IBM systems (OS/MVS/CICS
not sure what the model numbers were as the shop was designing/maintaining
software for many different clients and we had no control over the hardware)
and on those systems it seemed that memory paging considerations were more
important.  I believe that 4k pages were commanly used.  On those machines we
used GOTO's  and stuff to keep code in the same page , this stopped virtual
memory from rolling out to disk (or what ever else the sites used).  I like
structured programming but sometimes (when somebody expalines why) you may need
to rethink your style to fit the machine/system/funtionality that you are
given.  
PS.  I do write C++ programs but C++ programers can probably tell they were
writen by an old COBOL programmer!
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

```
Original note deleted

To me, the real issue of COBOL style is whether or not an "experienced"
COBOL programmer can "pick up and maintain" a program that I have written
WITHOUT knowing "my style" or conventions.

This comes from working in LARGE mainframe shops and I can see it is
(usually?) irrelevant for those doing the development/maintenance of their
applications in a one or two-person "shop".

I believe that I have read something of a "consensus" (but certainly NOT
unanimous) on Mr. Dilworth's style, that it REQUIRES you know "his"
conventions to easily maintain it.  I have not heard similar comments on Mr.
Dashwood's code.  This, to me, is the reason that I "prefer" the latter
style.

There is no doubt that the more complex any single program/application is,
the more "time" will be needed in understanding it - no matter what the
style is.  However, I believe that there are general principals of
programming that CAN make picking up someone else's code easier rather than
harder.  On the other hand, whatever YOU are used to, is always (or at least
almost always) easier for YOU to maintain.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T15:35:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4d1f3e_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <9ii83f$j8$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9ii83f$j8$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> Original note deleted
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Absolutely!

> This comes from working in LARGE mainframe shops and I can see it is
> (usually?) irrelevant for those doing the development/maintenance of their
> applications in a one or two-person "shop".
>
Ah, that's why I posted this...I am currently writing COBOL at home on my
own. The above principles STILL APPLY! And it doesn't just apply to COBOL,
it applies to other languages as well.

If I have to revisit my own code in 5 years I want it to be instantly
intelligible, not just to me, but to ANY COBOL programmer with the requisite
skill set (OO for instance...) because I may need to "sub-contract" work
based on the code I am writing today.

It is just common courtesy and good sense to make ANY program code as clear
as possible, not just to the Author, but to ANYONE who may need to pick it
up.

In fact, Bill, even if I KNEW beyond any shadow of doubt that NOBODY other
than myself would EVER read or maintain this code, I'd STILL try and help an
imaginary maintenance programmer. It takes very little more effort to do
this and the result is a more satisfying one.

(Job satisfaction is important even in one-man shops...<G>)

Richard made the point that Tony's "style" requires an innate "dictionary"
that Tony has developed and memorised over years. I would say that every
program he codes should contain a commentary that describes this
"dictionary" for ordinary mortals (maybe a COPY book). I wonder if they
do...?

<remainder snipped>

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T03:35:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010711233534.08956.00000062@ng-ml1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I've said it here once and I'll repeat it again.

I don't really care what the code looks like as long as it works and if it
doesn't work then:

1) I better be able to figure out what's wrong at 2am while 1/2 asleep

2) If it can wait until morning it should only require 1 cup of coffee to
understand

3) If it requires more than 1 cup of coffee rewrite the damned thing.

I have found Mr. Dillworth's code not only requires 2 cups of coffee but a dose
of whatever other mind altering drugs I can find. 

Mr. Dashwood's code - well - some of it is 2am material, some 1 cup of coffee
:)

My coding style has changed over the years as I gained experience and the
language has evolved.  That's the key - evolution.  Only something that evolves
is viable, language and programmers.  I certainly would shudder at maintaining
some of the code I did back then but I believe it still wouldn't require more
than 1 cup of coffee.
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T23:16:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4D24AC.D213344@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com>`

```
.

>
> I think that there is is a conscious effort on the part of the modern
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't want to comment on most of Tony's post, but he's right to a point:
there has been a definite trend towards constructs and programming styles
that are far less straightforward for the person who has to figure them out
(i.e., TPB, the poor beggar who does maintenance).  The comment-less,
label-less, in-line indented style seems to be the "in" thing these days.

One of the important points that gets lost in debates over style and the
"right" way to code is that the knowledge of the problem is not stated by the
program code.  The code incorporates the problem, to be sure, or else it
wouldn't work, but to someone lacking the original knowledge it may well be
impossible to understand.  "Why is this bit of code here?" is expressed but
not explained by the code.  The  program specs or documentation in theory
have the answer, but in practice nobody keeps them current.  The ONLY way to
preserve this sort of knowledge is to put it in the program source as a
comment.

People who have been around for a long time, when the GOTO debate began, may
recall a study that stated that comments are as important to maintainability
as is the programming style, but that "comments aren't as intellectually
exciting" (or some such phrase).  Anybody remember this?  (I have long since
forgotten where and when I saw it).

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T22:33:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"Foodman" <nospam@newsranger.com>
>
> A good example of cluttering programs up with useless stuff in order
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> today?  Why bother?

Actual test:

PERFORM VARYING MYINDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYINDX > BIG-NUMBER
   CONTINUE
   END-PERFORM

MYINDX  PIC S9(7)  time = 62 seconds
MYINDX  PIC S9(7) COMP-5  time = 6 seconds

From which one might infer that COMP-5 counters, etc. are about ten
times faster than DISPLAY.
```

Hang on, Bill...

Foodman's comment was:

" A good example of cluttering programs up with useless stuff in order
 to make them seem, perhaps, more stylish, is Mr. Dashwood's
use of 'binary-fields', indexing, COMP-5 and SECTION."

He is objecting to MY use of COMP-5, and is saying that it is "useless"
(implicitly, on ANY platform), and, indeed, pretentious.

The issue is not with the efficiency or otherwise of COMP-5, the issue is
with MY USE of it.

As I only use it on platforms where it IS efficient, he is on a hiding to
nothing, again <G>.

Jerry's example uses it on a platform where it IS efficient,  and is
therefore an acceptable test within the bounds laid down by Mr. Dilworth.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T00:58:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BU547.87574$Pf4.8384692@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9io1bt$f6j$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9io1bt$f6j$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> I would agree with your conclusion IF AND ONLY IF you tell us which
compiler
> you are using and what compiler options you are using.
>
> There are compilers that support the (extension) USAGE COMP-5 - but
not that
> efficiently.

Well, sure. I'm used Fujitsu's V5 PowerCobol, debug mode, normal
compiler options (including optimization).

And from my old Realia 16-bit manual:

--- begin quote
ADD A TO B

Where A and B are two numbers of the given usage and precision. The
relative speeds, normalized to COMP-5, are:

DISPLAY -- 7.67
COMP-3 -- 5.23
COMP-4 -- 1.48
COMP-5 -- 1.00

DISPLAY is about 50% slower than COMP-3, about 5 times slower than
COMP-4 and about 8 times slower than COMP-5. For small precision
numbers, such as subscripts, you should definitely use COMP-5 (with
precision 4 or less if possible).

--- end quote
```

I'll chime in with a brief comment on the Dashwood vs Foodman
controversy. (I didn't want to be included in the "silent" anything!)

In the original post by Tony, he states:

"Execution-time efficiency is totally irrelevant these days but
I personally enjoy reducing something to its essence rather than 
decorating it with useless stuff and trying to make it look
like something it isn't in the name of style."

Whatever descent arguments he may have made prior to that paragraph
became null and void with those words.  He clearly has no
understanding of the needs of modern technology and what really goes
on outside his own bedroom walls.  Try telling an I.T. manager of a
real time credit card processing center where millions of transactions
an hour may come in that execution time efficiency is "totally
irrelevant".

The only thing that is "totally irrelevant" is Tony Dilworth's views.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have learned there is little difference in wives,
you might as well keep the first.

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T22:33:20+12:00

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T09:33:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
The documentation supplied with the compilers seems sadly lacking
these days as does the description of optimisations. COMP-5 will
always be faster when used on Intel based processors as COMP-5
is based on the same structure/byte order as the Intel processors and
as such does not require intermediate code to translate the byte order.

"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Foodman" <nospam@newsranger.com>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T12:37:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Simply NOT true.

What COMP-5 means is totally compiler-dependent.

If you are using a compiler that defines it that way *and* you are running
on an Intel platform, then you are (probably) correct.  However, even there,
such issues as "truncation" may or may not impact this usage versus other
usages.

Again,
  *ALL* COMP-n usages are "implementor defined" and therefore, there are NO
generalizations (except this one <G>) that actually can be made about them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T12:43:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, there are occasions where theory does not always match practice.

What you are saying regarding implementor definition is theoretically true,
but I agree with Alastair, that on the Intel platform (which is what I am
using) comp-5 corresponds to "native mode" arithmetic.

I believe your statement at the start of your post below is unfair and
inaccurate.

It doesn't matter what "truncation" or anything else that is in effect, it
will ALWAYS be faster than the equivalent in ANY other USAGE, on the Intel
platform. As that is a paraphrase of what Alastair also stated, I can't see
a problem with Alastair's statement.

If you (or anyone) can show a case where comp-5 usage on an Intel platform
is slower than some other arithmetic usage on the same platform, given
equivalent data lengths, I will be VERY interested to see it.

After writing Intel Assembler for several years, and exploring various
implementations of the instruction set as new chips were released, I have
not been able to find any faster way of doing arithmetic than the native
mode. COBOL comp-5 implements this mode IN PRACTICE, although I think your
point is that it is IMPLEMENTOR DEFINED and could therefore THEORETICALLY be
ANYTHING.

Well, theory is one thing, practice is another.

Like I said when I came in, IN PRACTICE, it is the most efficient way to do
arithmetic on an Intel platform.
(At least, I don't know of any more efficient way.)

Pete.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> Simply NOT true.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> on an Intel platform, then you are (probably) correct.  However, even
there,
> such issues as "truncation" may or may not impact this usage versus other
> usages.
>
> Again,
>   *ALL* COMP-n usages are "implementor defined" and therefore, there are
NO
> generalizations (except this one <G>) that actually can be made about
them.
>
> --

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T21:29:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9itjki$vml$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
Pete,
  Look at NetExpres and check out the COMP-5 directive.  That is one case,
for example that may cause problems (on Intel platforms).  Similarly, I am
not positive, but I *believe* that COMP is actually faster than COMP-5 in
IBM VisualAge COBOL (again on Intel) - especially if the TRUNC(OPT) compiler
option is in effect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T16:48:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b527668_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <9itjki$vml$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Sorry, Bill, you'll need to do better than that...<G>

"...one case, for example that MAY cause problems..." (my emphasis.)

"...I *believe* that COMP is actually faster than COMP-5 in IBM VisualAge
COBOL..." (*believe*...your emphasis...)

"MAY" and "BELIEVE" are not going to cut it here...<G>

First off, we are NOT talking about compiler directives, we are talking
about COBOL USAGE.  Attempting to muddy the water by introducing the TRUNC
directive has nothing to do with Foodman's objection to my code, and you
know it....<G>

I remember the first implementations of Visual Age (I was working  on
contract to IBM, underground at Hursley Park when it was released <G>) and
they were based on MicroFocus COBOL. The TRUNC option was added for
mainframe compatibility (by MicroFocus, not IBM) and yes, it DOES impose an
overhead. But it is the SAME overhead whether it is in comp-5 or any other
usage. It does not alter the fact that native arithmetic is faster on the
Intel platform, WHETHER or NOT you specify truncation. COMP is certainly
faster on the mainframe because that is the mainframe's native arithmetic
mode (PACKED was an additional instruction set that was added to enable
commercial arithmetic AFTER Gene Amdahl designed the original 360
architecture); COMP-5 is faster on the PC because that is the PC's native
arithmetic mode.

I am amazed that you would even try to support Foodman's position this. He
says COMP-5 is "useless" (on ANY platform) and I only use it for style.

I say I use it because it is the most efficient way to do arithmetic on the
Intel platform, and that is the one I am using. You have questioned this,
but your statements are chicken soup with "May" and "Believe" in them...<G>

Now, show me the evidence (I'll accept it with or without TRUNC), or let it
go... <G>.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T08:42:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iur10$plm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <9itjki$vml$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3b527668_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
Pete,
  You are confusing the original point of the thread with the note that I
was commenting on.

The statement,

"COMP-5 will always be faster when used on Intel based processors as COMP-5
is based on the same structure/byte order as the Intel processors and as
such does not require intermediate code to translate the byte order."

is not true in ALL cases as the "always" says.  That is what I was objecting
to - and still believe is incorrect.  It is true for some (probably MOST)
compilers - but is not true in all cases and with all directives.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T07:31:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote in message <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com>...
[...]
>If you (or anyone) can show a case where comp-5 usage on an Intel platform
>is slower than some other arithmetic usage on the same platform, given
>equivalent data lengths, I will be VERY interested to see it.
[...]

For Micro Focus COBOL V3.2, the order (from fastest to slowest
execution time for arithmetic data items) is, normally: COMP-5,
COMP-X, COMP, COMP-3, DISPLAY.

The COBOL User Guide for this system adds:

"If the ON SIZE ERROR clause is used on 16-bit systems, or if the
data item is non-integer, the above ordering does not apply. For
example, in 16-bit COBOL systems arithmetic on such items uses
packed decimal registers inside the run-time system. In this
non-optimized mode the order is:

COMP-3, DISPLAY, COMP, COMP-X, COMP-5

However, individual cases might behave differently."

Do not expect me to supply an example, I accept what the user
guide says. I use COMP-5 for numeric data unless otherwise
required, such as COMP-2. I no longer use the ON SIZE ERROR
clause.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

produced some pretty bad code when mixing USAGE types (and
sometimes without mixing). I wondered if this text was taken from such
a description.

I use COMP-5 for all numeric data except data that has to be sorted
where I then use BINARY. I also tend to stay away from all complex
instructions such as COMPUTE, STRING, UNSTRING, &
INITIALIZE preferring to code in long-hand.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T12:26:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b53896b_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <9iv9t1$p2m$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9iv9t1$p2m$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk...
> I use COMP-5 for all numeric data except data that has to be sorted
> where I then use BINARY. I also tend to stay away from all complex
> instructions such as COMPUTE, STRING, UNSTRING, &
> INITIALIZE preferring to code in long-hand.
>
I am currently working on some stuff that has to parse COBOL source code,
and I would normally do this using transition diagramming. (I wrote an
article about this a few years back for Ed Arranga's COBOL report, in the
days when it was a paper document (ah, happy times...<G>)).

However, on this occasion I only need to analyse SELECT statements and FD/01
record definitions for ISAM files, so using the transition diagramming is a
bit like using a sledgehammer to crack a peanut. (And especially as the
number of COBOL reserved words (across the major implementations) has
escalated from 420 in 1979 to 611 currently).

I therefore decided to go for a "Quick" solution and am using UNSTRING
...DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE to load the source into separate words. The
Reserved word list is checked using SEARCH...ALL (I initialise it to
high-values and load the words in sequence, so the binary chop from SEARCH
ALL works fine).

I am agreeably surprised by the results. It was quick to write, is easily
understandable and the parse subroutine removes leading spaces and
translates commas, full, stops, and semi-colons to space (using a single
INSPECT) before loading every word in the current input record to a separate
table entry, where they can be easily manipulated and checked against the
reserved word list.

And it runs fast.

So, the point is Alister, that some of these "complex" COBOL constructs are
extremely useful once you get to grips with them...

I would urge people who are not already doing so to try using STRING,
UNSTRING, SEARCH and COMPUTE as they all have a place and can be valuable.
Yes, you CAN do everything with PERFORM...VARYING, but why do so when COBOL
has standard verbs that support these functions?

(And puh...LEASE, don't tell me it's more efficient...<G>; if you really
believe this, you are deluding yourself...<G>. The difference in efficiency
(if any) will be within 15% of how you do it now, and you get much more
readable code, that takes far less time to write and debug (once you
negotiate the learning curve)).

Pete.





-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T15:34:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j1egf$vg$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <9iv9t1$p2m$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b53896b_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
I don't use the standard ACCEPT / DISPLAY commands or SCREENS
in preference to my own code. This meant writing my own panel painter
during which I found that commands such as STRING, UNSTRING,
INSPECT etc. all performed at a snails pace.

The INITIALIZE statement produced way to much data/code as I restrict
myself to 64K data and 64K code (which I very rarely get close to with the
average being 8K/8K). I found it better to use a MOVE ALL x"00" TO
group-item-def and then use reference modification to fill in the character
defs.

I do code STRING commands but my pre-processor alters them to ref-
mods where possible. I do have 1 UNSTRING command to help process
the command line during the application initialisation phase.

I am using MF WB 2.4 which is around 12 years old now so maybe things
have improved. One thing I find is that it is very easy to convert COBOL
to C when I skip the complex instructions which I don't do but like the
possibility of.

I fully agree that there these instructions are very useful, do make things
more readable for most, and that the performance loss is negligible but
they lack the wow factor I get when I push my code that little bit further.
I am self-employed and I enjoy all aspects of computing so the time
factor is not a problem for me. When I used to work for a company I
was always being accused of treating my job as a hobby. What more
can you ask for ? I spend my time doing what I like best and I get paid
for it.

You left out INSPECT, and I think it belongs in that list as well.  I know
some implementations that generate blindingly fast object code for some
pretty complex INSPECT statements, particularly those that end up doing
single-character literal substitutions -- much, MUCH faster than any PERFORM
loop could accomplish with the same data -- and this before the introduction
of INSPECT ... CONVERTING.

Even more fascinating (or not) is that Itanium has not BCD instructions - so all
this decimal math will be handled by the runtime.

So there we go again saying COBOL slows down the processing because it has to
handle this in code.  

WE HAVE THE WRONG ATTITUDE!

The deficiency is not with COBOL - it's with the underlying processor.

In article <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood says...
>
>Fascinating!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T23:19:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I think you are not seeing the big picture. ;-)

In COBOL, all requirements for decimal arithmetic are handled
by the run time. This is without regard to the representation of
the numeric items involved. [Both binary and packed decimal
representation require decimal scaling to accommodate
implied decimal points. The difference is in the method used
to scale the number.]

With 64-bit registers, newer processors easily accommodate
the arithmetic operations and scaling required for COBOL. For
data items with up to 18 digits, no more than two 64-bit registers
are required for an intermediate result.

Older processors, with their smaller registers, could not
accommodate large intermediate results, as easily. This,
sometimes, made packed decimal a more efficient
representation. This is, in part, because decimal scaling by
shifting is more efficient that decimal scaling by multiple
precision binary arithmetic.

There is no deficiency with either COBOL or the newer
processors. The newer processors, with their larger registers,
have, simply, outgrown the need for even the most basic
decimal arithmetic instructions; and, COBOL never required
decimal arithmetic instructions to get correct results.
------------------
Rick Smith

so all

to

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T11:45:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j72q4$94d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <CfC57.501$Y47.5372@www.newsranger.com>`

```
FYI - the draft Standard's requirement for 31-digits is "independent" of the
ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD.  When ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD is  in effect, the
draft specifies that certain operations (and statements) use a "standard
intermediate data item" (SIDI) which is (or works AS IF) it were defined as
a DECIMAL floating-point item.  (This is as opposed to a "binary" floating
point item - where not every possible decimal floating-point item can be
represented).

To the best of my knowledge, there is no currently existing OS (or hardware)
that actually supports this data type.  However, there have been a FEW
comments about such a data type existing in the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T18:46:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WVF57.821$Y47.9810@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <CfC57.501$Y47.5372@www.newsranger.com> <9j72q4$94d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My turn to be like Pete! <G>

I know that the two issues - Standard Arithmetic and 31 digit precision are
separate and unrelated -- but I still want to know Rick's opinion on how these
TWO things might impact development of COBOL on 64 bit processors.  His comment
about the extra code being unnecessary at 18 digits -- and that decimal math was
not required to be accurate -- I think the first is impacted by the 31 digit
precision and the second by Standard Arithmetic.

I think we are not on the same page for standard arithmetic.
Standard arithmetic is not about accuracy; it is defined such
that where there is loss of precision, that loss will be the same
for all implementations. Currently, I do not know how to control
decimal loss of precision using binary representation.
------------------
Rick Smith

I think we are OK -- I respect your opinion and I have ASSUMED (probably
improperly) that COBOL vendors have taken advantage of BCD processor
instructions where available.  The Itanium has none.  That's going to mean the
creation of routines to handle decimal math using Binary.  No big deal I guess -
until someone complains that it's slow because it's COBOL - my argument is that
this is a processor deficiency.  But it's a silly argument.


In article <tlfgruj1k49f43@corp.supernews.com>, Rick Smith says...
>

…[101 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T10:26:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j9pjb$9k5$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <CfC57.501$Y47.5372@www.newsranger.com> <9j72q4$94d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <WVF57.821$Y47.9810@www.newsranger.com> <tlfgruj1k49f43@corp.supernews.com> <hyX57.873$ar1.3590@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Slightly tangential:

Many years ago, Burroughs seriously considered adding decimal-arithmetic
hardware to the Large System line.  There were lots of discussions among the
machine and language architects on the subject.  Clearly, converting from
display or packed to binary and back again was costly, and how better to
avoid that conversion than by doing the arithmetic directly on the data?

One of the counterarguments was that fast BINARY arithmetic was relatively
easy to achieve, and the best way to get fast OVERALL performance was to
speed up the conversion process, not build a new hardware box to avoid the
conversion altogether.  If both the arithmetic itself and the conversion
processes for the arguments are blindingly fast, who needs decimal hardware?
That counterargument won out.

Unisys MCP/AS systems do not include decimal arithmetic processors today;
they do their arithmetic in binary.

    -Chuck Stevens

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T23:50:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tlfev8s320oud3@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <CfC57.501$Y47.5372@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I had not considered either of these as important for my earlier
response, so I had to think about them for a bit.

I do not see 31 digits as being any more of a problem for a 64-bit
processor than 18 digits was for a 32-bit processor. The same
techniques can be used.

As for standard arithmetic, the biggest problem, I think, will be with
some of the intrinsic functions. For amendment 1 to the 85 standard,
it is possible, if I understand correctly, to calculate the results in
an available numeric coprocessor even though the accuracy was
less than 18 digits. Standard arithmetic in the draft COBOL standard,
as I understand it, will not permit such inaccuracy.

As Bill implied[*], in his reponse, standard arithmetic is defined "as if"
decimal floating-point were used. On a binary only processor, the
"concept" must be simulated. As for simulating decimal floating-point,
it is possible to perform decimal arithmetic with binary instructions.
If the decimal digits are not packed, addition and subtraction can be
done 8 digits at a time, on a 64-bit processor. It requires several
instructions for each set of 8 digits; but it is possible and may be
faster than looping through 2 digits at a time.

[*] Bill, I know it's not exactly what you said; but I needed to re-phrase
it to fit what I needed to say. :-)
------------------
Rick Smith

Interjecting.  I don't think a language should have any limit whatsoever - within
what can be typed in as a picture.   The implementation should give a warning if you
use PIC 9(5000)V9(400), saying that processing will be very slow.   And maybe
compilers can have limits similar to the ones they have now for table size.  But
these aren't business limits, they are implementation limits.

Rick,
  Do you know anything (much?) about IBM's OS/400 operating system (and
hardware)?  I know that they have a NATIVE (hardware/OS) packed decimal data
type that ON THAT OPERATING SYSTEM is actually "more efficient" than any
binary items.

I don't know whether or not "scaling" is still an added operation - but do
think that this is a LITTLE relevant to your comment.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T01:04:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tlfevb29g2phd4@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <9j72tl$g2s$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I know almost nothing about OS/400. I recall seeing an item
that I took to mean the AS/400 hardware is based on the
IBM PowerPC.

One thing I recall from IBM literature about the PowerPC, is
that IBM recommends using only certain integer registers for
applications and different set of registers for passing parameters
to subroutines. It might be that because so many of the 32
integer registers are reserved for the OS and COBOL, that
the compiler always converts binary items to packed decimal
and convert back, as needed. This forced conversion may
make packed decimal "more efficient"; it does not make
packed decimal "faster". I do not know how the hardware
actually operates on packed decimal; but I could guess that
either decimal hardware was added to the chip, or the
32 floating-point registers are used in some combination to
process the packed decimal.

It is peculiar that IBM mainframe COBOL stores COMP items
as binary; but, sometimes, converts those items to packed
decimal before use. I think the situation is similar, there is a
small set of general purpose registers available so it is "more
efficient" to use COMP-3 for non-integer items, in the first
place. I think this peculiarity may be traced back to the early
days of S/360 when the hardware for packed decimal (and
floating-point) were expensive options. If the hardware was
not present, the general purpose registers must be used. If
the data was stored as binary, the operations could be done
more quickly. If COMP data was stored as packed decimal,
the conversion to and from binary, or simulation of packed
decimal, would take longer.

While I profess ignorance of inner workings of the AS/400 and
engaged in a fair amount of speculation, the bottom line is:
as long as data fits within the binary capacity of the ALU
(Arithmetic Logic Unit), binary will be faster. When the data is
larger than the capacity of the ALU, other factors will determine
which representation is more efficient.
------------------
Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T10:44:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j8ufk$lg5bt$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <9j72tl$g2s$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill

AFAIK, this isn't (wasn't?) correct!  I don't think packed decimal is more
efficient than binary on AS400.

Back in 1990 when we converted a very performant COBOL application from Wang
VS to AS400, we had massive interactive performance issues.   The main
suggestion from IBM people was that the AS400 was a packed decimal machine
and we made too much use of binary.  I did some simple tests (adding 1 to
different format numbers a million times) and as expected, binary performed
better than packed decimal.  This was COBOL and maybe RPG does something
different but I'd be surprised.

Covering another thread,  I also tested subscripting over indexing.  On the
AS400 subscripting was faster than indexing.  This WAS AS400 specific and
was to do with the way OS400 held structures.  It was the reverse of Wang
timings.

I decided that in commercial applications this kind of performance tuning
didn't matter and so we kept to our standards of holding money as packed
decimal and indexes/subscripts as binary and that it was a matter of taste
whether or not indexing or subscripting was used on arrays.

BTW, on the performance issue we finally got hold of someone who knew
something and he told us our application was too modular.   I didn't really
believe this, but one of our core utilities was our (highly modular) screen
handler, written in COBOL.  So I quickly whacked the modules together into a
single module and there we were with sub second response times.

Covering yet another standard thread, I could do this quickly because we
alway use SECTIONs, which seems to be anathema to some people here.

While I'm here,  I agree with Rick (Smith) that it doesn't matter if the
processor doesn't have decimal instructions. IMHO there is usually
relatively little processing actually done on money fields in any given
transaction.

However I think that packed decimal is still the best format for money
fields.  There are real problems of precision in binary or floating point
arithmetic, not in display but in comparing and totalling.  Also18 digits
for money amounts is not really big enough in a scalable,
internationalisable, multi currency application.  It cuts out some markets.

I assume that both of these points are why the numeric picture size has been
increased to 31, Java had to create a BigDecimal class and they had to have
a numeric/decimal SQL data type.  Mind you, in some countries with low value
currencies and high inflation, maybe 31 digits won't be enough. <G>

Phew!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Packed-Decimal/Binary under OS/400 (was: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T16:30:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ja7te$s9n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <9j72tl$g2s$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9j8ufk$lg5bt$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`

```
I don't have the same "real world" evidence that Rick Price seems to have,
but I can confirm that the CURRENT ILE COBOL (follow-on to COBOL/400) manual
STILL says that the most  efficient usage is packed-decimal.  It does repeat
(which some other people may not know) that in this environment USAGE COMP =
USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL and *not* USAGE BINARY.

As far as I know this is the ONLY compiler (or series of compilers) where
this is true.

For further reference, see

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/pubs/html/as400/online/v4r5eng.htm

and look at the section
  "| 5.3.20.4 COMPUTATIONAL or COMP Phrase"

in
 Title: ILE COBOL for AS/400 Reference
 Document Number: SC09-2539-01

which states (in part),

"Of all USAGEs, USAGE COMP is the most efficient in terms of operational
performance. For the ILE COBOL compiler, the COMPUTATIONAL phrase is
synonymous with PACKED-DECIMAL. "

P.S.  Rick - if you think this information is actually wrong, you might try
submitting an RCF (Readers Comment Form) to IBM and see what they respond
with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed-Decimal/Binary under OS/400 (was: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T17:36:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jaipo$54h$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <9j72tl$g2s$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9j8ufk$lg5bt$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <9ja7te$s9n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9ja7te$s9n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> ...  that in this environment USAGE COMP =
> USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL and *not* USAGE BINARY.

> As far as I know this is the ONLY compiler (or series of compilers) where
> this is true.

I can't speak to the ex-Sperry systems, but the ex-Burroughs Unisys
compilers from COBOL74 onward have always treated COMP as a packed-decimal
format, primarily to foster portability among these systems.  That
convention applied to the B1000, V Series and MCP/AS COBOL74 compilers, and
it applies today to MCP/AS COBOL85.  It also applied to the B1000 and V
Series COBOL68 compilers; however, MCP/AS COBOL68 treated COMP as a
word-oriented integer format.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed-Decimal/Binary under OS/400 (was: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <dgallan1@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-22T02:25:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Unisys 1100/2200/Clearpath IX systems, COMP is always allocated as binary
storage. The number of digits specified in the PIC controlled how many bytes
were allocated.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9ja7te$s9n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> but I can confirm that the CURRENT ILE COBOL (follow-on to COBOL/400) ...
>says  that in this environment USAGE COMP = USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL and *not*
USAGE BINARY.
>
> As far as I know this is the ONLY compiler (or series of compilers) where
> this is true.


The first computer on which I saw COBOL, a Burroughs' CMS mini, had a COOBOL
compiler for which USAGE COMP = USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL.

The last CMS box was manufactured in 1987.

(Yes, I'm sure. I was GM of a Burroughs' VAR then, and up until that time we
had written ONLY for CMS!).

MCM
```

"Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com> wrote 
>
<SNIP>
> Also18 digits
> for money amounts is not really big enough in a scalable,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> increased to 31, 

<SNIP>

I was there when we discussed how big to make decimal numbers, and the
main consideration was intercommunication with other computer
languages.  RPG had 31-digit numbers, and no-one knew of a larger size
in any other language, at the time. So, 31 it was....  Why RPG picked
31 I do not know.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: COBOL needs your help! Join the committee! Get your company
involved!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T03:17:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rv667.45613$C81.3844350@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <3b5385a4_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <Kp_47.22488$Kf3.292683@www.newsranger.com> <tlckhba61uq628@corp.supernews.com> <9j72tl$g2s$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9j8ufk$lg5bt$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <4145699b.0107201532.7a2a2be8@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen,

As so it was in the selection of 30. Someone at David Taylor Model Basin
(perhaps Betty Holberton) noted that some numbers in some areas of
application were much bigger than the current "banking" needs. The comma
came into being about the same time. I know we had
programmers in Brazil, and Spain with those funny looking things to deal
with.

If truth be known, the 31 limit is probably too short for todays "world"
economy.

Warren Simmons

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T20:04:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oT057.40888$C81.3415086@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <KiN47.8081$gj1.768618@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9j1erk$166$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
    If you have the slightest interest in speed (in the current
module),
you should not.  But assuming that you are using INT/GNT programs
anyway, and not EXE/DLL programs, INT is good enough in areas
in which speed is not relevant, such as user interface, etc.

    I noticed the INT/COMP-5 problem in a program that
needed the extra speed, but need to be animated.  That
version of the compiler supported animation (debugging)
with INT's only.  When I changed the COMP-5's to
COMP, the INT'd code improved a little.

    Obviously this is of academic interest only.  If you need
speed, you should not use INT code.

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in
message news:9j1erk$166$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Why would you only wish to compile your code as far as INT ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T08:18:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B553832.942BABC5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<U0X27.15136$Kf3.175388@www.newsranger.com> <dHK37.4394$Kw2.375302@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9irgim$mpk$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <9isklf$hqr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b523d90_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tl5k51c7347n24@corp.supernews.com> <KiN47.8081$gj1.768618@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9j1erk$166$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> Why would you only wish to compile your code as far as INT ?

When my clients were using 8MHz 8086 with 1 MByte RAM to run 3 or 4
multiuser terminals it was critical to keep the memory requirements down
for most programs.  .INTs were 2 or 3 times smaller in the code section
than .GNTs and this did not necessarily slow down the processing as in
most cases it was limited by disk speed.  Measurements showed that there
was still spare CPU time.  However these were simple accounting systems
with not much computations at all.  Any program that did do intensive
calculations was made .GNT.

More recently the advantages of .INT (or OPT(0)) is that they are fully
transportable to different environments, even between processor types. 
.GNTs (or OPT(3)) are only portable within the same processor and OS
class.
```

---

<a id="t-9f4a71b195"></a>
## Thread: Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.54 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.54

- **From:** rpragana@acm.org (Rildo Pragana)
- **Date:** 2001-07-11T06:41:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1288833b.0107110541.38bcb82d@posting.google.com>`

```
The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.54 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

This release contains mainly bugs fixes, and enhancements.  
It includes updates to the preprocessor, main compiler, RTS,the
regression test suite, EVALUATE, GOTO, READ, WRITE,  RETURN,DELETE,
START, REWRITE statements.
Support for multiple WHEN clauses in SEARCH statement, [SIGN IS]
{LEADING/TRAILING} [SEPARATE] clauses, FLOAT-LONG (COMP-2) FLOAT-SHORT
(COMP-1) floating point types, INSPECT...CONVERTING clause, have been
added to the compiler.


Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

<a id="t-bf7ae2a2bf"></a>
## Thread: Style vs Substance — 9 msgs

### Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T13:37:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4D9A1C.2D911549@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> Mr. Dashwood accuses me of 'denigrating' his code. What I actually 
> said was 'Mr. Dashwood's post gives us all an opportunity to compare
> a structured program with one written in a sensible manner. . .'.

Implying that his was not sensible.

> This suggests to me that Mr. Dashwood judges COBOL code primarily
> by what it LOOKS LIKE rather than whether it is efficient, simple,
> compact, sensible, easy-to-comprehend, and so on.

What is 'easy-to-comprehend', or is considered 'simple', is entirely
what one is used to.  A point that you continually fail to understand. 
While your code is 'simple' and 'easy-to-understand' _TO_YOU_, this does
not make it these in any absolute way.

The reason that it is _NOT_ simple and easy-to-understand to others is
that they do not have the several decades of experience of working with
this code.  You have a built-in 'dictionary' so that when you look at a
single line of your code you can immediately see its place in your
universe.

Others do not.  For them to understand your program they have to
understand _all_ of it by tracing through each path and referring back
to each data item, keeping track of actual usage.

Similarly you think that other programmer's work is 'hard to understand'
and 'complicated' because it is not like one of yours, and doesn't use
the same 'dictionary' as you do.

You appear to consider that third parties _should_ think that your
programs are 'better', or at least 'easier-to-understand', simply
because you do. There are actually very good reasons why they do not.  

One reason is that, upon coming across a completely new system, it is
necessary to understand it in small parts and expand this knowledge
outwards.  Modular styles of programming cater for a modular approach to
understanding.  Your programs are not modular (though they are
repetitive), they must be understood in the large.  While your programs
are generally 'small' they are not as small and easy to understand as a
module in other programmer's work.

The other problem is that you do not use names that are meaningful to
others.  They are highly meaningful to you because you have created and
reused these for decades.  In order to understand your programs it is
first necessary to learn, or build, a dictionary that you have built in.

You 'know' that 'M1' (or whatever) has a very specific meaning, what you
completely fail to realise is that _nobody_else_knows_nor_cares_.  Nor
are they likely to bother learning what all those codes are for, which
is the prerequite for understanding your code.

> On the other hand, Mr. Dashwood's code is markedly different - upon
> a casual glance it might even be C or Java or something with

Most programmers continue to learn new languages and apply what they
consider to be improvements into their coding.  

> its nested construction and mostly lower-case style and lots of lines
> and wasted space.

It is rather 'confusing' until you get used to it, as is everything else
that does not look exactly what one expects to see.  However, while you
find 'nested contruction', lower-case, and spaced out style 'confusing'
and 'hard to understand', we do not.  We are confused and frustrated by
tangles of GO TOs, meaningless (to us) codes instead of names, and
clustered compact code.

> This suggests to me that Mr. Dashwood judges COBOL code primarily
> by what it LOOKS LIKE rather than whether it is efficient, simple,
> compact, sensible, easy-to-comprehend, and so on.

> A good example of cluttering programs up with useless stuff in order
> to make them seem, perhaps, more stylish, is Mr. Dashwood's 
> use of 'binary-fields', indexing, COMP-5 and SECTION.

How ironic.  You complain that PD judges code on appearance and not on
whether it is 'efficient', 'compact' and 'sensible', and then you
comment on the _appearance_ of using "'binary-fields', indexing, COMP-5"
when these actually produce more compact, more efficient and more
sensible code.

> It is categorically impossible to justify the use of these things
> on TECHNICAL GROUNDS, 

You are entitled to your _opinions_, but once again you treat these as
if they are some sort of absolute that must be accepted by everyone.

> On the other hand, my code is totally devoid of any unnecessary
> frou-frou, 

It is also devoid of anything that would make it understandable to
_others_.  You write for yourself, and judge your own code by how easy
it is for _you_ to read and understand.  You have no idea at all how
others view it (except from messages posted here which you completely
ignore).

> is utterly simple, compact, easy-to-read, neat,
> efficient, 

An APL programmer would say exactly that about their APL code.  To
others it is meaningless gibberish.

> and, although one could change it, it would be hard
> to make a significant improvement. 

I would agree that changing it would not make any improvement.  The
fault with it lies in your whole approach to programming which is to
base all code on an unspecified set of inbuilt (to you) codeification
which is unavailable to others.

> My approach to programming
> is to do what I need to do with the utmost economy both in terms
> of the time it takes to write and the efficiency of the code.

With no thought _at_all_ to others being able to understand it.

> My approach to programming
> is to do what I need to do with the utmost economy both in terms
> of the time it takes to write 

What makes you think that others take longer to finish a program than
you do.  While you may well reduce the number of keystrokes, others may
use more powerful editors that can produce the code more intelligently,
such as with key-macros and completions, or simple copy-paste with
multiple files open.

Now for example for a master file edit program, it may only take you
half a day to construct one for a new file (based on what you have said
about how you write programs).  But I have a huge complex file
maintenance program that will maintain all the files that I design with
a simple text dictionary and screen layout.  I'll bet I can put together
a simple system much faster than you can write new programs to do it.

I also have a screen code generator that will take a text layout and an
FD and generate a file maint program faster than you could load up your
editor.

Much more importantly, If there is an improvement that I can make in the
user interface then I only need do it once instead of into each separate
program that you seem to have.  You may well be able to write 'more
programs per day', but I will get the job done faster by not writing
programs.


> I don't really care if people write inane programs full of
> ostentatious stuff, but I do care when people criticize me for
> not doing the same thing. 

No. We criticise you for completely failing to understand, or accept,
what others have been saying.  You have been writing code in isolation
for decades and have completely failed to 'grow' with the industry, or
even to cater for others.

What you consider 'inane' and 'pretentious' is what makes the program
understandable by third parties (yourself being an obvious exception). 
While you write for yourself only (but don't understand that this is the
case), most others write for 'the next maintainer'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>

> > I don't really care if people write inane programs full of
> > ostentatious stuff, but I do care when people criticize me for
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> case), most others write for 'the next maintainer'.

As a systems manager over COBOL programmers, one of the things I always tell
them is to write your code as if you (the original programmer) were the one
coming back to maintain it 5 years from now.  It's not always _necessary_ to
write for the maintainer, but it's easier in the long run if you always
_do_.

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T12:41:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4EDE61.A00BBE3B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B4D9A1C.2D911549@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Peter Lacey (lacey@mb.sympatico.ca) wrote:

>  The  program specs or documentation in theory
> have the answer, but in practice nobody keeps them current.  The ONLY way to
> preserve this sort of knowledge is to put it in the program source as a
> comment.

The way that I deal with this is to have the user documentation for the
program incorporated into the program source code as HTML in comment
lines.  This is extracted into the help directory for the users to read
if required.  In the program source these lines are marked so that
commented code or other comments are not extracted.

In this way I can remember to update the documentation as I work on the
code and, more importantly, there is a direct relationship between the
code and the documentation lines.  Sometimes I shuffle the program
source to ensire that the resulting documents are structured better (the
extract is linear), but then this matters not because I haven't used
paragraph numbers, which always seemed a useless contrivence after I
progressed beyond card-packs and lineflow listings.
```
