###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style vs Substance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T08:32:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4FF576.59DA5336@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B4D9A1C.2D911549@Azonic.co.nz> <3B4EDE61.A00BBE3B@Azonic.co.nz> <3B4E71B1.B88EE22E@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey wrote:
> 
> A good practice!  But, one question: how do you differentiate between what the
> users need and what the programmers need?  They aren't always the same thing.

In the program the lines that are extracted are marked as a block by
tags <html> and </html>, all comment lines btween these are extracted.  

       *<html>
       * To customer type determines the price used with:
       *<dl> 
            EVALUATE Customer-Type
       *<dt> R <dd> Retail price.
            WHEN "R"        MOVE PR-Retail    TO WS-Price
                            MOVE ZERO         TO WS-Plusage
       *<dt> C <dd> Cost Plus, used for Interbarch transfers
       *            and staff sales.
           WHEN "C"         COMPUTE WS-Price = PR-Last-Cost 
                                  * ( 1 + ( CR-Plusage / 100 ) )
                            MOVE CR-Plussage  TO WS-Plussage
       ^ etc
       *</dl>
       *</html>

The extractor builds a basic html framework, there is a <title> line at
the start to use for the heading.  It also adds <p> tags whenever there
is a blank comment line to separate the paragraphs.  Some special
processing is done for <pre> blocks.  It can process COPY files by
following a *<include> filename, though I seldom need to do
this.              

Comments that are not within an <html> block are not extracted.
 
There is another program that builds an index for these from the menu
file so that this looks like the application menu and when they click on
an item it goes into the program document.  

This set of program documentation is intended to cover the detail
aspects of the programs, there are also overview pages as well.
```

<a id="t-76ac77042a"></a>
## Thread: Updating VSAM file from COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Updating VSAM file from COBOL

- **From:** "ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T02:09:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fh1qktcm57nasg9fj6b9d6reh7bb8jiuad@4ax.com>`

```
Hi,

I am going to write a COBOL batch program that will add/modify a VSAM file. There is an
alternate index file associated with this VSAM file, defined with option UPGRADE.
Do I need to add a DD with pathname or DD with ALTERNATEINDEX name to my JCL to be sure
that Alternate Index file will reflect the changes done to the base cluster by my program?

IBM manuals says: 
"UPGRADE specifies that the cluster's alternate index is upgraded to reflect changed data
when the base cluster's records are added to, updated, or erased. "
But if I do not need an additional DD with pathname, why do I need to define a PATH?

Thanks,

Zalek
```

#### ↳ Re: Updating VSAM file from COBOL

- **From:** jay_moseley@attglobal.net (Jay Moseley, CCP)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T12:28:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4d97b3.2246700@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<fh1qktcm57nasg9fj6b9d6reh7bb8jiuad@4ax.com>`

```
You do not need to provide a DD for the alternate index in order for
the index to be upgraded along with the primary cluster.


On Thu, 12 Jul 2001 02:09:07 GMT, "ZalekBloom@hotmail.com"
<ZalekBloom@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Updating VSAM file from COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T12:11:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ikm08$nbo$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fh1qktcm57nasg9fj6b9d6reh7bb8jiuad@4ax.com>`

```
Depending on exactly what your application does do with this file, look at
the LE (or COBOL) run-time option
   AIXBLD

You may need it for this application and usually the default is NOAIXBLD
```

## Thread: List dataset names on a Fujitsu Mainframe — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### List dataset names on a Fujitsu Mainframe

- **From:** liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T01:32:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9db5eaf6.0107120032.6eed3d03@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone know how I can automate a process to get a list of
sequential dataset names.  I want to be able to process hundreds of
sequential files whose names are in the following format;

PROD.XXXXXXXX.DATA (Where I dont know the value of XXXXXXXX).

ie;

PROD.ABCDEFG.DATA
PROD.TEST1.DATA
etc.

These datasets are basically cobol compile listings & I want to create
a JCL that will somehow process every one of these sequential datasets
but I dont know how to allocate the dataset names as I dont know part
of their name.

As I process each one of these sequential files, I will be writing
details about them to another file and part of the output needs to be
the middle portion of the dataset name.  So not only do I need to be
able to allocate the dataset but I also need its name passed to a
program so that I can write its name to another output file.

Any ideas?
```

#### ↳ Re: List dataset names on a Fujitsu Mainframe

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T11:40:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4D8C96.B5DC4B69@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107120032.6eed3d03@posting.google.com>`

```
Assuming an IBM MVS machine,

    You could use the IDCAMS utility with the LISTC command.

    Something like

                LISTC PROD.*.DATA

    (I don't have any manuals at hand, so the command may be a bit wrong)

The output can be directed to a file for subsequent processing by a COBOL
pgm or REXX proc

Bill


John Liptak wrote:

> Does anyone know how I can automate a process to get a list of
> sequential dataset names.  I want to be able to process hundreds of
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any ideas?
```

```
:>These datasets are basically cobol compile listings & I want to create
:>a JCL that will somehow process every one of these sequential datasets
:>but I dont know how to allocate the dataset names as I dont know part
:>of their name.

:>As I process each one of these sequential files, I will be writing
:>details about them to another file and part of the output needs to be
:>the middle portion of the dataset name.  So not only do I need to be
:>able to allocate the dataset but I also need its name passed to a
:>program so that I can write its name to another output file.

:>Any ideas?

Easiest approach is to parse the output of a LISTC LEV(PROD.*.DATA) command.
```

#### ↳ Re: List dataset names on a Fujitsu Mainframe

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T08:32:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107120732.1ed863b9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107120032.6eed3d03@posting.google.com>`

```
Reply at end

liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak) wrote in message news:<9db5eaf6.0107120032.6eed3d03@posting.google.com>...
> Does anyone know how I can automate a process to get a list of
> sequential dataset names.  I want to be able to process hundreds of
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any ideas?


STEP1
Do a LISTCAT with the NAME option, put the output to disk

STEP2
Read the output.  Skip records until you get to the data, I thnk PROD
will be in column 15, but I haven't done it in a while, and do not
have access to a mainframe now.  Look at a sample output to see
exactly where  everything is.  You will have to parse the data names,
because the second node is of varying size. UNSTRING will work for
this.    I do not think you can use a wildcard for the middle of a
dataname, so you will have to discard those that do not end in DATA. 
Write your list to disk, so you can read it back in the next step...
unless you want to do other processing as you go along.

COBOL/390 will allow you to allocate datasets dynamically, so you
don't need the names in advance, nor will you have to submit JCL from
your batch program.


Stephen J Spiro
```

## Thread: Request for feedback — 26 msgs

### Request for feedback

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T01:27:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
Although a number of people have responded to my request under the
"Alternate Standard" thread, I realise that a significant proportion of the
readership of this NG probably don't even look at this thread.

I really need to get a feel from the User Base so I am reproducing here the
PS from my last post in the above thread...

<quote>
PS to the Rest of You:

Please mail me with your thoughts, positive or negative. I need to get a
broader feel from the User Base as to how YOU feel about the standards
issue.

Is it really only important to the people involved in producing it? Even if
you feel it is of no relevance to you personally, drop me a line and say
that.

If you felt you could make a difference, would you participate?

Could/Would you contribute time to help run the public Forum? (edit prose,
count votes, etc).

Are you happy with what is being produced?

Are you happy with how it is produced?

(Maybe you don't care; tell me...<G>)

Over the past years, many of you have received help privately (and publicly)
from me. (Some of you even said "Thanks Pete..."<G>).

I am now asking for something from you.

Not your agreement to what I am proposing if you honestly don't support it.

Just your mark on a piece of electronic paper.

Just your input.

I would really value ALL inputs, and will respond to as many as I can. A
considerable amount of time and effort  (not JUST mine <G>) is going into
the exploration of an alternative. It would be ironic if no one really gives
a damn...(and funny <G>)

TIA,

Pete.
</quote>

Please post to me privately. You can reach me on:
dashwood@nospam.freenet.co.nz (remove the "nospam" before mailing)

If you don't speak now, don't complain later...<G>

Silence means you want no say in the future of COBOL.

That's a perfectly valid viewpoint, but some of us are spending time and
energy on your behalf and will stop doing so if the majority feel they don't
care.

Do me the courtesy of letting us know...<G>

Thanks,

Pete.

http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

#### ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T09:55:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Although a number of people have responded to my request under the
> "Alternate Standard" thread, I realise that a significant proportion of the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

IMHO, it is preferable to have a slowly-reacting standards process rather than
one which is responsive enough to adapt quickly to the theory of the moment.
You have been around for a while, I believe, so from your own experience you'll
be able to identify any number of bright ideas that took the IS community by
storm and then vanished without a trace.  Had the standards process incorporated
any of these it would now be loaded with utterly irrelevant clutter which would
render the standard unworkable.

As well, I'd be very leery of a popular vote on elements.  Vox populi, vox dei
is all very well in theory but mistaken enthusiasm and issue du jour are far
from being the best way to go.  For instance: according to the first principles
of structured programming, the EVALUATE verb is not necessary to the language.
Should it be voted out of existence, then?  Or the COMPUTE which some people
hate with a passion?  I think not!  Far better a standards process which defines
correctness of implementation rather than what is correct (dare I say
"politically correct"?).  Obsolete and unworkable constructs will eventually
fall by the wayside anyway (RIP ALTER).

Regards
PL
```

```
Mr. Lacey eloquently states my opinion as well.  The standards should
be defined and used to define and maintain the Cobol verbs and how
they work between platforms and vendors; to define and maintain data
types between platforms and vendors.  The standards should not be a
place where the style police and technique geeks can impose their
wills on the public.  Sadly, it seems that is what is happening.  

User involvement should be to the extent of how to improve the way a
verb works, new functions, new data types and should be an ongoing
process.  

Regards,

you might as well keep the first.
```

```

SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net...

> I would like to see it changed so that it is easier for the users to
> provide input and to be a part of the process.  However, as anyone who
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

I agree with this completely.  I would like to see the following changes:

1)  ALL users are allowed a voice.  Input should be provided in such a
format that it is easy to read for all users, regardless of experience.
Recognizing that fact that standards have to be fairly exact, perhaps a
bulleted summary could provided with the technical write up (IE - 2
documents - the complicated, complex, exact technical "legalese" and a
summary for the common user).

2)  A Congress will actually be responsible for changing the standard, based
upon user input.  This body should be responsible for explaining why
standard changes were accepted/rejected.

3)  Standard updates should be published on a schedule.  3 - 5 years sounds
right to me.

Just as a note, I think that standard "extensions" should be made available
for differing platforms (IE - extensions to the standard for that apply to
that platform/range of platforms only).  Programming environments are very
different from the PC to a mainframe or AS/400 (when was the last time you
needed to get a cartridge number on the PC, or update the registry on a
mainframe).  These are very basic functions within their respective
environments, and not allowing the standard to be flexible enough to
recognize these differences will only lead to less and less compilers using
the standards.

Dave
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T14:28:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107151328.20f0356a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca> <AC471222EBFE1E3A.56FFF64941937FC9.7F662DCFA5721D77@lp.airnews.net> <3b4e96a1_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net> <9ir4p7$k9hi2$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com>`

```
Comments below:

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:<3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com>...
> DBuck wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Jimmy, YOU should know better. We already have "official" "extensions"
to the standard.  They are called "implementor-defined" and
"processor-dependent" features.

We don't get everything; in fact, I am pissed that we are missing
several datatypes in this standard, besides "Low-endian binary"
(COMP-5 in many implementations).

But we DO pick up on implementor extensions, and get them into the
standard.  It helps when the implementors are represented.  If you are
using Liant, or AcuCobol, or CA or NCR or DEC, or many others,
complain to your vendor that they should have input to J4.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 Standards Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T05:27:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B527B60.FCEB5DC5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca> <AC471222EBFE1E3A.56FFF64941937FC9.7F662DCFA5721D77@lp.airnews.net> <3b4e96a1_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net> <9ir4p7$k9hi2$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com> <4145699b.0107151328.20f0356a@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Comments below:
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> complain to your vendor that they should have input to J4.

Stephen,

First off - Yep my vendor is represented on J4 - the chairman <G>

Before replying I've noted both Thane's and Pete's replies. Not surprisingly even developers
are not in agreement on this one - Pete's point that it wouldn't be a good thing to
acknowledge O/S. I'll confine myself to PCs (Windowing) because I know damn all about
mainframes or Unix/Linux - although I get the 'feeling' the latter two are 'close' to
Windowing.

I think examples may illustrate my points best :-

(a) Report Writer - after a LOT of musical chairs it is NOW entrenched in COBOL 2002. BUT -
you can't use it easily with Windowing. Either back when using dot matrix or now lasers,
which have been around now for a considerable time - you are confronted with how you get at
fonts, condensed, expanded etc. You can get yourself a nice fat tome on HP and look up the
control sequences to do the above.
Net result - PC users in the main look for an alternate, easier source - in Net Express we
have PC_PRINT, Flexus print routines (I believe) and others go another route - (oh dear,
forgotten name - but Pete once referred to it as being included with the F/J compiler ???).
Net result - R/W hasn't been commonly used with PCs and probably WONT be in the future. (Of
course - as Bill has pointed out - you can buy support 'extensions' for R/W - from - guess
who ?).

(b) File naming - for my specific application an absolute MUST - dealing with many
client/facility combinations. No problem on a mainframe - just load your appropriate JCL
cards. PC - inter-active, jumping from client A1, A2, B7, G3, A10 in one session. Both F/J
and M/F have provided their own extensions to do this - CBL_FILE routines :- create
filenames, check exists, rename, delete, copy etc. The alternative - delve into Windows
searching and testing for the appropriate APIs.

(c) Screen Section - now a part of COBOL 2002. It is a minimum of 5 YEARS since I last used
Micro Focus Screen Section, and that looks entirely different to the Screen Section I used
with RM/COBOL 74. I had a quick look at the FCD and I see it does include 'vendor
implementations'. However - truly used in a PC environment, Screen Section is as useful as
udders on a bull. To use the M/F Screen Section back in DOS mode, I needed both
CBL-SCREEN_ATTRIBUTE routines and CBL_MOUSE routines. Without these you are left with a very
vanilla-flavoured Screen Section. (I wonder how useful Screen Section is/will be on
mainframes if there are no CBL_routines).

Those are the three that immediately spring to mind. There may be others, but those are the
'majors'. Yes they are very O/S specific - but without them you are seriously lumbered when
attempting COBOL at the PC level.

Mainframe COBOL(90%)        2.7 mill
Other COBOL                         0.3 mill
Other Languages                    18.6 mill
------------------                   ---------
Total                                       21.6 mill        (Gartner/Microsoft figures)

1970 - why should COBOL even contemplate 'others'. 1980 - hmm this Windows looks interesting.
1985 - maybe we 'should' think about Windows. 1990 - Wow !.
1995 - Java - bejezuz ! Had COBOL had a less myopic view, it may well have had great sway on
how Windows or the the other O/S came into play and the above table today might look as
follows :-

Mainframe COBOL(90%)        2.7 mill
Other COBOL                        14.9 mill
Other Languages                       4.0 mill
------------------                   ---------
Total                                       21.6 mill

Even if the 'Other COBOL' figure was halved or quartered, I think you will agree it may well
have played a significant part in the on-going structure of COBOL and various O/S.

(Note: I haven't even mentioned GUIs <G>).

I just re-read both Thane and Pete. This made me recall a failry simple example Don Schricker
supplied for cobolreport about two years ago. Given a simple switch routine an application
could determine whether it was calling/invoking Windows, Unix or Linux. Now follow up on that
simple concept and we are getting somewhere - but - the standard needs to acknowledge the
'differences'.

Keep going as is guys - and expect to see the COBOL total of 3 million slowly but surely
diminish.

Jimmy
```

```
Sadly, Jimmy, Crystal Reports was only included with Version 4.2 of Fujitsu.

Neither Version 5 nor the latest controversial release (Version 6.0)
included it.

I have found it to be a fantastic package  and Users love it, as it enables
the production of ad hoc reports directly from ANY major Relational Database
and SOME ISAM and BTRIEVE files.

These days for standard reporting from COBOL I use MS WORD (invoked via OLE)
and this lets Users change fonts easily without re-programming, and it also
lets them preview the report and direct it to any printer, FAX, or e-mail
with no effort on my part...(love it!). I still use Crystal for ad-hoc
reporting and encourage Users to do so too.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca> <AC471222EBFE1E3A.56FFF64941937FC9.7F662DCFA5721D77@lp.airnews.net> <3b4e96a1_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net> <9ir4p7$k9hi2$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com> <4145699b.0107151328.20f0356a@posting.google.com> <3B527B60.FCEB5DC5@home.com> <3B527E74.67651136@home.com> <3b52b531_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Sadly, Jimmy, Crystal Reports was only included with Version 4.2 of Fujitsu.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Pete,

Still on the 'sad' note. I've noted before that you you have written '..... you
wouldn't use COBOL to do this.....". I wonder ? Take yourself back 25 years with
COBOL and Assembler. Had somebody offered the challenge of, say, 'produce COBOL
GUIs' for a price, (remembering that Smalltalk had already done this),  you and
others would probably have accepted the challenge, and come up with new COBOL
syntax to accommodate this. Regrettably it is now too late.

You've offered an alternative to R/W - using OLE to get at MS Word - and of
course that's limited to the OO world, currently F/J and M/F, possibly VisualAge
(?). (Interestingly the concept of 'previewing' was a suggestion made by one N/E
user about 18 months ago with PC_PRINT - but it never came to fruition). But
again, PC_PRINT is non-standard.

The alternative now is to look upon COBOL (albeit we LOVE it), as a mongrel
language. Turn to other languages or packages that do things COBOL is just
incapable of doing. There is a LEGITIMATE reason for doing fonts, previewing
etc. as you describe - just a great pity that this isn't possible within the R/W
module.

Ignoring COBOL file handling, for which there are still legitimate uses - COBOL
like other languages with ESQL is able to access DBs - so in that respect COBOL
is not lacking. Given the COBOL files module - it is one up on the other
languages which don't have a built in feature and need other file-handling
packages apart from DB usage.

Note that while now part of COBOL 2002 - Stephen is not exactly jumping up and
down about either R/W or Screen Section. And of course, he didn't comment on
file-naming etc. - it just isn't the same problem on a mainframe. Seeing that
file-naming etc. is a part of both F/J and M/F, you would logically think this
is a requirement for the others that Stephen referred to - CA, AcuCobol, RM etc.

Possibly they have their own routines that do this - be nice if there was a
'common' approach included in the Standard - but we're back to acknowledging
different O/S. Your comments about obsolescence - don't see Windows/Linux/Unix
dying any time soon - do you ?

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca> <AC471222EBFE1E3A.56FFF64941937FC9.7F662DCFA5721D77@lp.airnews.net> <3b4e96a1_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net> <9ir4p7$k9hi2$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com>`

```
A few years ago I was a rare breed.  I was one of the few programmers
around who had experience on both mainframes and PC.  The numbers of
us with this cross platform experience has increased recently. 
However, there's still an "us" vs "them" mentality amongst mainframe
and PC Programmers.

That's not that important - except where it impacts the willingness of
one type of programmer to learn the other type of programming.

How does this apply to your message?  I'm one of those programmers who
has converted code from platform to platform - going to and from
Mainframes and PC's going back and forth with code.  I have converted
from mainframes to PC's and from PC's to mainframes.  I have gone from
System 36 to PC and from PC to System 36.  I have ported code from
Wang to VMS etc etc etc.

Most of the time - BECAUSE COBOL is "Vanilla" - all that is required
is some modification to the Select statements and things run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T13:45:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b524b11_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca> <AC471222EBFE1E3A.56FFF64941937FC9.7F662DCFA5721D77@lp.airnews.net> <3b4e96a1_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <6C0A2FC1E8C1D578.E62DE47E6BB392D0.678B736AEF5C6BFA@lp.airnews.net> <9ir4p7$k9hi2$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com> <bfdfc3e8.0107151454.6db165c2@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0107151454.6db165c2@posting.google.com...
> A few years ago I was a rare breed.  I was one of the few programmers
> around who had experience on both mainframes and PC.  The numbers of
> us with this cross platform experience has increased recently.

I'm also one of this breed ,Thane, having spent twenty years of my career
working with IBM and the BUNCH (Burroughs, Univac, NCR, Control Data, and
Honeywell) platforms. (We should also mention ICL, now owned by Fujitsu, who
produced a killer mainframe in their 1900/2900 series...). For the last 15
years I have worked (almost) exclusively on the PC and enjoy this
environment more than I ever did mainframes.
(Mainly because it is more immediate (dedicated processor, not waiting for
bloody TSO to respond) and there is none of the nonsense with JCL and system
support and getting approval to implement a database, etc. etc. As I am not
innately a patient man, I have to work very hard at it to BE
patient...working with a PC removes this uncomfortable control...<G>)

Nowadays, most of my work is as a consultant or project manager and the only
way I get to keep my hand in the techie stuff is by writing software for the
PC.

(This background is pertinent to the points below...)

> However, there's still an "us" vs "them" mentality amongst mainframe
> and PC Programmers.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I see this often with COBOL  and PL/1 programmers and mostly mainframe
people, who see their livelihoods threatened by "new technology". I have
never worked with Java, VB, or PC COBOL programmers who exhibit antipathy
towards the mainframe guys...usually it is sympathy <G>.

It saddens me that it is becoming harder and harder to get Management to
accept that their existing COBOL teams can be "leveraged" into the
Client/Server world relatively easily. They won't do it because they do not
see PC COBOL as being credible. Despite some really wonderful
implementations of COBOL on the Intel platform (both N/E and Fujitsu are
fine products, to name but two...) the lack of a credible Standard, the
inability to use Classes across compilers, the lack of a common Run Time,
all these factors end up with a management decision to keep COBOL confined
to the "back end" and use VB or Java for the GUI stuff. This is one reason
why Components are becoming more and more attractive to many sites (it
removes the need to keep multi-lingual, cross-platform teams on site).

> How does this apply to your message?  I'm one of those programmers who
> has converted code from platform to platform - going to and from
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I too have done countless conversions across mainframes and 1 PC COBOL to
IBM Mainframe (made possible by separating the User interface from the
code).

However, my experience has not been the same as yours, Thane. There is
always considerable work and planning involved. Sometimes it is major. The
fact is that programmers DO use vendor extensions, most installations have
Assembler routines which are standardized for specific purposes, and these
must also be ported or re-written.

While I would agree that the essence of COBOL makes it easier to transport,
it has not been my experience that it is as easy as modifying a few
SELECTs... (Sometimes when it's all over, we forget the sweat...<G>).

I don't think that the idea to include specific platform dependent
constructs in the Standard is a good one, even though, on the face of it, it
LOOKS like a good idea.

I can understand what prompted David's suggestion and Jimmy's endorsement of
it, however, I believe, like Thane, that it could make things worse, rather
than better. What happens when platforms become "extinct"? Do we then modify
the Standard? Isn't it a lot of work to put the stuff in the Standard only
to remove it a few years later? If we don't remove it, we will have a
Standard that will grow indefinitely as new platforms are released and COBOL
implements specific stuff for them.

Stephen already made the point that this is catered for by "implementor
defined" and "vendor extension". Like I said above, these things alone cause
problems when porting COBOL across platforms.

I'm not sure that transportability is such a big issue as it once was. With
modern quick build tools and technology it is becoming viable to simply
re-create an existing system on the target platform (obviously, it depends
on the size of the system and how "mission critical" it is...)

Sometimes this can be a good opportunity to remove some of the bugs or User
Unfriendliness in the existing system.

The fact is that without a Standard there will be some problems with
transportability.

There are NOW, and we HAVE a standard.

The remaining question is: "How critical is this?"

My feeling is, that it is not as critical as it was a few years ago.

In fact, for myself, I would forget the whole Standard thing and let Market
Forces shape the Language...but then, I WOULD say that <G>...

Pete.


>
>
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:<3B5128AA.EA912399@home.com>...
> > DBuck wrote:
> >

available

apply to

very
> > > different from the PC to a mainframe or AS/400 (when was the last time
you

a

using

people,

Standard

million,

of
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T18:03:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4e930b_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Thanks for your comments.

Very quick responses below....

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3B4DBA78.E2D7DB60@mb.sympatico.ca...
>
> IMHO, it is preferable to have a slowly-reacting standards process rather
than
> one which is responsive enough to adapt quickly to the theory of the
moment.

So, how slow is good for YOU? 17 years about right?

> You have been around for a while, I believe, so from your own experience
you'll
> be able to identify any number of bright ideas that took the IS community
by
> storm and then vanished without a trace.  Had the standards process
incorporated
> any of these it would now be loaded with utterly irrelevant clutter which
would
> render the standard unworkable.
>

Yes, I have seen "good ideas" come and go and I agree that many of them
should not be part of the COBOL Standard. Your objection is based on the
premise that for something to be done "properly" it MUST take time. I
disagree with this. It depends on the tools and techniques you use and on
the attitude, motivation, and Management of the people employed.

If you have to build a road with a shovel, it will take much longer than if
you use a bulldozer. Will the quality of the finished road be any less,
because a bulldozer was used? How important is it, if both roads allow
traffic to pass safely? Also, the Chinese have shown that very acceptable
roads CAN be built with just a shovel, if you employ enough motivated
people... In either case, an outrageous amount of time is NOT necessary to
ensure a product which is fit for the purpose for which it was intended....

> As well, I'd be very leery of a popular vote on elements.  Vox populi, vox
dei
> is all very well in theory but mistaken enthusiasm and issue du jour are
far
> from being the best way to go.

I don't know about "vox Dei" never having heard it, but I DO know about "vox
populi".  No one is suggesting that "fashion will be followed"; there will
be enough of a cross section of backgrounds and skills to ensure that
conflicting viewpoints are considered. Deciding that some particular elite
know best what is required by the "populi" is specious. If said elite become
part of the "populi" they can still make their presence felt.

Look at the worst case: We get something that is flawed in parts. But we GET
it!

Do you honestly believe that what is being produced currently (if we ever
see it) is "perfect" and NOT flawed in any way?  What makes you think that?
Because a lot of very clever people took a long time to produce it? Think
again...

At least under what I am proposing it wouldn't take 2 years to fix it...

I appreciate that you took the trouble to post, Peter. I have noted your
comments with the others I have received privately and am still receiving.
The results will be made public.

Pete.
```

```
become

Chuck,
  Remember that the CURRENT procedure got character-based (ONLY) screen
section added about 10+ years *after* it was added to the X/Open Standard.

"consensus" among countries can get you as many "oddities" as any other
method of adding functionality.

At one time, I suggested to J4 (or WG4?) that there be a category that was
sort-of the opposite of "archaic" - something like "tentative".  This could
be a group of features that were added explicitly to last ONLY one revision
of the Standard in this category.  If they became "popular," then they would
stay in the next revision; if they didn't they would come out with NO time
in the OBSOLETE category.  Neeless to say, this idea "fell flat".

I actually think that might be a "useful" category in any future COBOL
Language Specification - whether ANSI/ISO Standard or Pete's "alternate"
approach.
```

#### ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T14:29:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9il4rl$153$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com...

I believe your ideas have merit, and I also believe there are some problems
they don't address.

First, as I recall you suggested elsewhere the use of electronic media
(e-mail, news groups, internet and the like) as the "heart" of the mechanism
by which decisions about the future of COBOL should be made.    While I feel
very strongly that this is a useful tool for communication between among
interested parties (I use it all the time!),  I do not agree that every
interested party has access to such media.  Not every COBOL programmer is
allowed access to the internet at work, not every parent wants to have
internet capability accessible to his children in his home, not every
government allows its citizenry unlimited access to the internet or to
e-mail or other similar mechanisms.  I do not believe it's appropriate to
presume that anyone with input that might be valuable to the future of COBOL
has access to the mechanisms proposed here, and would contend that the
requirement would discriminate against those whose only available
communications routes are exemplified by "snail mail" and diplomatic
courier.  I consider this a *serious* problem with the proposal.

Second, the proposal calls for a "timeboxed" approach.  This ties into 1)
above; a significantly longer "timebox" than you seem to envision might
mitigate my concerns somewhat.   Also, I believe an *incomplete* design for
any feature may well turn out to be worse than not having the feature at
all, and I am not convinced that the cautions of those who saw the danger of
an incomplete design would be heard by those who were pushing for a closure
of a particular issue on "timebox" grounds.   For example, I am not
convinced that Rick Smith's study of the EXIT rules, which he claims has
been going on for a year, was time ill-spent.  His study resulted in closure
of a very real concern by an *important* contributor, as far as I'm
concerned, and a "timebox" might have resulted in a premature closure
leading to an error in the standard.   I can envision this process working
very well for projects in which the participants (and their expertise) are
well-defined, but in combination with a fluid and ill-defined workforce of
uncertain expertise and dedication, I question whether it can be presumed
effective.   With a "captive" (and paid) workforce, the participants in a
project can be presumed to be working on it at times decreed by project
management.  The "timebox" approach requires a fixed workforce of known
capability, I believe, and this work force is both volunteer and ad-hoc on a
moment-by-moment basis.  I cannot help but doubt the applicability of
"timebox" project management with such a fluid workforce of indeterminate
talent and expertise.

Third, I understand your concerns about oligarchical tyranny on the part of
J4/WG4, but my studies lead me to believe that democracy in its purest form
tends to be no less tyrannical.  I agree with Plato, Hamilton, Madison and
Jay on this matter.   It does not strike me that your proposal allows for
either "senate" or "supreme court" beyond the voice of the people on any
issue, and I do not believe that is good for the future of COBOL.   There
are no "checks and balances".

Fourth and fifth (two issues hang upon this point):  I suspect we might
differ in our understandings about what a "COBOL user" is.  It looks to
me -- and I may be very much mistaken -- that your view focuses on the
application programmer writing in COBOL, and that is primarily what you mean
when you use the term.   I'm concerned at a much broader level than *just*
the programmer or his immediate management.  Thus:

Fourth, what is "responsive to user input" from the standpoint of the COBOL
programmer might be "unstable and poorly-defined" from the standpoint of the
CEO or CIO of his company.   The unerstandable desire for the programmer to
have access to the latest whizbang feature of the language has to be
balanced against the businessman's desire to have a *stable* language
platform upon which to base the applications that are his company's
lifeblood.  This is not intended to excuse the soon-to-be-seventeen-year gap
between the previous standard and the expected promulgation of the next one,
but it is intended to point out that *very* frequent promulgation of
standards (or amendments, for that matter), and frequent issuance of new
products built according to those standards, is not *necessarily* a good
thing from the viewpoint of all parties that could reasonably be considered
to have an interest!

Fifth, I am not convinced that  the insights of a sophomore COBOL intern
programmer at a 100,000-employee company should be presumed as being as
valuable to the future of COBOL as those of his mentor, or the most senior
COBOL programmer in the shop, or for that matter the CEO (or CIO) of that
company.  Nor am I convinced that the time of the latter folk is well-spent
trying to convince the new-hire that he's talking out of the side of his
neck.   This ties back to issue #3 above, and the need for a "supreme court"
or an "upper house" in the standards development process.

Sixth, you make a good point about the need for input from the "user base",
and I believe that's really important.  But I also think *corporate* input
(by other than companies that also market COBOL compilers, in particular) is
vital to the future of COBOL, and the needs of *corporations* need to be
considered in this process.   I also believe *countries* have the right to
establish national standards bodies, and to promulgate policies relating to
such standards, for companies within those countries.  I am not convinced
the process you've outlined accounts for those needs.   The current process
does -- through voting membership in the various "alphabet soup" agencies --
at least in this country, and overall through consensus at the international
level.

Seventh, I believe however lethargic the current standards process may
appear, one of its strengths is that once the standard is adopted, *national
standards bodies* have agreed to it.  In such countries as participate in
the agreement, this standard is usually adopted "de jure" for government
agencies in that country.  That fact lends a credibility as well as a
stability to the standard, as well as to compilers claiming conformance to
it, that companies investing Big Bucks to develop applications find
attractive.   This leads us back to the fourth item above.  I do not see
that a competing "populist" standard for COBOL would have the same
credibility with captains of industry that a standard that the likes of DIN,
ANSI, BSI, ITSCJ, etc., etc., have all hailed as normative for their
countries, and I do not see a mechanism in your process that strikes me as a
convincing argument for a CIO to abandon "standard COBOL" for "alternative
standard COBOL" presuming both exist, particularly when the latter is
perceived as a constantly-moving target by the business community.

Again, I think you make some strong points about areas that could benefit
from reexamination in the process of developing a COBOL standard, and I
applaud your efforts to work for a better COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T01:13:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B4E4B21.80C32EE3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <9il4rl$153$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com...
>

OK Chuck, well reasoned and some points on what you've written. Firstly where
Pete and I diverge, (he sees me in the role of Quisling <G>), so I haven't
responded privately to his original request. It's the 'final' mechanism or what
you refer to as the 'senate' or 'supreme court'. I don't define what is the
'supreme court' but am certain we need one, (note:  I wrote ONE),  to be
acceptable to compiler vendors. (Please don't use 'checks and balances' in the
same topic - I have a very jaundiced eye when I think how long it took you folks
to get rid of Tricky Dicky <G>)..

I can think of an immediate pitfall in Pete's democratic approach - use of ISO
facilities/expertise and I'm not referring to WG4. I'm thinking of Ann's latest
messages on 'international character handling', if that is a suitable term. A
COBOL group just doesn't have that expertise - and this is where ISO should LEAD
the way regardless of programming language. In fact, as a side issue, I question
that 'we' in COBOL should even bother. ISO should be able to determine an
international standard and to my mind it should be sufficient for any language
specification to limit itself to :-

    Character Handling - see ISO document ISO-102-304/2001

Simplistic if you like, but as ISO has created standards for credit cards etc.,
why shouldn't their 'bible' of character handling be the reference point for
compiler vendors ?

>
> I believe your ideas have merit, and I also believe there are some problems
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> courier.  I consider this a *serious* problem with the proposal.

Can't help feel that's a bit over stressed. So the mainframe guys go home at
night and don't have PCs to play with ? If communication is truly a problem,
allow for snail mail to be included in  the process. Given that the approach was
widely publicized, interested parties would make the effort to get involved
somehow.

>
> Second, the proposal calls for a "timeboxed" approach.  This ties into 1)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So how do either J4 or W4 handle it currently - Sequential. Get one topic
thrashed out, some time for thought, final deliberation. Move on to next topic.
It misses the point; Pete is talking in TANDEM. As many valid topic threads as
are required can be running in tandem - but moderated. Final conclusions are
passed to your 'senate'.

Rick's involvement with EXIT rules - publicized as a specific topic - he would
have jumped in, as would others. Even allowing that this topic took six or
twelve months - it would still be running in TANDEM. What you have at the moment
is an 800 page plus document and expect us all to read religiously from cover to
cover, picking out those things we wish to comment on.  Where people have
currently commented, a maximum of 12 people have to sift through all comments to
clarify wording, and possibly in some instances, change the draft rules. And the
12 have other jobs !

Your 'indeterminate talent and expertise'. I can see that works for vendor
representatives on J4, being selected by your employers. Independents ? If I
understand Stephen correctly, I pays me money and join - what determines my
talent or expertise ?

>
> Third, I understand your concerns about oligarchical tyranny on the part of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Covered this above. "check and balances". Took a piece of legislation introduced
as a result of FDR's third term to get rid of Bill Jefferson Clinton <G>

>
> Fourth and fifth (two issues hang upon this point):  I suspect we might
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now of course you are working for a mainframe vendor - so it's not uncommon to
schmooze with CEOs. But I seriously doubt the 'real world' has changed in the
last 40 years. Give a bunch of main board guys a decision to make and it falls
into the 'bicycle shed syndrome'. They all have relevant input to make, having
ridden a bike since they were kids. Now that $1 million computer - aahhhh !
Can't look a fool in front of the others, so I'll go with the argument which
sounds most plausible.

Do you really think the Richard Bransons, Warren Buffets, Ross Perots or Ted
Turners  of this world bother their heads with the name of either a computer or
a computer language. If some CEO is going to get into the nitty-gritty of
VALIDATE or EVALUATE - I would suggest he's in the wrong job, and wont keep it
much longer.

There may well be some CEOs who are switched on - but I would suggest they are a
minority when it comes to computing. Their prime interest has to be
marketing/selling. Computing like accounting - both are necessary
'administrative evils'. So happens department store group I was with in UK,
chairman was also a director of ICL - our mainframe provider. Although the ICL
salesman moseyed between us (EDP) and the chairman - it really had twiddly squat
to do with machinery purchased - unless of course somebody had suggested buying
Univac <G> !

Your point about frequency of new versions is valid. Bill has oft made the point
that PC compiler vendors can't make a decent buck. I can only guess, but if a
mainframe compiler vendor introduced COBOL 2002 with all whistles and bells, no
doubt they would have a serious challenge in marketing and actually selling. If
piqued at all, I would suggest your CEO's interest would be SPEED - we can get
more done in the same 24 hours.

If less frequency is desired by all, both mainframe and PC vendors - why drag
the process out as an on-going event. Shut shop between each version for 5-10
years. Then restart the process afresh. What logical reason can there be for the
seventeen year cycle you referred to. (I think back to that J4 document on
standard classes/collections - a TREMENDOUS amount of thought put into it by the
originators back in 93'-'96 - now all TOTALLY wasted. We are going through the
process, starting from scratch).

> Fifth, I am not convinced that  the insights of a sophomore COBOL intern
> programmer at a 100,000-employee company should be presumed as being as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes you know, that young kid can cut through a Gordian knot that we
venerables have set for ourselves.

>
> Sixth, you make a good point about the need for input from the "user base",
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> level.

I think what you are addressing here is that when COBOL first started there was
commercial corporate input. Does that still prevail today - what happened to
those 'big COBOL users' who used to participate. Country input - I've stressed
this one before about why two committees - particularly Joe in Toronto and me in
Calgary serving on two committees. Just plain bloody silly.

>
> Seventh, I believe however lethargic the current standards process may
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well this one - simple question - why TWO committees ? Reading Jerry Garfunkel,
the US(ANSI) or whatever the COBOL committee was called back in '84, they went
off to Vienna to 'sell' the idea to ISO as an 'International Standard'.
Somewhere along the tracks something went wrong with the original intent, and he
quotes another as referring to it as 'The St. Valentine's Day Massacre 1984".
Seems to have been a mess-up ever since dancing between J4 and WG4. Either J4
'truly represents' all country interests, which negates a need for WG4 - or
change the rules J4 = US citizens only. My preference - the COBOL Standards
Committee. To avoid the double whammy make it subordinate to ISO, but with those
'secrecy rules' changed.


> Again, I think you make some strong points about areas that could benefit
> from reexamination in the process of developing a COBOL standard, and I
> applaud your efforts to work for a better COBOL.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T18:42:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4e9dbd_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <9il4rl$153$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Thanks Chuck,

A very long post and I simply don't have time to cover it in detail.

Responses to your numbered points are as follows:

1. I don't accept that anyone who is involved with computing in the 21st
Century has no access to the Internet. If I didn't, and I REALLY wanted to
become involved in the future of the Language I use to make my living then
I'd very quickly GET access.

2. The use of timeboxing does NOT imply a "half-assed" job is done. See my
response to Peter Lacey in this thread. It means that the TOP priorities get
focussed on, rather than the effort being dispersed amongst a whole bunch of
things which happen to be on the "Agenda". Remember also these timeboxes are
iterated. As flaws come to light, they are fixed in a subsequent iteration.
It is analogous to learning by "Trial and Error". This process has been used
by Humans for the last 3,000,000 years and has led to men walking on the
Moon and us having this conversation across Continents. It is a tried and
proven technique.

3. There is no Senate or Congress because the people involved in the process
are "specialists" in the sense that they use the "product" every day in
their workplaces. Some are more experienced than others, some have more
specific experience or academic backgrounds, but ALL have an equal RIGHT to
be a part of something that has been designated as "Public Domain" since its
inception.

4 and 5. Nope. I do not see just Techies being part of the process. I have
stated many times how I see the user base, in this process. It should be
drawn from ALL interested parties who care about COBOL. That includes
Project Managers, Applications, Business, and Systems Analysts, Academics,
Consultants, Strategic Managers, Business Managers, as WELL as the COBOL
"grunts" who have to use the product every day to make a living. The broader
the spectrum, the better for COBOL. We NEED a mix of "ideas people", "expert
witnesses" and "technical programmer" opinions and comments.

6. Corporate respresentaives would be an essential part of the mix. See 4
and 5 above.

7. Sorry we differ here. I don't see endorsement by Alphabet Soup as lending
credibility to something. I am never impressed by the "argument from
Authority" (I have met and debated with University Professors who could not
make as valid and cogent an argument as the regulars drinking in my local
pub.) The rightness or wrongness of something stands on the merit or
otherwise of its inherent value, not the qualifications of the person
propounding it. (Having said that, a suitably qualified person USUALLY is in
a better position to examine the issues, but not INVARIABLY so, and in the
final analysis, it is the arguments which decide, not the arguers.)

Credibility is earned by a process when it is seen to be credible. That
means it delivers meaningful, useful, output in plain English (translated
for other Countries who may be interested), in a reasonable and useful
timeframe. When that happens, then it is credible. When it doesn't, then all
credibility is lost.

As a free lance Project Manager I know that I am as good as the last
project. My credibility depends on my performance. I have no problem with
that, in fact, I wouldn't have it any other way...<G>

Thanks for your kind closing comments. It is nice to know that, while we may
disagree, you are not doubting my integrity...<G>

Pete.

http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

```
. I do not see just Techies being part of the process. I have

> drawn from ALL interested parties who care about COBOL. That includes
> Project Managers, Applications, Business, and Systems Analysts, Academics,
> Consultants, Strategic Managers, Business Managers, as WELL as the COBOL
> "grunts" who have to use the product every day to make a living. The
broader
> the spectrum, the better for COBOL. We NEED a mix of "ideas people",
"expert
> witnesses" and "technical programmer" opinions and comments.
>
> 6. Corporate respresentaives would be an essential part of the mix. See 4
> and 5 above.

I'm not sure you've understood my point.  I believe if Company X has a
spokesman charged with stating formal positions on a given topic relating to
COBOL, that spokesman reasonably outranks any and all of the programmers
that work for that company.   I don't see a provision in your proposal for
the CIO (or his spokesman) of, say, Boeing or Bank of America having more of
a say in the future of COBOL than a grunt intern in his employ, and in all
honesty I think he should.  He's got a lot more invested in the outcome.
Same applies to national bodies, particularly when the positions they take
are produced by consortia of companies.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Academics,

> > "grunts" who have to use the product every day to make a living. The
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 6. Corporate respresentaives would be an essential part of the mix. See
4
> > and 5 above.
>
> I'm not sure you've understood my point.  I believe if Company X has a
> spokesman charged with stating formal positions on a given topic relating
to
> COBOL, that spokesman reasonably outranks any and all of the programmers
> that work for that company.   I don't see a provision in your proposal for
> the CIO (or his spokesman) of, say, Boeing or Bank of America having more
of
> a say in the future of COBOL than a grunt intern in his employ, and in all
> honesty I think he should.  He's got a lot more invested in the outcome.
> Same applies to national bodies, particularly when the positions they take
> are produced by consortia of companies.
>
No, Chuck, I totally understand your point. I just don't agree with it. (I
agree it is arguable...<G>)

I DON'T see a Corporate spokesman (or ANYBODY) having more of a say in the
future of COBOL than a "grunt intern" in his employ.

MY point is, that it is WHAT's right that is important, and NOT WHO's right.

I believe "grunt interns" (and I was one once...."long ago and far away",
but I still remember...<G>) are just as capable of having good ideas about
things as anybody else. Ideas will stand or fall on their own merit,
irrespective of WHO proposes them. (Remember, these interns are the Managers
of tomorrow...we'd BETTER hope they are capable of having good ideas...<G>)

Saying that the person with the most investment has the most say, is fine
when you are investing in a profit making startup that NEEDS your
investment, and where you take a calculated risk on the outcome.

COBOL is PUBLIC DOMAIN. You CANNOT buy it! One of the reasons we are having
this conversation is because I believe certain interests have done just
that, then they have abused it, and its time we got it back...<G>

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T14:02:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0107131302.4cd82a63@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
I am very doubtful that a unlimited group of people corresponding by
internet would get through any like as much business in the same time
as is done by a J4 meeting.  Although 17 years from one standard to
the next is a long time, I suspect that internet conferencing might
never do it at all, without some very careful thought about
organisation and participation and could still take considerably
longer to cover the same ground.

I myself have participated in the J4 activities almost completely by
internet since about June 1999 and have found the discipline of having
to carefully check what I write before despatch very important, it
generally takes me at least several days.  The successive series of
draft standards are large documents and only a limited number of
people have extensive knowledge and comprehension of their content and
even those generally want to look up things before committing
themselves to making responses.  Even the seemingly simplest of
changes can have unexpected ramifications that have to be checked in
perhaps many different places for many different features within the
draft standard.  The subject of backwards compatibility also means
that J4 has to check the impact of many changes on the previous
standard by looking at that standard, another substantial document.

I have been very impressed by the extent and depth of knowledge plus
long term commitment of the major participants in J4. I don't think
that their contributions should be discounted or discouraged, though I
am sure that they themselves agree that 17 years to issue a new
standard is undesirable and would look to ways of avoiding a repeat,
probably by trying to do less all at once.

As far as I was aware, anyone could submit proposals and comments to
J4. In my case I alway got a polite response, even when quite a few of
my comments were misunderstandings.  It is my understanding that
general participation is now not encouraged for the current revision
as J4 and WG4 are trying to tidy up loose ends with the aim of being
able to issue a reasonably correct and self-consistent standard fairly
soon, but suggestions regarding improvements to a future revision are
always welcomed.  Even so, suggestions regarding corrections to the
existing draft standard are still welcome and can be sent via any J4
member, some of whom, such as Thane Hubbell, Bill Klein and Steven
Spiro, are regular participants in comp.lang.cobol.

If internet conferencing were adopted, I suspect that individual such
conferences would probably best be very limited in their scope to
avoid excessive digression.

For internet conferencing, it would probably be desirable to have a
partipating group of acknowledged experts on the standard and to
ensure that as well as there always being a moderator, each decision
point would be observed by a set mininum number or quorum of such
experts available to make comments before the decision is taken.

I would certainly take an interest in internet conferencing were it to
be established, presumably there would be some initial trials to see
how it goes and to tune the mode of operation.  As a matter of
interest, I believe that a similar trial conference was undertaken
recently, I think for HTML, perhaps that would provide some useful
lessons, if anyone in this group participated perhaps they could let
us know.

I might be able to help with some of the activities, but now that I
have a job, my time would be much more limited.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com>...
> Although a number of people have responded to my request under the
> "Alternate Standard" thread, I realise that a significant proportion of the
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Request for feedback

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T13:37:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4fa64d_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4da8ff_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <fcd09c56.0107131302.4cd82a63@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert,

Thank you for this.

A well considered and expressed case.

Some comments below...

Pete.

"Robert Jones" <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:fcd09c56.0107131302.4cd82a63@posting.google.com...
> I am very doubtful that a unlimited group of people corresponding by
> internet would get through any like as much business in the same time
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, you are absolutely correct that careful thought regarding the
organisation and participation is the key to success here. A different model
(based on RAD type Project Management) is being proposed and I believe it
will be effective. (But then, I WOULD say that...<G>). It is different to
what you may have seen previously and has been described at length elsewhere
in this forum and other places.

> I myself have participated in the J4 activities almost completely by
> internet since about June 1999 and have found the discipline of having
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> themselves to making responses.

This is where I see one of the major problems with the existing (and
currently proposed) Standard. These documents are not in Plain English, use
convoluted prose, and legalese that MAKES them difficult to amend or even
utilise. (It certainly makes them difficult to read, but the authors would
argue they were never intended to be best sellers...<G>). Why should we have
a document that "only a limited number of
 people have extensive knowledge and comprehension of "?

I want to see a Standard that can be readily comprehended by everybody,
written in Plain English. I don't care if it isn't PERFECT, as long as it is
WORKABLE. We can refine it where it shows that it needs refining. (And that
is another thing which is being proposed: a much more timely, open, and
effective Revision process...)

The people writing it should NOT be terriffied to release it until every "i"
is dotted, in case they have it "wrong". It should be an iterative,
evolutionary process that gradually approaches the optimum. Due care and
diligence should be enough to get it out.



> Even the seemingly simplest of
> changes can have unexpected ramifications that have to be checked in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, backward compatibility is a millstone around our necks, but it is one
that we have to carry. I see one approach to this as being to allow both the
old and the new until the old can safely be phased out (could be 20
years...)


> I have been very impressed by the extent and depth of knowledge plus
> long term commitment of the major participants in J4. I don't think
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Despite what many people think, I have great sympathy for this viewpoint. I
KNOW that NOBODY deliberately sets out to fail, and I don't doubt the
individual contributions have been laudable.

Nevertheless, the fact remains it has not achieved what it should have.

We HAVE to ask, WHY? Not so we can whip cats or assign blame, but simply so
the process can be rectified. Unfortunately, the response to date has been
less than could reasonably be hoped.

I have seen prevarication, excuses, non-acceptance of responsibility or even
any concept of answerability to the COBOL Community. A high-handed "we did a
great job; it wasn't our fault" attitude which has become so intolerable (to
me at least) that I am now proposing a complete alternative.

> As far as I was aware, anyone could submit proposals and comments to
> J4. In my case I alway got a polite response, even when quite a few of
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, Robert, but submitting ideas for approval is NOT the same as discussing
and approving ideas yourself.  (cf. Royal Decree vs Parliament).

I am pleased to hear that your responses were always polite. Others were not
so fortunate and felt they were being summarily dismissed in an arrogant
way. I don't know whether this is real or imagined and it doesn't matter. If
there is an OPEN vote in a moderated on-topic thread, nobody need ever be
placed in the position of going cap in hand to try and get an idea
acknowledged.

> If internet conferencing were adopted, I suspect that individual such
> conferences would probably best be very limited in their scope to
> avoid excessive digression.
>
We expect to have them rigidly moderated to ensure that they remain
on-topic.

> For internet conferencing, it would probably be desirable to have a
> partipating group of acknowledged experts on the standard and to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
While that is not unreasonable, we would want to remove the "Us" and "Them"
mentality. Experts of all persuasions would be welcome to participate, but
on an EQUAL basis to everybody else. The only things to be considered would
be the ideas contributed, not who contributed them. I believe that thinking
people (like computer programmers, f'rinstance...<G>) are quite capable of
evaluating the merit of an idea for themselves, without requiring the
"interpretation" of an "expert" (Sorry, I sound like Martin Luther...<G> but
that IS how I feel.)

> I would certainly take an interest in internet conferencing were it to
> be established, presumably there would be some initial trials to see
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The sincerity of your post is much appreciated and so is your offer of help.

The current status is as follows:

I have posted elsewhere ("Alternate Standard" thread) a list of objectives
that must be realised before such a forum can eventuate. There is a four
month timebox on deciding whether the attainment of these objectives are
achievable. (Some of them will be difficult...but NONE of them are
unattainable).

We have three and a half months to go and all I will say at this point is
that support has been strong enough for me to continue the effort. I am no
longer doing this entirely on my own; a number of people are helping in
whatever way they can, and more have expressed support. At the end of the
timebox we will review where we are and decide whether to proceed or not.

It all comes down to this:

1. Does COBOL really need a Standard?

    This is very arguable. It may be DESIRABLE but is it ESSENTIAL? At the
moment Market Forces are driving Vendors to produce what is needed, but this
has ramifications when it comes to cross platform compatibility. (There is
also an argument which says that even when we HAD a standard ('85), there
were STILL problems with porting COBOL across platforms...)

2. If we agree that a Standard is desirable, how can we make it pertinent
and credible?

    We make it pertinent by writing it in Plain English with the OPEN
contribution of ALL interested parties, and ensuring that threads stay on
topic. We make it credible by producing it in the timeframe we said we
would, without excuses or prevarication. The answerability is ALWAYS to the
COBOL Community, who will be involved in the production of the Standard.

The rest of it is just the mechanics of how to achieve these goals.

Pete.

http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Harrisburg, PA - COBOL Programmer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T14:34:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4fb496_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ilC37.199$ym4.7652@iad-read.news.verio.net> <20010713193236.15139.00002757@ng-fj1.aol.com>`

```
Well, John, I care about your 2 cents...<G>

However, I believe the Doc does us all a service when he attempts to keep
agents honest.

After far too long as a free lance I have sadly come to the realisation that
there ARE cowboys out there who DO try and get resumes for very cynical
purposes.

Sometimes these resumes are actually sold on to other agents; sometimes they
cut deals with each other and the Client ends up paying several middlemen as
well as paying you.

(I had one case, where I was being charged out at ï¿½900 a day and being paid
ï¿½450. The Client was very happy with my work but explained he couldn't
afford to extend me... It turned out that my name and details had been
exchanged in a sauna by two agents who both felt entitled to a cut, just for
a handshake (well, they shook SOMETHING...<G>). When I found out, I got one
of them totally out of the equation and we settled on a ï¿½600 charge out with
me getting ï¿½500 and another six months work... These days I work on track
record and avoid agents altogether (with the exception of two London
agencies I have found to be reputable and honest). The point is that agents
DO troll for resumes by advertising non-existent jobs, because the Resumes
alone are worth money to them...)

Now, the same kind of thing can apply with permanent jobs also. The argument
that "if one person gets a job, then no harm is done" is valid, IF ONE
PERSON GETS A JOB! Sadly, this is seldom the case.

Finally, your point regarding salary negotiation.

If a Company is on the level and have a genuine permanent position or
contract vacancy, they will ALWAYS give a price range to the Agent. If the
Agent is not prepared to publish this, then there is reason to suspect
skullduggery.  Is the Agent's mark-up so outrageous that he dare not quote
what the Client is paying? Maybe there never was such a job, it was tailored
by the Agent to entice the best resumes, in the hope that he can get some
client credibility by showing these CVs with the names and contact details
blanked out, to prospects he is cold calling.

I can assure you that such things DO happen... (I have sat on both sides of
the fence here as employer and employee)

Would you answer an ad for a car where no price was stated? Why would you
then apply for a job, having no idea what was being offered <G>?

If an Agent responds to Doc's not unreasonable request by posting a range
(and some of them do), then I think everybody is better off. If nothing
else, it indicates validity of the offer. (It is still not a guarantee of
the Agent's integrity...CAVEAT EMPTOR!)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Harrisburg, PA - COBOL Programmer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T23:31:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ir60o$kkqo5$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<ilC37.199$ym4.7652@iad-read.news.verio.net> <20010713193236.15139.00002757@ng-fj1.aol.com>`

```

Yy JOHN yy <yyjohnyy@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010713193236.15139.00002757@ng-fj1.aol.com...
> Sorry DD, I must disagree with you too.  If one person did get a job, isnt
it
> worth it ?
>      Also, If I was hiring a programmer, and could pay up to X amount, why
> would I list that, if the programmer would accept Y amount.
>      Plus, how long does it take to email a resume anyway ?
>      Just my 2 cents.......If anyone cares.

I'm afraid that I have to agree with DD here.  If a job is available, then
providing details about the position (title, location, length of contract,
responsibilities, pay range) should be easy to provide.  It will save time,
both for the employer and the applicants.

As for pay, why, as an employer, would you want to underpay an employee.  Do
you really believe that the employee won't figure it out?  It is pretty easy
to get a good idea of what the pay range should be, so most applicants are
going to come in with that information.  Even if you do get them, how long
do you think it will take for them to figure it out.  When he/she does, do
you think that they will want to continue to work for you?

Why, as a potential employee, should I respond to an ad for a company that
is unwilling to release this information?  I can tell you that *I* won't.
Ever.  Failure to provide this information will simply eliminate many
potential applicants.

Any time that I see a post that doesn't provide employment information, I
assume it is a troll (and I know that I am not the only one).  If an
employer really wants his opening to be considered, they should provide the
information...

Dave
```

```
> information...

If a putative employer excludes viable candidates by virtue of a
poorly-written solicitation, that is his problem.

If a potential employee takes the time and makes the effort to respond to an
ad which someone else considers inadequate, well, that's the individual's
decision to make.

Bottom line: this newsgroup is 'comp.lang.cobol,' not
'alt.recruiter.writing.practice' or 'alt.find.a.job;' but it's still public
and unmoderated, and should remain thus.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Harrisburg, PA - COBOL Programmer

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T11:26:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sbA47.273$CQ4.26454@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<ilC37.199$ym4.7652@iad-read.news.verio.net> <Ljg47.1013$Ld1.48719@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <eTg47.432$ym4.17013@iad-read.news.verio.net> <t6j47.3148$Ld1.144305@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9qk47.458$ym4.17648@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In your boilerplate message you state...

"When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates,
associated with the position(s) offered; to do otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
determine rates."

From "to do otherwise" onward sure sounds like unsolicited and gratuitous
comments on how the recruiter should do his job.

If you asked a real programming question, like, "how do you use SEARCH
ALL?", I could answer this way:

" SEARCH ALL requires the object of the SEARCH verb to be sorted consistent
with the ASCENDING|DESCENDING KEY clause of the table to be searched."

But you'd be (rightly) PO'd if I added...

" to do otherwise leads many to believe to you don't know enough about COBOL
to use any form of the SEARCH verb, or you are trolling for the answers to
your homework."

If you dropped everything after, "position(s) offered" you'd be cool. And as
long as you are going to be editing this text anyway, please add "and
geographic location" to the part after "please include."

Thanks,
MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Harrisburg, PA - COBOL Programmer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T13:04:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cxg47.431$ym4.16888@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<ilC37.199$ym4.7652@iad-read.news.verio.net> <20010713193236.15139.00002757@ng-fj1.aol.com> <9ir60o$kkqo5$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```
In article <9ir60o$kkqo5$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>,
DBuck <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote:

[snip]

>As for pay, why, as an employer, would you want to underpay an employee. 

If this was supposed to be a question ('Why would you want to underpay an
employee?') then the answer is obvious: if labor is a commodity and it is
in an employer's best interest to pay as little as possible for as much of
any given commodity then it is in the employer's best interest to pay as
little as possible for labor.

Likewise, it is in the best interest of the supplier of any given
commodity to be paid as much as possible for said commodity... EXCEPT, of
course, if that commodity is labor, in which case the supplier, most
frequently an 'employee', is expected to demonstrate something called
'Employee Loyalty', which frequently translates as 'accepting less money
for the commodity than might be found elsewhere'.

I have never found a satisfactory explanation for this apparent
dichotomy... but I'se jes' a COBOL-codin' fool.

DD
```

```
I do not think it does make a difference.  However, as for myself, I am
grateful to DD for spending time and effort in pointing the obvious. If
after DD's posting the poster "offering" a job does not post again with
details about rate, or  range of rates,  then it is quite obvious to the
rest of us how serious the poster was. It does nothing to prevent the
unskilled and/or desperate unemployed COBOL programmer from sending his/her
resume.

I do not read DD's comments as implying that the poster was acting in bad
faith. What he is saying is that people see with suspicion posts like those.
It gives everyone the benefit of the doubt. If the poster prefers not to
follow up with a rate, or range of rates, then everyone is free to draw a
conclusion.

Regards,
Luis
```
