###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Harrisburg, PA - COBOL Programmer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T01:29:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xrr47.486$ym4.19515@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<15d0b1bf.0107120642.504d38e8@posting.google.com> <OPi37.54$ym4.2826@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9ilgt8$8i4o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <9it94p$88k$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
In article <9it94p$88k$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>,
Luis Gomez-Almeida <ar656@freenet.carleton.ca> wrote:

[snippage]

>I do not read DD's comments as implying that the poster was acting in bad
>faith. What he is saying is that people see with suspicion posts like those.
>It gives everyone the benefit of the doubt. If the poster prefers not to
>follow up with a rate, or range of rates, then everyone is free to draw a
>conclusion.

*Thank* you, Mr Gomez-Almeida... I was beginning to despair.

This is an unmoderated group... but it is, primarily, a *programmer's*
unmoderated group.  Someone wants to post a job, fine... but post by the
group's rules.

It is not the custom to mention rates in job-offerings in all places...
compare want-ads from major newspapers throughout the country and you will
find this to be so.  In *this* 'newspaper', however, if one does not
follow the custom - as mentioned in the FAQ - of posting a rate then some
of the readers *will* conclude - rightly or wrongly - that the person
posting is either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to determine
rates.

*That* is what I point out, and nothing more... and I am sufficiently
aware of some folks' unfamiliarity with this medium that I indulge in a
bit of 'poor manners' by posting *and* emailing the boilerplate.
Sometimes I get a 'thank you' and the rate follows, sometimes I get no
reply... but a rate follows, sometimes I get nothing and no rate
follows... but hey, someone's got to do the rough job.

DD
```

<a id="t-4d6a0c569d"></a>
## Thread: student having trouble with inspect tallying — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### student having trouble with inspect tallying

- **From:** "Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T16:43:01+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
I am having trouble with the inspect tallying verb here is my code
OS = Win2k
Compiler = MF personal COBOL (I also tested this on Fujitsu v3)
My explanations and questions are at the bottom of the code

       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 w-fld                        pic x(21) value
       'hhcarhhplane,iiboatjj'.
       01  ctr.
           05  ctr1                    pic 99  value 0.
           05  ctr2                    pic 99  value 0.
       procedure division.
       display spaces upon crt
       display w-fld
       display ctr1
       display ctr2
           inspect w-fld
               tallying
     *this line counts all the occurrences of H in the string
               ctr1 for all 'h'
     *this line counts all the occurrences of H in the string before the
comma
               ctr2 for all 'h' before initial ','
               replacing all 'i' by ' ' after initial ','
       display w-fld
       display ctr1
       display ctr2
       stop run.

Everything is correct except ctr2 it has a value of 0 and not 4 as expected.
If I change the code from [ all 'h' before initial ',' ] to all [ all 'a'
before initial ',' ] it works correctly and ctr2 has a value of 2.
The only way that I can get it to work correctly is to separate it into a
separate "inspect tallying".
What am I missing here ? The only logical explanation that I have is that
the first "inspect tallying" will over ride the second if it checks for the
same character.
I say this because if I change the last "j" in w-fld to "h" and the second
inspect to [ctr2 for all 'h' after initial ','] ctr2=0 and ctr1=5 but change
it to any other character and it works.
Am I right ?

Thanks
Henk
```

#### ↳ Re: student having trouble with inspect tallying

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T12:37:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Iil37.40$ga.2792@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
"Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za> wrote in message
news:3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za...
> I am having trouble with the inspect tallying verb here is my code
> OS = Win2k
> Compiler = MF personal COBOL (I also tested this on Fujitsu v3)
> My explanations and questions are at the bottom of the code
[snip]
>        01 w-fld                        pic x(21) value
>        'hhcarhhplane,iiboatjj'.
>        01  ctr.
>            05  ctr1                    pic 99  value 0.
>            05  ctr2                    pic 99  value 0.
[snip]
>            inspect w-fld  tallying
>                ctr1 for all 'h'
>                ctr2 for all 'h' before initial ','
>                replacing all 'i' by ' ' after initial ','
[snip]
> Everything is correct except ctr2 it has a value of 0 and not 4 as
expected.
[snip]
> What am I missing here ? The only logical explanation that I have is that
> the first "inspect tallying" will over ride the second if it checks for
the
> same character.
[snip]

Henk,

Your investigation led you to the correct conclusion.  The following rule is
found in the 1985 ISO standard:

"Whenever a match occurs, tallying or replacing takes place as described in
general rules 10 and 13.  The character position in the data item referenced
by identifier-1 [w-fld in your example] immediately to the right of the
rightmost character position that participated in the match is now
considered to be the leftmost character position of the data item referenced
by identifier-1, and the comparison cycle starts again with the first
literal-1 [the all 'h' associated with ctr1 in your example]." *

So, when the first all 'h' matches and ctr1 is incremented, that is the last
time that the matching 'h' is considered, and the inspect goes on to the
next character to the right.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
www.liant.com

* [This excerpt is from the RM/COBOL Language Reference Manual, which
closely follows the wording of the standard.]
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: student having trouble with inspect tallying

- **From:** "Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T22:31:41+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4e09cf.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`
- **References:** `<3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <Iil37.40$ga.2792@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
Thanks Tom

You gave the small nudge that I needed to solve this problem. After reading
your post it dawned upon me that I had been staring at the answer the whole
time. Each time I was reasoning I said that I could not duplicate the
problem with the [inspect replacing all 'h' by '*' ] because all the 'H' 's
would be changed to '*' 's
So it was proving the rule the whole time that the first "all" match will
match all the specified characters and any other match (leading, first ,all
,before/after) will find nothing because the first match got them all. So if
an 'all' match (this in include all before/after depending on the string
data) is done any other match type should be done in a separate inspect.
This will obviously only work with tallying as a replace all will also
render the separate inspect replacing's useless.

Regards
Henk

"Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com> wrote in message
news:Iil37.40$ga.2792@nnrp1.sbc.net...

that
> > the first "inspect tallying" will over ride the second if it checks for
> the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your investigation led you to the correct conclusion.  The following rule
is
> found in the 1985 ISO standard:
>
> "Whenever a match occurs, tallying or replacing takes place as described
in
> general rules 10 and 13.  The character position in the data item
referenced
> by identifier-1 [w-fld in your example] immediately to the right of the
> rightmost character position that participated in the match is now
> considered to be the leftmost character position of the data item
referenced
> by identifier-1, and the comparison cycle starts again with the first
> literal-1 [the all 'h' associated with ctr1 in your example]." *
>

last

> next character to the right.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: student having trouble with inspect tallying

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T13:09:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107121209.4212f692@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
Comments below:

"Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za> wrote in message news:<3b4db88b.0@news1.mweb.co.za>...
> I am having trouble with the inspect tallying verb here is my code
> OS = Win2k
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Henk


The IBM manuals are not clear and I do not have a MicroFocus manual.
However, the ANSI standard is pretty clear. Once you have a match
(have tallied an a character or string), you do not tally it again,
even if you have more TALLYING statements.  Kinda like EVALUATE ---
once you get a match, you stop checking.

HOWEVER, a combination of TALLYING and REPLACING is _specifically_
treated as 2 INSPECT statements.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: Come on, folks, join up! COBOL needs you!
```

#### ↳ Re: student having trouble with inspect tallying

I also think you can only use one tallying counter.

- **From:** "Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I created this code to test my answer to a question at the end of the
"inspect" chapter my study manual
The question was to count the number of 'H' s in the field and change al
'I's after the first comma to spaces. Also count the number of 'H's found
before the first comma.The data was provided.
 'hhcarhhplane,iiboatjj'

You are indeed corect 2 inspects are needed in this example because the
first inspect uses "all' so the second will find nothing.
No you can have multiple tallying counters as my first post shows that when
I count the 'a's it will work fine.

Regards
Henk

Hello,

Anyone have experience reading MicroFocus Line Sequential files on UNIX that
were produced on the PC?  I have to produce a variable-length, line
sequential file on Win98 that's going to be processed by a UNIX cobol app.
I don't *think* there's going to be a problem, since both platforms use the
ASCII character set, but you never know.

I intend to contact MicroFocus, but I'd feel better getting some feedback
from anyone having experience with this.

Thanks,
BG

## Thread: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods) — 4 this month / 52 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2001-06](2001-06.md#t-4617d11b2a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Joe Durnavich" <joejd@mcs.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T16:53:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cCk37.2005$gj1.105284@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261844.98de2b5@posting.google.com> <3B3C268E.35F0CE50@Azonic.co.nz> <dv7qjt4mk0md05mff2rf7dist79rk38a8l@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick writes:

>I have a question for you or anyone...  Here is a generalization
>of some code I wrote just today.  Being the 'good' programmer I
>am I wanted to avoid any gotos.
>EXIT PARAGRAPH is not available in my environment.  And even if it was,
>apparently that's bad programming as well.

[snip]

>1000-process-record.
>    perform 1010-check-for-discard
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>*   and sets discard-record to true if it is.  Otherwise it sets
keep-record
>*   to true.
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>    exit.

In my opinion, as long as the program doesn't change too
much in the future, this is okay.  However, this type of
paragraph is not as easy to maintain in the face of future
modifications:

(1)  You may have a need to perform the functionality that was
     easily accessible in 1100-really-process-record.  Now
     you have it embedded in a paragraph that performs
     multiple tasks.  A maintenance programmer looking for a
     a quick fix may add a skip-discard-check flag to
     isolate the discard logic.

(2)  You may have to add logic that applies to both the
     discard and non-discard cases.  Perhaps you have to
     delete or mark the record in some way to indicate it
     was processed in both cases.   Your "exit paragraph"
     may skip over this logic.

     Early exits can be big trouble makers when programmers
     add them to "get out" quickly.  There is a substantial
     risk of skipping over resource release logic such as
     closing files, closing windows, removing lock files,
     releasing memory, etc.

I would stick with your first version, except I might rename
1000-process-record to something like 1000-process-current-record
and rename 1100-really-process-record to 1100-process-record.
Or maybe I might just change the 1100-really- name to something
that describes what is done with the record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T06:26:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B503A81.20031C6E@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261844.98de2b5@posting.google.com> <3B3C268E.35F0CE50@Azonic.co.nz> <dv7qjt4mk0md05mff2rf7dist79rk38a8l@4ax.com> <cCk37.2005$gj1.105284@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Joe Durnavich wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick writes:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>      isolate the discard logic.

Well, the whole reason for this example is that, no matter where how I
retrieve the record (in my example there are three ways to retrieve the
record) I *never* want to actually process the record if I've determined
that I want to discard it.  That's the whole reason for having the
'check for discard' logic performed from within the 'process record'
paragraph.  I was trying to avoid repeating the following code in three
places:

*    first retrieve record
     perform check-for-discard
     if keep-record
         perform process-record
     end-if

Now if I only had that in one place it would be no big deal.  I just
didn't want allow a maintenance programmer five years down the road a
way to perform process-record without first checking to see if the
record should be discarded.
 
> (2)  You may have to add logic that applies to both the
>      discard and non-discard cases.  Perhaps you have to
>      delete or mark the record in some way to indicate it
>      was processed in both cases.   Your "exit paragraph"
>      may skip over this logic.

I would simply put this logic before I did the 'check for discard'
logic.

>      Early exits can be big trouble makers when programmers
>      add them to "get out" quickly.  There is a substantial
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe Durnavich
```

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T16:50:54+00:00

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> Well, the whole reason for this example is that, no matter where how I
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You could revise the process steps - to move the selection logic to a
read until valid record encountered - therefor the discard (or non
valid) record selection can be an part of the record read logic -
naturally this is not always efficient, but I have had applications
where that worked better then the more usual process.

	Susan A
```

## Thread: RENT vs. RENT — 4 msgs

### RENT vs. RENT

- **From:** dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale)
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T11:18:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<340ff33e.0107121018.741db0db@posting.google.com>`

```
If I compile using IBM's COBOL for OS/390 and specify compile option
RENT, the load module is not automatically marked RENT.  Is it
recommended to specify linkage editor (or binder) parameter RENT if
compile option RENT has been used?
```

#### ↳ Re: RENT vs. RENT

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T22:09:57+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<63trktc6i7b85a5h3lpmgccb0lv7qv22bn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0107121018.741db0db@posting.google.com>`

```
On 12 Jul 2001 11:18:46 -0700 dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale) wrote:

:>If I compile using IBM's COBOL for OS/390 and specify compile option
:>RENT, the load module is not automatically marked RENT.  

True.

:>                                                         Is it
:>recommended to specify linkage editor (or binder) parameter RENT if
:>compile option RENT has been used?

You may as well.

Of course, unless the load library is APF authorized it won't make a
difference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RENT vs. RENT

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T02:50:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5255F6.75CD89F2@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0107121018.741db0db@posting.google.com> <63trktc6i7b85a5h3lpmgccb0lv7qv22bn@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
<snip>

> :>recommended to specify linkage editor (or binder) parameter RENT if
> :>compile option RENT has been used?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference.

<snip>

Well, not entirely true. For every LOAD (or equivalent) a new copy of that
module will be fetched. If this is not the desired result, then one needs
to provide either the REUS or RENT option to the Binder/Linkage editor.

Granted, most COBOL programmers do not understand the requirements for
actual RE-Entrant coding. But assuming that this was done correctly, then
obtaining one copy of the code and entering it multiple times without
having to check to see if it is already loaded (by running the CDE chain
unless some other method is used)... Well you get my drift.

Regards,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907 office
```

#### ↳ Re: RENT vs. RENT

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-12T14:26:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iktm2$7l8$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0107121018.741db0db@posting.google.com>`

```
If you compile with the RENT compiler option and do NOT do anything to
override the linkage-editor (binder) attributes, I thought it did default to
RENT (assuming you aren't link-editing in a non-RENT module).  I would check
out the IBM documentation to see if you are doing something else to "turn
off" this default.
```

<a id="t-72c2c788e3"></a>
## Thread: Need help!!! — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help!!!

- **From:** "R.L." <roblau@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T02:26:13+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9inelf$14q5@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
Hi there,

I just start learning Cobol language. I have a question about accept
statement.  If I want to set a criteria for the accept statment like the
input amount(donation) cannot exceed 10% of the income.

How should I set this criteria?  Hope you can help!!

Regards

rob
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help!!!

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T20:58:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9inndi$n3b$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<9inelf$14q5@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
This is best handled as an error condition or as a loop.

One way to do this is to just output an error message and abort the program.
Another way to do this is put the accept in a routine that is run over and
over until the proper conditions are met.  

You could, as an alternative, accept up to 10% of gross and reject the amount
over 10% of gross.

If this is for tax purposes then you may have to go back and ask the person to
verify the amount explaining the result. 

For instance you could perform a routine until a certain condition is met. 
Ive seen some programs that save only part of a record based on how many
screens have been completed. 

Since you have to accept the amount before you can dertermine what it is, you
can not decide anything untill after you have the data.  You can always make
the person keep entering data till it is acceptable or they run out of
chances and the program is forced to end.

In article <9inelf$14q5@imsp212.netvigator.com>, R.L. <roblau@hotmail.com>
writes:
>Hi there,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


 -----  Posted via NewsOne.Net: Free (anonymous) Usenet News via the Web  -----

   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

- **From:** "Minora" <hnkatmweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 2001-07-13T23:16:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4f65dd.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
Hi Rob

You will have to claculate the maximum donation for the income and test it
against the value that you accepted for donation.
Post some code you will get more help that way.

Hi Rob,

An accept stmt does NO checking. What you ask must be programmed by you. If the
I/P doesn't match your criteria, you issue an error msg and repeat the accept. 

Regards, Jack.


In article <9inelf$14q5@imsp212.netvigator.com>, R.L. says...
>
>Hi there,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

will have to be done after the ACCEPT (within your program).  Have you tried
this yet?  Post your code...

Dave

Ha, seems like a small well qualified committee of do gooders have found
that it's hard to do and satisfy everyone. If it reminds you of the workings
of congress, that's they way it is. Underlying problems forseen by someone
who has a slightly different problem are not visable. Can that person go
back and say, we have to change the way it is said because *** can't handle
it that way.

been a great way for you guys to work over the usual name calling and
bickering often present.  Now, if you will read the Tiny COBOL projects news
letter for awhile, you might get really good at this. Create your own wicki
wicki. <VBFG>

Warren

### Re: New Wording for EXIT rules.

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T05:23:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BHQ37.35749$C81.2913912@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0106292212.366496bb@posting.google.com> <3B3DFB51.7679F400@iinet.net.au> <tjt407g9fv626@corp.supernews.com> <3b3f186d_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <tjv3m8p1rogabb@corp.supernews.com> <3b405a26_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <ggZ%6.368$_z5.39046@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b407371_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Every once in awhile someone makes a suggestion that sounds good. It
involves doing something no others are doing. The TC group are interested in
help to "define" the product they are producing.
This is a good chance for the content of this news group to add constructive
effort to it's schooling and bickering agenda.  Writing a manual is not to
difficult, but who will accept it.  Should this group take on that task,
they will need a lot of help. Hot blood over the small stuff will get in the
way.

Cool your jets, and build a work group with a common goal.

Good luck.

Warren
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b407371_2@Usenet.com...

>

>
> Personally, I think it could be (and endeavoured in the examples I posted
to
> make it so) but, officially, it is a Specification of how the Language
> should behave, and is aimed primarily at Implementers (Compiler writers,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1. Explore privately the chance of setting up a User Based group to
develop
> our own "Standard" and see if any major vendors will endorse it, or
> acknowledge that their product complies with it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> All that is then needed is Vendor acknowledgement, and some major
Corporate
> sponsors. As they would all have input to the Forum and it would cost them
> nothing (apart from voluntary sponsorship to pay for the server and some
> consultancy in specialist areas) I don't see why they would object. It
would
> certainly be a great deal cheaper than it is at the moment.
>
> 2.  Failing that, simply forget the whole thing and trust the vendors to
> keep it alive in response to Market demand, with or without an "Official"
> standard. The official J4/Alphabet soup thing is going nowhere, and
deserves
> no credibility. This option means that there may not be platform
> independence but there isn't anyway if no standard is produced.

write

>Stiamo cercando programmatori con i profili di cui di seguito.

When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates,
associated with the position(s) offered; to do otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
derermine rates.

http://babelfish.altavista.com :

Nell' inviare a comp.lang.cobol includere prego un tasso, o la gamma di
tassi, connessa con la posizione(i) offerta; fare al contrario conduce 
molti concludere che o trolling per i resumes o funzionamento che un 
annuncio cieco al derermine valuta. 

DD
```

Area: southeast

seatle .......

#### ↳ Re: How to design report

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T17:44:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iqi42$iiq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com>`

```
It is my guess that this is a homework question - but I am not positive.

If this is a "real world" question, then check to see if your "compiler of
choice" supports Report Writer.  This is the type of report that is
INCREDIBLY easy to do in RW.  (It will let you do all the line/page breaks
you want for areas or sections, or whatever - and do your totaling for you).

If this is a homework assignment, then find out if you are allowed to use
report writer (and if the compiler in use for your class supports it).  If
so, that is still the way that I would go.

Either way, you might want to go to:

 http://www.spc-systems.com/

and check out the "Tutorial" section on how to use RW (in COBOL)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to design report

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T05:46:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B512E21.286C0B56@home.com>`
- **References:** `<f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com> <W2547.4068$JX4.493959@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <f2693105.0107142117.14f4792a@posting.google.com>`

arpan wrote:

> Hi every one,
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> I need a rough idea...theni will do ..

I'm assuming you must be pretty new - isn't there another local guy you can ask ? Are you comfortable with Tables
(Arrays).

Is the data you are reading in to do the report in a logical print sequence. It's a bit difficult looking at your first
example, but as a 'foreigner' I "know* that California and Seattle aren't in the South-East Area <G> You could put (or
accumulate) values into a 2 or 3-dimensional table. (MAKE SURE you INITIALIZE the Table first !), e.g. (1) Area, (2)
Region, (3) Total . So you would be moving values into say, (1, 3, 4). But this is purely guesswork from what you have
shown.

From there either move to print lines, or as Bill suggested, use Report Writer if available.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T12:49:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ljg47.1014$Ld1.48592@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com> <W2547.4068$JX4.493959@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <f2693105.0107142117.14f4792a@posting.google.com>`

```
arpan <shaily_g@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:f2693105.0107142117.14f4792a@posting.google.com...
> Hi every one,
>
>  It is not a homwork,I have to do it in my JOB....

Damn, I thought maybe the schools were finally wising up. Oh, well..

You will need to sort your data into something like this

01  DATA-RECORD.
      05  AREA            PIC X(05).
      05  REGION        PIC X(05)
      05  SALES          PIC S9(07)V99 USAGE IS COMP.

Read all your input, then sort the data on Area +  Region

Then read the sorted input, accumulating totals, and printing the
appropriate values on a control break

READ Sorted-Input      ( or RETURN it, if you use SORT, which is what I
would do)
  AT END
     SET End-of-Sorted-Input to true


PERFORM UNTIL End-Of-Sorted-input
  IF New Region and not start-of-sorted input
     (calculate the percentage lines and print)
  ELSE
     if new-city
       Extend the print line to the right
     else
       add the sales from this city record to the current city total, update
'grand total' accumulators
     end-if
   END -IF
   IF new-area
     print area headers
   end-if
   Read next sorted-input
      at end  set end-of-sorted-input to true
END -PERFORM


You will have to play with the control break logic, but once you get the
data sorted in control break order, that should come in a fairly
straightforward manner.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: How to design report

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-14T23:46:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ir6sc$kc773$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com>`

```

arpan <shaily_g@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com...
> I have to create a report in a format like
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pl help.

There are lots of ways to do something like this.  They depend mainly upon
what platform you are on (PC, mainframe, other), what compiler you are
using, what tools you have available (report writer, crystal reports, etc),
and what the input looks like.  Before anyone can really begin to help you,
we need to know these things.  You should also post what you have tried (or
thought about) and any relevant code.  Nobody here is going to do it for
you, but several will help...

Dave
```

```
Hi Arpan,

As others have said, the trick is to sort you data in the order that the pgm
logic requires.
You seemed troubled by the fact that each Area may differ in its # of regions.
I assume there are a finite # of regions that can fit across the page. If
they're variable within
an Area just move zeros or spaces to the unused regions.
If you need help with control breaks go to tek-tips.com and enter the general
cobol forum.
Check out a FAQ by Slade on cntl brks. It will give you an idea of how to
approach the coding. 


In article <f2693105.0107141100.218b1ebb@posting.google.com>, arpan says...
>
>I have to create a report in a format like 
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pl help.
```

## Thread: What is UPDATE option in Define Path? — 4 msgs

### What is UPDATE option in Define Path?

- **From:** "ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T22:45:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com>`

```
What is UPDATE option in Define Path?

Thanks,

Zalek
```

#### ↳ Re: What is UPDATE option in Define Path?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-15T18:12:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107151712.26a38aa9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com>`

```
"ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com>...
> What is UPDATE option in Define Path?
> 
> Thanks,
> 
> Zalek

As it says in the manual *Hint, hint*

UPDATE|NOUPDATE 
specifies whether the base cluster's upgrade set is to be allocated
when the path is opened for processing.


The upgrade set is a group of alternate indexes associated with the
base cluster. The alternate indexes are opened whenever the base
cluster is opened.

Stephen J Spiro
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is UPDATE option in Define Path?

- **From:** "ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T01:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7h4lt804affv3ehf4ob6e9oro84gmf7e4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com> <4145699b.0107151712.26a38aa9@posting.google.com>`

```
On 15 Jul 2001 18:12:53 -0700, stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro) wrote:

>"ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com>...
>> What is UPDATE option in Define Path?
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Stephen J Spiro

Yeeh, I read it too, but do you translate it to English?
What does it mean:
 "path is open for processing" and:
"upgrade set is to be allocated"?

To test it - I created a base cluster and AIX with option UPGRADE. Then I created a PATH
with option NOUPDATE (on this base cluster and AIX).
I wrote a small COBOL program that accessed the VSAM file by alternate index and updated
some records. Using FILEAID i looked on AIX - all updates were there.
But PATH was defined with NOUPDATE!!!

Any idea why it happened?

Thanks,

Zalek
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is UPDATE option in Define Path?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T02:03:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107160103.e32d7a0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vl74lt0qrise1itnsad8brm0f7l1t2u3oa@4ax.com> <4145699b.0107151712.26a38aa9@posting.google.com> <v7h4lt804affv3ehf4ob6e9oro84gmf7e4@4ax.com>`

```
"ZalekBloom@hotmail.com" <ZalekBloom@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<v7h4lt804affv3ehf4ob6e9oro84gmf7e4@4ax.com>...
> On 15 Jul 2001 18:12:53 -0700, stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro) wrote:
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> Zalek

*-----------------------
Yes, I know exactly what happened. And if you had really read what I
quoted IN THE MANUAL, you would have seen the complete explanation, in
plain English, including your case, IN THE NEXT FIVE PARAGRAPHS! You
did NOT read the manual, and I am pissed off enough that I'm not going
to give you the answer.

The one thing I WILL tell you, is that you tested it backwards.  An
action using an alternate index ALWAYS updates the base cluster,
regardless of options. And if you make a change while accessing on the
alternate index, of course the record is updated on the AIX.... Did
you really think the WRITE or REWRITE would be ignored if you made it
NOUPDATE? RTFM!

Stephen J Spiro
```

Just FYI--WinExec is a 16-bit compatibility call, the standard
replacement is CreateProcess.  Personally, I go for ShellExecute
as it can be used to open (or even print) documents as well.

### Re: Alternative Standard

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-16T19:04:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107161804.2ff66d47@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 
<snip>

> 
> This is what would be needed to establish an alternative COBOL Standard:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

<snip>

Ok, Pete, I got this for you.
Get in touch with

Dave Murphy, CEO
Tek-Tips Forums
http://www.tek-tips.com
dmurphy@tecumsehgroup.com
USA phone 800-954-7800 

Tek-Tips Forums will provide you with a FREE news group forum in his
system for your "user standard".  They aleady have a pretty good
general COBOL forum, which you might want to check out, to get a "look
and feel" for what he has.

800- is for toll-free incoming calls in the USA, I don't know if that
would apply to you. But you can feel free to e-mail him.

If you can get the process started, I told you in the beginning I
would contribute; I just do not have the personal time or resources to
organize it.

*-------
One more thing. At this point, I think I have done more to get YOUR
plan off the ground than all of your whining and name calling.  Bill
Klein and I have done far more than you to actually promote public
participation in the COBOL standards process.
Your continuous refences to the "COBOL elite" trying to keep the
process private are not merely ignorant, they are vicious.  Your
absurd claims that ISO/ANSI/ETC do this to make money from the sales
of standards are profoundly ignorant.  [They are supported by member
fees. Most standards are only a few pages (How big do you think is the
standard for PVC plumbing pipes?  Or mylar film? Or flat topped wood
screws?).]
I find that you constantly attribute bad motives to anyone who
disagrees with you. In spite of the fact that you are technically
outstanding and have many excellent, well organized ideas, I think you
will find that, unless you change your attitude, many people will work
with your plan only if they do not have to work with YOU.

Now, I've done the first major piece of work for you.... you have to
take it from here.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0107161804.2ff66d47@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote

Thanks for this Stephen. We already have several people offering the use of
a server but I'll certainly contact Mr Murphy and find out what is
available.

The availability of a server is just one of a number of things which would
need to be in place and some of these things are still being evaluated. A
decision will be taken at the end of the timebox allocated for this
activity. Currently,  this is just over 3 months away.

If we go with it, I would be pleased to have your support.


>>>
> *-------
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> participation in the COBOL standards process.

I never realised it was a "contest"... I could've done so much more!

> Your continuous refences to the "COBOL elite" trying to keep the
> process private are not merely ignorant, they are vicious.

Based on the snobbery in a number of posts from people who believe that a
panel of "Experts" are the right way to adminster COBOL. Even if they are
right (and I don't think they are) it hasn't worked. You keep forgetting
Stephen, NONE of this would ever have started if it didn't take 12 years to
produce a bloody document, and then have insult added to injury by being
told we should all be grateful and what a fantastic job it was...

Yes, I'm annoyed about it. (Heartsick actually...) so if my comments are
perceived as "vicious" it's because I am REALLY pissed off about it. And the
people concerned have never once said they were sorry, or, better yet, shown
any desire to change the processes and procedures that are in place.
(I understand there is some kind of "post mortem" now being planned. I
wonder if it would ever have happened if there was no CLC?)

Did you think I would suggest an Alternative just because I am a mean,
spiteful, vicious character who wants to minimise the intense effort put in
by the people involved? Or because I have nothing else to do?

I am doing so because I VEHEMENTLY disagree with the current set up. I
disagree with its principles, I disagree with its elitism, I disagree with
its "acceptance of submissions", I disagree with the poncey convoluted
pompous prose that permeates it and places the end product outside the reach
of the people who really should have been involved in its production.

> Your
> absurd claims that ISO/ANSI/ETC do this to make money from the sales
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> screws?).]

Well, you have obviously been wounded by what I wrote. I'm genuinely sorry
about that.  I am neither ignorant, whining, nor vicious, nor did I set out
to attack any individuals. This is the first time for quite a while that I
have ranted about this. I did so because of the tone of your post... I feel
better now <G>

I have been the subject of some pretty vicious stuff myself, but you don't
hear me complaining <G>

> I find that you constantly attribute bad motives to anyone who
> disagrees with you.

I hope not. But I'm not blind either. (And I know that the only reason I'm
paranoid is because you're all out to get me...<G>)


> In spite of the fact that you are technically
> outstanding and have many excellent, well organized ideas, I think you
> will find that, unless you change your attitude, many people will work
> with your plan only if they do not have to work with YOU.
>
First off, I don't see it as "my plan". I have proposed what I hope is a
better approach. It is for the people concerned to decide whether it is or
not. Mail is still coming in on it. It is not unanimously favouring what I
have proposed, but there is definitely a substantial majority in favour.

(As for people not working with me personally,many people have in the past,
and still do, work  with me. I can't deny attitude; but it is not for the
sake of being "nasty"...the majority of people find me good humoured and fun
to be around; I certainly have no shortage of friends...maybe when it comes
to COBOL we all get too serious...?)

As I have ALWAYS professed to be interested in WHAT's right and NOT WHO's
right, I have no problem with that at all. I don't care who they work with
as long as it gets better...

I would withdraw from this debate tomorrow if I felt that my presence was
making the attainment of the goals impossible. (Believe me, there are days
when I would LIKE to be somewhere else...<G>)

As you really don't know me Stephen, could I suggest that you are not really
qualified to judge?

Currently a number of people are very happy to assist the evaluation phase
of the exercise and no one seems worried or upset that I am co-ordinating
it. If I was impossible to work with, I'd stand down in a heartbeat. Maybe
your perception of me depends on what side of the fence you are
standing...<G>?

Stephen, all I can say is there is nothing in this for me personally. I'm
not looking for anything other than the chance for EVERYONE to be involved
in the production of the COBOL standard. This is controversial and it is
right and proper there will be debate about it. (Sometimes the rhetoric gets
acrimonious, but at the end of the day it is just that, rhetoric. The
underlying ideas stand or fall on their own merit.)

I'm not even sure that there needs to BE a standard, but I AM certain that
the current mechanism is not serving us well. I know you've been deeply
involved in it and it is hard for you to take criticism about it (have you
noticed that you tend to compensate by waving your own banner...? you were
one of the first who pissed me off by claiming what a wonderful job it was
<G> and you're doing it again in this post...<G> You didn't really think
that no one else was doing anything about the possibility of an Alternate
Standard, now did you?).

I'm not going to return your vitriol because I believe it stems from your
own frustration and tiredness and I refuse to take it personally.

> Now, I've done the first major piece of work for you.... you have to
> take it from here.
>
No, Stephen, I don't HAVE to do anything.

I WILL do what I stated publicly I intend to do; evaluate the possibility of
setting up a viable alternative during a four month period. There are still
people posting their thoughts on this, I still have a number of people to
see one-on-one and some of this can't happen until after CW2001.

If we go ahead and get  it off the ground your assistance would be extremely
welcome.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative Standard

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T15:41:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j4sck$n9l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0107161804.2ff66d47@posting.google.com> <393B9C89003B06BD.1302A97E74631F6B.23624FB9FAD32AF8@lp.airnews.net> <4145699b.0107171842.1fa18d28@posting.google.com> <9j2tt6$1v8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <atj57.23739$Kf3.319877@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Hopefully, I have never made ANY final statement on whether I think that it
would or would not be beneficial to have "any COBOL Standard".  I am still
quite undecided on this.

I do STRONGLY doubt that the ANSI/ISO NCITS/SC22  J4/WG4 bureaucracy has any
real chance of being reformed in a manner that would actually provide for a
meaningful and timely revision/enhancement process to the COBOL Language.

I do think that the X/Open "consortium" approach failed - as it was
implemented, but I am slightly drawn to this type of approach.  Similarly,
the days when "whatever IBM did with COBOL, everyone else copied" seemed to
work quite well for a couple of decades.

Alternatively, I still think that "market force" is really the way that the
TRUE "portable" COBOL language specification is done today, so I have some
difficulty in believing that it won't be the best way for the future
(changing from "de facto" to "de jure").  On the other hand, I am truly
interested in the proposal that Pete has come up with and have an open mind
on whether I think it can be implemented in a manner that will actually
work.

Thane,
  Does this answer your question?
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3b427d1b_6@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0107161804.2ff66d47@posting.google.com> <393B9C89003B06BD.1302A97E74631F6B.23624FB9FAD32AF8@lp.airnews.net> <4145699b.0107171842.1fa18d28@posting.google.com> <3b55506b_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <4145699b.0107180749.32b21927@posting.google.com>`

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0107180749.32b21927@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3b55506b_2@news2.newsgroups.com>...
>
> Let's try to move beyond this and do some good for our profession.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The voice of Reason...excellent!

Progress towards the stated goals is being made. Critical areas are the
opinion of the User Base (the whole thing is pointless if nobody cares <G>),
agreement in principle for Corporate sponsorship and at least one credible
Vendor to benchmark their product against the proposed Standard.

It would be very wrong of me to state who I am, or will be talking to, at
this point, any more than I will divulge the names of people who have
responded, or indicated they would like to be positively involved.
I can assure you that there are still a number of activities to be carried
out and some of them cannot happen until I am in the US in October.

I am not seeking to attract people who only want to be on the "winning" side
<G> (those who will "sign up" once they know there is a large base of
support). As I have said before, ideas stand or fall on their own merit.

What I CAN say is the following:

1. Mail is continuing to be received from the User Base on a daily basis. It
is not a flood and I can't claim an avalanche of support (but I wouldn't do
so even if I could <G>). It is fair to say that those who HAVE responded are
generally in favour of a more open empowered process. It is gratifying to
see that even those who cannot contribute time to it, still support the
IDEA.

I would take this opportunity of urging ALL who may be reading this to take
a few minutes and drop me a line, even (and especially) if you DON'T CARE
about the COBOL Standard and how it is produced, or feel it has no impact on
you. My request for feedback is reproduced here:

========================= posted under "Request for feedback" on 13/07/2001
===========
Although a number of people have responded to my request under the
"Alternate Standard" thread, I realise that a significant proportion of the
readership of this NG probably don't even look at this thread.

I really need to get a feel from the User Base so I am reproducing here the
PS from my last post in the above thread...

<quote>
PS to the Rest of You:

Please mail me with your thoughts, positive or negative. I need to get a
broader feel from the User Base as to how YOU feel about the standards
issue.

Is it really only important to the people involved in producing it? Even if
you feel it is of no relevance to you personally, drop me a line and say
that.

If you felt you could make a difference, would you participate?

Could/Would you contribute time to help run the public Forum? (edit prose,
count votes, etc).

Are you happy with what is being produced?

Are you happy with how it is produced?

(Maybe you don't care; tell me...<G>)

Over the past years, many of you have received help privately (and publicly)
from me. (Some of you even said "Thanks Pete..."<G>).

I am now asking for something from you.

Not your agreement to what I am proposing if you honestly don't support it.

Just your mark on a piece of electronic paper.

Just your input.

I would really value ALL inputs, and will respond to as many as I can. A
considerable amount of time and effort  (not JUST mine <G>) is going into
the exploration of an alternative. It would be ironic if no one really gives
a damn...(and funny <G>)

TIA,

Pete.
</quote>

Please post to me privately. You can reach me on:
dashwood@nospam.freenet.co.nz (remove the "nospam" before mailing)

If you don't speak now, don't complain later...<G>

Silence means you want no say in the future of COBOL.

That's a perfectly valid viewpoint, but some of us are spending time and
energy on your behalf and will stop doing so if the majority feel they don't
care.

Do me the courtesy of letting us know...<G>

Thanks,

Pete.
========================= end quoted "Request for Feedback
==================

At the end of the timebox, if there is not a significant response or the
other Key Success Factors cannot be attained, we would be forced to the
conclusion that no-one really cares about it (other than those who are
currently involved in preparing it) which means it is totally out of contact
with the User Base and probably irrelevant.

That being so, we can all go about our business and forget it. It is too
early to decide at this point (and the Methodology I am using requires the
timebox to run to its conclusion anyway). Like I said, mail is still coming
in.

2. If you really want to help, try discussing with your local vendor
representative how important they feel a Standard is. Is it more important
than Market feedback from individual Companies? In the Vendor's company,
what impact does the Standard have? How is it used ? (Is it "translated"
into program specs or do they implement directly from the Standard
document?) How would they feel about having a "Plain English" version that
could be used as the basis of their User Documentation? Who would be the
person in the vendor Company to approach on these issues?

In other words try and find out what is needed and wanted with regard to a
Standard document. This has to  be a better approach than simply sitting
down writing a document that is aimed at no-one in particular.

ALL feedback in this area must be valuable, whether the Alternative process
proceeds or not. I guess I'm saying that some "Market Research" should be
done, irrespective of what process we use to produce a Standard.

It would also help to publicise the fact that the whole process of Standard
production is being reviewed (whether you agree with what I'm proposing or
not). Make sure that everyone who has an interest in COBOL is aware of the
debate going on here and is encouraged to contribute to it.

Finally, ask yourself whether you are honestly prepared to accept what the
results turn up. If there really is no need for a standard can you accept
that?  If the majority of the User Base have been excluded so long that they
no longer care about it, can you accept that?

I can.

If the majority feel that J4/Alphabet soup is doing a good job, the problems
have been resolved, the new Standard is just what the Doctor ordered, I can
also accept that. (To do otherwise would be a bit like me trying to free
people who LIKE being enslaved...<G>) But it needs to be a clear message
from people who are NOT involved in producing it...<G>

Said it before and I say  it again: "It is NOT WHO's right, it is WHAT's
right...".

3. There is currently enough indication of support for an open, empowered,
moderated forum, using timeboxed, interactive iteration as the management
process, to encourage me to take the necessary remaining actions to bring it
into being. However, the final decision on it cannot (and will not) be taken
until the end of the current timebox.

Meanwhile, anyone who is genuinely interested in helping this effort should
indicate that to me by private mail. Moral support is very acceptable, as
well as tangible help...<G>

Pete.

right (and I don't think they are) it hasn't worked. You keep forgetting
Stephen, NONE of this would ever have started if it didn't take 12 years to
produce a bloody document, and then have insult added to injury by being
told we should all be grateful and what a fantastic job it was...

Yes, I'm annoyed about it. (Heartsick actually...) so if my comments are
perceived as "vicious" it's because I am REALLY pissed off about it. And the
people concerned have never once said they were sorry, or, better yet, shown
any desire to change the processes and procedures that are in place.
(I understand there is some kind of "post mortem" now being planned. I
wonder if it would ever have happened if there was no CLC?)

Did you think I would suggest an Alternative just because I am a mean,
spiteful, vicious character who wants to minimise the intense effort put in
by the people involved? Or because I have nothing else to do?

I am doing so because I VEHEMENTLY disagree with the current set up. I
disagree with its principles, I disagree with its elitism, I disagree with
its "acceptance of submissions", I disagree with the poncey convoluted
pompous prose that permeates it and places the end product outside the reach
of the people who really should have been involved in its production.

> Your
> absurd claims that ISO/ANSI/ETC do this to make money from the sales
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> screws?).]

Well, you have obviously been wounded by what I wrote. I'm genuinely sorry
about that.  I am neither ignorant, whining, nor vicious, nor did I set out
to attack any individuals. This is the first time for quite a while that I
have ranted about this. I did so because of the tone of your post... I feel
better now <G>

I have been the subject of some pretty vicious stuff myself, but you don't
hear me complaining <G>

> I find that you constantly attribute bad motives to anyone who
> disagrees with you.

I hope not. But I'm not blind either. (And I know that the only reason I'm
paranoid is because you're all out to get me...<G>)


> In spite of the fact that you are technically
> outstanding and have many excellent, well organized ideas, I think you
> will find that, unless you change your attitude, many people will work

First off, I don't see it as "my plan". I have proposed what I hope is a
better approach. It is for the people concerned to decide whether it is or
not. Mail is still coming in on it. It is not unanimously favouring what I
have proposed, but there is definitely a substantial majority in favour.

(As for people not working with me personally,many people have in the past,
and still do, work  with me. I can't deny attitude; but it is not for the
sake of being "nasty"...the majority of people find me good humoured and fun
to be around; I certainly have no shortage of friends...maybe when it comes
to COBOL we all get too serious...?)

As I have ALWAYS professed to be interested in WHAT's right and NOT WHO's
right, I have no problem with that at all. I don't care who they work with
as long as it gets better...

I would withdraw from this debate tomorrow if I felt that my presence was
making the attainment of the goals impossible. (Believe me, there are days
when I would LIKE to be somewhere else...<G>)

As you really don't know me Stephen, could I suggest that you are not really
qualified to judge?

Currently a number of people are very happy to assist the evaluation phase
of the exercise and no one seems worried or upset that I am co-ordinating
it. If I was impossible to work with, I'd stand down in a heartbeat. Maybe
your perception of me depends on what side of the fence you are
standing...<G>?

Stephen, all I can say is there is nothing in this for me personally. I'm
not looking for anything other than the chance for EVERYONE to be involved
in the production of the COBOL standard. This is controversial and it is
right and proper there will be debate about it. (Sometimes the rhetoric gets
acrimonious, but at the end of the day it is just that, rhetoric. The
underlying ideas stand or fall on their own merit.)

I'm not even sure that there needs to BE a standard, but I AM certain that
the current mechanism is not serving us well. I know you've been deeply
involved in it and it is hard for you to take criticism about it (have you
noticed that you tend to compensate by waving your own banner...? you were
one of the first who pissed me off by claiming what a wonderful job it was
<G> and you're doing it again in this post...<G> You didn't really think
that no one else was doing anything about the possibility of an Alternate
Standard, now did you?).

I'm not going to return your vitriol because I believe it stems from your
own frustration and tiredness and I refuse to take it personally.

> Now, I've done the first major piece of work for you.... you have to
> take it from here.
>
No, Stephen, I don't HAVE to do anything.

I WILL do what I stated publicly I intend to do; evaluate the possibility of
setting up a viable alternative during a four month period. There are still
people posting their thoughts on this, I still have a number of people to
see one-on-one and some of this can't happen until after CW2001.

If we go ahead and get  it off the ground your assistance would be extremely
welcome.

Pete.

```

#### ↳ R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

- **From:** "cuckn" <cuckn@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T08:39:41+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com>`

```
You must create a new cobol program. Declare your old file (.dat) for input
and the new text file for output, so rewrite the new from the old. An
example:

program-id. newTextFile.
       environment division.
       configuration section.
       special-names.
    decimal-point is comma
    cursor is cursore
    copy 'tasto.sna '.
       input-output section.
       file-control.

    select olfFile assign to "oldFile.dat"
    organization is indexed.

    select newFile assign to "newFile.txt"
    organization is line sequential.

       data division.
       file section.

    fd  oldFile label record standard.
       01  rec-oldFile.
        02 cod                              pic 9(9).
        02 name                            pic x(35).

       fd  newFile label record omitted.
       01  rec-newFile                              pic x(44).

       working-storage section.

 procedure division.

    open input oldFile
    open output newfile.

readOldFile.
    read OldFile next
      at end
      go stop run.

write rec-newFile from rec-oldFile
go read OldFile






Silas Wu <silasw@tasan.com.hk> wrote in message
3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com...
> Hi,
>
> I have a customer using RmCobol 85 (Dos Version). They needed to convert
all
> the existing data file ( the file extension is .dat)  to text file for new
> system. Would anyone know where can I find the tools to do that?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T08:51:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
On Tue, 17 Jul 2001 08:39:41 +0200, "cuckn" <cuckn@libero.it> wrote:
>
>    fd  oldFile label record standard.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
....
>
>write rec-newFile from rec-oldFile
>go read OldFile
>
Although the main logic should be as stated, do not forget that the
COBOL file may have some numeric date in a format that should be
converted before writting to the new file.

so the correct thing to do unless the final system understands the
COBOL numeric format, or if the file in question only has alphnumeric
or numeric edited fields is as follows.

    fd  oldFile label record standard.
       01  rec-oldFile.
        02 cod                              pic 9(9) COMP-3.
        02 name                             pic x(35).
        02 maxcredit                        pic S9(9) COMP-3.

    fd  newFile label record omitted.
       01  rec-newFile                              pic x(60).

       working-storage section.

  01 new-record.
        02 w-cod                              pic 9(9).
        02 w-name                             pic x(35).
        02 w-maxcredit                        pic -9(9).
....

     move cod to w-cod
     move name to w-name
     move maxcredit to w-maxcredit
     write rec-newFile from new-record.
     go read OldFile


Frederico Fonseca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T18:24:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com> <2s_47.22490$Kf3.292704@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Better still - define the two records (in and out) with the same names and use
> Move with Corresponding....
>

Just curious - one of the first things you pick up when reading COBOL - ALTER an
absolute No-No, (well, it's out now anyway) and like you can *have* some problems
with COMPUTE on intermediary or different field sizes, same goes for MOVE
CORRESPONDING - some texts tell you to beware.

One other point, taking Frederico's example. Assuming you were outputting an
Ascii-delimited(CSV) with commas separating fields. The MOVE CORRESPONDING syntax
states IN-field1 will get moved to OUT-field-1, IN-field2 to OUT-field2 etc., as
though you had specified separate MOVES.

Based on cautionary notes in texts, I've never attempted MOVE CORRESPONDING.

Any comments ?

Jimmy

>
> In article <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com>, Frederico Fonseca
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Frederico Fonseca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T13:47:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j21am$ehp$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com> <2s_47.22490$Kf3.292704@www.newsranger.com> <3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com>`

```
My experience (PC and mainframe - legacy and new - systems) is that
CORRESPONDING is almost as "religious" an issue as SECTION versus
PARAGRAPH - and with as little "conclusive" evidence on either side.

It CLEARLY allows for "maintenance" errors - if you don't use COPY members
for the defined items.  All you need to do is add a field or a level to "get
in trouble".  On the other hand (ESPECIALLY for "quick and dirty" programs)
it can be a REAL time saver.  When used with COPY statements (with or
without REPLACING), it is medium-easily maintained.

There was a (false) rumor many years ago about it being "removed" from the
Standard (due to its removal - and subsequent re-appearing) in the CODASYL
JOD.  However, there is NO current ANSI/ISO J4/WG4 interest in even
"denigrating" it.

One thought (and I will admit that I haven't ever REALLY done this) is to
put a COMMENT in the record-description of the sending/receiving fields
making it CLEAR that they are used in CORR statements.  This might alert a
future maintenance programmer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T21:00:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b555404_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com> <2s_47.22490$Kf3.292704@www.newsranger.com> <3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com...
>
> Based on cautionary notes in texts, I've never attempted MOVE
CORRESPONDING.
>
> Any comments ?
>
I used to use it and it was useful. IBM also implemented ADD...CORRESPONDING
which was also useful...

Like so many things in COBOL, there is often more than one way to achieve
your goal and the specifc method chosen depends on factors at the time you
are coding (pressure to get it out, maintenance, efficiency if you still
care, and so on...

There is ALWAYS an alternative to either ADD or MOVE... CORRESPONDING (do
individual MOVES or ADDS) but sometimes they can save time.  (Especially
where LARGE record layouts with many fields are involved).

In these days of on-line editors when we have the ability to quickly cut and
paste ("clone") source code it is maybe less important than in the days when
every line of code had to be carved into a cardboard card...

Why did I stop using them?

Well, I found the necessity to qualify fields in the remainder of my code,
more than offset the time I saved with the MOVE or ADD... CORRESPONDING in
the first place!

Remember, you can use duplicate names in COBOL (and you MUST if you intend
to use the CORRESPONDING options...<G>) but then you must qualify every
subsequent reference to EITHER of the Duplicated names....

So you end up coding "field1 of input-rec", "field1 of output-rec"  and so
on for every one of the fields...tedious. As I have observed elsewhere,
patience does not come naturally to me (I have to work at it) so I pretty
soon got fed up with doing this...

Hence, today I clone the moves and adds and use unique names.

(Hey! you asked for comments...<G>)

Pete.

In article <3b555404_1@news2.newsgroups.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood says...
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>which was also useful...

It's in the standard as well (has been) and is implemented in other standards
compliant compilers.

>
>In these days of on-line editors when we have the ability to quickly cut and
>paste ("clone") source code it is maybe less important than in the days when

With these modern editors that should be no problem or slowdown.  

However - I agree, I dislike having to use qualification so I avoid it.  But for
FULL record moves using corresponding (for me) helps avoid the typo I am sure to
make where I move date-1 to date-2 when coding the individual moves...


>So you end up coding "field1 of input-rec", "field1 of output-rec"  and so
>on for every one of the fields...tedious. As I have observed elsewhere,
>patience does not come naturally to me (I have to work at it) so I pretty
>soon got fed up with doing this...

And that's why - you use the best tool for the job.  The conversion of the file
from Indexed to Sequential is an ideal place to use this.  Places where you
reference individual fields of the record, directly, in many locations isn't the
place to use this.  

However, just not using it at ALL seems to be overkill to me.

>
>Hence, today I clone the moves and adds and use unique names.
>
>(Hey! you asked for comments...<G>)

So he did.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T09:10:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j4cd7$cvv$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com> <2s_47.22490$Kf3.292704@www.newsranger.com> <3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com> <3b555404_1@news2.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b555404_1@news2.newsgroups.com...

> I used to use it and it was useful. IBM also implemented
ADD...CORRESPONDING
> which was also useful...

This is, and has been, a feature of the standards since *at least* ANSI
X3.23-1974 (albeit at Nucleus Level 2).  Any compiler claiming *full*
conformance with that standard (or with the '85 standard) at the highest
level of specification supports ADD .. CORRESPONDING, as well as SUBTRACT
... CORRESPONDING.   See ANSI X3.23-1974 pages II-55 and II-89, and ANSI
X3.23-1985 pages VI-73 and VI-134.   I don't have a copy of the '68 standard
to check, but secondary evidence (the lack of any indication that this
documented feature is an implementor extension in the documentation for
COBOL '68 from two different vendors) leads me to believe these variants
were part of that standard as well.

ADD ... CORRESPONDING is not an IBM extension, it's been standard COBOL for
a very long time.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-cbb2a25d5f"></a>
## Thread: COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT — 6 msgs

### COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT

- **From:** liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak)
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T02:03:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>`

```
A am getting the following compiler error on Fujitsu.  Cobol version is COBOL 85.

"REVERSED PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT"  for the statement below;

--->  START PARAM-FILE KEY IS <= PARAM-RECORD-KEY   

Does anyone know what this is trying to tell me.

/john
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T00:54:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b543b76_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>`

```
John

Trying to establish position with a relational operator involving "LESS
THAN" is pointless for a file which is in ascending sequence.  You will
always return the first record on the file, or no record.

Fujitsu recognise this and offer an extension which allows ISAM files to be
in DESCENDING sequence, and in this case it makes sense to issue a
positioning START with a relational operator which includes "<".

However, if you use such a relational operator you must use the REVERSED
phrase with your START.

Think again about what you are trying to do... If the file is indeed in
ascending sequence (and every ISAM file I have ever dealt with was) then it
is illogical to issue a "START  <="

Imagine:  Start <= 'A'        gets first record on file, or none if there
are no As
                 Start <= 'B'        gets first record on file or none if
there is nothing prior to C. It doesn't get the first B, it gets the first
record on file...probably an A if there is one.
                 Start <= 'G'        Gets first record on file (probably A
if there is one) or none if there is nothing prior to H.

Although you may have intended to start at the beginning of the Bs or the
Gs, you will always be returned the first record on the file (which will be
an A if there are any, or whatever...)

Hope this helps,

Pete.


"John Liptak" <liptakj@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com...
> A am getting the following compiler error on Fujitsu.  Cobol version is
COBOL 85.
>
> "REVERSED PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT"  for the statement
below;
>
> --->  START PARAM-FILE KEY IS <= PARAM-RECORD-KEY
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> /john

```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T11:38:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378AFE2DE3299A49.F8CE590FF240DD27.774D119BB51E9CD0@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>`

```
On 17 Jul 2001 02:03:20 -0700, liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak)
enlightened us:

>A am getting the following compiler error on Fujitsu.  Cobol version is COBOL 85.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>/john

Pete has responded with the long answer.  The short answer is, you
cannot do a START with a LESS THAN operator.  You can use EQUAL or
GREATER THAN or GREATER THAN OR EQUAL only.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Clones are people two.


Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T19:32:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107171832.56824f73@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>`

```
liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak) wrote in message news:<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>...
> A am getting the following compiler error on Fujitsu.  Cobol version is COBOL 85.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> /john

*----------------------
The "less than" conditions on START are implementor extensions, where
they exist at all.
Pete has explained how it works on Fujitsu (your case). Here is how it
works in IBM (NOT the mainfrme, where it has not yet been implemented
at all):

X >_Workstation_> Under AIX and Windows, the following relational
operators
X are allowed in the KEY phrase: 


 X LESS THAN                               < 
 X NOT GREATER THAN                        NOT > 
 X LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO                   <=        <_Workstation_< 




X If you specify the KEY to be 'less than', or 'less than or equal to'
the
X data item, the file position indicator is positioned to the last
logical
X record currently existing in the file satisfying the comparison. 



X For an indexed file, if the key that satisfies the comparison has 
X duplicate entries, the file position indicator is positioned to the
last
X of these entries. 

(The x's indicate an IBM extension to the standard.)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
In the proposed new standard, the "less than" relations WILL be
allowed. These are the rules:

For Relative files:
b) If the relational operator is LESS, NOT GREATER, or LESS OR EQUAL,
the file position indicator is set to the relative record number of
the first logical record in the file whose key satisfies the
comparison searching the file in reverse order.

For indexed files:
d) If the relational operator is LESS, NOT GREATER,or LESS OR EQUAL,
the file is searched in reverse order

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: Get involved in the standards process! Get your company to send
you to 3-star hotels in the suburbs, where you can argue grammar and
usage for 8 hours a day!
```

- **Date:** 2001-07-18T20:22:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b554cd2_2@news2.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0107171832.56824f73@posting.google.com>`

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL "REVERSED" PHRASE MUST BE SPECIFIED IN START STATMENT

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T08:00:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B55341B.8018F0EC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0107170103.3fb8eef3@posting.google.com>`

```
John Liptak wrote:
> 
> A am getting the following compiler error on Fujitsu.  Cobol version is COBOL 85.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anyone know what this is trying to tell me.

It is trying to tell you that for the '<=' comparison to work you must
specify 'REVERSED'.

If you don't want to read reversed then you should specify 'NOT <' or
'>='.
```

<a id="t-4b6123bd60"></a>
## Thread: What is the clause COMP ? — 9 msgs

### What is the clause COMP ?

- **From:** "cuckn" <cuckn@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T14:40:53+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
example

.......
......
cod       pic 9(9) COMP-3
.....
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the clause COMP ?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-17T11:46:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EECCD9E335BDBE3B.B8E57AFEF46DF65A.03B0A789D6D08F32@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
On Tue, 17 Jul 2001 14:40:53 +0200, "cuckn" <cuckn@libero.it>
enlightened us:

>example
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What platform?  It depends.  If this is IBM mainframe OS/390 Cobol,
then COMP-3  is packed decimal format.  One byte holds two values
except the last byte which holds 1 value and the sign (assuming all
other options are standard).

For example, assuming you had the number +6880976..in display format
it would look like F6F8F8F0F9F7F6.  If you were to move this number to
cod, it would be converted to packed decimal and would look like this:

006880976F (it could also look like 006880976C if the compiler
generates a ZAP instruction to do the move or does an OI on the last
byte).  The size of a packed decimal field is the number of digits
divided by 2 plus 1.  You should always use an odd number of digits to
define your packed decimal fields.

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Clones are people two.


Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```

http://www.flexus.com/download.html

Get file COBDATA.ZIP
