#### ↳ Re: What is the clause COMP ?

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T14:32:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M5h57.23557$Kf3.315900@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
In article <9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>, cuckn says...
>
One of the most common comp or oomp-3 formats is created using some technique to
"pack" numbers to save space.  normally a one digit number takes up the space
required for a single character.  When you pack a number, the space is devided
in half.  Half of the first byte is used for the sign.  Then after that each
byte is capable of holding 2 numeric digits.

pic 9(1) comp-3    = 1 Character
pic 9(3) comp-3    = 2 Characters
pic 9(5) comp-3    = 3 Characters

Normally if you are packing numbers you use an odd amount of digits to use the
entire byte. Half a byte by itself isnt worth much.  Many compilers can not
access just half a byte.  Some languages like Assembly may be able to access the
left or right side of the packed bytes.  Some compilers have different data
types like binary, character, zoned decimal(number), packed(number), boolean,
etc, and these are referred to as comp-? to indicate a non character-field.
How the compiler implements the packing may be different.

>example
>cod       pic 9(9) COMP-3
>.....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the clause COMP ?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T09:47:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B55BDAD.3680@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9j1avn$gca$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <M5h57.23557$Kf3.315900@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Charles wrote:

> One of the most common comp or oomp-3 formats is created using some technique to
> "pack" numbers to save space.  normally a one digit number takes up the space
> required for a single character.  When you pack a number, the space is devided
> in half.  Half of the first byte is used for the sign.  Then after that each
> byte is capable of holding 2 numeric digits.

For clarity (because I know which end you are counting from, but somebody might not) 
that would be the low order half of the low order byte.
```

### Cobol migration

- **From:** "andrea bizzocchi" <andrew_63@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T13:11:02+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j3q83$ds7$1@nreadB.inwind.it>`

```
hello!!
I would like to know if exist a utility to convert MSCOBOL into FUJITSU
COBOL or VISUALAGE IBM COBOL.
If possible free.
Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol migration

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T09:40:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b55a152$1_1@feed1.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<9j3q83$ds7$1@nreadB.inwind.it>`

```
Our DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit could be used for this purpose.
It is generalized compiler technology for carrying out automated
custom analyses/enhancements/translations of software sources.
DMS already knows COBOL VS II, and can easily be taught dialects,
so handling MSCOBOL and Fujitsu COBOL should be straightforward.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
Especially look for the slides on porting software under "Applications of
DMS".
```

```
Siber Systems has developed the converter that you need.

It is called MF2FSC but one of the dialects it accepts is MS Cobol.
Just specify -lang=ms

Fujitsu also sells it under their name but
if you want faster/better support 
you can talk to us.

http://www.siber.com/sct/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

## Thread: COBOL Report Writer Documentation — 7 msgs

### COBOL Report Writer Documentation

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-18T08:52:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B55B0B6.174B42CC@ibm.net>`

```
Does anyone know of a softcopy (BookManager, Adobe Acrobat Reader, etc.)
COBOL Language Manual that our programmers in Northern Ireland could
download from the Web, or otherwise obtain, that would contain
documentation of the COBOL Report Writer feature?

My company is using these programmers to do a lot of conversion of OS/VS
COBOL programs to COBOL for OS/390 & VM.  We have roughly 800 programs
that contain Report Writer code (out of tens of thousands).

In California, we have old copies of OS/VS COBOL and even MicroFocus
COBOL language manuals that contain documentation of this feature, but
newer IBM manuals that are easily obtainable on the Web don't include
the feature, because IBM dropped it when the 1985 ISO / ANSI Standard
made it "optional" for a "Full" compiler.

Please reply to "ccampbe2@csc.com", and many thanks in advance.
Colin Campbell
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer Documentation

- **Message-ID:** `<3B55CA4F.44A084C2@certcoinc.com>`
- **References:** `<3B55B0B6.174B42CC@ibm.net>`

```
Colin you might try http://WWW.SPC-SYSTEMS.COM/ they are the ones marketing
the Report Writer precompiler now.  Otherwise I have an IBM manual
(SC26-4301-04) COBOL Report Writer Precompiler Programmers Manual.  I don't
know if it is available on the web though.

PatH
```

## Thread: Pseudocode — 10 msgs

### Pseudocode

- **From:** "R.L." <roblau@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T16:20:44+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
Hi there,
I am trying to write cobol.  The following is the pseudocode that I write
for my program.  I want to know is the format correct or not?  Could you
please correct me if there is any mistake. Thank you.  My email is
roblau@hongkong.com

      Write heading line
      Input the amount of Annual Income
      Input the amount of Donation
      Compute Chargeable Income = Annual Income - Allowance - Donation
      If Chargeable Income <= 0
            Tax Payable = 0
If Chargeable Income  > 105000
     Tax Payable = 7350 + (Chargeable Income -105000) * .17
If Chargeable Income  > 70000 and Chargeable Income <= 105000
 Tax Payable = 3150 + (Chargeable Income - 70000) * .12
If Chargeable Income > 35000 and Chargeable Income  <= 70000
 Tax Payable = 700 + (Chargeable Income - 35000) * .07
If Chargeable Income  > 0 and Chargeable Income <= 35000
 Tax Payable = CI * .02
Write Tax Payable
Stop run
```

```
>

I haven't written any Pseudo Code since I took a basic programming logic course.
However, Yours would have failed our course standards, because we were required
to use an "IF, THEN, ENDIF" formal Pseudo Code Standard.

In your case you have several if statements that test for amounts of income in
decreasing amounts that lets the flow of the code just drop thru.  You may want
to Consider using an If, Else If, Else Structure.  That way it Skips over the
unused portion of the code.  For COBOL language, Evaluate may be appropriate.

>Hi there,
>I am trying to write cobol.  The following is the pseudocode that I write
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudocode

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T13:02:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B572ECA.2F3CD8CE@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com> <XEF57.807$Y47.9364@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Charles wrote:

> I haven't written any Pseudo Code since I took a basic programming logic course.
> However, Yours would have failed our course standards, because we were required
> to use an "IF, THEN, ENDIF" formal Pseudo Code Standard.

Periodically, I see or use pseudo/code, often written on a white board.  It is
useful when going over a program design with a beginner programmer.  I have never
seen pseudo-code standards though - it is just as an aid to work through program
flow.
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudocode

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T14:50:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com> <XEF57.807$Y47.9364@www.newsranger.com> <3B572ECA.2F3CD8CE@cusys.edu>`

```
I thought the programming looked like a homework assignment myself.

So I assumed the teacher would want to see the "ENDIF". So I mentioned using the
"ENDIF" to indicate where the "IF" ended.  Since it is pseudo-code, you can not
just end it with a period like COBOL.  It looks like a bunch of "IF, THEN"
statements with no clear ending.  I did make a suggestion to use "IF, ELSE IF,
ELSE" however.  

It isn't wrong to use several "IF, THEN" statements, it just causes the program
to check them all.  Normally if you have historical information, it is best also
to use the most popular choice first; however, this would throw the the flow of
the logic off a bit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudocode

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T12:02:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0107201102.6cb49310@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com> <XEF57.807$Y47.9364@www.newsranger.com> <3B572ECA.2F3CD8CE@cusys.edu> <izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Charles <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com>...
> I thought the programming looked like a homework assignment myself.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the logic off a bit.

I used to prefer that and if the "if test" is easy to understand then
I will go that route.  My first preference is to make the if test easy
to comprehend.  I avoid not logic in the if test like the plague.  I
mostly end up using not logic in the "if test" when I am working with
existing code and not using the not logic would cause a fair amount
more work then it is worth.

Now to the main point of the thread.  I rarely pseudo-code.  I usually
take a stab at it straight away as code and some time debugging.  If
the debugging is not getting anywhere then I will step back
pseudo-code the process and run some thought experiments with the
pseudo-code.  Then I will re-code.

Pseudo-code is a thing that usually defies standardization, it is
primarily meant as an aid to the original coder of code.

ttyl
KA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudocode

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T20:29:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Dl67.2373$ar1.6415@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com> <XEF57.807$Y47.9364@www.newsranger.com> <3B572ECA.2F3CD8CE@cusys.edu> <izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com> <677e6c81.0107201102.6cb49310@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Kano,

While I agree with you re. "nots" in IF stmts, I also feel that they're
warrented when the reader thinks in terms of "not" in the context 
of the IF or if a "not" makes the point more clearly, e.g.:

IF NOT TASTY SAY 'UGH' makes the point more clearly than IF TASTY CONTINUE ELSE
SAY 'UGH'.

Granted, it's hair splitting, but most of these elements of style are. It's easy
to say "Yeah, yeah, but I still like my way." It takes 
a real committment to honesty to evaluate the value of these issues because it's
very close to what those Rennaisance Philos-
ophers (or was it the Middle Ages?) used to do when they engaged in discussions
of how many Angels could dance on the head
of a pin.  

Regards, Jack.  


In article <677e6c81.0107201102.6cb49310@posting.google.com>, Kano Ako says...
>
>Charles <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com>...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>KA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudocode

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T13:20:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5EC7C1.89713A4A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9j65e7$sjc7@imsp212.netvigator.com> <XEF57.807$Y47.9364@www.newsranger.com> <3B572ECA.2F3CD8CE@cusys.edu> <izX57.875$ar1.3619@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Charles wrote:

> I thought the programming looked like a homework assignment myself.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the logic off a bit.

Typically, I recommend  pseudo code with IF statements such as his.  But don't
convert pseudo code line by line to CoBOL.  You have the flow and idea, write the
code right!
```

```
long running debate here about this when V6 was first released.
```

## Thread: Flooding Computer Room? — 15 msgs

### Flooding Computer Room?

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T17:59:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j775m$jgl$1@news.netmar.com>`

```
I was wondering if anyone else ever has problems with their computer
facilities getting seepage from rain.  We have been mopping up for 2 hours. 


To top that off, we were installing a new mainframe and had 220 power cords on
the floor. We had to move them first.

I had this vision of being zapped in a pool of water with a mop in my hand.

You can never underestimate janitorial skills.  That is something I will have
to put on my resume.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flooding Computer Room?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T22:29:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B57C1D6.68A1@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9j775m$jgl$1@news.netmar.com> <3B574116.E30B0573@boeing.com>`

```
Susan Allen wrote:
> 
> chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> up any of the messes)

Plumber turned off water supply to air-to-water heat exchanger air conditioner on a 
Friday night to make a repair.  Forgot to turn it back on.  Well over 40 Celsius in the 
room by Monday AM.  Cabinets were almost too hot to touch.

Amazingly everything was still working, however IBM had been pitching an upgrade to us 
so we VERY quickly bought it. We also installed some environmental monitoring equipment 
with auto-dialers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flooding Computer Room?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T21:25:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Wk167.1382$Px1.147858@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9j775m$jgl$1@news.netmar.com> <3B574116.E30B0573@boeing.com> <3B57C1D6.68A1@paralynx.com>`

```
Yeah, we had two air conditioners servicing the computer room. One had
a thermocouple that refused to allow the AC to start if the outside
temperature was below freezing.

One night the temperature dropped. The computer room, however, was
VERY toasty. So toasty, in fact, that the mainframe
thermal-overloaded.

Said the operator when asked whether he noticed the room was over 100
degrees Fahrenheit: "Man, I had to get the work out!" The follow-up
question regarding why it didn't occur to him to open the goddamn
outside door elicited blank stares and much scratching of his naughty
bits (slight drool evident also).

Some people are fit only for third-shift computer operators and
medical experimentation.
```

```
About 12 years ago, I was on a contract with Nabisco in New Jersey. I
was on the telephone with the data center near San Francisco, when the
guy on the other end said, "I have to leave know, we're having an
earthquake!"  It was that big one that wrecked buildings and roads
from LA to north of Frisco....   They all stood outside while the
building shook.... very surprisingly, no interruptions to service, and
no significant damage to the facility.

Stephen J Spiro
```

```
I worked at Dana Perfumes in Mountaintop one summer; stayed in a hotel
in Wilkes Barre.  Yes, the whole area has not recovered from the
coal-mining days;  it's about the northern end of Appalachia.  "Sad"
is a good word.  Even across the river in Whitewater, it seemed to be
stuck in the thirties.

Stephen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flooding Computer Room?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T19:23:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XEk67.1405$ym4.50549@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<9j775m$jgl$1@news.netmar.com> <4145699b.0107201950.5ea8baa6@posting.google.com> <QAf67.1339$ym4.48283@iad-read.news.verio.net> <4145699b.0107210934.5160a3b6@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <4145699b.0107210934.5160a3b6@posting.google.com>,
Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote in message news:<QAf67.1339$ym4.48283@iad-read.news.verio.net>...
>> In article <4145699b.0107201950.5ea8baa6@posting.google.com>,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>stuck in the thirties.

You picked up on that, too?  I noticed that when I was in South Bend, IN
folks would talk about the Good Old Days and mean the late 1950's - early
1960's; in Wilkes-Barre the same phrase of Good Old Days referred to...
sometime during Prohibition.

That isn't what I intended by 'sad place', though... it was supposed to
indicate that Nabisco was the sad place.  I may have mentioned this before
but... In The Beginning there was Standard Brands; they made cookies and
crackers and peanut-snacks and lo, all were satisfied.  Then... in the
span of about a dozen or so years... Standard Brands was bought out by
Nabisco, Nabisco was bought out by R J Reynolds and R J Reynolds was
bought out by KKR (about which the book 'Barbarians At The Gates' was
written).

With every buyout, of course, things got 'rightsized'... in other words,
folks got fired.  By the time I got there the only folks left were the
most socially adept ones... they were jovial, pleasant people, full of
goodwill... and every smile was tinged with 'See, I'm a nice person and a
good Team Player... please, *please* don't fire me!'

In terms of technical ability... they weren't worth a bucket of warm spit,
either.

Sad.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flooding Computer Room?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-22T00:35:46-07:00

#### ↳ Re: Flooding Computer Room?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>

```
How about thith one? I was part of a committee that reccommended installation of
a UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) System
for a major NYC bank. My job was to reccommend the H/W, determine the critical
apps to be serviced, and estimate the project 
costs. Thank God (for me)  installation was handled, at their insistance, by
facilities mgmt. It cost between 4 & 5 mil.  

The day came when we had a flood and the only areas affected were those
connected to the UPS!  Apparently the FM group 
installed the UPS System directly below the cafeteria washing machines, the site
of the water main break that shorted the UPS!

The best laid plans of mice and men.

Regards, Jack.
```

### Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-19T12:05:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Terry Heinze wrote:

>     Having written code and maintained others' code for 25 years, I think
> I've seen about every kind of style and personal preference there is.  I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> This has serious implications.  :)

I don't understand - if your GO TO's don't branch out of a performed paragraph,
then they must branch to the top of the existing paragraph.   Do you believe
that is a good coding practice?   I have never seen it in anything close to what
I would call structured code, and have never considered it (I have been
programming longer than you have, but we may have started structured programming
at the same time).

As I like full stop periods and haven't seen a good reason to eliminate them, I
will entertain a notion that your GO TO statements aren't automatically bad,
just because the only times I have seen them have been in spaghetti code
programs.   But I am not coming up with a scenario where I would be tempted to
use them.
```

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T03:16:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu>`

```
    The types of GO TOs I'm referring to are as follows:
.
.
.
       PERFORM M10-LOAD-TABLE THRU M10-X
.
.
.
M10-LOAD-TABLE.
       (do some initializing stuff)
       .
M10-GET-NEXT.
       READ INPUT-FILE
           AT END
               GO TO M10-END
       END-READ
       SET TBL-NDX            UP BY 1
       MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
       ADD 1                          TO TBL-SIZE
       GO TO M10-GET-NEXT
        .
M10-END.
       DISPLAY 'TABLE SIZE IS ' TBL-SIZE
        .
M10-X.
       EXIT
       .
.
.
.
    I left out much of the validity checking, but you get the picture.
There is only one entrance and one exit to the "Table Load" function.  Note
the identical prefix for all the paragraphs.  I use this convention to
indicate that the paragraphs all belong to the same function.  This table
load function could be written in at least a half-dozen different ways, all
of which would be readable and maintainable.  I'm just saying that some
things are, in my opinion, a matter of personal preference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T05:50:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MEP57.114$LP2.16961@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
    I also do this sort of thing.  So long as you
insure that ALL paragraphs in the range start with the
same prefix, and always use the same ending (-X here),
you should have few problems.

    Of course, it is not perfect.  An easy place to go wrong
is the go to after the read, that checks the file status for a
successfull read.  That go to (not in this sample)  might
 never be used during testing or normal use, and can easily slip
by you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T11:33:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j9mj5$orv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I fully accept that MANY programmers consider your approach to be
"structured"  - HOWEVER,  you are TECHNICALLY incorrect in thinking that
there is "one entry - one exit" for each program.

The routine
  M10-GET-NEXT

actually has TWO possible exits.

If the read is successful (no AT END) then the "exit" point is the physical
end of the source code in that paragraph - and there is a "fall thru" to the
next paragraph (the EXIT paragraph).

If the AT END condition does occur, then the physical end of that paragraph
is SKIPPED and there is an explicit transfer of control to the EXIT
paragraph.

Having said that, it is true that the "logical unit of work"
   M10-LOAD-TABLE THRU M10-X

does have a "single entry and exit" - But that would ALSO be true of the
following code (that MOST people would consider NOT to be structured)

Perform Para1 thru Para5

Para1.
    Display "here"
    If A = "2"
        go to Para2
    Else
         Go to Para3
     End-If
      .
Para2.
   Display "Here 2"
   Go To Para4
     .
Para3.
   Display "Here 3"
    Go To Para5
      .
Para4.
   Display "Here 4"
      .
Para5.
   Display "everything exits from here"
      .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T21:12:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v9167.12808$gj1.1338551@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <9j9mj5$orv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
    I personally have little interest in structuring for the sake
of
structuring.  I find this sort of thing (perform thru) to be easy
to right,
easy to understand, small and efficient.

   Having said that, it has some severe problems.  It is possible
to have it performed as a thru in one place, and not in another.

Ie:
    PERFORM AAA.
    PERFORM AAA THRU AAA-X.

AAA.
    DISPLAY "AAA".
    ADD 1 TO COUNTER.
   IF COUNTER > 99
      GO TO AAA-X.
BBB.
   GO TO AAA..
AAA-X.
    EXIT.

    Another problem happens when you "clone"
a paragraph range.  It is easy to forget to change
all of the go to's, especially the ones that are not
executed very often, such as error code.

    I have in the past wished for some way to
exit a perform range without a go to, like exit paragraph.

    Lately, I have started trying to eliminate these things,
but it is easy to do things with perform thru that are
difficult to duplicate any other way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-22T11:24:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tlls2do3604faf@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <9j9mj5$orv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, your description of what happens does not match the code.
[additional comments interspersed]

William M. Klein wrote in message <9j9mj5$orv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I fully accept that MANY programmers consider your approach to be
>"structured"  - HOWEVER,  you are TECHNICALLY incorrect in thinking that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>end of the source code in that paragraph - and there is a "fall thru" to
the
>next paragraph (the EXIT paragraph).

The GO TO at the end of M10-GET-NEXT prevents "fall-thru".

>If the AT END condition does occur, then the physical end of that paragraph
>is SKIPPED and there is an explicit transfer of control to the EXIT
>paragraph.

Control is transferred to M10-END which has no EXIT statement.

[...]
>
>--
>Bill Klein
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

>"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
>news:7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>>        .
[...]
M10-GET-NEXT through the paragraph-name, M10-END
constitutes a "Mid-Loop Exit" structure. The content of this
structure is reducible to a process block with a single entry
and a single exit. I discussed the "Mid-Loop Exit" structure,
last year.

The draft COBOL standard provides PERFORM ...
EXIT PERFORM ... END-PERFORM which may be used
to eliminate the paragraph-names that form the loop and
structure boundaries. However, the draft COBOL standard
has no syntax to identify the loop as indefinite, therefore
a condition such as UNTIL 1 = 0 is needed. I also mentioned
this, last year, and suggested PERFORM WITH NO TEST
as a means for identifying an indefinite loop.

While I discussed these issues in the newsgroup, I never
submitted a comment to J4 for their consideration.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T17:21:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote 
> The types of GO TOs I'm referring to are as follows:
> .
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

*---------------------------

Bill Klein is right; this is, at best, pseudo-structured.
See how easy it is to do in pure structured, modular format:


       PERFORM M10-LOAD-TABLE THRU M10-X
.
.
.
 M10-LOAD-TABLE.
       (do some initializing stuff)
       PERFORM M10-GET-NEXT
           UNTIL INPUT-FILE-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO '00'.

 M10-X.
       EXIT
       .

       .
 M10-GET-NEXT.
       READ INPUT-FILE
       EVALUATE INPUT-FILE-STATUS
           WHEN '00' 
               SET TBL-NDX            UP BY 1
               MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
               ADD 1                          TO TBL-SIZE
           WHEN '10'
*          ** AT END
               PERFORM M10-END
           WHEN OTHER       
               do unusual result procesing, maybe ABEND.       
       .
 M10-END.
       DISPLAY 'TABLE SIZE IS ' TBL-SIZE
        .
.
.
Personally, I think this is neater, and less subject to being messed
up during maintenance.

I put in the comment because, even today, not everyone recognizes the
"at end" file status.


A note on simplifying the code further, not related to the discussion
of structure:

 M10-LOAD-TABLE.
       (do some initializing stuff)
       PERFORM M10-GET-NEXT
           VARYING TBL-NDX
              FROM 1
                BY 1
           UNTIL INPUT-FILE-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO '00'.
           

 M10-X.
       EXIT
       .

       .
 M10-GET-NEXT.
       READ INPUT-FILE
       EVALUATE INPUT-FILE-STATUS
           WHEN '00' 
               MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
           WHEN '10'
*          ** AT END
               SET TBL-SIZE TO TBL-NDX
               PERFORM M10-END
           WHEN OTHER       
               do unusual result procesing, maybe ABEND.

A further note: Testing for 'good' first is more efficient, because
the 'at end' (or 'other') will only happen once; the 'good' will be
all the other times. (This may or may not apply when using AT END ...
NOT AT END, depending on compiler logic. NOT AT END means a
_successful_ READ was done.)

Stephen J Spiro       
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: Come on out! J4 can use your help! At the top of your pay scale?
Have to go into management to get a raise? Have your company sponsor
you for membership on J4 instead!  Bringing your family can be very
cheap!
```

- **Date:** 2001-07-21T03:50:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> A note on simplifying the code further, not related to the discussion
> of structure:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK to a point but, 'picky-picky'  :-

Assume we are reading an ascending keyed/sorted file :-

 M10-GET-NEXT.
       READ INPUT-FILE
       EVALUATE INPUT-FILE-STATUS
           WHEN '00'

                if  record-key (or some other field) > MyRange-key
                    move "10" to File-Status  ???????

                else MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
                End-if

          WHEN '10'
*          ** AT END

Sure you could 'cheat' and change FILE-STATUS but it would be more "honest"
to set a Flag such as FileFinished or EOFile. (Depending upon the circumstances,
it *may* be undesirable to actually alter the file-status).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T08:45:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote > 
> Assume we are reading an ascending keyed/sorted file :-
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

*-----------------
Yes, that would work, Jimmy, and you demonstrate the advantage of
structured, modular programming: It is easy to maintain without
destroying the structure.

Personnaly, I would not mess with the File Status, and I don't like to
use flags (Personal Preference; I'm not going to defend it).

How about...


          .
  M10-GET-NEXT.
        READ INPUT-FILE
        EVALUATE INPUT-FILE-STATUS  ALSO   record-key > MyRange-key
            WHEN '00'               ALSO   FALSE
                MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
            WHEN '00'               ALSO   TRUE
            WHEN '10'               ALSO   ANY
 *          ** AT END
                SET TBL-SIZE TO TBL-NDX
                PERFORM M10-END
            WHEN OTHER
                do unusual result procesing, maybe ABEND.

EVALUATE  is great! I did an article on it for "Enterprise Systems
Journal" a few years ago. You can code entire decision tables with
EVALUATE!  And it will get better in the new standard, when we allow
"partial conditional expressions".

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standrds Committee

PS: Join the Standards committee.  It's better than being hit with
sharp sticks!
```

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> 
>   M10-GET-NEXT.
>         READ INPUT-FILE
>         EVALUATE INPUT-FILE-STATUS  ALSO   record-key > MyRange-key
>             WHEN '00'               ALSO   FALSE

You should only check the first byte to be zero for successfull file
operations.  The 2nd byte may have other values, such as '2' indicating
duplicate key information.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T23:59:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107212259.76d7bb45@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com> <3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> > 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> duplicate key information.

Depends what you consider successful file operations.  '04' often
means a problem with the program or the file.  And if ANY ONE of the
0x codes is no good, you cannot test for only the first character.

Additionally, you are assuming that FUTURE codes will be considered
"successful" for your installation.  A risky assumption. I will go for
testing two-digit codes.  I HAVE sometimes coded " '00' or '04' ",
when the I knew the 04 was OK.

Stephen J Spiro
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T13:40:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5ECC64.EB07BEE7@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com> <3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> duplicate key information.

Possibly.  We have a vendor supplied called program to handle VSAM I/O.
It assumes an open with a status code of other than '00' is a failure and
returns a '7' to the calling program which aborts.   But an open status
code of '97' is also a successful open.   The job aborts.  If we restart it
and it works, we can guess that the original return was '97'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T12:55:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jn1da$4k9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com> <3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz> <3B5ECC64.EB07BEE7@cusys.edu>`

```
I have mentioned this before, but there are real questions whether IBM is
(was) conforming when they treat "97" (VSAM file not closed properly - my
words, not theirs) as "conforming".  All the 9x file status codes are
SUPPOSED to be unsuccessful.

HOWEVER,

A) Current compilers/run-time issue 97 *far* less often then they used to.
(I don't know if they ever occur in anything OTHER than real "problem"
situations, i.e. a real problem when the file was LAST used).

B) the *next* Standard adds "0x" (implementor defined) file status codes for
"implementor defined status codes".  My hope/expectation is that IBM will
(eventually) provide a way to select a 97 or a (new) 0x status code for this
situation.

For "upward compatibility of existing programs" purposes, I hope/assume that
IBM will make this either a run-time or compile-time OPTION.  Any SHARE
members might want to submit a requirement NOW to suggest/request this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T15:05:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hlW77.732$ou4.73585@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com> <3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz> <3B5ECC64.EB07BEE7@cusys.edu> <9jn1da$4k9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
At one of my client sites, they were often getting status '97' on an open of
a VSAM file, because the previous run of the job had crashed at a later
step. (No, the crash was not in any of *my* software).

They solved the problem by adding a step at the beginning of the job to do
an IDCAMS VERIFY on all the VSAM files. VERIFY 'resets' the "unclosed"
status.

I'm not sure I like this approach, but it works for them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-23T12:35:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5C0C1C.72FCD950@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz>...
> > Stephen J Spiro wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 0x codes is no good, you cannot test for only the first character.

The '85 standard specifies that the first byte being "0" indicates
successful completion.  When the status is "02" or "04" the READ _has_
successfully completed and has read a record.  It may well be that the
program or file has problems if these occur and the programmer did not
expect these codes, but then that is primarily a programmer problem -
inadequate training perhaps.
 
> Additionally, you are assuming that FUTURE codes will be considered
> "successful" for your installation.  A risky assumption. I will go for
> testing two-digit codes.  I HAVE sometimes coded " '00' or '04' ",
> when the I knew the 04 was OK.

Future codes that have the first byte as "0" _will_ indicate that the
operation completed successfully.
```

- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com> <3B5C0C1C.72FCD950@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B5C0C1C.72FCD950@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> > Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B5A954C.37BA6C99@Azonic.co.nz>...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> operation completed successfully.

*----------------------

You remind me very much of the junior programmer with whom I worked
many years ago.  She could not understand why her job ABENDed.  After
all, she had a SUCCESSFUL, clean compile of the program.

A "successful operation" on garbage is still garbage.  

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-24T18:49:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5DB517.891B1A55@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <4145699b.0107210745.771c0512@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:
>           .
>   M10-GET-NEXT.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 do unusual result procesing, maybe ABEND.

> You remind me very much of the junior programmer with whom I worked
> many years ago. 

Why, did she write code that processed File Status codes correctly too ?

> She could not understand why her job ABENDed.  After
> all, she had a SUCCESSFUL, clean compile of the program.

Interesting, because it seems to be your code that would "maybe ABEND"
when it found a file status of, say, '02', even though that was a
SUCCESSFUL read, and perfectly valid data.

But I don't see the relationship you seem to be trying to draw between a
successful compile and correctly written code.
 

> A "successful operation" on garbage is still garbage.  

There is no direct relationship between a file status of '0x', where x
is not '0', and 'garbage' or vice versa.  A file status code of '00'
does not indicate that the record is not garbage, a file status of '02',
or even of '04' or other '0x', does not indicate that the record data is
garbage. 

An '04' may simply be that the program hasn't been updated to include a
new record type that will be ignored anyway, something to contemplate at
2am.

Protection of the program against garbage data should never rely on
file-status codes, nor should this protection be ignored just because a
read completed successfully, or even gave a file status of '00'.
```

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B5DB517.891B1A55@Azonic.co.nz...
> Protection of the program against garbage data should never rely on
> file-status codes, nor should this protection be ignored just because a
> read completed successfully, or even gave a file status of '00'.
>
Amen!

Just about every time in my career that I have been called out (and on a
multitude of platforms) the problem has occurred AFTER successful data
transfer... The file status or SQL State is fine and data was transferred
successfully. The problem was that an upstream process has corrupted it or
failed to put correct values in it...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T12:43:14-05:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T18:45:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B59CDD5.71003B14@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <3B59BF32.906ED491@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```


Peter Lacey wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> L

I think it's a question of preference of style. My own - 'belt and braces/suspenders' -
do both, certainly always checking File-Status; (same goes for checking DB Tables with
SQLSTATE as a result of each EXEC statement).

You can get this scenario without file-status. Open file and then do a perform loop with
READS. When you stick in the file-status check - you find you haven't OPENED the file or
it my not have a legit filename/path. (I've done it <G>).

For the most part I see both File-Status and SQLSTATE, (or  SQLERROR) as prime tools for
testing. User-wise the only things that can really happen are (1a) Non-existence of a
file, (1b) Non existence of record-type in a file, or (2) Crashed index for an ISAM.

Remember - I'm talking PCs and 'interactive', not something controlled by JCL cards. The
other point, when you come to OO - your file-handling class(program) may be 'three-steps
removed' from the 'EditCustomers' which is invoking it - so it behoves you to make damn
sure you  cover all error-conditons, (hopefully strengthened by the EC conditions in
COBOL 2002), otherwise you get back to the original invoker with unknown
error-condtioins that can cause your application to go belly-up.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T20:51:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Xl67.758$O31.309105@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <3B59BF32.906ED491@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3B59BF32.906ED491@mb.sympatico.ca...
>
> This will sound like a troll but it isn't because I really want to know:
when did
> it become the accepted practice to examine the file status instead of
using "AT END"
> and "INVALID KEY"?

Programming with FILE STATUS is a little trickier and somewhat wordier than
using only AT END and INVALID KEY, so it's not terribly popular.

With FILE STATUS, you can check for so much more... you can detect the error
codes on bad OPENs, physical boundary violations, bad hardware and many
other things. But... in the Real World of IBM/Mainframe programming, I've
found that even if you can detect some of these problems, the default action
(ABEND) is usually the action the applications programmer takes anyway. You
just end up with a nicer message in SYSOUT before the memory dump.

DECLARATIVES ("USE procedures")  can take you another level beyond FILE
STATUS, but I've only tried using those a couple of times.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T15:47:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107211447.688cbb3b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <3B59BF32.906ED491@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message > This will sound like a troll but it isn't because I really want to know:  when did
> it become the accepted practice to examine the file status instead of using "AT END"
> and "INVALID KEY"?
> 
> PL

As far as I can tell, around 1986, soon after they became available in
the standard.  "AT END" doesn't tell you if you have bad I/O, and
"INVALID KEY" misses many of the errors (and you can't even USE it on
sequential files!).  As my (and Jimmy's) examples show, you can write
very neatly structured, modular code with file statuses, which you
cannot do with "AT END" or "INVALID KEY".  Even "NOT AT END" or "NOT
INVALID KEY" assume SUCCESSFUL executions.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: J4 needs new blood! Sacrifice yourself on the Altar of COBOL!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T22:55:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jdiqf$p1r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <4145699b.0107201621.321ab72@posting.google.com> <3B58FC2F.EDC2FDFA@home.com> <3B59BF32.906ED491@mb.sympatico.ca> <4145699b.0107211447.688cbb3b@posting.google.com>`

```
Huh ???
  File status codes have been in the Standard (and in every conforming
compiler) certainly since the '74 Standard - and I am pretty certain that
they were available in '68.

In the IBM mainframe world, they CERTAINLY were available (but not often
used) with OS/VS COBOL.

In the IBM mainframe world, there is "general" view that for QSAM (not
VSAM - or its predecessor ISAM) that if you don't get a "normal" return
(such as "0x" or At End or Invalid Key) that your PROBABLY want your program
to ABEND.  IBM mainframe does lots of "nice" things with programs that ABEND
that are "nice" for the way traditional batch job streams work.

In IBM online environments it is unusual (bordering on VERY strange) to use
"native" COBOL I/O statements.  Instead you use CALL "CBLTDLI" or EXEC CICS
READ/whatever.  Therefore, these are all "by their nature" outside of the
normal "file status" checking.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T11:01:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5952E0.29023467@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com> <3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz> <CNw07.2515$xp1.250458@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <76I07.206$_93.29600957@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B572177.D1709713@cusys.edu> <7oN57.2471$e35.343530834@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
>"Terry Heinze" wrote
> M10-LOAD-TABLE.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>        EXIT
William M. Klein wrote:
> The routine
>   M10-GET-NEXT
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> next paragraph (the EXIT paragraph).

Actually it never will 'drop through' out of the paragraph (see
definition of GO TO).


Of course there _should_ be the opportunity to drop through, for example
by having the line added to check table size:

> M10-GET-NEXT.
>        READ INPUT-FILE
>            AT END
>                GO TO M10-END
>        END-READ
         IF ( Tbl-Ndx < Tbl-Occurs )
>            SET TBL-NDX            UP BY 1
>            MOVE INPUT-DATA TO TBL-OCCURRENCE (TBL-NDX)
>            ADD 1                          TO TBL-SIZE
>            GO TO M10-GET-NEXT
         END-IF
>        .
```

```
I don't disagree with using "conforming" source code, but just so you know,
GOBACK *is* a part of the draft of the next COBOL Standard.  Therefore, it
seems to me that avoiding it for this reason is LESS valid than it used to
be.
```

```
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > someone better learn COBOL.

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 
> My compiler won't let me.  IBM says EXIT must be in a paragraph of its own.  Of course,
> CONTINUE works fine.

*----------------
I just checked.  I thought the new standard had changed it, but it
still says that
"The EXIT statement shall appear in a sentence by itself that shall be
the only sentence in the paragraph."

'Tis getting late, but maybe someone could write up a change.... 
submit it to
<Don.Schricker at merant.com> 



IBM COBOL/390 has a strange extension:
" As an IBM extension, the EXIT statement does not need to appear in a
 sentence by itself. Any statements following the EXIT statement are 
 executed; the EXIT statement is treated as the CONTINUE statement. "

Currently I always perform every paragraph (except the driver) thru an
exit paragraph.  I lIKE the idea of eliminating the THRU in favor of
making EXIT. the last statement in every paragraph. But I can't with
IBM....

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
PS: COBOL NEEDS YOU! Sign up now for the J4 Committee! A week at a
time of boring but professionally rewarding nit-picking, trying to get
it RIGHT!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T21:06:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y9m67.3516$Px1.417399@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz> <9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3CA665.EA37DCE8@Azonic.co.nz> <9hhsen$as8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B3D750F.9DA79A25@Azonic.co.nz> <3B42EE91.64C4A172@Azonic.co.nz> <8ep7kt0tgqrbih2sroblb1nfboqcu768ha@4ax.com> <3B57225D.B16BFD64@cusys.edu> <4145699b.0107191422.33fbc044@posting.google.com>`

```
Then just use a period in column 12 and nothing else on that line, as your
final statement of each paragraph. All compilers I've used lately accept
it. Then you can get rid of Perform Thru which is an accident waiting to
happen.
```

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T07:23:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B592D98.37AAAFE4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz> <9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3CA665.EA37DCE8@Azonic.co.nz> <9hhsen$as8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B3D750F.9DA79A25@Azonic.co.nz> <3B42EE91.64C4A172@Azonic.co.nz> <8ep7kt0tgqrbih2sroblb1nfboqcu768ha@4ax.com> <3B57225D.B16BFD64@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> CONTINUE works fine.

Which IBM compiler are you using?  I just built a small test program
in COBOL for MVS & VM, version 1.2.2, with no periods at end of
statements.  I put an "EXIT." statement at the end of every paragraph,
and the compiler didn't even generate a single I-level diagnostic. 
The program executes correctly too.

Every IBM mainframe COBOL compiler I have ever used would allow EXIT
as the last statement of a multi-statement paragraph.  That includes
DOS/VS FCOBOL, OS/VS COBOL, VS COBOL II, and I expect it will work for
COBOL for OS/390 & VM when we install it this fall.

The first time I ever saw this curious use of "EXIT." was with DOS/VS
FCOBOL around 1981.  When we first started writing structured programs
in that shop we frequently had a "READ ... AT END..." sentence at the
end of a paragraph.  The compiler would generate a really ugly W-LEVEL
message that read something like "EXIT FROM PERFORMED PROCEDURE
ASSUMED BEFORE PROCEDURE-NAME".  We eventually figured out that
putting any imperative statement after the READ would eliminate the
diagnostic, such as MOVE SPACE TO DUMMY-FIELD.  One wise guy just
figured out he could put EXIT at the end of any paragraph, no matter
how many statements it had, and it would eliminate the diagnostic with
no bad side-effects.

I think IBM documents that they allow EXIT as the LAST statement of a
paragraph (as an extension) in the COBOL for MVS & VM manual. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T13:30:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5ECA41.2E06301C@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz> <9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3CA665.EA37DCE8@Azonic.co.nz> <9hhsen$as8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B3D750F.9DA79A25@Azonic.co.nz> <3B42EE91.64C4A172@Azonic.co.nz> <8ep7kt0tgqrbih2sroblb1nfboqcu768ha@4ax.com> <3B57225D.B16BFD64@cusys.edu> <3B592D98.37AAAFE4@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

>
> > My compiler won't let me.  IBM says EXIT must be in a paragraph of its own.  Of course,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The program executes correctly too.

I just tested - it will allow it, but with the proper compiler options turned on, it says it
is a nonconforming nonstandard, IBM extension to ANS/ISO 1985.

I have worked with so many mainframe companies, that I just get out of the habit of doing
stuff like that.


>
> Every IBM mainframe COBOL compiler I have ever used would allow EXIT
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/

Does CONTINUE work just as well, if you want to be conforming (but not be as obvious)?
```

```
as well as maintenance and enhancements to existing applications.

and DB2. Should have experience with the full software development
lifecycle.

attachment to Ian Barocas, ian@marc-allen.com.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: training

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T16:34:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ja84o$i42$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3b57660a_3@news1.vip.uk.com> <Sb067.1270$ar1.5407@www.newsranger.com>`

```
The original poster seems to have "disappeared" without ever explaining why
he (she?) started this thread.

However, I thought it might be time for me to repeat post the section on
"homework" from the COBOL FAQ at
  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

"18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?

Yes, you can get help with your homework via these newsgroups. HOWEVER, that
does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT find many participants
who will be very happy if you ask them to do your homework FOR YOU. Some
hints that you should consider when drafting your post for assistance with
homework include:

 � Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking for homework help.
(Most of the newsgroup participants are very good at sniffing out those who
try and pose homework questions as "business need" questions - and they are
not very polite in replying to such questions).

 � Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating system your
homework is targeted at. Solutions may vary significantly based on this.
(You might also want to include what text book you are using.)

 � The more information that you can give that demonstrates that you really
have tried to solve the problem yourself (using your text book, class
material/presentations, lab assistance, etc), the more likely it is that you
will get useful and friendly responses. If you let us know what you have
found on your own and why you are still uncertain or confused, you will
usually get helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very pointed
replies.

NOTE: if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't want to see the "Do
your own homework" notes that occur in the COBOL newsgroups), you might want
to try,

    http://www.tutoraid.org/
"
```

<a id="t-20ecac5b49"></a>
## Thread: Strange Problem with Fujitsu 6.1 Command Shell — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Strange Problem with Fujitsu 6.1 Command Shell

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T02:51:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1N57.2427$DC3.337563825@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Good evening,

I've written a Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 6.1 program that seems to be having a
rather strange problem and I'm wondering if anyone else has run into
something like this.  The program is running under NT 4.0 service pack 5.
And I really don't expect the problem to be with Fujitsu per se, but I'm
kind of lost at the moment, so we'll start there.

The program basically uses the timer control and every 60 seconds it looks
for a file on our mainframe using a mapped drive to the mainframe and the
CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST library routine.

If it sees that the file is present, it reformats the file into what the
customer requires and calls a ftp batch command via the "INVOKE CmDDE1
Execute  USING  Command-Line" option which sends the file to the appropriate
server based on a table file that is read in at beginning of job.

We never know how many files per day we are going to process, so some days
it could be 10 and some days it could be 30.  But the hard part is, some
days everything works fine and some days it doesn't.  I can't really get a
handle on if it's only on busy days that this happens.  It really seems to
be purely random, but as a long, long time programmer, deep inside, I know
better.  Sometimes, the "cmd.exe" prompt stays on the task bar, never closes
out and never FTP's the file.  However, if I exit the PowerCOBOL program and
restart it, it usually runs just fine and FTP's all files.

I'm suspecting a memory leak or a stack file problem that goes away when I
restart the program, but how in the heck can I:

a. Trace what is going on?
b. Fix the problem once I figure out what it is, especially if it's a stack
problem and NT doesn't do stacks?

I love these intermittent problems.  That's part of the reason I have so
much gray hair!!!

Thanks in advance,

Denny
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Problem with Fujitsu 6.1 Command Shell

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T21:17:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Dd167.1368$Px1.146551@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<d1N57.2427$DC3.337563825@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Can't help with your immediate problem, but for sure you should use:

CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST2

which handles imbedded spaces in the directory names.

The other trick we've tried in situations similar is to have the
program write a brief note as to what it's doing to a log file.

Good luck.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Problem with Fujitsu 6.1 Command Shell

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-24T02:18:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jW477.9174$Px1.1048793@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<d1N57.2427$DC3.337563825@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <Dd167.1368$Px1.146551@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <Qe477.385$G_4.65866988@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:Qe477.385$G_4.65866988@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com...
> Jerry,
>
> We've kept all directory file names space free.

I'm sure you do. We do too! Windows itself uses imbedded spaces in
some folders ("Program Files," "Temporary Internet Files," etc.) and
in some filenames. We can't always anticipate or control these uses -
such as the Get Temp Directory API.

Further, we've found that some users can get awfully frisky if given
an opportunity to name something ("The Melancholy House Of
Usher.DAT").

So, what the heck, it don't cost us any more to use the more robust
command.
```

```
Thane,

I won't be able to compile your code into the program and test it until
Friday, but with all the result codes your routine offers, even if it
doesn't work, it looks like I could get a much better handle on what
problems are occurring.

My last conversion from MF COBOL to Fujitsu is a program that does the same
"File Exist" routine, but when it sees a file, it automatically "prints" the
file to Winfax Pro using DDE, which in turn, automatically faxes the file to
the customer.  So if I can get a handle on this situation here, I'll feel
much more comfortable with the fax program.

Thanks very much for allowing me to use the code.  I'll post back the
results once I get it into production.  It's hard for me to duplicate the
situation in testing.

Denny
```

<a id="t-da1f6bb32e"></a>
## Thread: VSAM Question — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### VSAM Question

- **From:** "Fergus" <fergus.obrien@ie-technology.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T15:43:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AIY57.18152$Fk7.167043@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Hi
Can some IBM mainframer tell me if this can be done.

I have an indexed VSAM file with the following key structure:

03 ACC-KEY.
     05 ACC-NUM       PIC 9(05).
     05 ACC-TYPE       PIC X(01).

ACC-TYPE can be one of four values and an ACC-NUM can only have one
associated ACC-TYPE (i.e. there cannot be a 12345A and a 12345B).

I need to check the existence of the ACCount on the file but I don't know
the ACC-TYPE.

In my program (COBOL BTW) I could write 4 READ statements that check for
each ACC-TYPE in turn until one is found.

e.g
MOVE 12345 TO ACC-NUM
MOVE 'A'       TO ACC-TYPE

READ FILE
IF STATUS = 23      (i.e. not found)

MOVE 'B'       TO ACC-TYPE
READ FILE
check STATUS
etc.

However, this seems to me to be rather inelegant.

Is there any way to read the file with the ACC-TYPE set to SPACES (LOW
VALUES?) and then to query the resulting returned record.  I can always
compare the ACC-NUM's to check if I have the right account.

I know I can do something similar in DL/1 (GET NEXT) but is there any
similar code for a read greater in COBOL.

TIA

Fergus
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Question

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T18:30:00+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.ggsmi00.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<AIY57.18152$Fk7.167043@news.indigo.ie>`

```
On Fri, 20 Jul 2001 15:43:34 +0100, Fergus wrote:

>Hi
>Can some IBM mainframer tell me if this can be done.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The START statement with the KEY NOT LESS clause permits this.

Just issue a START and a READ NEXT on this file and check if the read record
is the one you want.

Hope this helps,
Willem Clements
BrainBench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T11:41:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j9muq$ac0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<AIY57.18152$Fk7.167043@news.indigo.ie>`

```
If you have your ACCESS as DYNAMIC, you can do a START statement with your
type set to low-values and do "greater than".  This will get you the first
record that has that acct-num (*OR* the next acct-num).

I think that is what you want.
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Question

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T13:34:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rxf67.1896$ar1.6231@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<AIY57.18152$Fk7.167043@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Use START, then READ.

In article <AIY57.18152$Fk7.167043@news.indigo.ie>, Fergus says...
>
>Hi
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: See Delete Records

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-07-20T20:43:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j9tmp$qjl$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<m1_57.13007$fd2.38274061@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
Reading the data file as if it were sequential should give all records
including those deleted.

"PorTy" <euromercante1@clix.pt> wrote in message
news:m1_57.13007$fd2.38274061@newsserver.ip.pt...
> Hello,
> How could see delete records in a Micro Focus File (indexed).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: See Delete Records

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T01:30:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NW467.45486$C81.3831329@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<m1_57.13007$fd2.38274061@newsserver.ip.pt> <9j9tmp$qjl$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
    This might be a problem.  If the file has fixed length
reccords, and NO
compression, this might work, but you will probably need to
read the file as a byte stream, not as a micro focus variable
record size (with 128 byte header) sequential file.

    See the manual, it has a fair explanation of file structure,
but no help on what the various "system records" are.

    Try creating a (very) short test file, and examining it.
With known data and history, you will have a better
chance of figuring things out.


"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in
message news:9j9tmp$qjl$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Reading the data file as if it were sequential should give all
records
> including those deleted.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-de40835315"></a>
## Thread: Linking Problem — 4 msgs

### Linking Problem

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T00:34:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B58CD85.6D46FB07@neo.rr.com>`

```
I'm doing some development with Fujitsu V4 (got it from one
of the books on a CD).

What I have done is written at least one subroutine and one
main program. They compile correctly when done individually.
However, when the BUILD is done (which ultimately brings in
the LINKER -- or is that clinker?), the link fails
complaining about there not being a valid entry.

Now this is rather interesting, and the manual says that you
must change the parms so that the "main" module is marked
MAIN. The problem is, this causes the subroutine to now get
compiled with MAIN! (what a way to run an army!) The build
function seems to want to use the same options/parms for ALL
the "cobol" parts!?!

The whole reason that I'm doing all of this is so that I can
develope and test on an NT system and then move the code to
a different platform (and all we have to do is change the
common subroutine(s) to use the I/O of the intended platform
and then recompile and link). So yes, I want static linkage,
not dynamic.

Anyone have any ideas as to what the problem is?

Later,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking Problem

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-21T13:29:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ntf67.1890$ar1.6245@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B58CD85.6D46FB07@neo.rr.com>`

```
It appears you may have set MAIN as a project level compiler option.  Remove
that.  Instead, in the project view, expand the tree and right click on the
COBOL file icon that is to be the main program.  Then choose MAIN and WINMAIN
and off you go!


In article <3B58CD85.6D46FB07@neo.rr.com>, Steve Thompson says...
>
>I'm doing some development with Fujitsu V4 (got it from one
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linking Problem

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-23T13:49:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5C2AC8.ACE77A75@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3B58CD85.6D46FB07@neo.rr.com> <ntf67.1890$ar1.6245@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> It appears you may have set MAIN as a project level compiler option.  Remove
> that.  Instead, in the project view, expand the tree and right click on the
> COBOL file icon that is to be the main program.  Then choose MAIN and WINMAIN
> and off you go!

<snip>

I guess I did not make myself clear in the original post. The icon is RED
(indicating that it is a MAIN program).

If I do a build, NO program gets marked MAIN! If I go back and attempt to set the
options for just this program (the one that is supposed to be "MAIN") then ALL
programs are marked main!

I have since found that doing individual compiles -- I really need to check the
COBOL Options list, because the MAIN program was being compiled with option
NOMAIN!

Going back to "WINMAIN" why would I do this when this system is batch and batch
only?

Also, during a build, all compiles are now failing, because the COPY Library
information is being dropped from the compile options (what a pain in the
******).

Why won't the build just use the objects created from the individual compiles, as
those objects are dated after any source dates?

Later,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linking Problem

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-23T14:58:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QYW67.4003$ar1.8153@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B58CD85.6D46FB07@neo.rr.com> <ntf67.1890$ar1.6245@www.newsranger.com> <3B5C2AC8.ACE77A75@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve - 

I suspect something fundamental is wrong with the project setup.

If you care to - email me the .PRJ file (the whole thing, source too if you want
me to do a full test) and I'll see what I can see and help you fix it up.

The PRJ management with Fujitsu has been solid for me.

Email it to thane at softwaresimple.com

Thanks!
```

<a id="t-2aed5dbcc3"></a>
## Thread: Try New Things From Time to Time — 1 msgs

### Try New Things From Time to Time

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-22T23:45:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tAJ67.8270$Xn.949932@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
I tried turning on the "COUNT" option in Fujitsu's compiler. This
creates a list of every verb and statement in the program along with
hit counters and percentages. The resulting tabulation purports to
show the internal activity of the program with an eye toward
fine-tuning - that is, the report shows the programmer where the
program spends its time.

Compiled the program (a large DLL), began execution, and exited right
away just to see what I'd get.

The resulting text file was 1.2 megabytes in size; over 500 printed
pages (of fine print, I might add). While I haven't studied the report
in depth (I'm having trouble lifting it), the report may very well
tell me more than I want to know.

After several hours of debugging another problem, I discovered that
turning on this COUNT feature caused an exception violation in the
upstream calling program.

But what's art without suffering, eh?

The "TRACE" option I'll leave for anon.
```

```
Pete.

An Anonymous illiterate, obviously...<G>


> ---------------------------------------
```
