```
> ---------------------------------------
>
OBOY! Tips on COBOL AND a credit card! This is just SOOOO good...!


> Daryl Wessex wrote in message ...
> >I would call the local Sheriff's office, to find out what the laws are
for
> >protecting your property.  Here in Texas it is legal to use deadly force,
if
> >needed, to protect your property.
> >

Well, only if you have a spread to die for...<G>

> >"Martin & Carol Marino" <mmarino@shawnlink.com> wrote in message
> >news:3B536BC.74@shawnlink.com...
> >> We were warned, early on, about the Millstone water districts past
> >> tactics:  threats, bullying, lying, turning neighbor against neighbor.
>

See, Utilities are the same everywhere... I wouldn't mind betting our local
Power Company here is affiliated to the same outfit..<G>

Some of the local Teddy Bears here get very agitated when stupidly designed
computer systems keep sending them reminders after they've paid. This
particular age group takes this kind of thing very seriously  (the rest of
us just bin it, and swear at computer technology...) and think there's a
little man writing to them, and they are liable to have their power cut off.

Instead of spending a few bucks and fixing the system, the local authority
said through a spokesman: "We only cut people off as a very last resort. If
anyone is having difficulty they should contact us."

All well and good as far as it goes, but lots of the Teddys are imbued with
fierce independent Kiwi spirit and have too much pride to ask for help. And
most of them pay their bills anyway. So instead, they just use less power
(Cool Marketing by the power Company, right?<G>).

Fortunately, we have a temperate climate here in the Bay, and although
daytime Winter temperatures at the moment are around 12 to 14 Centigrade, it
can drop to -1 or -2 if there is an overnight frost. Not so bad that people
are going to die of hypothermia (as they do in the UK if the power goes
off), but nevertheless, uncomfortable for the Teddys, and unnecessary.

Bottom line: Utilities in Texas and here seem to attend the same "Marketing
101" course, I think it was originally written by someone brutally stupid...

Now, as for being off-topic....well, their bills are produced by
COBOL....<G>

Pete.

```

```
Bad news, Pete. The Committee is meeting in Oregon even as you read
this. After approval of the agenda and reviewing the minutes, it was
decided that the 2020 (formerly the 2002)  standard would slip another
2 years.
The GOOD news is that our internal procedure review has been
completed, and we have decided to abandon the "voluntary, non-profit"
ISO/IEC/ANSI/NCITS/SNZ model and use a market-tested computer-industry
corporate model.  In order to set deadlines accurately, and actually
MEET them, we have voted to become a subsidiary of Microsoft.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

<a id="t-4d3ec3ff68"></a>
## Thread: Have my landowner rights been violated? — 1 msgs

### Re: Have my landowner rights been violated?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-23T22:32:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GB177.10682$Xn.1264540@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<D1N67.2545$5k3.479433@news>`

```

<volksl_nospam_@hotwail.com
>
> Daryl Wessex wrote in message ...
> >I would call the local Sheriff's office, to find out what the laws
are for
> >protecting your property.  Here in Texas it is legal to use deadly
force, if
> >needed, to protect your property.
> >
> >Daryl Wessex

Since this newsgroup is the repository of all that is true and
beautiful, please permit a clarification. Texas does NOT permit the
use of deadly force, per se, to protect property.
Specifically, deadly force is permitted
a) to prevent arson, burglary, robbery, theft at night, or criminal
mischief at night
b) to prevent the dobad from fleeing WITH the property after a
burglary, robbery, or theft at night
AND
a) the land or property cannot be protected by any other means, or
b) the use of force less than deadly would expose one to substantial
risk of death or serious bodily injury.

So, you can't shoot someone in Texas just for breaking out your
windshield, pulling up your lettuce, kicking your dog, or not serving
your steak rare.

We do, however, have the "But you honor, he needed killin'" defense.
```

---

<a id="t-b629543b78"></a>

#### ↳ Re: Terminal Fonts

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-24T19:53:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jl6cb02sq5@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9jk1m8$gk$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
It's just a straight character mapping--the font file won't be
of much use if you want the file converted.

There are a number of utilities that do this (just search for
"Dos Text" and "Windows Text",) or if you want to code it
yourself, here's some example code:

http://www.vbdiamond.com/Sources/ViewSource.asp?ID=1007

The code is in VB, but it's just the char-to-char should be
apparent.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Terminal Fonts

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T14:43:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fWA77.51858$C81.4403662@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9jk1m8$gk$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <sFz77.20588$eY6.2400179@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
    Not totally - A DOS text file would have a CR/LF pair at the
end of every line,
while a WINDOWS text file would have a CR/LF pair at the end of
the
paragraph only.

    They can be the same if you press enter at the end of each
line
instead of waiting for word wrap.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Not unless you specify them as so.  There have been lots of DOS text files
that fit you criteria for windows, and vice versa.  "TEXT" is printable
Ascii, on both systems.  A Cobol source file ia perfect example, and is
absolutely identical on both systems.
```

<a id="t-2eda63cbe3"></a>
## Thread: How do I get DSN into COBOL Program — 13 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I get DSN into COBOL Program

- **From:** jim.steelman@primerica.com (Jim Steelman)
- **Date:** 2001-07-24T10:02:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1743c876.0107240902.384b93f5@posting.google.com>`

```
A couple of years ago I was able to use the following COBOL subprogram
to return a list of DDnames and DSnames to a main program. This has
stopped working with the new release of OS/390. Can someone suggest
the alteration needed to this program or an alternative way for me to
get the DSN for a particular DD statement?

Thanks in advance.

Here is the old COBOL program

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.  COBOLIIB.
000300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000400 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000500 FILE-CONTROL.
000600 DATA DIVISION.
000700 FILE SECTION.
000800 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000900 01  TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
001000     05  TCB-ADDR-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
001100 01  TIOT-SEG-POINT.
001200     05  TIOT-SEG-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
001300     05  TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES TIOT-SEG-POINTER
001400           PIC S9(9) COMP.
001500 01  JFCB-POINT.
001600     05  JFCB-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
001700     05  JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES JFCB-POINTER.
001800         10  FILLER PIC X.
001900         10  JFCB-LOW-3 PIC X(3).
002000 LINKAGE SECTION.
002100 01  DDNAME-DSN-ARRAY.
002200     05  DDNAME-DSN   OCCURS 100 TIMES INDEXED BY NDX1.
002300         10  DDA-DDNAME  PIC X(8).
002400         10  DDA-DSN     PIC X(44).
002500*
002600 01  TCB-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
002700 01  TCB.
002800     05  FILLER   PIC X(12).
002900     05  TIOT-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
003000 01  TIOT-START PIC X(24).
003100 01  TIOT-SEG.
003200     05  TIO-LEN PIC X.
003300     05  FILLER  PIC X(3).
003400     05  DD-NAME  PIC X(8).
003500     05  JFCB-ADDR PIC X(3).
003600 01  JFCB.
003700     05  FILLER PIC X(16).
003800     05  DS-NAME PIC X(44).
003900 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DDNAME-DSN-ARRAY.
004000     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO JFCB-POINT.
004100     MOVE X'0000021C' TO TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
004200     SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
004300     SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
004400     SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-START TO TIOT-POINTER.
004500     SET TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
004600     ADD 24 TO TIOT-SEG-PNT.
004700     SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
004800     SET NDX1 TO 1.
004900     PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES OR NDX1 > 100
005000       MOVE DD-NAME TO DDA-DDNAME(NDX1)
005100       MOVE JFCB-ADDR TO JFCB-LOW-3
005200       SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO JFCB-POINTER
005300       MOVE DS-NAME TO DDA-DSN(NDX1)
005400       ADD 20 TO TIOT-SEG-PNT
005500       SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO TIOT-SEG-POINTER
005600       SET NDX1 UP BY 1
005700     END-PERFORM.
005800     GOBACK.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I get DSN into COBOL Program

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-24T21:33:02+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pnfrltoq75rkmlksqkkfl15rboe556ho78@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1743c876.0107240902.384b93f5@posting.google.com>`

```
On 24 Jul 2001 10:02:06 -0700 jim.steelman@primerica.com (Jim Steelman) wrote:

:>A couple of years ago I was able to use the following COBOL subprogram
:>to return a list of DDnames and DSnames to a main program. This has
:>stopped working with the new release of OS/390. Can someone suggest
:>the alteration needed to this program or an alternative way for me to
:>get the DSN for a particular DD statement?

Your system programmers changed to SWA above. You must use SWA access to get
to the JFCB.

I don't think that SWA access is available to COBOL.

You can always write an assembler program.

:>Thanks in advance.

:>Here is the old COBOL program

:>000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
:>000200 PROGRAM-ID.  COBOLIIB.
:>000300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
:>000400 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
:>000500 FILE-CONTROL.
:>000600 DATA DIVISION.
:>000700 FILE SECTION.
:>000800 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
:>000900 01  TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
:>001000     05  TCB-ADDR-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
:>001100 01  TIOT-SEG-POINT.
:>001200     05  TIOT-SEG-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
:>001300     05  TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES TIOT-SEG-POINTER
:>001400           PIC S9(9) COMP.
:>001500 01  JFCB-POINT.
:>001600     05  JFCB-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
:>001700     05  JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES JFCB-POINTER.
:>001800         10  FILLER PIC X.
:>001900         10  JFCB-LOW-3 PIC X(3).
:>002000 LINKAGE SECTION.
:>002100 01  DDNAME-DSN-ARRAY.
:>002200     05  DDNAME-DSN   OCCURS 100 TIMES INDEXED BY NDX1.
:>002300         10  DDA-DDNAME  PIC X(8).
:>002400         10  DDA-DSN     PIC X(44).
:>002500*
:>002600 01  TCB-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
:>002700 01  TCB.
:>002800     05  FILLER   PIC X(12).
:>002900     05  TIOT-POINTER USAGE IS POINTER.
:>003000 01  TIOT-START PIC X(24).
:>003100 01  TIOT-SEG.
:>003200     05  TIO-LEN PIC X.
:>003300     05  FILLER  PIC X(3).
:>003400     05  DD-NAME  PIC X(8).
:>003500     05  JFCB-ADDR PIC X(3).
:>003600 01  JFCB.
:>003700     05  FILLER PIC X(16).
:>003800     05  DS-NAME PIC X(44).
:>003900 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DDNAME-DSN-ARRAY.
:>004000     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO JFCB-POINT.
:>004100     MOVE X'0000021C' TO TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
:>004200     SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
:>004300     SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
:>004400     SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-START TO TIOT-POINTER.
:>004500     SET TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
:>004600     ADD 24 TO TIOT-SEG-PNT.
:>004700     SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
:>004800     SET NDX1 TO 1.
:>004900     PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES OR NDX1 > 100
:>005000       MOVE DD-NAME TO DDA-DDNAME(NDX1)
:>005100       MOVE JFCB-ADDR TO JFCB-LOW-3
:>005200       SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO JFCB-POINTER
:>005300       MOVE DS-NAME TO DDA-DSN(NDX1)
:>005400       ADD 20 TO TIOT-SEG-PNT
:>005500       SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO TIOT-SEG-POINTER
:>005600       SET NDX1 UP BY 1
:>005700     END-PERFORM.
:>005800     GOBACK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I get DSN into COBOL Program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T12:29:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010725082938.08175.00000769@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<cqtslt83tbmbuicmq1ajjrd214envo1q9j@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote...

>On Tue, 24 Jul 2001 17:48:32 -0500 "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I didn't know that COBOL could do a call by address.

For any of the LE COBOL compilers:

      set procedure-ptr-item to entry 'subroutine'
      call procedure-ptr-item using ...

where "procedure-ptr-item" is declared something like:

     01  proceure-ptr-item procedure-pointer.

Or, in general, if you can get any entry point address into procedure-ptr-item
(passed as an argument, say), you can call specifying that name.


>
>:>When/if he makes it available, I will post a reference to it.
>
>Would be interesting.
>


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
On 25 Jul 2001 12:29:38 GMT scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote...

:>>On Tue, 24 Jul 2001 17:48:32 -0500 "William M. Klein"
:>><wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

:>>:>Gilbert Saint-Flour has indicated that he DOES have a (COBOL) program that
:>>:>will handle SWA=ABOVE.

:>>I didn't know that COBOL could do a call by address.

:>For any of the LE COBOL compilers:

:>      set procedure-ptr-item to entry 'subroutine'
:>      call procedure-ptr-item using ...

:>where "procedure-ptr-item" is declared something like:

:>     01  proceure-ptr-item procedure-pointer.

:>Or, in general, if you can get any entry point address into procedure-ptr-item
:>(passed as an argument, say), you can call specifying that name.

So SWA access can be called by COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I get DSN into COBOL Program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-Flour <gsf@home.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T12:36:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b600ee4$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<1743c876.0107240902.384b93f5@posting.google.com> <pnfrltoq75rkmlksqkkfl15rboe556ho78@4ax.com> <9jku3v$6vf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <cqtslt83tbmbuicmq1ajjrd214envo1q9j@4ax.com>`

```
On 25 Jul 2001 at 10:40, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> said:

>On Tue, 24 Jul 2001 17:48:32 -0500 "William M. Klein" wrote:

>:>Gilbert Saint-Flour has indicated that he DOES have a (COBOL) program
>:>that will handle SWA=ABOVE.

>I didn't know that COBOL could do a call by address.

You don't need to CALL anything to handle SWA=ABOVE.

>:>When/if he makes it available, I will post a reference to it.

>Would be interesting.

The COBOL code uses the same algorithm as the REXX function called SWAREQ which is available in the Tools&docs section of my Web page.  The algorithm hasn't changed since SWA=ABOVE was introduced in 1987.

 Gilbert Saint-flour
 Automated Migration Services
 http://members.home.net/gsf/
```

```
z/Architecture, you will have to go back and revisit the control blocks
you are using.

Jim Steelman wrote:

> A couple of years ago I was able to use the following COBOL subprogram
> to return a list of DDnames and DSnames to a main program. This has
> stopped working with the new release of OS/390. Can someone suggest
> the alteration needed to this program or an alternative way for me to
> get the DSN for a particular DD statement?

Regards,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I get DSN into COBOL Program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T14:03:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B5ED1EC.C93FB5AC@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<1743c876.0107240902.384b93f5@posting.google.com>`

```


Jim Steelman wrote:

> A couple of years ago I was able to use the following COBOL subprogram
> to return a list of DDnames and DSnames to a main program. This has
> stopped working with the new release of OS/390. Can someone suggest
> the alteration needed to this program or an alternative way for me to
> get the DSN for a particular DD statement?

I use the following, created by Gilbert Saint-Flour:    I just tested it
and it works (I use it to check my DD names to find out what database I am
running against).


000010  CBL XREF,MAP
000100 CBL NODYNAM,NOOPT,NOSSRANGE
000110*NODMLIST
000200 TITLE 'RETRIEVE JOB/STEP NAME PLUS DATASET INFO.'
000300 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000400
000500 PROGRAM-ID.  DDNAME.
000600*---------------------------------------------------------------*
000700*                                                               *
000800*  USING POINTERS, RETRIEVE OPERATING-SYSTEM INFORMATION AND    *
000900*  RETURN IT TO THE CALLER.                                     *
001000*                                                               *
001010*  IT ONLY LOOKS AT THE DDNAME ASSOCIATED WITH THIS JOB STEP    *
001020*                                                               *
001030*  IT DOES NOT YET HANDLE CONCATENATED DATA SETS.               *
001040*                                                               *
001100*---------------------------------------------------------------*
001200     EJECT
001300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
001400 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001500
001600 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
001700 FILE-CONTROL.
001800
001900 DATA DIVISION.
002000 FILE SECTION.
002100
002200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002300* BEGINNING OF WORKING STORAGE
002400*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002500 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
002600
002700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002800* DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS
002900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
003000
003100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE 'DDN-SUBS-PNTRS**'.
003200
003300 01  DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS.
003400     10  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X.
003500         15  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER       POINTER.
003600
003700     10  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER           POINTER.
003800     10  DDN-TIOT-BINARY  REDEFINES  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
003900                                        PIC S9(09)  COMP.
004000
004100     10  DDN-JFCB-POINTER               POINTER.
004200     10  DDN-JFCB-X       REDEFINES  DDN-JFCB-POINTER.
004300         15  FILLER                     PIC X(01).
004400         15  DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3           PIC X(03).
004500     EJECT
004600
004700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
004800* WORK-MISCELLANEOUS
004900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
005000
005100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE '**WORK-MISC*****'.
005200
005300 01  WORK-MISCELLANEOUS.
005400     10  WORK-BINARY-2               PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE ZERO.
005500     10  FILLER  REDEFINES WORK-BINARY-2.
005600         15  WORK-BINARY-2L          PIC X(01).
005700         15  WORK-BINARY-2R          PIC X(01).
005710
005800 01  SWITCHES.
005810     05  SW-MAIN-SWITCH              PIC X(01)  VALUE 'N'.
005820     88  SW-MAIN                                VALUE 'Y'.
005900     EJECT
006000*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006100*  LINKAGE AREA
006200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006300 LINKAGE SECTION.
006400
006500 01  LINK-COMM-AREA.
006600     10  LINK-JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
006700     10  LINK-STEP-NAME          PIC X(08).
006800     10  LINK-DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
006900     10  LINK-DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
007000     10  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER      PIC X(08).
007100     10  LINK-CATALOG-IND        PIC X(01).
007200         88  LINK-FILE-IS-CATALOGED  VALUE 'Y'.
007300     10  LINK-FILE-TYPE          PIC X(01).
007400         88  LINK-FILE-IS-GDG        VALUE 'G'.
007500         88  LINK-FILE-IS-JES        VALUE 'J'.
007600         88  LINK-FILE-IS-PDS        VALUE 'P'.
007700         88  LINK-FILE-IS-REGULAR    VALUE 'R'.
007800     10  LINK-VOLUME-COUNT       PIC 9(01).
007900     10  LINK-VOLUMES            PIC X(30).
007910 01  PASSED-RECORD REDEFINES LINK-COMM-AREA.
007920     05  PASSED-LENGTH     PIC 9(4)  COMP.
007930     05  PASSED-UPDATE-PARM.
007940         10  PASSED-LABEL  PIC X(14).
007950***      10  FILLER        PIC X(14) VALUE 'PASSED DDNAME='
007960         10  PASSED-DDNAME PIC X(08) VALUE 'NAME=='.
007970     05  FILLER            PIC X(99).
008000
008100 01  L02-TCB-POINTER                 POINTER.
008200
008300 01  L03-TCB.
008400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(012).
008500     10  L03-TIOT-POINTER            POINTER.
008600
008700 01  L04-TIOT.
008800     10  L04-JOB-NAME                PIC X(08).
008900     10  L04-STEP-NAME               PIC X(08).
009000
009100 01  L04-TIOT-START                  PIC X(24).
009200
009300 01  L04-TIOT-SEGMENT.
009400     10  L04-TIOT-LENGTH             PIC X(01).
009500     10  FILLER                      PIC X(03).
009600     10  L04-DD-NAME                 PIC X(08).
009700     10  L04-JFCB-ADDRESS            PIC X(03).
009800
009900 01  L05-JFCB.
010000     10  FILLER                      PIC X(16).
010100     10  L05-DS-NAME                 PIC X(44).
010200     10  L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER           PIC X(08).
010300     10  L05-DMI-BYTE                PIC X(01).
010400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(33).
010500     10  L05-DSTYPE-BYTE             PIC X(01).
010600     10  FILLER                      PIC X(30).
010700     10  L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE       PIC X(01).
010800     10  L05-VOLUME-LIST             PIC X(30).
010900     EJECT
011000 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-COMM-AREA.
011100
011120     IF  PASSED-LABEL  = 'PASSED DDNAME='
011121         MOVE SPACES                 TO PASSED-LABEL
011130         SET SW-MAIN                 TO TRUE
011140     END-IF
011200     MOVE X'0000021C'                TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X
011300     SET ADDRESS OF L02-TCB-POINTER  TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
011400     SET ADDRESS OF L03-TCB          TO L02-TCB-POINTER.
011500     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT         TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
011600
011700     MOVE L04-JOB-NAME               TO LINK-JOB-NAME.
011800     MOVE L04-STEP-NAME              TO LINK-STEP-NAME.
011900     INITIALIZE LINK-DS-NAME  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER LINK-CATALOG-IND
012000                LINK-FILE-TYPE LINK-VOLUME-COUNT LINK-VOLUMES.
012100
012200****  IF THEY DID NOT SUPPLY A DD NAME, EXIT STAGE LEFT.
012300
012400     IF LINK-DD-NAME < 'A'
012500        GOBACK
012600     END-IF.
012700
012800     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DDN-JFCB-X.
012900     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-START   TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013000     SET DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER        TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013100     ADD 24                          TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY.
013200     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
013300
013400****  LOOP THROUGH ALL POINTERS TO THE DD NAMES.
013500****  WHEN WE GET A MATCH, MOVE IN ALL THE FILE DATA AND EXIT.
013600
013610****  PERFORM UNTIL DD-NAME IS BLANK
013700     PERFORM UNTIL L04-TIOT-LENGTH = LOW-VALUES
013800        MOVE L04-JFCB-ADDRESS           TO DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3
013900        SET ADDRESS OF L05-JFCB         TO DDN-JFCB-POINTER
014000        IF L04-DD-NAME = LINK-DD-NAME
014100           MOVE L04-DD-NAME             TO LINK-DD-NAME
014200           MOVE L05-DS-NAME             TO LINK-DS-NAME
014300           MOVE L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER       TO LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER
014400           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'79' AND < X'90'
014500              MOVE 'Y'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014600           ELSE
014700              MOVE 'N'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014800           END-IF
014900           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'
015000              MOVE 'J'                  TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015100           ELSE
015200              IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'00'
015300                 MOVE 'R'               TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015400              ELSE
015500                 IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'01'
015600                    MOVE 'P'            TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015700                 ELSE
015800                    IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'02'
015900                       MOVE 'G'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016000                    ELSE
016100                       MOVE '?'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016200                    END-IF
016300                 END-IF
016400              END-IF
016500           END-IF
016600           MOVE L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE   TO WORK-BINARY-2R
016700           MOVE WORK-BINARY-2           TO LINK-VOLUME-COUNT
016800           MOVE L05-VOLUME-LIST         TO LINK-VOLUMES
016810           IF SW-MAIN
016811               DISPLAY
016812               'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
016813               'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTCVVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016814               'VVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016815               DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
016820           END-IF
016900           GOBACK
017000        ELSE
017100           ADD 20                    TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY
017200           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT
017300                                     TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
017400        END-IF
017500     END-PERFORM.
017501
017510     IF SW-MAIN
017520         DISPLAY
017530         'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
017540         'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTC'
017560         DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
017570     END-IF.
017600
017700     GOBACK.
017800*------------ END OF SUBROUTINE DDNAME -------------------------*


Calling program is

000100*NODMLIST
000200 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000300 PROGRAM-ID.    BRAZEE.
000400 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000500
000600 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000700 IDMS-CONTROL SECTION.
000800 PROTOCOL.    MODE IS BATCH-AUTOSTATUS DEBUG
000900              IDMS-RECORDS MANUAL.
001000
001100 DATA DIVISION.
001200 FILE SECTION.
001300
001400
001500 SCHEMA SECTION.
001600
001700 DB     IASSS302 WITHIN IAZSC001.
001800
001900 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
002000
002100     COPY ABEND01.
002200
002300     COPY IDMS SUBSCHEMA-CONTROL.
002400
002500     COPY IDMS IASRCEM.
002510
002520 01  DISPLAY-LINE.
002530     10  FILLER       PIC X(13)   VALUE ', RUNNING IN '.
002540     10  DL-CV        PIC X(5)    VALUE ' CV '.
002550     10  FILLER       PIC X(6)    VALUE ' MODE.'.
002560 01  DDNAME           PIC x(8)    value 'DDNAME'.
002600
002610 01  FIND-COMM-AREA.
002620     10  FIND-JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
002630     10  FIND-STEP-NAME          PIC X(08).
002640     10  FIND-DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
002650     10  FIND-DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
002660     10  FIND-GDG-OR-MEMBER.
002661         15  FIND-SI             PIC X(02).
002662         15  FIND-CV-NO          PIC X(02).
002663         15  FIND-CV-L1-L2       PIC X(04).
002670     10  FIND-CATALOG-IND        PIC X(01).
002680         88  FIND-FILE-IS-CATALOGED  VALUE 'Y'.
002690     10  FIND-FILE-TYPE          PIC X(01).
002691         88  FIND-FILE-IS-GDG        VALUE 'G'.
002692         88  FIND-FILE-IS-JES        VALUE 'J'.
002693         88  FIND-FILE-IS-PDS        VALUE 'P'.
002694         88  FIND-FILE-IS-REGULAR    VALUE 'R'.
002695     10  FIND-VOLUME-COUNT       PIC 9(01).
002696     10  FIND-VOLUMES            PIC X(30).
002697
005000
005091 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
005200
005300 000-MAIN-section    SECTION.
005301 000-MAIN.
005310     DISPLAY              ' STARTED ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (5:2)
005320                          '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (7:2)
005330                          '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:4)
005340                          ' ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (09:2)
005350                          ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:2)
005360                          ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (13:2)
005370
005400***  COPY    IDMS     SUBSCHEMA-BINDS.
005500
005600***  READY   IASAR101-REGION USAGE-MODE IS RETRIEVAL.
005800
005810     DISPLAY ' '
005900     MOVE 'SYSCTL'         TO FIND-DD-NAME.
006100     CALL DDNAME        USING FIND-COMM-AREA.
006101     IF FIND-DS-NAME = SPACES
006102         MOVE 'LOCAL'            TO DL-CV
006103     END-IF.
006104
006105     MOVE 'SYSIDMS'        TO FIND-DD-NAME.
006106     CALL DDNAME        USING FIND-COMM-AREA.
006107
006120     IF FIND-DS-NAME = SPACES
006130         DISPLAY ' THIS JOB IS NOT USING THE DATABASE'
006140         GOBACK
006150     END-IF.
006160
006200     IF FIND-CV-NO = '50'
006201         DISPLAY ' THIS JOB IS RUNNING AGAINST PRODUCTION'
006202         DISPLAY-LINE
006203         GOBACK
006204     END-IF.
006205
006214     IF   FIND-CV-NO = '60'
006215     AND (FIND-CV-L1-L2 = 'L1'
006216     OR   FIND-CV-L1-L2 = 'CV')
006217         DISPLAY ' THIS JOB IS RUNNING AGAINST DEVELOPMENT'
006218         DISPLAY-LINE
006219         GOBACK
006220     END-IF.
006221
006230     IF   FIND-CV-NO = '60'
006240     AND (FIND-CV-L1-L2 = 'L2'
006241     OR   FIND-CV-L1-L2 = 'CVT')
006250         DISPLAY ' THIS JOB IS RUNNING AGAINST TESTPROD'
006260         DISPLAY-LINE
006261         GOBACK
006262     END-IF.
006263
006280     DISPLAY " I DON'T RECOGNIZE " FIND-GDG-OR-MEMBER
006290
006400     GOBACK.
007770
007800 IDMS-ABORT.
007810     DISPLAY 'IDMS-ABORT'.
007900     COPY ABEND.
008000     COPY    IDMS IDMS-STATUS.
008100
008200*
:
```

<a id="t-3a622ae2af"></a>
## Thread: Improved Comb Sort with pre-defined gap table — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Improved Comb Sort with pre-defined gap table

- **From:** jdveale@world.std.com (James D. Veale)
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T08:02:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GH0ro0.Lr2@world.std.com>`

```
The April 1991 edition of Byte Magazine described a "comb sort"
by Stephen Lacey and Richard Box.  The sort described was easy to
code, worked well with random number sequences,  and had an "N log(N)"
growth characteristic with execution times close to that of a heap
sort.

Although the original comb sort generally works well,  it is
vulnerable to seriously degraded performance with certain repetitive
input sequences. The improved comb sort uses a pre-defined gap table
and results in the following:

   - Effectively eliminates turtles for random input sequences.

   - Limits the worst case performance for repetitive input sequences
     to about twice that of normal sequences, as well as substantially
     reducing vulnerability to repetitive input sequences.

   - Slightly worse than N log(N) execution growth with increasing N.

For more details see:

     http://world.std.com/~jdveale/combsort.htm

     Sorry the pseudo-code isn't in a cobol style.  A newsgroup
     and web search produced a number of hits in the cobol community,
     so I thought it might be of interest.

NOTE: This message is a technical note to a technical community
about an algorithm I've found very useful for nearly a decade.
It addresses a recently encountered weakness which is apparently
not well known.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Thread: STD - (proposed) Revised FCD wording for EXIT, CONTINUE, etc — 2 msgs

### STD - (proposed) Revised FCD wording for EXIT, CONTINUE, etc

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-25T14:41:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jn7gq$cj3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who participated in the discussion of the wording for the
"transfer of control" rules related to EXIT (particularly EXIT
PARAGRAPH/SECTION), you should look at:

 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0492.doc

I believe that this reflects input from this NG, from the J4 and WG4 email
lists, and the actual "meeting discussion" going on in Portland this week.
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - (proposed) Revised FCD wording for EXIT, CONTINUE, etc

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T10:05:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0107260905.39a8e496@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9jn7gq$cj3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill - they discussed 492 but when I spoke on the telephone with Chuck
last night - they didn't like it.

Chuck is going to rework it again - and combine a couple of things
from other proposals.

In a nutshell (and it will probably change before anyone reads this):

The transfer of control correction is going to be accepted but put
somewhere slightly different than where I out it.

GR 10 and 11 will both be changed.  As I warned Rick - and noted in
our paper - the Note being Informative wasn't sufficient.

One (I don't have the paper in front of me) will be changed to use the
"phantom" paragraph idea that you suggested.  The other is more like
Rick's original rewording and Chucks first paper.

Anyway - we should see the approved wording tonight.  Don S has done a
tremendous job of keeping the updated documents available.
```

<a id="t-3f5f3d4d30"></a>
## Thread: "Terms" - not necessarily of "enderment" — 2 msgs

### "Terms" - not necessarily of "enderment"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T02:30:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jogvk$6mb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just thought that I would start a new thread giving my VERY personal
opinion on a few topics in a current (unnamed) thread.

1) For me, the term "vaporware" has a specific meaning.  It means software
(or hardware?) that a vendor markets (and possibly even sells) which is NOT
available to those who follow THAT vendor's rules for acquiring and
installing it.  This means that if someone SELLS me V1.2 of product XYZ and
all they can deliver to me is V1.1, then V1.2 is vaporware.  If they CAN
deliver V1.2 to me - but I don't have the environment (money? resources?
etc) to install and use it, that does NOT make it vaporware, unless they
sold/marketed it to me (or others) without telling me what would be required
to use it. (In that case, I consider this more "false/deceptive advertising
than "vaporware".)  If they offer me a BETA or "pre-release" copy of V1.2,
then (to me) V1.2 is still vaporware.

2) No one knows when what is currently a DRAFT ANSI and ISO Standard will
become an official Standard.  Certainly the best guess (and I agree with
this) is that it will be "about" December 2002 for ISO and very soon after
that for ANSI (possibly even simultaneous).  However, until the very last
approval step actually occurs NO ONE (no vendor, committee member, or other)
can claim "conformance" to what the final Standard will be.  They can claim
to conform to much (all? part?) of the current FCD (with or without some of
the TENTATIVE changes that are being worked on by J4 - but won't even be
looked at by WG4 until November).  On the other hand, there are some (but
certainly not all) features that have been pretty "stable" for several
iterations of the draft, and my best GUESS is that "we all" know how they
will end up (if/when the standard is approved).  Letting the readers of
comp.lang.cobol know when there is such a feature in the draft Standard to
meet their "real world" problems of today (as raised in this forum), seems
like a good and useful thing (to me) to do.  I have and will continue to do
this - even as I *vocally* oppose the current ANSI/ISO J4/WG4 "procedures"
which (again in my personal opinion) have done more harm than good.

When such features are mentioned, it is often useful to mention which
vendors already support such features (in part or whole) AND (again in my
opinion) to recommend that paying customers try and get their "vendors of
choice" to implement these specific features as quickly and completely (and
conforming to the latest draft Standard) as possible.

3) Data "validity" is (has been - always will be) a major issue in all
multi-program applications.  The draft Standard provides a number of ways in
which data integrity can (programmatically) be checked and hopefully be
improved.  NOTHING will ever make it possible to create a "large real-world
application" totally fool-proof - although various design and implementation
approaches AS WELL AS program "tools" (syntax) can help with this.

The draft Standard includes a VALIDATE feature that is (in my opinion)
particularly useful for this.  To the best of my knowledge the only existing
"production" implementation of this facility is for ICL - and even that is
not all that close to the current FCD.

Strongly typed items I think (but am not certain) are available in a couple
of implementations (Fujitsu V6.1 being one - NetExpress with ISO2000
directive possibly being another).  I can GUARANTEE that neither of these
are exactly what will be in the final version of the Standard, because the
last public review found some problems that are being "fixed" even as we
speak.  There was an excellent article in CobolReport a few months ago
talking about this feature - and anyone interested in it might want to look
at this.

Common Exception Handling, Standard Arithmetic, and a number of other new
and enhanced features may well help with "data integrity" (and
validation/validity) issues.  However, none of these are implemented in
their final Standard version (as far as I know) by any current COBOL
implementation.  This doesn't mean that existing COBOL programmers shouldn't
know about them - and possibly even structure new programs to take advantage
of such features (when/if their vendor of choice provides them).

4) There are advantages and disadvantages of changing "subject lines" and
snipping portions of text when replying to newsgroup messages.  I know that
recently one person thought that I was replying to the "original" point of a
thread when I thought my "snipping" made it clear that I was responding to a
later comment.  Certainly when you read someone's reply to a thread that you
started (or recently posted to), I would recommend that you look and see if
it was YOUR note that the response is directed to - or someone else's.  This
may or may not impact how you read the reply.

   ***

I hope that this note doesn't sound like I am trying to "waffle" too much on
multiple sides of issues, but did want to share MY opinions - without in any
way trying to say that other posters aren't entitled to their own.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Terms" - not necessarily of "enderment"

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T22:30:10+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b5ff470_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9jogvk$6mb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Good and reasoned response, Bill.

I believe I may have over-reacted and should withdraw the term "vapourware"
in connection with the proposed Standard (just out of respect for the effort
that has gone into it and with which I have never been at issue). I realise
(on clam reflection) that this is offensive and provocative and should not
have been surprised when it provoked an extreme reaction.

Like everyone else, I have days when I get tired of being constantly
attacked and I guess sometimes my frustration overcomes my better judgement.

Your post is reasoned and fair to all concerned. I'll leave it at that.

Pete.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9jogvk$6mb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T10:11:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_996142223@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-06500493f1"></a>
## Thread: Securing Sensitive Information — 12 msgs

### Securing Sensitive Information

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T11:46:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KqT77.20061$gj1.2004942@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Greetings All,

In my shop, we have some sensitive information that we are storing in a VSAM
file.  This information is stored in the clear.  The CICS transaction is
secure, but the VSAM file itself is not.  I've been considering some
different methods of securing this data, but I don't really know which is
the best way to go.  Encryption and VSAM passwords are the two options I've
considered so far.  Does anybody have any other ideas?  How have others done
this in the past?  Any input would be appreciated.

Thanks,

Paul
```

```
Paul Pigott <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>Greetings All,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>this in the past?  Any input would be appreciated.

My memory is, admittedly, porous but I recall that Mr Svaalgard's ETKPAK
contains an encryption-routine which encodes a text-string as a number;
that might be a place to begin.

DD
```

```
Trying to secure the data (a) on general principles, it's not data that
should be readily accessible , and (b) to protect it from employees who may
not be able to resist temptation.  My concern is that, even if we encrypt
the information and put a password on the dataset, what about backups? The
data is still readable in the backup as well.

Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Securing Sensitive Information

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T14:31:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0107261331.6ee4b08d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<KqT77.20061$gj1.2004942@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <O2U77.7502$ar1.24156@www.newsranger.com> <E7Z77.53375$C81.4597507@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Paul Pigott" <ppigott61@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<E7Z77.53375$C81.4597507@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...

It comes down to determining how sensitive the data *REALLY* is.  When
it is determined how sensitive the data is then you determine how to
secure the data.  "General principle" is pretty vague.  What would
happen if the data does get into the "wrong hands"?  How determined
are the wrong hands to get at it?  It is one thing to secure the data
from employees who can not resist temptation or just stumble upon
it(quite simple) and another when one is bound and determined to get
at it (let us say a foreign govt conducting deadly serious espionage).
 Most security situations fall into the former category and are
usually dealt with readily acessible security utilities (RACF has been
mentioned) or system functionality.

Is it possible to get the data out of the VSAM dataset (yes I know
that perhaps 2000 programs read that VSAM and it would take a major
effort to get the data into DB2 or whatever) and perhaps into a
Relational DB?  It is possible to get
real fine control on who/what can read data out of a relational DB.

It is my guess that existing system functionality will probably take
care of you.  Does your company run its own hardware or is it farmed
out?

Anyway
KA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Securing Sensitive Information

```
Yes, I think you're right.  It's possible I'm over-analyzing (I have been
known to do that every so often).  I think we'll start out with basic system
security, and see where it goes from there.

Thanks to all who replied :)

Paul

Kano Ako <kanoako@netnet.net> wrote in message
news:677e6c81.0107261331.6ee4b08d@posting.google.com...
> "Paul Pigott" <ppigott61@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:<E7Z77.53375$C81.4597507@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...
```
```
> Is it possible to get the data out of the VSAM dataset (yes I know
> that perhaps 2000 programs read that VSAM and it would take a major
> effort to get the data into DB2 or whatever) ....

Let me give  Mr. Pigott a head start on this project.

Start now, writing the VSAM data access as a dynamically called module,
using a syntax something like,

  CALL VSAM-ACCESS-PROGRAM USING ACTION,

INPUT-BUFFER,

RETURN-BUFFER,

RETURN-STATUS


 At this time,VSAM-ACCESS-PROGRAM will use conventional OPEN, READ, WRITE,
REWRITE, CLOSE, etc

Perhaps at at some point in the future, VSAM-ACCESS-PROGRAM will use EXEC
SQL CONNECT| SELECT| INSERT| UPDATE|DISCONNECT  END-EXEC.

The thing is, once all programs have been converted to use this syntax - on
whatever schedule is convenient - it really makes no difference "how" or
"where" the data are accessed. He'll be able to change the
VSAM-ACCESS-PROGRAM to read a DB2 database, an Internet URL or even another
VSAM file.

MCM

Hmm, might be fun to write a generic "skeleton" program to "replace vsam
access with DB2 access"... let me think on that...including replacements for
START, READ NEXT..?
```

#### ↳ Re: Securing Sensitive Information

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T03:39:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b60b6eb_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<KqT77.20061$gj1.2004942@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
First stop, RACF.

If it is "sensitive" it should be password protected.

Both Thane and the Doc have made points which are valid regarding who should
access it and how you might encrypt it. I am assuming you want access only
from those who are authorised, whether they are inside or outside the
organisation?

In this case physical password protection has to be the first stop, closely
followed by serious encryption...

I have dealt with similar problems in a major American Bank (based at the
time in South London) and the one inescapable conclusion I reached was this:

Dashwood's first law of system security:

"It is IMPOSSIBLE to totally protect a data asset from unauthorised access,
so long as there is LEGITIMATE access to it."

(The best you can do is make access to the stable as cumbersome as possible,
and close the door after the horse has bolted...At least know that the horse
has been taken, who had access to the stable at the time it disappeared, who
was responsible for locking the stable door, etc. ...and then look around
for someone riding a newly acquired horse...<G>)

Pete.


"Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
```

#### ↳ Re: Securing Sensitive Information

- **From:** "Robert Fish" <robert.fish@bluewin.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T22:43:10+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b65cc8a_3@news.bluewin.ch>`
- **References:** `<KqT77.20061$gj1.2004942@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I would hope that your security people would have the case covered. It's
normal to protect datasets at the generic HLQ level. If they have not got
this or something similar in place for your production files then your file
should be the least of your worries. If security is so bad at your site that
you have to think about things like that, then NB, make sure that the prying
eyes can't get to the CICS log file as that has all your updates on it (and
probably your screen input output images as well, I may stand corrected, I'm
IMS not CICS). Also make sure that when people carry your system over into
the test environment they make their own independent file, and that they
don't just take a production image copy. (That is far more common than one
would hope, no matter what the data protection people say).
"Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:KqT77.20061$gj1.2004942@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

VSAM

I've
```

<a id="t-974309a2f7"></a>
## Thread: Acucobol how check clients connected ? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Acucobol how check clients connected ?

- **From:** Mario Desiderio <mandm@flashnet.it>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T21:15:35+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B606C57.4C02A676@flashnet.it>`

```
Hello,

We use Acucobol in a Unix SCo Open Server, the clients are windows.

There are soem files in the unix system:

activelog*
acuserver*

specially activelog* file contains some data that seams to give info of
the client sessions connected.

Everytime a new cliet connects it logs something.

Can anybody please tell me how can I read this data in order to know
number of open session, what the clients connected are ?

Thanks,

Mario Desiderio
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol how check clients connected ?

- **From:** "Acucorp News" <shaun_gough@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T14:59:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1451C740A890D411B65C00104B95B1C02DC80A@ras.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3B606C57.4C02A676@flashnet.it>`

```
Dear Mario.

Activelog it is not a utility that is supplied by Acucorp, so I am unable to
provide you with any information concerning this program.

To find out how many users are using AcuServer, you just need to use
'acuserve -info'.   This will display the users that are currently connected
to AcuServer, along with a list of files that they are using, so that you
can keep watch of what files are currently be accessed.

I hope this helps.

Shaun Gough - Snr Technical Support Representative.

Technical Experts. Hear how Acucorp's technology users solve their business
problems. Discover tomorrow's technology trends from industry experts. To
find out more, please give Acucorp a call, 1-800-262-6585 or visit our Web
site at http://www.acucorp.com/confr/confr.html.
```

<a id="t-b8d2629d5b"></a>
## Thread: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400 — 6 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** brad.cook@aurora.org (Scooter DNeedle)
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T13:51:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```
I am writing out a packed decimal field on our os/390 mainframe that
has the following value for the number 1: 00 00 00 00 1C (in HEX)

The as/400 is expecting it to be: 00 00 00 00 1F (in HEX)

How can I force my os/390 COBOL program to write out: 00 00 00 00 1F,
instead of 00 00 00 00 1C.

Any help would be much appreciated

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T23:09:25+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gh43fp0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Jul 2001 13:51:06 -0700, Scooter DNeedle wrote:

>I am writing out a packed decimal field on our os/390 mainframe that
>has the following value for the number 1: 00 00 00 00 1C (in HEX)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks!

It looks like your field is defined S9(9) COMP-3.
Move it to a 9(9) COMP-3 field and you get rid of the sign.

Willem Clements
BrainBench MVP for Cobol II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T16:12:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jq15a$kbp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```
You aren't showing us the PICTURE clause (assuming this is COBOL in both
environments).  It sounds to me as if one is signed and one isn't - but that
is just a guess. (i.e that the OS/390 defines it as signed and the OS/400
defines it as unsigned)
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T00:30:44+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn11mt0i8ipj6o1trfodh4ncolmrccui55@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```

:>I am writing out a packed decimal field on our os/390 mainframe that
:>has the following value for the number 1: 00 00 00 00 1C (in HEX)

:>The as/400 is expecting it to be: 00 00 00 00 1F (in HEX)

:>How can I force my os/390 COBOL program to write out: 00 00 00 00 1F,
:>instead of 00 00 00 00 1C.

Have you tried an unsigned picture?
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T08:46:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jr659$14e4h$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```
I can't think why the AS400 would care from a decimal point of view.  The
only thing I can think of is the data is unformatted on the AS400 and this
is being treated as a string or part of a string, and is used in matching..
In that case obviously X'1C' is a different character to X'1F'.

If this is true then you have to fix it on the OS390.   I can't remember for
sure but I think that when a numeric string is first moved to a packed
decimal field it has an 'F' sign and only gets the 'C' sign if arithmetic is
done on it.  If this is the case, you might be able to fix it by moving the
field to a pic 9 field and back again.

If you had the reverse problem, and had to change the field signs from 'F to
'C',  just add zero to each field.

Rick

Scooter DNeedle <brad.cook@aurora.org> wrote in message
news:9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com...
> I am writing out a packed decimal field on our os/390 mainframe that
> has the following value for the number 1: 00 00 00 00 1C (in HEX)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>

```
Hi Scooter,

As Binyamin says use an unsigned pic, but you must move the signed field to it
in an elementary move to get the sign changed. 
Moving it as part of a group move won't do it.

Jack
```

<a id="t-51502af3fb"></a>
## Thread: XML parser called by a cobol program - fantasy or fact? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML parser called by a cobol program - fantasy or fact?

- **From:** Glazza@Glazza.co.uk (Glazza)
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T21:17:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns90EBE2D325A24NickGlasspoolvirginn@62.253.162.109>`

```
Anybody out there know of an xml parser that can be called by a Cobol 
program? Don't laugh but we have a requirement to validate xml which is 
contained within a "mainframe" file processed by a Cobol program.
A case of new technology in amongst the dinosaurs - trouble is, the 
dinosaurs ain't extinct yet! Having the file pre-processed by an xml parser 
on say a unix box before processing the file on the mainframe is not an 
option - I won't go into details, lets just say we are talking of millions 
of records processed per day and everything is time critical.

Any help would be appreciated

Glazza
```

#### ↳ Re: XML parser called by a cobol program - fantasy or fact?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-26T22:51:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v9187.86400$2V.19187930@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90EBE2D325A24NickGlasspoolvirginn@62.253.162.109>`

```
You could try http://www.xml4cobol.com. When last I looked it's premise was to provide a callable routine based on parsing specific DTDs, which is probably OK for a lot of mainframe applications. Features may have changed.

I wrote my own SAX-ish parser in COBOL because I wanted to handle XML Schema and provide some dynamic support for unrecognized DTDs. If 'twere me, I'd probably set up a java application on the mainframe that returns the data from an XML document as a COBOL structure, or use MQSeries to accomplish the same thing rather than trying to use something proprietary with an evolving technology like XML. Otherwise you're obsolete before you start.

- Karl
```

```
I'm not sure about how to do this in Power Cobol, but in
general, you can send a message to the listview that will
register a callback function to do your sorts--this gives you
absolute control over the row ordering.

A lesser, but simpler alternative, is to format the date such
that it alphanumerically sorts as well.  (ie, "yyyy/mm/dd")
```

```
I do not know how PowerCOBOL implements the listview - but if it uses

property is different than the actual displayed text and can be used to do the
sort.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bar codes

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-28T08:32:20+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B626A84.4D351A7D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xd487.4253$UH6.1009715@news02.optonline.net> <3B60F209.6C92@paralynx.com>`

```
Gary Romanowski wrote:
>
> I need to create bar codes on a HP laser using Net Express. Net Express does
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> up this info?

Barcodes are just black and white rectangles.  A simple code, such as
Code-39 (3 of 9) can be table driven once the required patterns are
known.  I found a public domain C program which did this some time ago
and converted it to Cobol in half a day.  To intermix text printing and
barcodes I delay the barcode printing until the end of the page and then
use positioning and draw rectangle codes to output the bars, this way I
don't lose control of what 'write before 1' is doing.

I can send you the C source and some notes.
```

#### ↳ Re: Bar codes

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-07-27T19:13:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kvm87.40814$eY6.4344640@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<Xd487.4253$UH6.1009715@news02.optonline.net>`

```
I have sent you a barcode font, let me know if you do not get it.
Just install it into your font directory, and use it to print.
```

<a id="t-d6dd3754b9"></a>
## Thread: Edited PICs — 11 msgs

### Edited PICs

- **From:** "bloggs" <bloggs@neil-1234.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T02:32:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
*******Homework Question**************
Hi,
    I defined two WS data items :-
01   ws-num-field        pic 9999v99.
and
01   ws-edited-field    pic 9(4).99.

I then coded the Procedure Division statements :-
 Procedure Division.
 only-para.
        move ws-num-field to ws-edited-field
*ws-num-field should contain 1234v56                               *
*yes I know the v is only an implied d.p. and that no            *
*internal storage is assigned to it
*
*and ws-edited-field should contain 1234.56, right?            *
my problem is this, I am using Micro"out of" Focus Personal COBOL
and when I "examine" the data in ws-numeric-field, after running the
program, it contains :
        3456.00
and ws-edited-field contains 3456.00 also.
-----------------------------------------------
Heres the actual code,
1  data division.
2  working-storage section.
3  01    ws-num-field            pic 9999v99    value 123456.
4  01    ws-edited-field         pic 9(4).99.
5*
6 procedure division.
7 only-para.
8*
9       move ws-num-field to ws-edited-field
10     display ws-edited-field
11     stop run.
* the display was added just to re-confirm that the data output was not as
required*
Somebody suggested to me that if I change the VALUE at line 3 to "1234.56"
the problem would cease.
Yes this solution worked but what if I wanted to keep the data in
ws-num-field strictly numeric?
Why are my leading 2 digits being lopped off?
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Bloggs sent you this e-mail :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T03:39:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aAL87.10171$ar1.32463@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Everything is behaving as it should.

Pic 9(4)v99 Value 1234.56.

when examined in memory will contain 123456.

The reason your example is truncated is because the compiler has to choose where
to align the implied decimal.  It uses the actual decimal from your VALUE clause
-- which is at the end -- So the result is just the same as if you had moved
123456 to this item -- it will contail 3456.



In article <9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>, bloggs says...
>
>*******Homework Question**************
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T03:43:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6386C6.FD0C8629@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


bloggs wrote:

> *******Homework Question**************
> Hi,
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bloggs sent you this e-mail :-)

The various points made are valid.

Take both your fields :-
3  01    ws-num-field            pic 9999v99    value 123456.
                                            REAL VALUE = 3456.00.
4  01    ws-edited-field         pic 9(4).99.    value 123456.
                                            REAL VALUE = 3456.00.

That's a very basic thing about COBOL when MOVING,  the receiving field must
be big enough to take the number, plus the sending number must also contain a
decimal point/implied decimal point, when appropriate.(Also don't forget about
negative signs).

move 1,234,567,890      to num3     (pic 9(03).)          = 890
move 1,234,567,890.12 to num3d2 (pic 9(03)v9(02)  = 890.12

Go back and read your notes or manuals on MOVING numbers.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T12:52:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0GT87.10414$ar1.32588@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B6386C6.FD0C8629@home.com>`

```
This is a subtle point - but the "MOVE" rules do not always apply to "VALUE"
clauses.  Reading about "MOVE" in relation to "VALUE" won't always lead to
getting the right answer.

Case in point:

01  Some-Edited-Field Pic 999,999.99  Value 12345678.

This gives you a field that contains "12345678  " and not
"123,456.78" as you would expect if you applied the things you read about"MOVE".

In order to get the value you expect you have to code:

01  Some-Edited-Field Pic 999,999.99 Value "123,456.78".

While in the present situation - reading about truncation as related to MOVE
gives the same result, there are actually separate rules for MOVE and VALUE.


In article <3B6386C6.FD0C8629@home.com>, James J. Gavan says...
>
>
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T15:16:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k1r01$pbr$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B6386C6.FD0C8629@home.com> <0GT87.10414$ar1.32588@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane - my friend,
  Can we say "nonconforming source code" (aka extensions to the '85
Standard) ???

1) Page VI-48, SR(3) says that the value clause *must not* cause truncation.
Therefore,

      01 ws-num-field   pic 9999v99  123456.
          is NON-conforming (although I suspect many/most compilers accept
it and DO truncate)

2) Page VI-48 GR(1b) says that a numeric-edited field must have an
alphanumeric literal in its VALUE clause.  Therefore,
       01  Some-Edited-Field Pic 999,999.99  Value 12345678.
            is NON-conforming (and I think that only a LIMITED number of
compilers accept it)

        ***

In both cases, if your compiler does accept it, then they should provide a
DOCUMENTED extension telling what they do with the results - and results are
(at least theoretically) non-portable.  Also any ANSI/ISO/FIPS flagging
should detect the syntax "extension".

NOTE: The 2nd "item" has (correctly - IMHO) been changed to a syntax rule in
the FCD - so flagging will be required there too.  It took me a while to
confirm that it is correct in the FCD, because the '85 Standard talks about
the category of the data item while the FCD talks about its class.  However,
I think the "results" are the same/compatible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T13:01:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B655AFA.A3D80EA6@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B6386C6.FD0C8629@home.com> <0GT87.10414$ar1.32588@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> This is a subtle point - but the "MOVE" rules do not always apply to "VALUE"
> clauses.  Reading about "MOVE" in relation to "VALUE" won't always lead to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "123,456.78" as you would expect if you applied the things you read about"MOVE".

I don't understand.  If I moved 12345678 to Some-Edited-Field, I would expect to see
345678.00


> In order to get the value you expect you have to code:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> gives the same result, there are actually separate rules for MOVE and VALUE.

It seems that he was expecting them to be different, but they were the same.  How
are these rules different?
```

#### ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T10:57:07+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gh8pj70.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 29 Jul 2001 02:32:31 +0100, bloggs wrote:

>*******Homework Question**************
>Hi,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi,

If you define a field 9(4)V99 you are defining a field with a maximum value
of 9999.99. Moving a higher value, in your case 123456 will chop off the
first two positions.
If you use value 1234.56 you ARE using a strictly numeric value and the data
in ws-num-field is strictly numeric (123456 and the compiler assuming the
last two positions to be decimals)..
The VALUE clause is the same as using a MOVE statement

Hope this helps

Willem Clements
BrainBench MVP for COBOL II
http://www brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T21:05:28+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iu98mtsbg1j0ujq4n54okskblcadd0rvb9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 29 Jul 2001 02:32:31 +0100 "bloggs" <bloggs@neil-1234.freeserve.co.uk>
wrote:

:>*******Homework Question**************

Noted.

:>Hi,
:>    I defined two WS data items :-
:>01   ws-num-field        pic 9999v99.
:>and
:>01   ws-edited-field    pic 9(4).99.

:>I then coded the Procedure Division statements :-
:> Procedure Division.
:> only-para.
:>        move ws-num-field to ws-edited-field
:>*ws-num-field should contain 1234v56                               *
:>*yes I know the v is only an implied d.p. and that no            *
:>*internal storage is assigned to it
:>*
:>*and ws-edited-field should contain 1234.56, right?            *
:>my problem is this, I am using Micro"out of" Focus Personal COBOL
:>and when I "examine" the data in ws-numeric-field, after running the
:>program, it contains :
:>        3456.00
:>and ws-edited-field contains 3456.00 also.
:>-----------------------------------------------
:>Heres the actual code,
:>1  data division.
:>2  working-storage section.
:>3  01    ws-num-field            pic 9999v99    value 123456.
:>4  01    ws-edited-field         pic 9(4).99.
:>5*
:>6 procedure division.
:>7 only-para.
:>8*
:>9       move ws-num-field to ws-edited-field
:>10     display ws-edited-field
:>11     stop run.
:>* the display was added just to re-confirm that the data output was not as
:>required*
:>Somebody suggested to me that if I change the VALUE at line 3 to "1234.56"
:>the problem would cease.

Exactly.

:>Yes this solution worked but what if I wanted to keep the data in
:>ws-num-field strictly numeric?
:>Why are my leading 2 digits being lopped off?

The value field is strictly numeric even with the "."

It is exactly the equivalent of

       MOVE  1234.56 TO ws-num-field

at the beginning of your program.

Numeric literals are allowed to have periods and are treated as strictly
numeric.
```

#### ↳ Re: Edited PICs

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T19:02:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25Z87.10665$ar1.32693@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9jvp2v$usf$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi Bloggs,

To summarize the discussion: Don't confuse the notation for the value of the
field with the content of the field.
1234.56 is just the notation used by the compiler to decimal-align the field
properly in memory.

Regards, Jack.
```

```
examine it, you will find it is also offset.   Put a decimal in the value
clause.
```

<a id="t-c94f4fe9c1"></a>
## Thread: text file format — 6 msgs

### text file format

- **From:** "Ray Gurganus" <ray@<nospam>gurganus.org>
- **Date:** 2001-07-28T22:01:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net>`

```
I need to produce a text file to be imported into someone else's database,
and from the specs I have, it appears to be COBOL?  At least that's what it
looks like from some web searching I've done.

I list some of the stuff below, and I'm supposed to output a text file in
this format.  Could someone translate this into English for me?  Thanks.

fd  assrecdc
    record contains 823 characters
    value of filename    is assrecdc-fil
             library     is assrecdc-lib
             volume      is assrecdc-vol
    label records are standard.
01  assrecdc-record.
    05 as-key.
       07 as-caseno                    pic x(010).
    05 as-affiliate.
       07 as-a-name1                   pic x(025).
       07 as-a-name2                   pic x(025).
       07 as-a-addr1                   pic x(025).
       07 as-a-addr2                   pic x(025).
       07 as-a-csz                     pic x(025).
       07 as-a-phone                   pic x(025).
    05 as-affil redefines as-affiliate pic x(025) occurs 8.
    05 as-airport                      pic x(003).
    05 as-cityst                       pic x(028).
```

#### ↳ Re: text file format

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-28T19:56:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B637B5F.7C16@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net>`

```
Ray Gurganus <ray@ wrote:
> 
> I need to produce a text file to be imported into someone else's database,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>     05 as-cityst                       pic x(028).

Assuming that the field names are meaningful to you, these are just alphanumeric fields 
of the lengths in the pic x(XXX) bits. Consider the other lines like "sub headings" in 
the description.

You'll need to know exactly what the user wants for a label record and add this at the 
front and you won't want to write CR/LFs at the end of the line unless they ask you to.

Ignore the line with "redefines", it's just another view of the "as-affiliate" group but 
I'd ask why the redefines is 8 occurrences (in most other languages that's just called 
an 8 element array) when the bit being redefined is only 6.
```

#### ↳ Re: text file format

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T19:05:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_7Z87.5847$bl1.890520@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net>`

```

"Ray Gurganus gurganus.org

  Could someone translate this into English for me?  Thanks.

The neat thing about COBOL is that it IS in ENGLISH.

"Pic" is an abbreviation for PICTURE - the byte layout of the field;
"X" means alphanumeric; (nn) means repeat 'nn' times. What else do you
need?

I assume you have some data: with the data, the record layout, and the
above clues, you should be able to decode the data.

>
> fd  assrecdc
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: text file format

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T21:34:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k555q$2lldi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net>`

```

Ray Gurganus gurganus.org> <ray@<nospam> wrote in message
news:g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net...
> I need to produce a text file to be imported into someone else's database,
> and from the specs I have, it appears to be COBOL?  At least that's what
it
> looks like from some web searching I've done.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This layout doesn't even make sense.  The RECORD CONTAINS clause tells you
that the record created will be 823 bytes long.  The record layout, as
shown, is 191 bytes long (and includes an invalid redefinition).

As it sits though, you appear to have the following fields:

case number - 10 bytes
name 1 - 25 bytes
name 2 - 25 bytes
address line 1 - 25 bytes
address line 2 - 25 bytes
city/state/zip - 25 bytes
phone number - 25 bytes
airport code - 3 bytes
city/state (airport?) - 28 bytes

You should have the files, as well as the rest of the layout, to figure out
the rest...
```

#### ↳ Re: text file format

- **From:** "Ray Gurganus" <ray@--nospam--gurganus.org>
- **Date:** 2001-07-31T00:41:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QFq97.184$ia5.27574@news.abs.net>`
- **References:** `<g9K87.27$NC.18814@news.abs.net>`

```
Thanks, all.  The code that I posted is not complete, as I didn't want to
type it all in.  So this would account for the numbers not adding up.  So
I'll go with it and see what they say with what I generate....  Thanks again
```
