<a id="t-6f1bb7b730"></a>
## Thread: Strange change in PERFORM UNTIL in COBOL/390 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Strange change in PERFORM UNTIL in COBOL/390

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-07-28T20:16:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0107281916.fd15cc0@posting.google.com>`

```
I just sent this e-mail to Ann Bennett at IBM.....

Dear Ann,

The following is a current thread in Tek-tips General COBOL Forum.
http://www.tek-tips.com/viewthread.cfm?SQID=112570&SPID=209&page=1&CFID=13808766&CFTOKEN=72178597

The IBM COBOL/390 Migration guide (GC26-4764-05)
appears to forbid "combined  conditions" in PERFORM .... UNTIL    This
was NOT in GC26-4764-04.
It now reads:

| PERFORM statement--second UNTIL 

| OS/VS COBOL allowed a second UNTIL in a PERFORM statement, as in 
| the following example: 


 |             PERFORM CHECK-FOR-MATCH THRU CHECK-FOR-MATCH-EXIT 
 |                 UNTIL PARM-COUNT = 7 
 |                 OR SSREJADV-EOF.


| IBM COBOL does not allow a second UNTIL statement. It must be 
| removed.

On-line Bookmanager has not yet updated the Language Reference to
release 05, and 04 allows combined, complex and combined, complex
conditions.
  

I re-read the draft standard, and it specifically allows combined,
complex and combined, complex conditions.

Looks to me as if someone at IBM misread a standard.

This will break a LOT of production code if it remains in IBM COBOL.

Please let me know if I have misunderstood something, or, if IBM will
change this back.

Stephen J Spiro


*--------------------------------------------
RoseAnnM (Programmer) Jul 27, 2001 
We are beginning a conversion to Cobol OS/390. I read in the migration
manual that a perform until condition 1 or condition 2 is not allowed.
Does anyone know if this is the case?

*-----------------------------------------------

 
StephenJSpiro (Programmer) Jul 27, 2001 
I checked the IBM COBOL/390 Conversion manual, and could not find the
rule you state. Could you give me a section reference?

In "6.2.27.4 PERFORM with UNTIL phrase", 
The COBOL/390 Language Reference diagram shows "UNTIL condition-1"
only, but explains further that "condition-1" can be any type of
condition in "6.1.6 Conditional expressions", which includes  combined
conditions (condition-1 and/or condition-2), as well as complex
conditions.

If you can show me where to look up your rule, I will investigate
further.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
check it out at
http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/
and
http://www.merant.com/Standards/

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
 

*----------------------------------

 
RoseAnnM (Programmer) Jul 27, 2001 
I found the reference in the Cobol for OS/390 IBM Compiler and
Run-Time Migration Guide (GC26-4764-05) page 126.
Thanks for your help. 

*----------------------------------------

 
StephenJSpiro (Programmer) Jul 27, 2001 
You must have a brand-new book; the IBM versions on the web are
GC26-4764-04.  It must be a brand-new rule. Can you quote the section,
please?

I just checked the Draft Standard, and the rule has NOT changed there;
it can be as I desribed in the last post: combined  and complex
conditions are allowed.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
check it out at
http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/
and
http://www.merant.com/Standards/

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
 


*----------------------------------------------------

 
NinaToo (Programmer) Jul 27, 2001 
This is news to me. Because I just wrote a program in VS COBOL II
which contains a ton of PERFORM UNTIL paragraphs where the PERFORM
UNTIL reach either one of 2, or sometimes 3 or sometimes even more,
conditions. I don't think that COBOL OS/390 is any different.

The program is in our final testing and will soon go into production.

You have to be very careful about how you word the conditions, but you
can have as many conditions as you wish, I believe.

Nina Too 

*------------------------------------------------

 
slade (Programmer) Jul 28, 2001 
Hi All,

The Section Rose Ann cites is 4.1.6 and it calls multiple UNTIL
conditions "an undocumented extension" and states that it "must be
removed". Are we misinterpreting? Did IBM err? Is it just a bad dream?

I use multiple UNTIL conditions all the time, and I'm sure others do
too.

Regards, Jack.
    

*----------------------------------------------------

 
StephenJSpiro (Programmer) Jul 28, 2001 
It appears to me that IBM has screwed up. I will contact the IBM
Standards person on J4 and see what I can find.
 
Stephen J Spiro
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange change in PERFORM UNTIL in COBOL/390

- **From:** Leonard Lehew <leonard.lehew@nc.EXTRAJUNK.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T14:31:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kda8mtodjan282mjlnbn2ijrj2f57k8rdl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0107281916.fd15cc0@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Jul 2001 20:16:52 -0700, stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J
Spiro) wrote:

>
>| OS/VS COBOL allowed a second UNTIL in a PERFORM statement, as in 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I believe the problem here is with the example in the manual. The
example in the manual is still valid in OS/390 COBOL. My recollection
is that old versions of COBOL supported constructs like the following:

             PERFORM CHECK-FOR-MATCH THRU CHECK-FOR-MATCH-EXIT 
                 UNTIL PARM-COUNT = 7 
                 UNTIL SSREJADV-EOF.

In other words, two separate UNTIL clauses were permitted on a single
PERFORM statement.

Leonard
```

<a id="t-4ea9fcb5e1"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Datatype Translation — 16 msgs

### Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** Gary Scott <scottg@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T05:53:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net>`

```

Hi,

I need to read in an MVS "binary" file (via Visual Fortran) created by
Cobol with a data
item(s) defined by:

PIC ... S9(005) COMP-3

The record description states that this takes 3 bytes and is signed.

Is that basically a definition for an integer with a range of -99999 -
+99999 stored in 20 bits with the rightmost 4 bits of the 3rd byte
representing the sign (sorry, don't know Cobol, just trying to interpret
from Murach's "Structured Cobol")?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-28T23:33:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B63AE4C.CAD@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net>`

```
Gary Scott wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> from Murach's "Structured Cobol")?

In most implementations this is "packed decimal", which you might want to look up.

Basically, it squeezes 2 decimal digits into each byte (i.e. one digit in each nibble) 
except the last one which holds the sign in the low order nibble (a "nibble" is 4 bits, 
or half a byte - we were all great punsters 30 years ago).

You are right about the magnitude, but not about the storage method - it's not a 20 bit 
binary.  You'll have to "unpack" it nibble by nibble. The easiest way may be to do take 
each byte and do a /16 to get the high order nibble's decimal value and a %16 to get the 
low one. I'm not sure whether Visual Fortran (my Fortran stopped at Fortran IV) can bit 
twiddle, but I'm sure it can do modulo arithmetic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** Gary Scott <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T08:54:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6415A5.5075C69@lmtas.lmco.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net> <3B63AE4C.CAD@paralynx.com>`

```
Thanks very much.  Yes bit twiddling is standard in Fortran 95.  It has
come a long way since Fortran IV:  free form source, a step towards
object orientation with modules, derived types, array syntax, and
overloading.  It's not your father's Fortran (or yours apparently) :-).

Ed Guy wrote:
> 
> Gary Scott wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>  and ParseRat, the File Parser, Converter and Reorganizer"
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** Gary Scott <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T08:58:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net>`

```
Hi,
A followup:  This storage format does not seem to make very efficient
use of most systems native number crunching abilities.  Is this
definition intended for storage compactness or data portability?  Does
that not slow down the processing of large datasets?

Gary Scott wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** "Charles Godwin" <charles@godwin.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T10:39:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LcV87.4346$uH4.172992@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net> <3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com>`

```
The IBM mainframe has native opcodes / machine instructions for working with
pack numeric data. The chips are fast and efficient and include invalid
numeric hardware interrupts. I believe the data format was designed to allow
for arbitrary sized compressed business numeric data when approximations
implemented by floating point would not do the job.
"Gary Scott" <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com> wrote in message
news:3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com...
> Hi,
> A followup:  This storage format does not seem to make very efficient
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T18:57:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w0Z87.5823$bl1.869263@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net> <3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com>`

```
Just the reverse.

The earliest 360 mainframes had circuitry to handle packed decimal
values up to a whole bunch of digits of precision (I seem to remember
18): far, far more than double-precision in Fortran. Remember, for the
most part, early mainframes did commercial work.
```

```
Well, of course.

The PC generation tend to think in terms of colors and JPGs and
counting mouse movements and stuff like that. Decimal notation was
some human evil that one must visit on user input and user output, not
something that could (or should) be native to the machine.

I meant to say that the rationale for getting a mainframe in the first
place was to do commercial work. As such, the mainframe had to be able
to handle commercial - base 10 - arithmetic.
```

```
Hi Gary.  Packed-decimal is a natural native number storage for IBM
S/390 mainframes (and it's predecessors), which is probably why it came
to be defined in COBOL.  In other words, S/390 machine code has
instructions to handle packed-decimal as easy (or easier? I don't know
much about assembler) as binary data.

Gary Scott wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks
```

```
megabyte mainframe dump of accounting data, it takes way too long to
crunch it on my puny little PC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T21:03:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B64DC82.3EAB@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net> <3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com> <3B64BE0E.ED9E0AEB@flash.net>`

```
Gary Scott wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> crunch it on my puny little PC.

With today's PC speeds I'd be surprised if the conversion routines are the bottleneck, 
you'll more likely be I/O bound.

I just repeated a test I did for a ParseRat user with a 1040 character input record 
length and 95 fields. The conversions were from a binary file to an ASCII delimited 
string file with some being binary-number-to-string conversions and others simple string 
extractions. On a 455 MHz PC it did 1627 records in ten seconds (almost 10,000 records 
per minute) and I'm sure I/O was the limiting factor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Gary Scott <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T09:05:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6569C4.B35BA53@lmtas.lmco.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net> <3B641689.4D9953B5@lmtas.lmco.com> <3B64BE0E.ED9E0AEB@flash.net> <3B64DC82.3EAB@paralynx.com>`

```
Hi,
Yes, likely I/O bound, however conversion using Fortran formatted reads
is also somewhat slow.  I'll probably change it to use stream I/O
("binary") into a single data item of sufficient size to hold one
complete record.  This usually significantly improves I/O performance. 
Then, change it to only convert the particular portion of the record
that I'm using to sort on and only convert the remaining parts of the
record if it is the record of interest. 

Ed Guy wrote:
> 
> Gary Scott wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>  and ParseRat, the File Parser, Converter and Reorganizer"
```

```
> Hi,
> Yes, likely I/O bound, however conversion using Fortran formatted reads
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
*-----------------------------------

On MOST mainframes, record size is not important (unless the records
are "spanned", i.e., too large for a block).  The crucial factors in
I/O management are blocksize and number of buffers.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

```
The question was about using Visual Fortran (I think) on a PC to do the conversion. The 
speed limiting factor is likely the actual volume of bytes per second processed through 
the disk controller.

I'll agree that on a mainframe the situation is different.  I once worked with a man who 
would bet people that he could double their application speeds by adjusting JCL alone, 
and he didn't lose many bets.
```

- **From:** Gary Scott <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T15:12:52-05:00

```
Hi, the source has been downloaded on to a PC.  The PC performance
problem is largely the result of my reading the file in as a series of
separate data items rather than as a single 135 byte stream.  I've shown
about a 20% increase in performance by reading multiple whole records
before performing the datatype conversions.

Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> 
> Gary Scott <Gary.L.Scott@lmtas.lmco.com> wrote
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Datatype Translation

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T19:36:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mBZ87.10678$ar1.32543@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net>`

```
Hi Gary, 

The only thing you left out is that each 4 bit group (nibble) except for the
sign nibble represents a decimal digit (0-9).
And if the data is EBCIDIC the sign nibble is hex C, A, E or F (positive) ; D or
B (negative).

Regards, Jack.  

In article <3B63A526.67F44A1D@flash.net>, Gary Scott says...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks
```

<a id="t-838e388ffe"></a>
## Thread: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs — 16 this month / 26 total

### Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** Steven Lucas <smlucas@flashmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T19:02:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com>`

```
I work for a state agency and have worked on a Windows version of an
existing DOS program created by an older version of Micro Focus COBOL.
The 2.4 version of MF COBOL would not install on my Win 98 machine
flashing some error during the installation process and would not
install at all.  So we get a few copies of Net Express at $3500 and
proceed to make my program.  After finishing the debugging, I make my
static build executable, copy the necessary files and try running it on
another computer.  It doesn't work, it wants dsgrun.  Then it wants a
few other modules as well.  After linking those in on a static build, it
wants pan2nt.dll.  After emailing Merant and getting a call back, we
find out an outrageous detail.  ANYONE who wants to run any programs
created by Net Express has to shell out $80-$90 for the runtime licenses
or do without.  This is a load of CRAP!  What do you think would happen
to Microsoft if THEY required you pay a sizable sum of money to run any
program someone created with Visual Basic or Visual C++?  The government
would be on their butts a LOT LOT worse than they are now.  And the
salesmen who sold us the copies of Net Express said NOTHING about it,
and the documentation says NOTHING about it.  What in the world is the
benefit for such a setup like it?  Its only for Merant trying to squeeze
a few more bucks out of people like they aren't making enough from
copies of the software or selling tech support for $600 bucks a year.

So my question is this...is there any way to make a Windows COBOL
program with Net Express and not have to install the run-time stuff on
other computers for it to run?  And certainly to avoid all of our
providers from having to pay this cost for a Windows program that they
have for free in a DOS program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T12:42:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b64acc4_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com>`

```
Can you start to understand why COBOL is "dying"?

Why would a developer use it when he is clobbered every which way with
costs?
(With VB and C(++) there is no Runtime charge; with Java the whole thing is
FREE...

Although I am a firm believer in using the best tools for the job (and
sometimes COBOL is the BEST tool), as a small developer I have to be pretty
sure there is a return on investment before I can commit to spending
thousands of dollars for a COBOL development environment, and then  being
hit again for every customer I manage to find...)

Fujitsu (to their credit) do NOT charge a license fee (at the moment) for
their Runtime. (maybe you could get your source compiled with Fujitsu...?)

I'm not sure what the position is with AcuCOBOL. It might be good for
representatives of various vendors to state in this NG what their current
and proposed Company policy will be regarding Runtime licenses. (I bet they
won't...<G>) Maybe some Users can do it for them.

We know the position with Fujitsu and Merant, can anyone tell us about other
versions they are using?

Like you, I hanker for the old days when there WAS no runtime, you could
statically link your code into an EXE and distribute it. Last time I
remember doing this was on 16 bit MicroFocus... for Win 3.1.

You could understand the need for a "RunTime" if everything was compiled to
intermediate code then JIT linked when it was loaded (as is planned with
.NET). This would allow components and subroutines to be developed in
whatever language was most suitable because the object code would be the
same intermediate code from ALL compliant compilers. As this is NOT the case
currently, we have to ask ourselves WHY a Runtime is necessary, instead of
optionally static linking all necessary code...

I believe you may have answered this question...<G>

Pete.


"Steven Lucas" <smlucas@flashmail.com> wrote in message
news:3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com...

…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T22:51:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k2ll20i50@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <3b64acc4_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in
message news:3b64acc4_7@goliath.newsgroups.com...
> I'm not sure what the position is with AcuCOBOL. It might be
good for
> representatives of various vendors to state in this NG what
their current
> and proposed Company policy will be regarding Runtime
licenses. (I bet they
> won't...<G>) Maybe some Users can do it for them.

AcuCobol's current scheme is $55/1st user + $11/add'l user.
What's more, you're even further from a stand-alone executable.
The compiler essentially generates a document file that's
interpreted by the runtime system.  (You can compile to native
code, but it won't be linked into an executable though.)

> We know the position with Fujitsu and Merant, can anyone tell
us about other
> versions they are using?

CA Realia, IBM Visual Age COBOL, and Fujitsu all make compilers
that do not incur additional runtime fees.  Fujitsu, though,
appears to be the only one that's targetting PC development.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T18:32:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kLh97.11780$ar1.35966@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <3b64acc4_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
Well- Development tools aren't cheap anymore. Even VB and Visual C++ can get
very expensive.  If you want the whole thing from MS (Who has taken to grouping
everything plus the kitchen sink together) you are going to pay upwards of
2000.00 (MSDN Universal).  For Java development (While Java is free - I would
never write Java without an IDE!) the costs can be staggering.  

For the small developer these costs are a killer.  I have fought this battle
since my days using CA-Realia COBOL.  I bought a C compiler from Borland for <
80.00, but to do COBOL it cost be over 900.00.  That's chicken feed today.  Pick
up a copy of Programmers Paradise and compare the costs of tools.

Now - a solution for this indivual might be to use the free Fujitsu compiler and
convert dialects from Merant to Fujitsu.  The compiler is also available
packaged with many books like my Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours, COBOL for
Dummies, COBOL Unleashed, Mastering COBOL and the like. 

When considering COBOL compilers - here's the state of affairs as related to
Runtimes - as I know them.

Note:  This is US Centric - some vendors might charge runtimes in Europe and
other contries, but not in the US.

Fujitsu - No runtime fee for the single threaded runtime.  Enterprise Edition
users are exempt from the Multi-Threaded runtime fee.  There are runtime fees
for optional things like PowerBSort (even in the US).

Merant - Runtime fees are confusing.  There's a special allowance for small
developers upgradeing from NetExpress 3.0 to 3.1 - but no allowance that I am
aware of for new 3.1 customers.  For Server Express licensing - running on a
server - it's EXPENSIVE (Around $10,000 per server).

RM - There is a per user runtime fee.
AcuCorp - There is a per user runtime fee.

PerCOBOL - No runtime Fee
CA-Realia COBOL - No Runtime Fee

TinyCOBOL (Free/Open Source/Linux) No runtime Fee

ICOBOL - There is a runtime fee

Objective COBOL - (From Brazil - Unknown)

IBM Visual Age COBOL - I've heard both, but I think it's No Runtime Fee (but you
have to RENAME the modules so the user can't easily tell they are from IBM)






In article <3b64acc4_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood says...
>
>Can you start to understand why COBOL is "dying"?
…[82 more quoted lines elided]…
>-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

#### ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T20:50:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k2elh$eqp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com>`

```
Steven,
  Just so you understand (and there was a LONG thread about this "feature"
of current NetExpress earlier this year in comp.lang.cobol),  if you DO find
a way of creating a "stand-alone" executable with NetExpress, the current
MERANT position is that you still MUST pay the run-time fee to be "legal"
(according to their current licensing agreements).

FYI,
  the $80-$90 that you quote is actually BETTER than what they originally
claimed you would need to pay.  Furthermore, they made it pretty clear that
they did NOT commit to keeping the price "this low" with future run-time
fees.

As Pete indicated, Fujitsu has a quite different position on this.

My understanding (and someone from MERANT is certainly welcome to contradict
this) is that MERANT is targeting "large enterprise" users and is NOT
targeting "small vendors" of sold/distributed applications.

All of this *might* change in August when MERANT no longer will own "Micro
Focus" - but that is totally a GUESS and not based on any public or private
information.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** Steven Lucas <smlucas@flashmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-29T21:24:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B64C57A.D0C8AF71@flashmail.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <9k2elh$eqp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

>   Just so you understand (and there was a LONG thread about this "feature"
> of current NetExpress earlier this year in comp.lang.cobol),  if you DO find
> a way of creating a "stand-alone" executable with NetExpress, the current
> MERANT position is that you still MUST pay the run-time fee to be "legal"
> (according to their current licensing agreements).

Like I said earlier...if Microsoft did this they'd have worse trouble with the
government.

> FYI,
>   the $80-$90 that you quote is actually BETTER than what they originally
> claimed you would need to pay.  Furthermore, they made it pretty clear that
> they did NOT commit to keeping the price "this low" with future run-time
> fees.

How much would THAT be?

> My understanding (and someone from MERANT is certainly welcome to contradict
> this) is that MERANT is targeting "large enterprise" users and is NOT
> targeting "small vendors" of sold/distributed applications.

My firm is about 25 employees.  Which makes us a small vendor, but the freeware
program I have is to be given to as many as 300 locations.  My supervisor is
pissed off that they neglected to inform him of this BEFORE he bought the
product.  This is borderline bait-n-switch.

> All of this *might* change in August when MERANT no longer will own "Micro
> Focus" - but that is totally a GUESS and not based on any public or private
> information.

They better change...if they want to sell any more copies of this software.
Personally, I would never buy the thing...it's a rip off.  Seeing Merant
unloading MF COBOL after acquiring it not too long ago shows they've badly
messed up themselves with their mishandling of the product.
```

```
about to change. I can't help think that Mr. Hill is quite aware of the problem and
is prepared to do whatever it takes to regain market share. But thats me talking
not him!
```

```
Steven Lucas <smlucas@flashmail.com> wrote in message
news:3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com...
>....  So we get a few copies of Net Express at $3500 and
> proceed to make my program.....  After emailing Merant and getting a call
back, we
> find out an outrageous detail.  ANYONE who wants to run any programs
> created by Net Express has to shell out $80-$90 for the runtime licenses
> or do without....

Someone has to ask:  didn't you read the "terms and conditions" BEFORE you
chose Net Express as your development platform?

If the requirement for a run-time license was in fact hidden, you probably
have something you can take to the FTC or your corporate attorney, but I've
never heard the requirement for a run-time license for Microfocus/Merant
being anything other than a "known cost of deployment."

(Disclaimer: I have not read any MF/Merant sales material in about eight
years, so I cannot claim familiarity with the 'current' materials.)
```

## Thread: Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines?

- **From:** "Guian Smith" <gsmith14@nyc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T04:23:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com>`

```
I am trying to call a C function, packaged in a DLL, from Realia-II
Workbench COBOL program. The DLL works fine when I call it from another C
program, but the COBOL program fails to link - I get an unresolved external
reference. The DLL was created with Visual C++ 6, and I copied the DLL and
associated LIB file into the same directory as my COBOL program, and even
included the Lib file in the Link/Libraries option setting.

I believe I am following the calling conventions described in the Realia
documentation - so can anyone tell me what I might be doing wrong?

Here is the COBOL program and the DLL:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.   TESTCALL..
 AUTHOR.       G SMITH.
 DATE-WRITTEN. JULY 18, 2001.
 DATE-COMPILED.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

  01 P1             PIC X(80).
  01 WS-INT-VAL     PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
  01 WS-DISP-INT    PIC 99999.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 100-MAINLINE.

     MOVE 'Calling program argument' TO P1
     DISPLAY 'CALLING EXTERNAL FUNCTION...'
     CALL 'C_FUNC1' USING BY REFERENCE P1 GIVING WS-INT-VAL
     MOVE WS-INT-VAL TO WS-DISP-INT
     DISPLAY 'RETURNED: ' WS-DISP-INT.
     GOBACK.

 100-EXIT.
     EXIT.

C program:
// CobLib.cpp : Test DLL to be called from Realia COBOL programs
//

#include "stdafx.h"

#define COBLIB_API   __declspec(dllexport)

BOOL APIENTRY DllMain( HANDLE hModule,
                       DWORD  ul_reason_for_call,
                       LPVOID lpReserved)
{
  switch (ul_reason_for_call)
 {
  case DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH:
  case DLL_THREAD_ATTACH:
  case DLL_THREAD_DETACH:
  case DLL_PROCESS_DETACH:
   break;
  }

  return TRUE;
}

COBLIB_API int FUNC1(char *CobVar)
{
 return 42;
}


And here is the linker error message...

============ Begin Link Step ================
Link as EXE
Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

D:\CICSCBK\Work\Obj\testcall.OBJ
/OUT:D:\CICSCBK\Exe\testcall.EXE
C:\CAWB30\lib\carclw60.lib
d:\MyProjects\CobLib\Debug\CobLib.lib
testcall.OBJ : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _FUNC1
D:\CICSCBK\Exe\testcall.EXE : fatal error LNK1120: 1 unresolved externals
--------------------------------------------------------
..Link Error WBL006- Link Step Failed.  Please examine
..the linker output (above) for details
--------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines?

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T00:13:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k2qf20nlv@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com>`

```
I wish I could give you more specific info...

Given what you've said, my first suspicion would lie with the
way in which VC++ is name-mangling the exports.  Do the names in
a DUMPBIN of your DLL and the linker's error message match up?

"Guian Smith" <gsmith14@nyc.rr.com> wrote in message
news:gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com...
> I am trying to call a C function, packaged in a DLL, from
Realia-II
> Workbench COBOL program. The DLL works fine when I call it
from another C
> program, but the COBOL program fails to link - I get an
unresolved external
> reference. The DLL was created with Visual C++ 6, and I copied
the DLL and
> associated LIB file into the same directory as my COBOL
program, and even
> included the Lib file in the Link/Libraries option setting.
>
> I believe I am following the calling conventions described in
the Realia
> documentation - so can anyone tell me what I might be doing
wrong?
>
> Here is the COBOL program and the DLL:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> // CobLib.cpp : Test DLL to be called from Realia COBOL
programs
> //
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> testcall.OBJ : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol
_FUNC1
> D:\CICSCBK\Exe\testcall.EXE : fatal error LNK1120: 1
unresolved externals
> --------------------------------------------------------
> ..Link Error WBL006- Link Step Failed.  Please examine
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines?

- **From:** "Robert Stankowic" <pcdoktor@netway.at>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T12:31:13+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k3db5$fjv$1@news.netway.at>`
- **References:** `<gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com>`

```

"Guian Smith" <gsmith14@nyc.rr.com> schrieb im Newsbeitrag
news:gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com...
[....]

Guian,
with filename extension .cpp your compiler compiles it as C++ code,
thus mangling function names.
Tere are two ways out:
1) rename your file to CobLib.c and check your project settings
2) See my additions in your code...

> C program:
> // CobLib.cpp : Test DLL to be called from Realia COBOL programs
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

extern "C"
{

/*this prevents the name mangling, don't forget the closing } at the
end*/

> BOOL APIENTRY DllMain( HANDLE hModule,
>                        DWORD  ul_reason_for_call,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

}

HTH, HAND

-- .
Robert Stankowic
pcdoktor@netway.at
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines?

- **From:** "Guian Smith" <gsmith14@nyc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T14:18:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m1e97.22125$I5.4864088@typhoon.nyc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com> <9k3db5$fjv$1@news.netway.at>`

```
Thank you for yor help - I really appreciate it!

Here's a follow-up report:
I renamed the source to coblib.c, and that seems to have the same effect as
putting 'extern "C"' in the cpp source - it eliminated the name-mangling in
the exported LIB symbol - but then I got the following rather nasty-looking
error when Realia tried to link coblib.lib with the COBOL program:

D:\CICSCBK\Programs\TestCall\CobLib.lib : fatal error LNK1106: invalid file
or disk full: cannot seek to 0x3b656c75


Since the DLL and LIB files looked OK (DUMPBIN had no problem reading them),
and of course the disk was not full, I tried replacing Realia's older
(version 4) LINK.EXE and LIB.EXE with the current version 6 programs. And
now it works! Thanks again for the help
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia-II COBOL calling C subroutines?

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-07-30T12:02:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k43v602bfe@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<gj597.45676$16.6396407@typhoon.nyc.rr.com> <9k3db5$fjv$1@news.netway.at> <m1e97.22125$I5.4864088@typhoon.nyc.rr.com>`

```

with FSC because i can't read the old data files maked with MS-ISAM.
```

```
Knowledge Systems Corporation is looking for a Cobol programmer for a
"Technical Product Developer".  This is an intermediate position.

The position is in Memphis, TN and is a 6 month contract.

This position performs complex development
activities which may require extensive research and analysis. This job
is
under general supervision of a line manager or project lead.

ï¿½ High experience with IBM COBOL programming language and MVS
environment
ï¿½ Required high working knowledge of IBM COBOL programming knowledge
ï¿½ Required high working knowledge of IBM mainframe environment (MVS,
JCL,
VSAM)
ï¿½ Knowledge of IBM CPCS application is a plus.

You will:

ï¿½ Performs design, implementation, and maintenance of multiple
software or
systems in accordance with product specification.
ï¿½ Participates or conducts product level design meeting with
development
team.
ï¿½ Writes code, performs unit tests, performs system tests, and
participates
in system integration tests.
ï¿½ Supports the installation and implementation of software into
customer's
test and production environment.
ï¿½ Investigates, analyzes, and develops appropriate solutions to
reported
problems when required.

Please let me know if you would be interested in this position in
Memphis,
TN.

If so, please send your resume to hleague@ksc.com
thank you,
Heather League
KSC
hleague@ksc.com
919-522-0750
```

```
experience.  $50 is not bad for that level, even in NY (direct would

Processing and Control System, a bank package).... not many know it,
and the ones who do are expensive.
```
